_note: Detailed remediation knowledge base. Per-check and per-CVE entries carry a long-form observation (risk), a 15-20 step remediation, platform-correct CLI, verification, rollback and operational impact. Family-prefix entries (e.g. MGMT) are fallbacks.

MGMT.risk: Weak or unencrypted device management (missing enable secret, Type-7/cleartext passwords, no AAA, Telnet or SSHv1, unrestricted VTY, HTTP management) lets an attacker reach the control plane, brute-force or sniff admin credentials, and take administrative control of the device.
steps: Set an encrypted enable secret and encrypt all plaintext passwords.
Enable AAA and require authenticated, authorized, accounted login.
Force SSHv2 and remove Telnet/HTTP; restrict VTY with an access-class ACL.
Apply an exec-timeout on all lines and disable the AUX port.
MGMT.gui: N/A (CLI-managed). On DNA Center / device manager, apply the corresponding device-hardening template.
MGMT.cli:
enable secret <STRONG-SECRET>
service password-encryption
aaa new-model
aaa authentication login default group tacacs+ local
ip ssh version 2
line vty 0 4
 transport input ssh
 access-class MGMT-ACL in
 exec-timeout 5 0
no ip http server
ip http secure-server
line aux 0
 no exec
 transport input none
MGMT.verify: show run | include enable secret|service password-encryption|aaa new-model|ip ssh version ; show run | section ^line vty ; show ip ssh
MGMT.rollback: Reverse each change with its 'no' form (e.g. 'no ip ssh version 2', 'no aaa new-model'). Note: removing AAA can lock you out — keep a console session open.
MGMT.impact: Non-disruptive to data-plane traffic. Administrators must switch to SSH; verify the MGMT-ACL permits the admin subnets BEFORE applying access-class to avoid lockout.
references: Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices

CRYPTO.risk: Weak cryptographic posture (SSHv1, small RSA keys, weak DH groups, cleartext/Type-7 secrets, SNMP v1/v2c) allows management sessions and community strings to be intercepted or brute-forced.
steps: Generate a >=2048-bit RSA key and force SSH version 2.
Replace SNMP v1/v2c with SNMPv3 authPriv.
Use 'enable secret' (Type-8/9 scrypt/pbkdf2 where supported) rather than Type-7.
CRYPTO.cli:
crypto key generate rsa modulus 2048
ip ssh version 2
ip ssh server algorithm encryption aes256-ctr aes192-ctr
snmp-server group NSSGRP v3 priv
snmp-server user NSSUSER NSSGRP v3 auth sha <AUTHPW> priv aes 256 <PRIVPW>
CRYPTO.verify: show ip ssh ; show crypto key mypubkey rsa ; show snmp user
CRYPTO.rollback: Regenerate keys / remove SNMPv3 config with 'no' forms as required.
CRYPTO.impact: Regenerating the RSA key drops active SSH sessions briefly. Non-disruptive to data plane.

CTRL.risk: An unprotected control plane (no CoPP, unauthenticated routing adjacencies, unauthenticated NTP, no BPDU Guard, CDP/LLDP leaking topology) lets an attacker DoS the CPU, inject false routes, or map the network.
steps: Apply Control Plane Policing (CoPP) to rate-limit control traffic.
Authenticate routing protocols (OSPF/EIGRP MD5/SHA, BGP password).
Authenticate NTP and enable BPDU Guard on access edge; disable CDP/LLDP on untrusted ports.
CTRL.cli:
! OSPF authentication
router ospf 1
 area 0 authentication message-digest
interface <IF>
 ip ospf message-digest-key 1 md5 <KEY>
! BGP
router bgp <ASN>
 neighbor <PEER> password <KEY>
! NTP + edge
ntp authenticate
ntp authentication-key 1 md5 <KEY>
ntp server <IP> key 1
spanning-tree portfast bpduguard default
no cdp run
CTRL.verify: show policy-map control-plane ; show ip ospf interface brief ; show ntp status ; show spanning-tree summary
CTRL.rollback: Remove authentication keys / CoPP policy with 'no' forms. Coordinate routing-auth changes with peers to avoid adjacency loss.
CTRL.impact: Routing-auth changes must be applied to BOTH peers or the adjacency drops. CoPP mis-scoped policies can drop legitimate control traffic — test in a window.

DATA.risk: Weak data-plane hardening (no uRPF/anti-spoofing, IP source routing, ICMP redirects, missing edge ACLs) enables source-address spoofing, traffic redirection, and reconnaissance.
steps: Enable unicast RPF on external interfaces.
Disable IP source routing and ICMP redirects/unreachables on edge interfaces.
Apply anti-spoofing ingress ACLs at the perimeter.
DATA.cli:
no ip source-route
interface <EXT-IF>
 ip verify unicast source reachable-via rx
 no ip redirects
 no ip proxy-arp
DATA.verify: show run | include ip source-route ; show ip interface <EXT-IF> | include verify|redirect|proxy
DATA.rollback: Re-enable with 'no ip verify ...' / 'ip redirects' as needed.
DATA.impact: uRPF strict mode can drop asymmetrically-routed traffic — use 'reachable-via any' where asymmetric routing is expected.
references: Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices

SVC.risk: Unnecessary services (small-servers, finger, BOOTP, PAD, MOP, unused HTTP/DHCP) expand the attack surface and have historically carried remote-exploitable bugs.
steps: Disable all unused management/discovery services.
Disable TCP/UDP small servers and legacy protocols.
SVC.cli:
no service tcp-small-servers
no service udp-small-servers
no ip finger
no service finger
no ip bootp server
no service pad
no mop enabled
SVC.rollback: Re-enable a specific service only if a documented dependency requires it.
SVC.impact: Non-disruptive on modern networks; confirm no legacy device depends on BOOTP/MOP before disabling.
references: Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices

SW.risk: Weak L2 hardening (DTP auto-trunking, default native VLAN, no port-security, no DHCP snooping / Dynamic ARP Inspection) enables VLAN hopping, MAC flooding, rogue DHCP, and ARP spoofing.
steps: Set access ports to mode access and disable DTP negotiation.
Enable port-security on access ports; move the native VLAN off VLAN 1.
Enable DHCP snooping and Dynamic ARP Inspection on user VLANs.
SW.cli:
interface range <ACCESS-PORTS>
 switchport mode access
 switchport nonegotiate
 switchport port-security
 switchport port-security maximum 2
 switchport port-security violation restrict
ip dhcp snooping
ip dhcp snooping vlan <USER-VLANS>
ip arp inspection vlan <USER-VLANS>
SW.verify: show interfaces status ; show port-security ; show ip dhcp snooping ; show ip arp inspection
SW.rollback: Remove with 'no switchport port-security' / 'no ip dhcp snooping' as needed.
SW.impact: port-security violation shutdown can err-disable a port on a MAC change; 'restrict' is safer. Configure DHCP-snooping trust on uplinks/server ports first or DHCP breaks.

WIFI.risk: Weak wireless security (WEP/WPA-TKIP, PSK on enterprise SSIDs, no 802.11w management-frame protection, no rogue/WIDS) lets an attacker crack keys, deauth clients, or stand up an evil-twin AP.
steps: Use WPA2/WPA3-Enterprise (802.1X) and remove WEP/TKIP/PSK on corporate SSIDs.
Enable Protected Management Frames (802.11w).
Enable rogue-AP detection / aWIPS.
WIFI.gui: Catalyst 9800 WebUI: Configuration > Tags & Profiles > WLANs > Security.
WIFI.cli:
wlan CORP 1 CORP
 security wpa wpa3
 security wpa akm dot1x
 security pmf mandatory
 no security wpa wpa2
 no shutdown
wireless wps rogue detection
WIFI.verify: show wlan summary ; show wlan id 1 ; show wireless wps summary
WIFI.rollback: Restore the prior security profile; re-enabling WPA2 personal is discouraged.
WIFI.impact: Changing SSID security re-authenticates all clients on that WLAN; schedule a window. 802.11w mandatory can drop legacy clients.

NGFW.risk: Weak firewall policy (any-any permit rules, no threat inspection, no logging on ACEs, unrestricted management/VPN) allows unfiltered lateral movement and hides malicious traffic.
steps: Remove permit ip any any rules; scope source/destination/service to least privilege.
Enable IPS/malware/URL inspection and log on deny (and key permit) rules.
Restrict device management and VPN to trusted sources.
NGFW.gui: FMC: Policies > Access Control; ASDM: Configuration > Firewall > Access Rules.
NGFW.cli:
! ASA — replace the any-any rule with a scoped one
no access-list OUTSIDE_IN extended permit ip any any
access-list OUTSIDE_IN extended permit tcp <SRC> host <DST> eq <PORT> log
access-group OUTSIDE_IN in interface outside
! FTD/Firepower — set inspection + logging in the Access Control Policy (FMC)
NGFW.verify: show access-list ; show run access-group ; (FMC) review the Access Control Policy rule table
NGFW.rollback: Restore the previous ACL/rule set from the saved config backup.
NGFW.impact: Tightening ACLs can drop production flows — validate against connection logs / flow data in a maintenance window before removing the broad rule.

LOG.risk: Without centralized logging, accurate timestamps, and synchronized time, malicious activity is invisible and incident forensics/attribution are impossible.
steps: Send logs to a central syslog/SIEM collector at an appropriate level.
Enable millisecond timestamps and log the source interface.
Synchronize and authenticate time via NTP.
LOG.cli:
logging host <SIEM-IP>
logging trap informational
logging source-interface <MGMT-IF>
service timestamps log datetime msec show-timezone
service timestamps debug datetime msec
ntp authenticate
ntp server <NTP-IP> key 1
LOG.verify: show logging ; show ntp status ; show run | include service timestamps
LOG.rollback: Remove with 'no logging host <ip>' / 'no ntp server <ip>'.
LOG.impact: Non-disruptive. Ensure the syslog collector can absorb the volume; 'informational' can be chatty on busy devices.

CISCO-CVE.risk: The device is running a software train affected by a published Cisco PSIRT advisory. Depending on the CVE, an attacker may achieve remote code execution, authentication bypass, privilege escalation, or denial of service.
steps: Identify the exact running version (show version).
Consult the referenced Cisco Security Advisory and upgrade to its first-fixed release for your train.
If patching must be deferred, apply the advisory's mitigation and/or disable or ACL-restrict the affected feature (e.g. WebVPN, HTTP server, Smart Install).
CISCO-CVE.gui: Cisco Software Download / DNA Center SWIM for image upgrade; feature toggles per platform manager.
CISCO-CVE.cli:
show version
! example interim mitigations (feature-dependent):
no ip http server
no ip http secure-server
no vstack           ! disable Smart Install
! ASA/FTD: restrict or disable WebVPN per the advisory until patched
CISCO-CVE.verify: show version ; (after upgrade) confirm the build is at or above the advisory's first-fixed release
CISCO-CVE.rollback: Roll back to the previous image from bootflash/disk0 if the upgrade regresses; re-enable a feature only if required and patched.
CISCO-CVE.impact: A software upgrade REBOOTS the device — schedule a maintenance window and ensure HA/redundancy. Disabling WebVPN/HTTP drops those services until the fix is applied.

CRYPTO-001.risk:
The scanner parsed the running-config with the regex `crypto key generate rsa.*modulus\s+(\d+)`, extracted the modulus integer, and fired at HIGH because the captured value is below 2048 bits (e.g. 512 or 1024). This line is normally the archived/scripted key-generation command that produced the device's SSH host key pair, so a modulus under 2048 means the SSH server is very likely running a weak host key today.

RSA moduli of 512 and 768 bits are factorable with modest compute, and 1024-bit RSA is deprecated by NIST SP 800-131A (disallowed after 2013 for new keys) and considered feasible for well-resourced adversaries. A weak host key undermines the authenticity guarantee of SSH: an attacker who factors the modulus can impersonate the device.

Concrete attack: an on-path adversary (ATT&CK T1557 Adversary-in-the-Middle, aided by T1040 Network Sniffing) presents a forged host key derived from the factored modulus, terminates the admin's SSH session, harvests the login/enable credentials (T1110/T1078), and proxies traffic to the real device — the admin sees no host-key-mismatch warning because the forged key validates.

Blast radius: full administrative compromise of the network device, which on a core switch/router or WLC means control over routing, ACLs, VLANs, and the ability to pivot laterally or persist. A compromised management plane is effectively total device compromise.

Platform nuances: `crypto key generate rsa` is IOS/IOS-XE syntax (it is a privileged-EXEC action, not a stored config line, though it is often captured in build scripts or annotated configs — which is what the scanner keys on). Catalyst 9800 WLC runs IOS-XE and uses the identical command. AireOS WLC, NX-OS, and ASA/FTD use different key management and are out of scope for this check (SUPPORTED_PLATFORMS = ios, iosxe, wlc). Modern IOS-XE also supports EC keys via `crypto key generate ec keysize 384`, which are a stronger alternative.

Standards angle: maps to CWE-326 (Inadequate Encryption Strength), NIST SP 800-131A key-length requirements, and NIST 800-53 SC-12/SC-13. CIS Cisco IOS benchmarks require the SSH RSA modulus be >= 2048 bits.
steps: Pre-check: inventory the affected devices and confirm current key size with `show crypto key mypubkey rsa` — note the modulus/bit-length line and whether keys are named (label) or default (`<hostname>.<domain>`).
Confirm which key pair SSH actually uses with `show ip ssh` (shows SSH version and, on newer IOS-XE, the active host key) so you replace the correct pair.

Schedule a maintenance window: regenerating the RSA key tears down existing SSH sessions and forces every SSH client to re-accept a new host key (known_hosts change), so coordinate with anyone connected.
Ensure out-of-band/console access is available before you start, in case the SSH session drops during regeneration.
Enter global config: `configure terminal`.
Set the identity prerequisites if not already present: `hostname CORE-SW1` and `ip domain-name example.com` (default RSA keys are named after hostname+domain and cannot be generated without them).
Exit to privileged EXEC (`end`) because `crypto key ...` is an EXEC action, not a config-mode command.
Zeroize the weak key pair: `crypto key zeroize rsa` (or `crypto key zeroize rsa label <name>` for a named pair) — confirm the prompt.
Generate a strong pair: `crypto key generate rsa general-keys modulus 4096 label SSH-KEYS` (2048 is the minimum acceptable; 4096 preferred).
If you prefer elliptic-curve host keys on IOS-XE, instead run `crypto key generate ec keysize 384 label SSH-EC`.
Re-enter config and pin SSH to the new key if you used a label: `configure terminal` then `ip ssh rsa keypair-name SSH-KEYS`.
Enforce SSH v2 (which mandates a stronger key): `ip ssh version 2`.
Harden session parameters while here: `ip ssh time-out 60` and `ip ssh authentication-retries 3`.
Return to EXEC with `end`.
Validate the new key size with `show crypto key mypubkey rsa` and SSH state with `show ip ssh`.
Open a fresh SSH session from a management host to confirm connectivity with the new key before closing your console session.
Persist the change: `copy running-config startup-config` (or `write memory`).
Update client `known_hosts`/SSH fingerprints and any monitoring that pinned the old host key fingerprint.
CRYPTO-001.cli:
configure terminal
 hostname CORE-SW1
 ip domain-name example.com
end
! --- privileged EXEC (key ops are not config-mode) ---
crypto key zeroize rsa
crypto key generate rsa general-keys modulus 4096 label SSH-KEYS
configure terminal
 ip ssh rsa keypair-name SSH-KEYS
 ip ssh version 2
 ip ssh time-out 60
 ip ssh authentication-retries 3
end
copy running-config startup-config
CRYPTO-001.verify: `show crypto key mypubkey rsa` — the key header/usage line should report the new modulus size (look for `4096` bits). Then `show ip ssh` should report `SSH Enabled - version 2.0` and reference the new keypair. Opening a new SSH session must succeed after the client accepts the new host-key fingerprint.
CRYPTO-001.rollback: Regenerate a smaller key: `crypto key zeroize rsa` then `crypto key generate rsa modulus 1024`. Caveat: reverting to <2048-bit RSA re-exposes the device to host-key factoring and SSH server impersonation (adversary-in-the-middle), and breaks SSHv2's minimum-key requirement.
CRYPTO-001.impact: Disruptive but brief. Regenerating the host key immediately drops all active SSH sessions and every client must re-accept the new fingerprint; there is no reload and no data-plane/traffic impact. Console access is unaffected. Automation using stored host-key fingerprints (Ansible, monitoring) will fail until the new fingerprint is trusted.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 7.1 — SSH: set RSA modulus >= 2048
NIST SP 800-131A — Transitions: RSA key-length requirements
NIST SP 800-53 Rev.5 — SC-12 (Cryptographic Key Establishment) / SC-13 (Cryptographic Protection)
Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — Use SSHv2 and adequate key length

CRYPTO-002.risk:
This is a LOW-severity verifiability finding, not a confirmed weakness. The scanner found NO `crypto key generate rsa ... modulus N` line in the config (so it could not read the modulus) yet it DID match `^ip ssh version`, proving SSH is configured and in use. Because the RSA key material and its size are not represented in the running-config, the scanner cannot prove the host key meets the >= 2048-bit minimum, so it raises an advisory to force manual verification rather than silently assuming the key is strong.

This is the normal case on production devices: `crypto key generate rsa` is a privileged-EXEC action and the resulting key size is not stored as a config statement — it lives only in the key store. So a clean config here does not mean the key is safe; it means the auditor is blind to it. Treat this as 'unknown until proven', because a device could have been built years ago with a legacy 1024-bit or even 512-bit host key that still functions.

The underlying risk, if the key does turn out to be undersized, is identical to CRYPTO-001: a factorable RSA host key enables SSH server impersonation and adversary-in-the-middle (ATT&CK T1557) against administrators, leading to credential capture and full management-plane compromise.

Blast radius depends entirely on the actual key size, which is why verification is mandatory: an undersized key on a core device is a HIGH-impact exposure; a 4096-bit key is fine. The finding exists to close the visibility gap.

Platform nuances: applies to IOS/IOS-XE and Catalyst 9800 WLC (all share `show crypto key mypubkey rsa`). The check will not fire on ASA/FTD/NX-OS (out of scope). On IOS-XE the device may instead use an EC host key, which `show crypto key mypubkey rsa` will not display — check `show crypto key mypubkey ec` too.

Standards angle: relates to CWE-326 and NIST SP 800-131A; the control objective is verifiable assurance of key strength (auditability), aligned with CIS Cisco IOS SSH guidance.
steps: Log in to the device (SSH or console) with an account that has privileged EXEC.
Display the RSA host key and read its size: `show crypto key mypubkey rsa` — note the modulus/bit length reported in the key header.
If no RSA key is shown, check for an elliptic-curve host key: `show crypto key mypubkey ec`.
Confirm SSH is actually enabled and which version: `show ip ssh` (expect `version 2.0`).
Determine which key SSH is bound to: `show running-config | include ip ssh rsa keypair-name` (a named key) or infer the default `<hostname>.<domain>` pair.
If the reported key is >= 2048 bits: no change needed — record the verified size in your audit evidence and close the finding.
If the reported key is < 2048 bits (or EC < 256/384 as policy dictates): treat as CRYPTO-001 and remediate.
Schedule a maintenance window before regenerating, since key regeneration drops SSH sessions.
Ensure console/OOB access is available as a fallback.
Set prerequisites if generating default keys: `configure terminal`, `hostname <name>`, `ip domain-name <domain>`, then `end`.
Zeroize the old pair in EXEC: `crypto key zeroize rsa`.
Generate a compliant pair: `crypto key generate rsa general-keys modulus 4096 label SSH-KEYS`.
Bind SSH to it and enforce v2: `configure terminal`, `ip ssh rsa keypair-name SSH-KEYS`, `ip ssh version 2`, `end`.
Re-run `show crypto key mypubkey rsa` and `show ip ssh` to confirm the new size and version.
Open a new SSH session to validate connectivity after clients accept the new fingerprint.
Save the config: `copy running-config startup-config`.
Record the verified key size and date in the audit trail so future scans have documented evidence.
CRYPTO-002.cli:
! Verification only — run in privileged EXEC
show crypto key mypubkey rsa
show crypto key mypubkey ec
show ip ssh
show running-config | include ip ssh rsa keypair-name
! If key < 2048 bits, remediate:
crypto key zeroize rsa
crypto key generate rsa general-keys modulus 4096 label SSH-KEYS
configure terminal
 ip ssh rsa keypair-name SSH-KEYS
 ip ssh version 2
end
copy running-config startup-config
CRYPTO-002.verify: `show crypto key mypubkey rsa` prints the key with its modulus; confirm the bit length is >= 2048 (ideally 4096). `show ip ssh` reports `SSH Enabled - version 2.0`. Once verified >= 2048, the CRYPTO-002 advisory is satisfied.
CRYPTO-002.rollback: No configuration change is required to satisfy this advisory (it is verification-only). If you regenerated a key and must revert, restore from a config/key backup. Caveat: leaving the key size undocumented re-opens the visibility gap and could mask a legacy undersized host key.
CRYPTO-002.impact: None if you only run the show commands (read-only). If verification reveals an undersized key and you regenerate it, impact matches CRYPTO-001: active SSH sessions drop and clients must re-accept the new fingerprint; no reload, no traffic impact.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 7.1 — Verify SSH RSA key length
NIST SP 800-53 Rev.5 — SC-12 / AU-6 (auditability of controls)

CRYPTO-003.risk:
The scanner matched an SSH server encryption/cipher line via `ip ssh.*cipher.*server\s+(.*)` and found one of the known-weak ciphers in its list — `aes128-cbc`, `3des-cbc`, `arcfour`, or `blowfish` — present in the configured algorithm set. The finding fires HIGH because these algorithms are enabled for the SSH server, meaning a client can negotiate them for the management session.

The weakness is twofold. CBC-mode SSH ciphers (aes*-cbc, 3des-cbc) are vulnerable to the well-known SSH CBC plaintext-recovery attack (CPNI-957037 / CVE-2008-5161), which can leak up to 32 bits of plaintext per block under the right conditions. `arcfour` (RC4) has statistical biases that make keystream recovery feasible, and `blowfish`/`3des` offer only 64-bit block sizes and legacy 112-bit effective strength, exposing them to birthday-bound (Sweet32-style) collisions on long-lived sessions.

Concrete attack: an on-path adversary performing ATT&CK T1040 (Network Sniffing) captures the encrypted management session; against a CBC or RC4 cipher they mount the known plaintext-recovery/keystream attack to extract fragments of the session — potentially command output, ACL contents, or credentials typed after login — enabling T1557 Adversary-in-the-Middle and downstream credential access. It also lets a downgrade-capable attacker force the weakest mutually-supported cipher.

Blast radius: exposure of live management traffic on the device; on a core/aggregation device or WLC this can reveal secrets, topology, and admin actions across the managed domain.

Platform nuances: the actionable remediation command on IOS/IOS-XE is `ip ssh server algorithm encryption` with CTR/GCM ciphers only. The scanner keys on a line combining `ip ssh`, `cipher`, and `server`; the real IOS-XE knob is the `ip ssh server algorithm encryption` allow-list — configure only strong ciphers there. Catalyst 9800 WLC (IOS-XE) uses the same syntax. AES-GCM (`aes256-gcm`, `aes128-gcm`) is available on recent IOS-XE and is preferred. ASA/FTD/NX-OS use different `ssh cipher`/`ssh key-exchange` syntax and are out of scope for this module.

Standards angle: CWE-327 (Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm), NIST SP 800-131A/800-52, NIST 800-53 SC-8 (Transmission Confidentiality/Integrity). CIS Cisco IOS benchmarks require restricting SSH to strong ciphers.
steps: Inventory current SSH server algorithms: `show ip ssh` (recent IOS-XE lists `Encryption Algorithms:`) and `show running-config | include ip ssh server algorithm`.
Confirm which strong ciphers your client tooling supports (jump hosts, Ansible/Netmiko, monitoring) so restricting the server does not lock out automation.

Note the maintenance impact: changing the allow-list affects only NEW SSH sessions; existing sessions keep their negotiated cipher, so the change is low-risk but coordinate to avoid a stale-tooling lockout.
Keep a console/OOB path available in case an SSH client cannot negotiate the new set.
Enter config mode: `configure terminal`.
Restrict encryption to strong CTR/GCM ciphers: `ip ssh server algorithm encryption aes256-gcm aes128-gcm aes256-ctr aes192-ctr aes128-ctr` (drop the `-gcm` entries on platforms that lack them).
This single allow-list command implicitly disables `aes128-cbc`, `3des-cbc`, `arcfour`, and `blowfish` — you do not remove them individually.
While hardening SSH, also set strong MACs (see CRYPTO-004): `ip ssh server algorithm mac hmac-sha2-256 hmac-sha2-512`.
Optionally restrict key exchange: `ip ssh server algorithm kex ecdh-sha2-nistp521 ecdh-sha2-nistp384 ecdh-sha2-nistp256`.
Ensure SSHv2 is enforced: `ip ssh version 2`.
Exit: `end`.
Validate the negotiated algorithm set: `show ip ssh` and confirm no CBC/arcfour/blowfish/3des appears under Encryption Algorithms.
From a management host, open a NEW SSH session with verbose output (`ssh -vv <device>`) and confirm the negotiated cipher is aes*-ctr or aes*-gcm.
Test your automation tooling can still connect (some legacy libraries default to CBC).
If a required client cannot negotiate, upgrade the client rather than re-adding a weak cipher.
Save the configuration: `copy running-config startup-config`.
Update the SSH hardening baseline/template so new devices inherit the strong allow-list.
CRYPTO-003.cli:
configure terminal
 ip ssh server algorithm encryption aes256-gcm aes128-gcm aes256-ctr aes192-ctr aes128-ctr
 ip ssh server algorithm mac hmac-sha2-256 hmac-sha2-512
 ip ssh server algorithm kex ecdh-sha2-nistp521 ecdh-sha2-nistp384 ecdh-sha2-nistp256
 ip ssh version 2
end
copy running-config startup-config
CRYPTO-003.verify: `show ip ssh` — the `Encryption Algorithms:` list should show only `aes256-gcm/aes128-gcm/aes256-ctr/aes192-ctr/aes128-ctr` with no `*-cbc`, `arcfour`, `blowfish`, or `3des-cbc`. A verbose client connection (`ssh -vv <device>`) should report `cipher: aes256-ctr` (or a GCM variant) in the negotiation.
CRYPTO-003.rollback: Restore the previous set, e.g. `ip ssh server algorithm encryption aes256-ctr aes128-ctr aes128-cbc 3des-cbc`, or remove the allow-list entirely with `no ip ssh server algorithm encryption` (reverts to the platform default set, which may include CBC). Caveat: re-adding CBC/RC4/3DES re-exposes management sessions to the SSH CBC plaintext-recovery and RC4 keystream attacks.
CRYPTO-003.impact: Non-disruptive to the data plane and to existing SSH sessions (only new sessions are affected). Risk is operational: a legacy SSH client or automation library that only offers CBC/arcfour will fail to connect until updated. No reload, no traffic impact.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 7.2 — Restrict SSH to strong encryption algorithms
NIST SP 800-131A / SP 800-52 Rev.2 — approved algorithms
NIST SP 800-53 Rev.5 — SC-8 (Transmission Confidentiality and Integrity)
CWE-327: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm

CRYPTO-004.risk:
The scanner matched the SSH server MAC line via `ip ssh.*mac.*server\s+(.*)` and found one of the deprecated HMAC algorithms in its list — `hmac-md5`, `hmac-sha1-96`, or `hmac-md5-96` — enabled for message authentication on the SSH server. It is rated MEDIUM because a weak MAC degrades the integrity/anti-tamper guarantee of the SSH channel rather than directly leaking key material.

The weakness: MD5-based HMACs (`hmac-md5`, `hmac-md5-96`) rely on the broken MD5 primitive and are removed from modern SSH implementations and disallowed by NIST SP 800-131A. The `-96` truncated variants (`hmac-sha1-96`, `hmac-md5-96`) shorten the authentication tag to 96 bits, reducing forgery resistance. SHA-1 itself is deprecated for integrity assurance.

Concrete attack: a weak or truncated MAC lowers the bar for message forgery/tampering of the SSH stream and, combined with a weak cipher (CRYPTO-003), strengthens an on-path adversary's ability to manipulate or replay session data (ATT&CK T1557 Adversary-in-the-Middle). A downgrade-capable attacker can also force negotiation of the weakest mutual MAC.

Blast radius: reduced integrity assurance on the management channel of the device; on a core device or WLC this weakens tamper-evidence for all administrative traffic.

Platform nuances: remediation on IOS/IOS-XE is `ip ssh server algorithm mac hmac-sha2-256 hmac-sha2-512`, which allow-lists only strong SHA-2 HMACs and implicitly disables the MD5/SHA1-96 variants. Catalyst 9800 WLC (IOS-XE) uses identical syntax. The scanner keys on a line combining `ip ssh`, `mac`, and `server`; the real knob is `ip ssh server algorithm mac`. ASA/FTD/NX-OS use different `ssh` MAC syntax and are out of scope for this module.

Standards angle: CWE-327 (Broken/Risky Crypto Algorithm), NIST SP 800-131A, NIST 800-53 SC-8. CIS Cisco IOS benchmarks require SHA-2 HMACs for SSH.
steps: Inventory current MACs: `show ip ssh` (recent IOS-XE prints `MAC Algorithms:`) and `show running-config | include ip ssh server algorithm mac`.
Verify client/automation tooling supports hmac-sha2-256/512 (virtually all modern clients do).
Note impact scope: MAC allow-list changes affect only NEW SSH sessions, so risk is minimal; still keep OOB/console access available.
Enter config mode: `configure terminal`.
Set the strong MAC allow-list: `ip ssh server algorithm mac hmac-sha2-256 hmac-sha2-512`.
This implicitly disables `hmac-md5`, `hmac-md5-96`, and `hmac-sha1-96` — no per-algorithm removal is needed.
Pair with a strong cipher allow-list (CRYPTO-003): `ip ssh server algorithm encryption aes256-ctr aes192-ctr aes128-ctr`.
Ensure SSHv2 is enforced: `ip ssh version 2`.
Exit config: `end`.
Confirm the active MAC set: `show ip ssh` — verify only SHA-2 HMACs appear.
Open a new verbose SSH session (`ssh -vv <device>`) and confirm the negotiated MAC is `hmac-sha2-256` or `hmac-sha2-512`.
Test automation/monitoring connectivity to catch any legacy library still requesting MD5/SHA1-96.
If a client cannot negotiate SHA-2 MACs, upgrade the client instead of re-enabling MD5.
Update your standard SSH hardening template with the MAC allow-list.
Save the config: `copy running-config startup-config`.
Re-run the scanner to confirm CRYPTO-004 clears.
CRYPTO-004.cli:
configure terminal
 ip ssh server algorithm mac hmac-sha2-256 hmac-sha2-512
 ip ssh server algorithm encryption aes256-ctr aes192-ctr aes128-ctr
 ip ssh version 2
end
copy running-config startup-config
CRYPTO-004.verify: `show ip ssh` — the `MAC Algorithms:` list should contain only `hmac-sha2-256` and `hmac-sha2-512`, with no `hmac-md5`, `hmac-md5-96`, or `hmac-sha1-96`. A verbose client (`ssh -vv <device>`) should report `MAC: hmac-sha2-256` (or 512) in the negotiation.
CRYPTO-004.rollback: Re-add the legacy set, e.g. `ip ssh server algorithm mac hmac-sha2-256 hmac-sha1 hmac-md5`, or remove the allow-list with `no ip ssh server algorithm mac` to return to the platform default. Caveat: re-enabling MD5/truncated HMACs restores a weak integrity guarantee, easing SSH message forgery/tampering by an on-path attacker.
CRYPTO-004.impact: Non-disruptive to the data plane and to existing sessions (only new SSH sessions negotiate the new MAC set). No reload, no traffic effect. Only risk is a legacy client/automation library that lacks SHA-2 HMACs failing to connect until upgraded.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 7.3 — Require SHA-2 HMAC for SSH
NIST SP 800-131A — deprecation of MD5/SHA-1 for HMAC assurance
NIST SP 800-53 Rev.5 — SC-8 (Transmission Confidentiality and Integrity)
CWE-327: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm

CRYPTO-005.risk:
The scanner scanned every line matching `(ssl|tls)\s*(version|protocol)` and flagged the config HIGH because at least one such line contains `1.0`, `sslv3`, or `ssl3` — i.e. a deprecated TLS/SSL protocol version is explicitly enabled. On IOS/IOS-XE this most commonly comes from the embedded HTTPS management server (`ip http secure-server`) TLS-version setting, or from other TLS-consuming features that let you pin a protocol floor. Note the detection keys specifically on the `1.0`/`sslv3`/`ssl3` substrings.

The weakness: SSLv3 is broken (POODLE, CVE-2014-3566), TLS 1.0 is vulnerable to BEAST and inherits SSLv3-era cipher/MAC weaknesses, and both are prohibited by NIST SP 800-52 Rev.2 and PCI-DSS. A device offering these protocols lets a downgrade-capable attacker negotiate the weakest option.

Concrete attack: an on-path adversary (ATT&CK T1557 Adversary-in-the-Middle) forces a protocol downgrade to SSLv3/TLS1.0 on the device's web management interface, then applies POODLE/BEAST-style block-cipher attacks to recover session cookies or authentication tokens from the HTTPS admin session — leading to management-plane takeover (T1078/T1040).

Blast radius: compromise of the device's web administration channel (and any WLC web/GUI or guest-portal TLS surface), which on a WLC can also expose the wireless management UI and captive-portal credentials.

Platform nuances: on IOS-XE 16.x+ the fix is `ip http tls-version TLSv1.2` (or leave default, which is TLS 1.2-only on current releases); older IOS lacked a TLS-version selector, so the remediation there is to upgrade code or disable the HTTPS server (`no ip http secure-server`) if unused. Catalyst 9800 WLC (IOS-XE) uses the same `ip http tls-version` knob for its management/web surface. TLS 1.3 support depends on release. ASA/FTD (out of scope for this module) use `ssl server-version tlsv1.2` — different syntax.

Standards angle: CWE-327, NIST SP 800-52 Rev.2 (TLS configuration), NIST 800-53 SC-8/SC-13, and CIS Cisco IOS HTTPS/management guidance.
steps: Identify the source of the deprecated setting: `show running-config | include ssl|tls|secure-server|http` and `show ip http server status`.
Determine whether the HTTPS management server is even required; if not, plan to disable it entirely (`no ip http secure-server`) as the strongest fix.
Check platform capability: `show version` — confirm the IOS-XE release supports `ip http tls-version` (16.x+); older code may require an upgrade.
Note change control: raising the TLS floor can break legacy management browsers/tools that only speak TLS 1.0; schedule accordingly and confirm admin browsers support TLS 1.2/1.3.
Keep SSH/console access available in case the web session is your only current path.
Enter config mode: `configure terminal`.
Set the minimum TLS version to 1.2: `ip http tls-version TLSv1.2` (use TLSv1.3 only if the release and clients support it).

For the HTTPS cipher suite, prefer leaving the default on modern IOS-XE (which selects AEAD/GCM suites); only pin `ip http secure-ciphersuite ...` if your release exposes GCM suites, and avoid legacy CBC/RC4 suites.
Ensure the HTTP (cleartext) server is disabled: `no ip http server` (keep only `ip http secure-server` if web mgmt is needed).
If web management is not needed at all, disable it: `no ip http secure-server` and `no ip http server`.
Restrict management access with an ACL: `ip http access-class 10` referencing a permit list of admin subnets.
Exit config: `end`.
Validate: `show ip http server secure status` and confirm the reported TLS version is 1.2 (or 1.3) and SSLv3/TLS1.0 are absent.
From an admin host, connect with a TLS-testing tool: `openssl s_client -connect <device>:443 -tls1` should FAIL, `-tls1_2` should succeed.
Confirm admin browsers/tools can still reach the GUI over TLS 1.2+.
Save the config: `copy running-config startup-config`.
If remediation required a code upgrade, schedule the IOS-XE upgrade in a separate maintenance window.
Re-run the scanner to confirm CRYPTO-005 clears.
CRYPTO-005.cli:
configure terminal
 no ip http server
 ip http secure-server
 ip http tls-version TLSv1.2
 ip http access-class 10
end
! optional if web mgmt is unnecessary:
! configure terminal
!  no ip http secure-server
! end
copy running-config startup-config
CRYPTO-005.verify: `show ip http server secure status` should report `HTTP secure server status: Enabled` and a `TLS version: TLSv1.2` (no SSLv3/TLS1.0). External proof: `openssl s_client -connect <device>:443 -tls1` and `-tls1_1` should be refused, while `-tls1_2` completes the handshake.
CRYPTO-005.rollback: Re-allow the old floor with `ip http tls-version TLSv1.0` (if the release permits) or re-enable the HTTPS server. Caveat: allowing TLS 1.0/SSLv3 again re-exposes the web management interface to POODLE/BEAST downgrade attacks and session-token theft.
CRYPTO-005.impact: Low data-plane impact. The change affects only the device's HTTPS management surface. Existing web sessions may be dropped when the server re-initializes its TLS stack; legacy management browsers/tools stuck on TLS 1.0 will lose GUI access until upgraded. Disabling the HTTP/HTTPS server removes web/REST/GUI management — verify no orchestration tool depends on it. No reload required for the `tls-version` change.
references: NIST SP 800-52 Rev.2 — Guidelines for TLS Implementations (TLS 1.2 minimum)
CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 7.4 — HTTPS management / disable HTTP server
CWE-327: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm

CRYPTO-006.risk:
The scanner enumerated every `crypto ipsec transform-set` line and flagged HIGH because one or more contain a weak token: `des ` (single-DES), `3des`, `md5`, or `ah-md5`. These transform sets define the ESP/AH algorithms that protect IPsec (site-to-site or remote-access VPN) traffic, so a weak transform means production VPN data is encrypted/authenticated with broken primitives.

The weaknesses: single-DES (`esp-des`) offers only 56-bit keys and is trivially brute-forced; 3DES (`esp-3des`) has a 64-bit block and only ~112-bit effective strength, exposing long-lived tunnels to Sweet32 (CVE-2016-2183) birthday collisions and is deprecated by NIST SP 800-131A; MD5-based integrity (`esp-md5-hmac`, `ah-md5-hmac`) uses the broken MD5 primitive with weak collision/forgery resistance.

Concrete attack: an adversary who captures VPN ciphertext (ATT&CK T1040 Network Sniffing) performs offline cryptanalysis — brute-forcing DES, or exploiting Sweet32 against a high-volume 3DES tunnel to recover plaintext, or forging/altering packets protected only by MD5 integrity. This yields decryption or tampering of inter-site/remote-access traffic (T1557 Adversary-in-the-Middle) and potential recovery of anything traversing the tunnel.

Blast radius: confidentiality and integrity of all traffic on the affected VPN tunnel(s) — often the crown-jewel inter-datacenter or branch link.

Platform nuances: syntax is IOS/IOS-XE. Strong replacement is `crypto ipsec transform-set <name> esp-gcm 256` (AEAD, combined cipher+integrity, no separate HMAC) or `esp-aes 256 esp-sha384-hmac`. Set `mode tunnel` (or `transport`) as appropriate. The transform-set must then be referenced by the crypto map (IKEv1) or IPsec profile (IKEv2/DMVPN/FlexVPN). Changing a transform-set forces the SA to renegotiate. Catalyst 9800 WLC (IOS-XE) shares this syntax if it terminates IPsec. ASA/FTD/NX-OS (out of scope) use different IPsec proposal syntax.

Standards angle: CWE-327, NIST SP 800-131A, NIST 800-53 SC-8/SC-13, and CIS Cisco IOS IPsec/VPN guidance.
steps: Inventory transform sets and their bindings: `show crypto ipsec transform-set` and `show running-config | section crypto`.
Identify which crypto maps / IPsec profiles reference the weak sets, and which peers/tunnels they protect, so you can coordinate with the remote end.

CRITICAL ordering/compatibility: the remote peer must support the new proposal. Confirm the far end can do AES-GCM-256 or AES-256/SHA-384 before you change anything, or the tunnel will fail to establish.
Schedule a maintenance window — changing the transform-set renegotiates the IPsec SA and briefly interrupts tunnel traffic.
Enter config mode: `configure terminal`.
Create a strong transform-set (preferred AEAD): `crypto ipsec transform-set TS-STRONG esp-gcm 256` then `mode tunnel`.
Or, for cipher+HMAC style: `crypto ipsec transform-set TS-STRONG esp-aes 256 esp-sha384-hmac` then `mode tunnel`.
For IKEv1 crypto maps, add the new set to the map entry so both are offered during migration: `crypto map CMAP 10 ipsec-isakmp` then `set transform-set TS-STRONG`.
For IKEv2/FlexVPN, bind it to the IPsec profile: `crypto ipsec profile PROF` then `set transform-set TS-STRONG`.
Coordinate the remote peer change to the matching strong proposal at the same time.
Once both ends are updated and the tunnel is confirmed on the strong set, remove the weak transform-set: `no crypto ipsec transform-set <weakname>`.
Exit config: `end`.
Clear the SA to force immediate renegotiation on the new transform (disruptive — do in the window): `clear crypto sa peer <peer-ip>`.
Validate the active SA algorithms: `show crypto ipsec sa`.
Confirm the tunnel is up and passing traffic: `show crypto session` and `show crypto isakmp sa` (or `show crypto ikev2 sa`).
Verify the transform-set definition: `show crypto ipsec transform-set`.
Save the config: `copy running-config startup-config`.
Repeat on the peer device and re-run the scanner to confirm CRYPTO-006 clears.
CRYPTO-006.cli:
configure terminal
 crypto ipsec transform-set TS-STRONG esp-gcm 256
  mode tunnel
 crypto map CMAP 10 ipsec-isakmp
  set transform-set TS-STRONG
 exit
! IKEv2/FlexVPN alternative:
! crypto ipsec profile PROF
!  set transform-set TS-STRONG
end
! after both peers migrated:
configure terminal
 no crypto ipsec transform-set TS-3DES-MD5
end
copy running-config startup-config
CRYPTO-006.verify: `show crypto ipsec transform-set` shows `TS-STRONG: { esp-gcm 256 }` (or esp-aes 256 / esp-sha384-hmac) and no DES/3DES/MD5 sets. `show crypto ipsec sa` on an active tunnel should report the ESP transform as AES-GCM-256 (or AES-256 + SHA), and `show crypto session` should show the peer in `UP-ACTIVE` state.
CRYPTO-006.rollback: Re-add and re-bind the old set: `crypto ipsec transform-set TS-3DES-MD5 esp-3des esp-md5-hmac`, then `crypto map CMAP 10 ipsec-isakmp` / `set transform-set TS-3DES-MD5`, and `clear crypto sa peer <peer>`. Caveat: reverting re-encrypts VPN traffic with brute-forceable/Sweet32-vulnerable algorithms and MD5 integrity, re-exposing tunnel data to offline decryption and forgery.
CRYPTO-006.impact: Disruptive to the affected VPN tunnel: changing the transform-set and clearing the SA drops and renegotiates the tunnel, causing a brief traffic outage across that link, and requires a coordinated change on the peer. If the peer is not migrated in lockstep the tunnel will not come up. No device reload; no impact to non-VPN data plane.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 7.5 — Use strong IPsec transform sets
NIST SP 800-131A — DES/3DES deprecation; AES requirements
CWE-327: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm

CRYPTO-007.risk:
The scanner walked every `key chain` section and, for each `key-string` line, applied `key-string\s+[^0-9]` while excluding lines containing `7 ` — i.e. it flags (HIGH) a key string that is NOT stored as a type-7-encrypted value (a `key-string 7 <hash>` form is skipped) and appears to be a plaintext/cleartext secret. Key chains supply the shared secrets for routing-protocol authentication (EIGRP, OSPFv2 with keychains, RIPv2, BGP where applicable, IS-IS, and BFD), so a plaintext key-string means a routing authentication secret sits in the config in the clear.

The risk: this is cleartext storage of a credential (CWE-312) — anyone who can read the running-config (a backup file, a TFTP/SCP archive, a monitoring pull, a screen-share, or a low-privilege show session) obtains the exact routing-authentication key. Even the `7 ` form the scanner excludes is only Cisco type-7 (Vigenère) encoding, which is trivially reversible; but the plaintext case is strictly worse because no obfuscation exists at all.

Concrete attack: an adversary with config read access (ATT&CK T1552.001 Unsecured Credentials in files / T1602 Data from Configuration Repository) harvests the key-string, then injects or modifies routing adjacencies (T1557 Adversary-in-the-Middle via route manipulation) — advertising false routes to blackhole or redirect traffic through an attacker-controlled path. This can reroute or intercept traffic across the routed domain.

Blast radius: integrity of the routing control plane across every neighbor that shares the key chain — potentially domain-wide traffic redirection or denial of service.

Platform nuances: syntax is IOS/IOS-XE. The key-string itself must exist to function, so the fix is (a) protect it at rest and (b) use a strong authentication algorithm. Enable type-6 (AES) password encryption with a master key: `key config-key password-encrypt` then `password encryption aes` — this encrypts key-strings with a cipher recoverable only with the master key. `service password-encryption` only yields weak type-7. Also add `cryptographic-algorithm hmac-sha-256` under the key so the protocol uses SHA-256 rather than MD5. Catalyst 9800 WLC (IOS-XE) shares this syntax. NX-OS/ASA/FTD are out of scope.

Standards angle: CWE-312 (Cleartext Storage) and CWE-321 (Use of Hard-coded/embedded Cryptographic Key), NIST 800-53 IA-5/SC-12/SC-28, and Cisco key-chain security guidance.
steps: Inventory key chains and their consumers: `show key chain` and `show running-config | section key chain`, then `show running-config | include key-chain` to see which routing protocols/interfaces reference them.
Note that the key-string must remain present for routing auth to work — the goal is at-rest protection plus a strong algorithm, not deletion.
Decide on a master-key passphrase for type-6 encryption and store it securely in your secrets vault (it is required to decrypt on restore/migration).
Change control: enabling type-6 and rotating keys can disrupt routing adjacencies if neighbors are not updated in step — plan a window and update neighbors together.
Enter config mode: `configure terminal`.
Configure the type-6 master key: `key config-key password-encrypt <MasterPassphrase>`.
Enable AES (type-6) encryption of stored keys: `password encryption aes`.
Enter the key chain: `key chain OSPF-KEYS` then `key 1`.
Set a strong authentication algorithm: `cryptographic-algorithm hmac-sha-256`.
Re-enter the secret so it is stored encrypted going forward: `key-string <NewStrongSecret>` (it will now be saved as a type-6 `key-string 6 ...`).
Optionally scope validity with `send-lifetime` / `accept-lifetime` and add a second key for hitless rotation.
Ensure the consuming protocol references the chain, e.g. under the interface `ip ospf authentication key-chain OSPF-KEYS` or under EIGRP address-family `authentication key-chain OSPF-KEYS`.
Update all neighbor devices with the same key-string and algorithm within the window.
Exit config: `end`.
Verify the stored form is now encrypted: `show running-config | section key chain` should show `key-string 6 <cipher>` (not cleartext, not `7`).
Confirm routing adjacencies stay up: `show ip ospf neighbor` / `show ip eigrp neighbors` as applicable.
Save the config: `copy running-config startup-config`.
Restrict and encrypt config backups (SCP not TFTP) and re-run the scanner to confirm CRYPTO-007 clears.
CRYPTO-007.cli:
configure terminal
 key config-key password-encrypt <MasterPassphrase>
 password encryption aes
 key chain OSPF-KEYS
  key 1
   cryptographic-algorithm hmac-sha-256
   key-string <NewStrongSecret>
   send-lifetime 00:00:00 Jan 1 2026 infinite
   accept-lifetime 00:00:00 Jan 1 2026 infinite
end
copy running-config startup-config
CRYPTO-007.verify: `show running-config | section key chain` — the key should now read `key-string 6 <encrypted-blob>` (type-6/AES) rather than a cleartext secret or `key-string 7 ...`. `show key chain` confirms the key is active with `cryptographic-algorithm hmac-sha-256`. Routing neighbors remain up (`show ip ospf neighbor` / `show ip eigrp neighbors`).
CRYPTO-007.rollback: Disable AES encryption with `no password encryption aes` and remove the master key with `no key config-key password-encrypt` (this reverts stored keys toward cleartext/type-7). Caveat: reverting re-exposes the routing-authentication secret in readable form in the config and every backup, enabling route-injection / adjacency spoofing by anyone who reads the config.
CRYPTO-007.impact: Low-to-moderate. Enabling type-6 encryption and re-entering the same key-string is non-disruptive to routing. However, ROTATING the key-string (changing its value) will break authentication with neighbors that still use the old key until they are updated, dropping adjacencies and reconverging routes — do it in a coordinated window. Losing the type-6 master passphrase makes encrypted keys unrecoverable on migration. No reload; no direct data-plane change beyond routing reconvergence during key rotation.
references: Cisco IOS — Key Chain and type-6 (AES) password encryption (key config-key password-encrypt)
NIST SP 800-53 Rev.5 — IA-5 (Authenticator Management) / SC-28 (Protection of Information at Rest)
CWE-312: Cleartext Storage of Sensitive Information
CWE-321: Use of Hard-coded Cryptographic Key

CRYPTO-008.risk:
The scanner iterated the lines inside each `crypto isakmp policy` section and flagged HIGH when a line contains `encryption des` or `encryption 3des`. This is the IKEv1 (ISAKMP) Phase-1 negotiation policy: the `encryption` statement sets the cipher that protects the IKE key-exchange itself, so a weak value means the tunnel-establishment channel — inside which pre-shared keys, identities, and session keys are negotiated — is weakly encrypted.

The weakness: `des` (single-DES, 56-bit) is brute-forceable in hours with commodity hardware; `3des` has a 64-bit block and ~112-bit effective strength, is Sweet32-affected (CVE-2016-2183), and is deprecated by NIST SP 800-131A. Because Phase 1 protects the negotiation of the data-plane keys, breaking it can cascade to compromising the whole VPN.

Concrete attack: an adversary captures the IKE Phase-1 exchange (ATT&CK T1040 Network Sniffing) and brute-forces DES / cryptanalyzes 3DES offline to recover the ISAKMP SA keys, exposing the negotiated identities and, combined with a weak PSK, enabling decryption of the derived IPsec session (T1557 Adversary-in-the-Middle). Weak Phase-1 crypto is a classic enabler of full VPN compromise.

Blast radius: confidentiality of the IKE negotiation and, by extension, the derived IPsec tunnels — the crown-jewel site-to-site or remote-access VPN.

Platform nuances: syntax is IOS/IOS-XE IKEv1. The fix is `encryption aes 256` inside the policy. Strongly consider migrating from IKEv1/ISAKMP to IKEv2 (`crypto ikev2 proposal`), which uses AEAD and is the modern standard. Both peers must agree on the Phase-1 proposal, so change them together. Catalyst 9800 WLC (IOS-XE) shares this syntax if it runs IPsec. NX-OS/ASA/FTD (out of scope) use different IKE proposal syntax.

Standards angle: CWE-327, NIST SP 800-131A, NIST 800-53 SC-12/SC-13, and CIS Cisco IOS ISAKMP/IKE guidance.
steps: Inventory IKEv1 policies and peers: `show crypto isakmp policy` and `show running-config | section crypto isakmp`.
Identify which peers/crypto maps use each policy so you can coordinate the matching change on the far end.
CRITICAL: confirm the remote peer supports AES-256 for Phase 1 before changing — a Phase-1 proposal mismatch prevents the tunnel from establishing.
Schedule a maintenance window; changing the ISAKMP policy will renegotiate Phase 1 (and dependent SAs), briefly interrupting the VPN.
Keep SSH/console access open in case the tunnel is your management path.
Enter config mode: `configure terminal`.
Create/modify a strong ISAKMP policy with a low (preferred) priority number: `crypto isakmp policy 10`.
Set strong encryption: `encryption aes 256`.
Set strong hash (see CRYPTO-009) and DH group (see CRYPTO-010): `hash sha384` and `group 19` (or 14/20/21).
Set authentication and a lifetime: `authentication pre-share` (or `rsa-sig` for certificates) and `lifetime 3600`.
Coordinate the identical Phase-1 proposal change on the remote peer.
Once both ends have the strong policy, remove the weak policy so it cannot be negotiated: `no crypto isakmp policy <weak#>`.
Exit config: `end`.
Force renegotiation in the window: `clear crypto isakmp` (or `clear crypto sa`) to drop and rebuild Phase 1 on the new policy.
Verify the negotiated Phase-1 cipher: `show crypto isakmp sa detail` (or `show crypto ikev2 sa` if migrated).
Confirm the policy content: `show crypto isakmp policy` shows AES-256, no DES/3DES.
Confirm the tunnel is up and passing traffic: `show crypto session`.
Save the config (`copy running-config startup-config`), repeat on the peer, and re-scan to confirm CRYPTO-008 clears.
CRYPTO-008.cli:
configure terminal
 crypto isakmp policy 10
  encryption aes 256
  hash sha384
  authentication pre-share
  group 19
  lifetime 3600
end
! after both peers migrated, remove the weak policy:
configure terminal
 no crypto isakmp policy 30
end
clear crypto isakmp
copy running-config startup-config
CRYPTO-008.verify: `show crypto isakmp policy` lists policy 10 with `encryption: AES - Advanced Encryption Standard (256 bit keys)` and no DES/3DES policies remain reachable. On an active tunnel, `show crypto isakmp sa detail` shows the negotiated Phase-1 encryption as AES-256, and `show crypto session` shows the peer `UP-ACTIVE`.
CRYPTO-008.rollback: Recreate the weak policy: `crypto isakmp policy 30` / `encryption 3des` (and matching hash/group), then `clear crypto isakmp`. Caveat: reverting weakens the IKE Phase-1 channel to brute-forceable/Sweet32-vulnerable crypto, re-exposing the key exchange and derived VPN keys to offline attack.
CRYPTO-008.impact: Disruptive to the VPN. Changing the ISAKMP policy and clearing the SA renegotiates Phase 1 and rebuilds the tunnel, causing a brief outage on that VPN, and it must be mirrored on the peer or the tunnel will fail to come up. No device reload; no impact to non-VPN data plane.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 7.6 — Use strong ISAKMP/IKE encryption
NIST SP 800-131A — DES/3DES deprecation; AES requirements
CWE-327: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm

CRYPTO-009.risk:
The scanner examined the lines within each `crypto isakmp policy` section and flagged HIGH when a line contains `hash md5`. In IKEv1/ISAKMP Phase-1, the `hash` statement selects the integrity/PRF primitive used to authenticate the key exchange and derive keying material, so `hash md5` means the VPN's Phase-1 negotiation relies on the broken MD5 algorithm.

The weakness: MD5 is cryptographically broken for collision resistance (practical collisions since 2004-2008) and is disallowed for integrity/authentication assurance by NIST SP 800-131A. Within IKE it weakens the integrity protection of the Phase-1 exchange and the strength of the derived PRF, lowering the effort required to tamper with or attack the negotiation.

Concrete attack: combined with a weak cipher (CRYPTO-008) or weak DH group (CRYPTO-010), an MD5 hash reduces the overall Phase-1 security so an on-path adversary (ATT&CK T1557 Adversary-in-the-Middle, with T1040 Network Sniffing) can more feasibly forge or manipulate IKE messages / attack the key derivation, contributing to VPN compromise. MD5 also undermines any HMAC-based message integrity in the exchange.

Blast radius: integrity assurance of the IKE Phase-1 negotiation and, by extension, the trustworthiness of the derived IPsec tunnels between the affected peers.

Platform nuances: IOS/IOS-XE IKEv1 syntax. The fix is `hash sha384` (or `hash sha256`/`sha512`) inside the ISAKMP policy. IKEv1 policy hash options are md5, sha (SHA-1), sha256, sha384, sha512 — SHA-1 is itself deprecated, so prefer SHA-256+. Migrating to IKEv2 (`crypto ikev2 proposal` with `integrity sha256`/`sha384` and a separate `prf`) is the modern path. Both peers must agree on the Phase-1 proposal. Catalyst 9800 WLC (IOS-XE) shares this syntax. NX-OS/ASA/FTD are out of scope.

Standards angle: CWE-327, NIST SP 800-131A (MD5 disallowed), NIST 800-53 SC-13, and CIS Cisco IOS ISAKMP guidance.
steps: Inventory policies: `show crypto isakmp policy` and `show running-config | section crypto isakmp`, noting which show `hash md5`.
Map each affected policy to its peers/crypto maps so the change can be coordinated with the far end.
CRITICAL: confirm the peer supports SHA-256/384 for Phase 1 — a hash mismatch in the proposal prevents Phase-1 establishment.
Schedule a maintenance window; changing the policy renegotiates Phase 1 and briefly interrupts the VPN.
Keep console/SSH access available in case the VPN is your management path.
Enter config mode: `configure terminal`.
Enter the policy: `crypto isakmp policy 10`.
Set a strong hash: `hash sha384` (or `sha256`).
While in the policy, also ensure strong cipher and DH group: `encryption aes 256` and `group 19` (CRYPTO-008/010).
Confirm authentication and lifetime: `authentication pre-share` and `lifetime 3600`.
Mirror the identical hash/proposal change on the remote peer.
Once both ends are updated, remove any residual weak policy: `no crypto isakmp policy <weak#>`.
Exit config: `end`.
Force renegotiation in the window: `clear crypto isakmp` (or `clear crypto sa`).
Verify the negotiated hash: `show crypto isakmp sa detail` (Phase-1 hash should be SHA).
Confirm policy content: `show crypto isakmp policy` shows a SHA-2 hash and no MD5.
Confirm the tunnel is up: `show crypto session`.
Save the config (`copy running-config startup-config`), repeat on the peer, and re-scan to confirm CRYPTO-009 clears.
CRYPTO-009.cli:
configure terminal
 crypto isakmp policy 10
  encryption aes 256
  hash sha384
  authentication pre-share
  group 19
  lifetime 3600
end
clear crypto isakmp
copy running-config startup-config
CRYPTO-009.verify: `show crypto isakmp policy` shows the policy with a Secure Hash Standard 2 (SHA-256/384) hash and no `hash md5` remains. On a live tunnel, `show crypto isakmp sa detail` reports the negotiated Phase-1 hash as SHA-256/384, and `show crypto session` shows the peer `UP-ACTIVE`.
CRYPTO-009.rollback: Restore the weak hash: `crypto isakmp policy 10` / `hash md5`, then `clear crypto isakmp`. Caveat: reverting to MD5 restores broken integrity/PRF in the IKE Phase-1 exchange, weakening tamper-resistance of the key negotiation and the derived VPN keys.
CRYPTO-009.impact: Disruptive to the VPN. Changing the hash and clearing the SA renegotiates Phase 1 and rebuilds the tunnel (brief outage), and must be mirrored on the peer or the tunnel will not establish. No reload; no non-VPN data-plane impact.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 7.7 — Use strong ISAKMP/IKE hash (SHA-2)
NIST SP 800-131A — MD5 disallowed for integrity/authentication
CWE-327: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm

CRYPTO-010.risk:
The scanner inspected the lines inside each `crypto isakmp policy` section and flagged HIGH when a line contains the substring `group 1` or `group 2`. The intended target is IKEv1 Diffie-Hellman `group 1` (768-bit MODP) and `group 2` (1024-bit MODP) — the DH groups used to establish the shared secret in Phase-1. These small MODP groups are weak: group 1/2 provide well below current key-strength requirements and are within reach of nation-state precomputation.

IMPORTANT detection caveat: because the match is a naive substring test (`"group 1" in line` / `"group 2" in line`), it will ALSO match the strong groups whose numbers begin with 1 or 2 — e.g. `group 14`, `group 15`, `group 19`, `group 20`, `group 21`, `group 24`. So this finding can be a FALSE POSITIVE if the policy already uses group 14/19/20/21/24. Always read the actual `group` value in the config before acting: only DH group 1 (768-bit) and group 2 (1024-bit) are genuinely weak and must be replaced.

The real weakness (for genuine group 1/2): 768/1024-bit MODP DH is vulnerable to precomputation attacks — the Logjam research (CVE-2015-4000) showed that a well-resourced adversary can precompute the discrete log for a common 1024-bit group and then break individual key exchanges in near-real-time.

Concrete attack: an adversary who has done the group precomputation and can capture the IKE exchange (ATT&CK T1040 Network Sniffing) recovers the DH shared secret, decrypting the Phase-1 negotiation and derived IPsec keys (T1557 Adversary-in-the-Middle) — full passive VPN decryption.

Blast radius: confidentiality of every VPN tunnel negotiated with the weak group; passive decryption is possible without active tampering.

Platform nuances: IOS/IOS-XE IKEv1 syntax. Replace with `group 14` (2048-bit MODP) minimum, preferring `group 19`/`group 20`/`group 21` (256/384/521-bit ECDH) or `group 24`. Both peers must support the chosen group. Migrating to IKEv2 is recommended. Catalyst 9800 WLC (IOS-XE) shares this syntax. NX-OS/ASA/FTD are out of scope.

Standards angle: CWE-326 (Inadequate Encryption Strength), NIST SP 800-131A (DH group/key-size requirements), NIST 800-53 SC-12, and CIS Cisco IOS IKE guidance.
steps: FIRST rule out a false positive: `show crypto isakmp policy` and read the exact DH group number — if it is already 14/19/20/21/24, this is a substring false match and no change is required (record as verified).
If the group is genuinely 1 or 2, inventory affected policies and peers: `show running-config | section crypto isakmp`.
Map each weak policy to its peers/crypto maps so the DH change is coordinated with the far end.
CRITICAL: confirm the remote peer supports the target group (14 or 19/20/21) — a DH group mismatch prevents Phase-1 establishment.
Schedule a maintenance window; changing the DH group renegotiates Phase 1 and briefly interrupts the VPN.
Keep console/SSH access open in case the VPN is your management path.
Enter config mode: `configure terminal`.
Enter the policy: `crypto isakmp policy 10`.
Set a strong DH group: `group 19` (ECDH P-256) preferred, or `group 14` (2048-bit MODP) as the minimum.
While here, ensure strong cipher and hash: `encryption aes 256` and `hash sha384` (CRYPTO-008/009).
Set authentication/lifetime: `authentication pre-share` and `lifetime 3600`.
For Perfect Forward Secrecy on the IPsec SA, set a matching strong group on the crypto map/profile: `set pfs group19` (or `group14`).
Mirror the identical group change on the remote peer.
Remove the weak policy once both ends use the strong one: `no crypto isakmp policy <weak#>`.
Exit config: `end`, then force renegotiation in the window: `clear crypto isakmp`.
Verify: `show crypto isakmp policy` shows the strong group; `show crypto isakmp sa detail` shows the negotiated DH group on the live SA.
Confirm the tunnel is up and passing traffic: `show crypto session`.
Save the config (`copy running-config startup-config`), repeat on the peer, and re-scan to confirm CRYPTO-010 clears.
CRYPTO-010.cli:
configure terminal
 crypto isakmp policy 10
  encryption aes 256
  hash sha384
  authentication pre-share
  group 19
  lifetime 3600
 exit
! optional PFS on the IPsec map/profile:
 crypto map CMAP 10 ipsec-isakmp
  set pfs group19
end
clear crypto isakmp
copy running-config startup-config
CRYPTO-010.verify: `show crypto isakmp policy` shows the policy with `Diffie-Hellman group: #19 (256 bit)` (or #14 2048-bit) and no group 1/2 remains. On a live tunnel, `show crypto isakmp sa detail` shows the negotiated DH group as 14/19/20/21, and `show crypto session` shows the peer `UP-ACTIVE`.
CRYPTO-010.rollback: Restore the weak group: `crypto isakmp policy 10` / `group 2`, then `clear crypto isakmp`. Caveat: reverting to DH group 1/2 re-exposes the key exchange to Logjam-style precomputation attacks, allowing passive decryption of the VPN.
CRYPTO-010.impact: Disruptive to the VPN. Changing the DH group (and PFS) renegotiates Phase 1 / Phase 2 and rebuilds the tunnel (brief outage), and must be mirrored on the peer or the tunnel will fail to establish. No reload; no non-VPN data-plane impact. Note that running the show command to rule out a false positive is completely non-disruptive.
references: NIST SP 800-131A — Diffie-Hellman group / key-size requirements
CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark — Use DH group 14 or stronger for IKE
NIST SP 800-53 Rev.5 — SC-12 (Cryptographic Key Establishment)

CTRL-001.risk:
The scanner searched the entire running-config and found none of the three CoPP markers it looks for: a top-level `control-plane` section (regex `^control-plane`), a `policy-map` whose name/body references `control-plane` (regex `policy-map.*control-plane`), or a `service-policy input ...copp` binding (regex `service-policy input.*copp`). Because none matched, `check_copp()` concluded that no Control Plane Policing policy is applied and raised CTRL-001 at HIGH severity. This runs only on IOS, IOS-XE and Catalyst 9800 WLC (the auditor's SUPPORTED_PLATFORMS); NX-OS and ASA/FTD are skipped because CoPP is expressed with different syntax there.

CoPP protects the router/switch CPU (the control and management plane) by classifying and rate-limiting traffic punted to the CPU: routing-protocol hellos, ARP, ICMP, SSH/SNMP/NTP destined to the box, and exception/unknown traffic. Without CoPP, any host that can reach the device can saturate the CPU.

Concrete attack scenario: an adversary on an adjacent segment floods the device with ICMP echoes, fragmented packets, TTL-expiry packets, or a routing-protocol SYN storm. All of this is punted to the CPU (ATT&CK T1499 Endpoint Denial of Service / Network DoS). CPU hits 100%, the device stops servicing routing adjacencies, BGP/OSPF neighbors flap, and the box may become unmanageable over SSH.

Blast radius: a single flooded core or distribution device can black-hole traffic for an entire site or peering domain when adjacencies drop. Recovery may require out-of-band console access.

Platform nuance: IOS/IOS-XE use MQC (`class-map` -> `policy-map` -> `control-plane` service-policy). NX-OS instead uses `copp profile strict|moderate|lenient` and a differently bound `control-plane` policy — out of scope for this module. ASA has connection/ICMP limits rather than MQC CoPP. On Catalyst switches, hardware rate-limiters supplement software CoPP.

Compliance: maps to DoS-protection and least-functionality expectations in CIS control-plane guidance, NIST 800-53 SC-5, and Cisco's device-hardening CoPP recommendation.
steps: Confirm platform and CPU baseline: `show version` and `show processes cpu history` so you can measure improvement after the change.
Check whether any CoPP remnants exist: `show run | section control-plane` and `show policy-map control-plane`.
Inventory legitimate control/management sources you must permit (management subnets, NTP/SNMP/AAA servers, routing peers) so the policy does not drop production traffic.
Enter config mode: `configure terminal`.
Build ACLs to match management/critical traffic, e.g. `ip access-list extended CoPP-Mgmt` then `permit tcp 10.0.0.0 0.0.0.255 any eq 22`, and similar ACLs for SNMP, NTP and routing.
Create class-maps: `class-map match-any COPP-ROUTING` then `match access-group name CoPP-Routing`, repeating for management, normal and undesirable classes.
Create the policy-map: `policy-map CoPP-Policy`.
Under each class add a rate limiter, e.g. `class COPP-MGMT` then `police 8000 conform-action transmit exceed-action drop` (tune bps/pps to your platform; values shown are illustrative).
Add an explicit catch-all: `class class-default` then `police 32000 conform-action transmit exceed-action drop` — set conservatively so you do not starve legitimate exception traffic.
Apply the policy to the control plane: `control-plane` then `service-policy input CoPP-Policy`.
On Catalyst platforms, also review hardware rate-limiters and control-plane host protection if supported by the image.
Because a mis-scoped ACL can lock you out, schedule a maintenance window and keep an out-of-band console session open during the change.
Apply during a low-traffic period and immediately watch for dropped management/routing sessions.
Validate classification counters: `show policy-map control-plane input`.
Confirm CPU is stable/lower under load: `show processes cpu sorted | exclude 0.00`.
Save the configuration: `copy running-config startup-config` (or `write memory`).
Document the policy and rate values in change control, and add the ACLs/policy to your golden template.
CTRL-001.cli:
ip access-list extended CoPP-Routing
 permit ospf any any
 permit tcp any any eq bgp
 permit tcp any eq bgp any
ip access-list extended CoPP-Mgmt
 permit tcp 10.0.0.0 0.0.0.255 any eq 22
 permit udp 10.0.0.0 0.0.0.255 any eq snmp
!
class-map match-any COPP-ROUTING
 match access-group name CoPP-Routing
class-map match-any COPP-MGMT
 match access-group name CoPP-Mgmt
!
policy-map CoPP-Policy
 class COPP-ROUTING
  police 8000 conform-action transmit exceed-action transmit
 class COPP-MGMT
  police 4000 conform-action transmit exceed-action drop
 class class-default
  police 32000 conform-action transmit exceed-action drop
!
control-plane
 service-policy input CoPP-Policy
!
end
copy running-config startup-config
CTRL-001.verify: `show policy-map control-plane input` shows CoPP-Policy attached with per-class conform/exceed byte and packet counters incrementing. `show run | section control-plane` returns `service-policy input CoPP-Policy`. CPU under `show processes cpu history` should remain low during a control-plane load test.
CTRL-001.rollback: `configure terminal` -> `control-plane` -> `no service-policy input CoPP-Policy`, then `copy running-config startup-config`. Caveat: removing CoPP re-exposes the CPU to control-plane DoS/flood attacks that can crash routing adjacencies.
CTRL-001.impact: Applying an over-restrictive policy can drop management (SSH/SNMP) or routing traffic and, in the worst case, lock you out — hence the maintenance window and console session. A correctly scoped policy is non-disruptive to data-plane forwarding and does not require a reload; it only meters CPU-punted traffic.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 2.1.1 — Control Plane (CoPP)
Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — Control Plane Policing

CTRL-002.risk:
For every `router ospf ...` section present, the scanner enumerated interfaces (via get_interfaces) and selected any whose config contains an `ip ospf` line, marking them as OSPF-speaking. For each such interface it then checked for either `ip ospf authentication` or `ip ospf message-digest-key`. The named interface {intf} had neither, so CTRL-002 fired at HIGH. This check runs on IOS, IOS-XE and Catalyst 9800 (WLC); NX-OS/ASA/FTD are out of scope for this module.

OSPF without authentication accepts any Hello/LSA from any device that can reach the OSPF multicast (224.0.0.5/6) on the segment. There is no cryptographic proof of peer identity.

Concrete attack: an adversary who gains L2 presence (rogue switch, compromised host, or a laptop on an unsecured access port that carries the OSPF VLAN) speaks OSPF, forms an adjacency, and injects LSAs advertising itself as the best path — classic route injection / traffic redirection enabling man-in-the-middle or black-hole (ATT&CK T1557 Adversary-in-the-Middle, T1565 Data Manipulation). They can also flood malformed LSAs to churn SPF and destabilize the area (DoS).

Blast radius: because LSAs flood area-wide, a single unauthenticated interface can poison the routing table of every router in the area, redirecting or dropping traffic for many subnets.

Platform nuance: the strongest option on modern IOS-XE is SHA via key-chains (`ip ospf authentication key-chain NAME`); legacy MD5 uses `ip ospf message-digest-key <id> md5 <key>` plus `ip ospf authentication message-digest`. Detection is per-interface — if you enable authentication only with `area <n> authentication message-digest` under the process but the interface lacks interface-level keying, the finding can still be raised, so key every OSPF interface. Passive-interface does not remove the risk on transit links.

Compliance: aligns with CIS OSPF authentication guidance, NIST 800-53 IA-3 (device identification/authentication) and SC-8, and CWE-306.
steps: Identify OSPF interfaces and current auth state: `show ip ospf interface brief` and `show ip ospf interface {intf}`.
Confirm the OSPF process/area and neighbor list: `show ip ospf neighbor`.
Decide the algorithm: prefer SHA via key-chain on IOS-XE; use MD5 if peers only support message-digest.
Enter config mode: `configure terminal`.
For key-chain/SHA: `key chain OSPF-KEYS` -> `key 1` -> `key-string <secret>` -> `cryptographic-algorithm hmac-sha-256`.
Apply on the interface: `interface {intf}` -> `ip ospf authentication key-chain OSPF-KEYS`.
For MD5 instead: `interface {intf}` -> `ip ospf authentication message-digest` and `ip ospf message-digest-key 1 md5 <secret>`.
Coordinate a matching key on the neighbor first or during a window — a one-sided change drops the adjacency until both ends match.
Repeat for every OSPF-speaking interface flagged, including the neighbor's corresponding interfaces.
Optionally enforce area-wide with `router ospf <pid>` -> `area <n> authentication message-digest`, but still key each interface.
During key rollover, use overlapping keys in the key-chain (`accept-lifetime`/`send-lifetime`) to avoid an adjacency drop.
Watch the adjacency re-form: it will pass through INIT/EXSTART then reach FULL once both sides match.
Validate authentication is active on the interface (see verify).
Confirm no unexpected neighbor loss: `show ip ospf neighbor`.
Save: `copy running-config startup-config`.
Add OSPF authentication to the golden template so new interfaces inherit it.
Document keys in your secrets store and schedule periodic key rotation.
CTRL-002.cli:
key chain OSPF-KEYS
 key 1
  key-string S3cureOspfKey
  cryptographic-algorithm hmac-sha-256
!
interface {intf}
 ip ospf authentication key-chain OSPF-KEYS
!
end
copy running-config startup-config
CTRL-002.verify: `show ip ospf interface {intf}` shows a line such as `Cryptographic authentication enabled` or `Message digest authentication enabled` with the key id. `show ip ospf neighbor` still lists the peer in FULL state, confirming the authenticated adjacency re-formed.
CTRL-002.rollback: `interface {intf}` -> `no ip ospf authentication key-chain OSPF-KEYS` (or `no ip ospf authentication message-digest` and `no ip ospf message-digest-key 1 md5 <secret>`). Caveat: reverting re-opens the interface to rogue OSPF adjacencies and LSA/route injection on that segment.
CTRL-002.impact: Disruptive if applied one-sided: the OSPF adjacency drops and reconverges (brief loss of routes learned via that neighbor) until both ends share the key. No reload required. Use overlapping key lifetimes to avoid an outage during rollover.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 2.2.1 — OSPF Neighbor Authentication
Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — Routing Protocol Authentication
NIST SP 800-53 IA-3 (Device Identification and Authentication)
CWE-306 Missing Authentication for Critical Function

CTRL-003.risk:
Within each `router bgp ...` section the scanner collected every line containing both `neighbor` and `remote-as` (i.e. defined BGP peers), extracted the neighbor address/peer-group token with a regex, and checked whether any line in that same BGP section matches `neighbor <ip> ... password`. For peer {ip} no password line was found, so CTRL-003 fired at HIGH. Runs on IOS/IOS-XE/WLC; NX-OS and ASA/FTD are skipped by this module.

BGP rides on a plain TCP session (port 179). Without MD5/TCP-AO authentication the session integrity depends only on correct TCP sequence numbers.

Concrete attack: an off-path or on-path adversary who can guess/observe the TCP tuple injects a spoofed TCP RST to tear down the BGP session (ATT&CK T1498/T1499 style disruption) causing route withdrawal and reconvergence storms; or, on a shared segment, hijacks the session to inject bogus prefixes (route/prefix hijack, T1565 Data Manipulation) to redirect or black-hole traffic. eBGP peers across an exchange or WAN are especially exposed.

Blast radius: a torn-down or hijacked eBGP session can withdraw or poison many prefixes, impacting reachability for entire networks downstream and, for internet edges, enabling large-scale traffic interception.

Platform nuance: the classic control is `neighbor <ip> password <secret>` (TCP MD5, RFC 2385). Modern IOS-XE also supports TCP-AO via `neighbor <ip> ao <keychain> include-tcp-options` which is stronger but less widely interoperable. Detection matches password on a line referencing the neighbor's address; if you set the password on a peer-group and the neighbor only inherits it, the per-neighbor line may be absent and the check can still flag it — so verify the effective password with show commands and, if needed, key the peer-group explicitly.

Compliance: CIS BGP authentication guidance, NIST 800-53 IA-3/SC-8, CWE-306/CWE-345.
steps: Identify the peer and session state: `show ip bgp summary` and `show ip bgp neighbors {ip}`.
Confirm whether the peer is direct or inherits from a peer-group: `show run | section router bgp`.
Coordinate the shared secret with the peer's administrator (eBGP) or the remote team (iBGP) before applying.
Enter config mode: `configure terminal` -> `router bgp <asn>`.
Apply the password: `neighbor {ip} password <shared-secret>`.
If the peer is a peer-group member, apply on the peer-group: `neighbor <PG-NAME> password <shared-secret>` so all members inherit it.
Optionally prefer TCP-AO on capable IOS-XE: define a key chain then `neighbor {ip} ao <keychain-name> include-tcp-options`.
Apply during a maintenance window — setting the password resets the TCP session, so the BGP peer will bounce.
Ensure both ends configure the identical secret within the TCP hold time to avoid a prolonged down state.
Watch the session re-establish: it moves through Idle/Connect/OpenSent to Established.
Verify authentication is active (see verify) and prefixes are re-learned.
Repeat for every flagged neighbor, including route-reflector clients and eBGP peers.
Confirm prefix counts returned to expected values: `show ip bgp summary`.
Check logs for MD5 mismatch messages: `show logging | include BGP`.
Save: `copy running-config startup-config`.
Store the secret in your secrets manager and schedule rotation.
Add neighbor authentication to the BGP golden template.
CTRL-003.cli:
router bgp 65001
 neighbor {ip} password S3cureBgpSecret
!
end
copy running-config startup-config
CTRL-003.verify: `show ip bgp neighbors {ip}` shows the session `Established`. `show run | section router bgp` contains `neighbor {ip} password ...` (or the TCP-AO `ao` binding). `show ip bgp summary` shows the expected accepted-prefix count, confirming the authenticated session recovered.
CTRL-003.rollback: `router bgp <asn>` -> `no neighbor {ip} password`, then `copy running-config startup-config`. Caveat: removing the password re-exposes the session to TCP RST teardown and BGP route-injection/hijack attacks.
CTRL-003.impact: Disruptive at apply time: configuring or changing the password resets the BGP TCP session, so the peer drops and reconverges (temporary loss of that peer's routes) until both ends match. No reload. Mismatched secrets leave the session down, so tight coordination is essential.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 2.2.2 — BGP Neighbor Authentication
