Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — BGP TCP MD5 / TCP-AO
CWE-345 Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity

CTRL-004.risk:
For each `router eigrp ...` section the scanner scanned the section's lines for the substring `authentication`. The flagged section {section} contained no such line, so CTRL-004 fired at HIGH. Runs on IOS/IOS-XE/WLC; NX-OS/ASA/FTD skipped.

EIGRP with no authentication trusts any neighbor that can send EIGRP Hellos (multicast 224.0.0.10) on a connected segment.

Concrete attack: a rogue device on the segment forms an EIGRP adjacency and advertises attractive routes (low delay/bandwidth composite metric) to draw traffic to itself for interception or to black-hole it (route injection; ATT&CK T1557/T1565). Malformed update floods can also churn the topology table (DoS).

Blast radius: injected routes propagate through the EIGRP AS via queries/updates, so one rogue neighbor can influence the forwarding tables of many routers, redirecting or dropping traffic for numerous subnets.

Important detection nuance: this check inspects only lines inside the `router eigrp` section. In CLASSIC EIGRP, authentication is configured on the interface (`ip authentication mode eigrp <as> md5` and `ip authentication key-chain eigrp <as> <chain>`), NOT under the router process — so a classic-mode config that is actually authenticated can still be flagged here (a false positive to be aware of). In NAMED-MODE EIGRP, authentication lives under the process (`address-family` -> `af-interface` -> `authentication mode hmac-sha-256`), which the check does detect. To satisfy both the strongest security posture and this detection, migrate to named mode with HMAC-SHA-256, or at minimum ensure key-chain MD5 auth is present on every EIGRP interface.

Platform nuance: HMAC-SHA-256 is available in named mode on modern IOS/IOS-XE; classic mode supports MD5 only. Compliance: CIS EIGRP authentication guidance, NIST 800-53 IA-3, CWE-306.
steps: Identify EIGRP mode and interfaces: `show run | section router eigrp` and `show ip eigrp interfaces`.
List current neighbors: `show ip eigrp neighbors`.
Choose named mode with HMAC-SHA-256 (preferred) or classic-mode MD5 key-chains.
Create a key chain: `key chain EIGRP-KEYS` -> `key 1` -> `key-string <secret>`.
Enter config: `configure terminal` -> `router eigrp <name>` (named mode).
Enter the address family: `address-family ipv4 autonomous-system <as>`.
Apply per-interface auth: `af-interface {intf}` -> `authentication mode hmac-sha-256 0 <secret>` (or `authentication key-chain EIGRP-KEYS`).
For classic mode instead: on each EIGRP interface set `ip authentication mode eigrp <as> md5` and `ip authentication key-chain eigrp <as> EIGRP-KEYS`.
Configure the identical key on all neighbors before or during the window to avoid adjacency loss.
Use overlapping key lifetimes in the key chain for hitless rollover.
Apply and watch adjacencies drop then re-form to an up state.
Verify authentication is active (see verify).
Confirm neighbor count and routes returned: `show ip eigrp neighbors` / `show ip route eigrp`.
Repeat across all EIGRP-speaking interfaces and routers in the AS.
Save: `copy running-config startup-config`.
Add EIGRP authentication to the golden template; store keys in the secrets manager.
Schedule periodic key rotation using the key-chain lifetimes.
CTRL-004.cli:
key chain EIGRP-KEYS
 key 1
  key-string S3cureEigrpKey
!
router eigrp CORP
 address-family ipv4 autonomous-system 100
  af-interface GigabitEthernet0/1
   authentication mode hmac-sha-256 0 S3cureEigrpKey
  exit-af-interface
!
end
copy running-config startup-config
CTRL-004.verify: `show ip eigrp interfaces detail {intf}` shows the authentication mode (HMAC-SHA-256 / MD5) and key-chain in use. `show ip eigrp neighbors` still lists the peer as up. For classic mode, `show run interface {intf}` shows `ip authentication mode eigrp` and the key-chain binding.
CTRL-004.rollback: Remove the af-interface `no authentication mode hmac-sha-256 ...` (named) or `no ip authentication mode eigrp <as> md5` on the interface (classic), then save. Caveat: reverting re-opens EIGRP to rogue-neighbor route injection on that segment.
CTRL-004.impact: Disruptive if applied one-sided: EIGRP adjacencies drop and reconverge (temporary loss of EIGRP-learned routes via that peer) until keys match. No reload. Overlapping key lifetimes make rollover hitless.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 2.2.3 — EIGRP Authentication
Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — EIGRP Authentication
NIST SP 800-53 IA-3 (Device Identification and Authentication)
CWE-306 Missing Authentication for Critical Function

CTRL-005.risk:
The scanner collected all NTP config lines (via get_ntp_config). Because at least one existed, NTP is considered configured. It then checked whether any NTP line contains the substring `authenticate`; none did, so CTRL-005 fired at MEDIUM. Runs on IOS/IOS-XE/WLC; NX-OS/ASA/FTD skipped.

Without NTP authentication the device accepts time from any source that can answer as its configured server (or, worse, any peer), with no cryptographic verification of the time source's identity.

Concrete attack: an adversary spoofs NTP replies or stands up a rogue NTP server and shifts the device clock (ATT&CK T1565.001 Stored Data Manipulation / time tampering). Consequences: certificate/CRL validity checks pass or fail incorrectly (bypassing or breaking TLS/SSH PKI), Kerberos and time-sensitive auth fail, scheduled ACLs/time-ranges and EEM jobs mis-fire, and — critically for forensics — log and audit timestamps become unreliable, letting an attacker cover tracks (T1070 Indicator Removal, defeating log correlation).

Blast radius: every security control that depends on accurate time (cert validation, cross-SIEM log correlation, time-based access) is undermined on this device; if the device is itself an NTP server for others, the skew propagates downstream.

Platform nuance: IOS/IOS-XE support `ntp authenticate`, `ntp authentication-key <id> md5|sha1|sha2 <key>`, `ntp trusted-key <id>`, and per-server `ntp server <ip> key <id>`. Newer images support SHA1/SHA2 keys; prefer those over MD5. On Catalyst 9800 WLC (IOS-XE) the syntax is identical. Note that NTP authentication alone does not restrict which servers you sync to — combine with `ntp access-group` and the trusted-key list.

Compliance: CIS NTP authentication guidance, NIST 800-53 AU-8 (time stamps) and SC-45 (time synchronization), CWE-345.
steps: Review current NTP config and sync status: `show run | include ntp` and `show ntp status` / `show ntp associations`.
Confirm reachable, trusted NTP servers and obtain the shared key material for them.
Enter config mode: `configure terminal`.
Define an authentication key: `ntp authentication-key 1 md5 <key>` (prefer `sha1`/`sha2` if the image supports it).
Mark the key trusted: `ntp trusted-key 1`.
Enable authentication enforcement: `ntp authenticate`.
Associate each server with the key: `ntp server <ip> key 1` (this also clears CTRL-006).
Optionally restrict NTP peers with an ACL: `ntp access-group peer <acl>` or `ntp access-group serve-only <acl>`.
Ensure the upstream NTP server is configured with the identical key id and secret.
Apply and allow a few minutes for re-synchronization.
Confirm the device is synchronized and the source is authenticated (see verify).
Check logs for auth failures: `show logging | include NTP`.
Repeat for redundant NTP servers so all associations are keyed.
Verify dependent services (cert validation, logging timestamps) show correct time.
Save: `copy running-config startup-config`.
Add authenticated NTP to the golden template.
Store NTP keys in the secrets manager and rotate periodically.
CTRL-005.cli:
ntp authentication-key 1 md5 S3cureNtpKey
ntp trusted-key 1
ntp authenticate
ntp server 10.0.0.10 key 1
ntp server 10.0.0.11 key 1
!
end
copy running-config startup-config
CTRL-005.verify: `show ntp associations detail` shows the server as `authenticated` with the trusted key id. `show ntp status` reports `Clock is synchronized` with a valid reference. `show run | include ntp` lists `ntp authenticate`, `ntp trusted-key 1`, and each server with `key 1`.
CTRL-005.rollback: `no ntp authenticate`, `no ntp trusted-key 1`, `no ntp authentication-key 1 md5 <key>`, and remove `key 1` from each `ntp server` line. Caveat: reverting re-exposes the device to NTP spoofing and time manipulation that can break cert validation and corrupt log timestamps.
CTRL-005.impact: Low disruption. Enabling authentication briefly re-synchronizes the clock; if the upstream key does not match, the device will refuse to sync (falls back to local time) until corrected — so verify the server key first. No reload, no data-plane impact.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 2.3.1 — NTP Authentication
Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — Network Time Protocol
NIST SP 800-53 AU-8 (Time Stamps) / SC-45 (System Time Synchronization)
CWE-345 Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity

CTRL-006.risk:
The scanner examined every `ntp server <ip>` line and, for the flagged server {ip}, found no `key` token on that line. This means the server association is not bound to a trusted authentication key, so CTRL-006 fired at MEDIUM. Runs on IOS/IOS-XE/WLC; NX-OS/ASA/FTD skipped. This is the per-server companion to CTRL-005 (which flags the absence of global NTP authentication); a device can have `ntp authenticate` enabled yet still leave individual server lines unkeyed, which this check catches.

An unkeyed server association means the device will accept time from that server without verifying its identity via the authentication-key/trusted-key mechanism. Even if `ntp authenticate` is globally enabled, an association lacking a `key` reference is not cryptographically validated.

Concrete attack: an adversary who can intercept or spoof the path to that NTP server (on-path L2/L3 position, or by impersonating the server IP) injects forged time, drifting the clock (ATT&CK T1565.001). Downstream effects match CTRL-005: broken/bypassed certificate validity windows, mis-firing time-based ACLs and scheduled jobs, and unreliable log/audit timestamps that hamper incident correlation and let attackers hide activity.

Blast radius: any security or operational control on this device that trusts the clock is affected; if the device redistributes time to clients, the skew spreads.

Platform nuance: the fix is to append `key <id>` to the server line and ensure the matching `ntp authentication-key`, `ntp trusted-key`, and `ntp authenticate` are present — otherwise the key reference is inert. IOS, IOS-XE, and Catalyst 9800 WLC share this syntax. Prefer SHA1/SHA2 keys where the image allows over MD5.

Compliance: CIS NTP guidance, NIST 800-53 AU-8/SC-45, CWE-345.
steps: List NTP servers and their key bindings: `show run | include ntp server` and `show ntp associations detail`.
Confirm the authentication scaffolding exists: `show run | include ntp authenticate` and `show run | include ntp trusted-key`.
Obtain or confirm the shared key material for server {ip}.
Enter config mode: `configure terminal`.
If not already present, define the key: `ntp authentication-key 1 md5 <key>` (or sha1/sha2).
Mark it trusted: `ntp trusted-key 1`.
Enable enforcement globally: `ntp authenticate`.
Bind the flagged server to the key by re-entering the server line with the key: `ntp server {ip} key 1`.
Ensure the upstream server {ip} is configured with the identical key id and secret.
Repeat for any other unkeyed server associations reported.
Allow the device to re-synchronize (a few minutes).
Verify the association is now authenticated (see verify).
Check logs for NTP auth failures: `show logging | include NTP`.
Confirm time is correct and stable: `show ntp status`.
Save: `copy running-config startup-config`.
Update the golden template so all NTP server lines carry `key`.
Store and schedule rotation of NTP keys.
CTRL-006.cli:
ntp authentication-key 1 md5 S3cureNtpKey
ntp trusted-key 1
ntp authenticate
ntp server {ip} key 1
!
end
copy running-config startup-config
CTRL-006.verify: `show run | include ntp server` shows `ntp server {ip} key 1`. `show ntp associations detail` reports the {ip} association as `authenticated` with the correct key id and its candidate/`sys.peer` status. `show ntp status` shows the clock synchronized.
CTRL-006.rollback: Re-enter the server without the key: `ntp server {ip}` (removes the key binding). Caveat: the association reverts to unauthenticated and re-exposes the device to time spoofing from that path.
CTRL-006.impact: Low disruption. Adding the key re-establishes the association authenticated; if the server-side key does not match, the device will stop syncing to {ip} until corrected. No reload, no data-plane impact.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 2.3.2 — NTP Trusted Keys
Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — NTP Authentication Keys
CWE-345 Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity

CTRL-007.risk:
The scanner walked Ethernet/GigabitEthernet/FastEthernet/TenGig interface sections. For the flagged interface {intf} it found `switchport mode access` (an access/edge port), a `spanning-tree portfast` line, but no `spanning-tree bpduguard` line. Portfast without BPDU Guard triggered CTRL-007 at MEDIUM. Runs on IOS/IOS-XE/WLC; NX-OS/ASA/FTD skipped.

Portfast moves an access port straight to STP forwarding, skipping listening/learning. That is correct for a host/AP/printer port, but it removes the delay that would otherwise limit the damage from an unexpected switch. BPDU Guard is the safety interlock: it err-disables the port the instant a BPDU arrives, because a BPDU on an access edge port means someone plugged in a switch (or a bridging device).

Concrete attack: an adversary plugs a small switch, a laptop running bridging/STP, or a device advertising a superior bridge priority into {intf}. With portfast and no BPDU guard, the rogue device participates in STP, can win the root election (STP root hijack, ATT&CK T1557 Adversary-in-the-Middle), and forces topology recalculation so traffic flows through attacker-controlled paths for sniffing/interception, or causes repeated TCN churn as a DoS.

Blast radius: an STP root takeover can redirect traffic for an entire VLAN/broadcast domain, and TCN storms can destabilize forwarding switch-wide, affecting every host on the affected VLANs.

Platform nuance: enable per-interface with `spanning-tree bpduguard enable`, or globally for all portfast-edge ports with `spanning-tree portfast bpduguard default`. NOTE the detection is per-interface: relying only on the global default will NOT clear this specific finding because the scanner looks for the interface-level `spanning-tree bpduguard` command — apply the interface command (recommended alongside the global default) to both harden and satisfy the check. Do not substitute BPDU Filter for BPDU Guard — filter hides BPDUs rather than acting on them.

Compliance: CIS STP protection guidance, NIST 800-53 SC-7/CM-7, CWE-693.
steps: Identify access/edge ports with portfast: `show spanning-tree interface {intf} detail` and `show run interface {intf}`.
Confirm {intf} truly faces a host/AP/printer and not a legitimate switch/trunk uplink.
Enter config mode: `configure terminal`.
Select the interface: `interface {intf}`.
Enable BPDU Guard on the port: `spanning-tree bpduguard enable`.
Confirm portfast is edge/access-appropriate: `spanning-tree portfast` (not `portfast trunk` unless it is an edge trunk to an AP).
Optionally set the global safety net: `spanning-tree portfast bpduguard default`.
Only use `spanning-tree bpdufilter enable` where truly required — never as a replacement for BPDU guard.
For AP/voice edge trunks, use `spanning-tree portfast trunk` plus `spanning-tree bpduguard enable`.
Apply to all flagged access ports, not just one, using a range: `interface range GigabitEthernet1/0/1 - 24`.
Plan for auto-recovery of err-disabled ports: `errdisable recovery cause bpduguard` and `errdisable recovery interval 300`.
Warn the operations team that a rogue switch will now err-disable the port (expected behavior).
Validate BPDU guard is active (see verify).
Confirm a port receiving an inbound BPDU goes err-disabled: `show interfaces status err-disabled`.
Save: `copy running-config startup-config`.
Add BPDU guard + portfast to the access-port golden template.
Document the errdisable recovery policy in operations runbooks.
CTRL-007.cli:
interface {intf}
 switchport mode access
 spanning-tree portfast
 spanning-tree bpduguard enable
!
spanning-tree portfast bpduguard default
errdisable recovery cause bpduguard
errdisable recovery interval 300
!
end
copy running-config startup-config
CTRL-007.verify: `show spanning-tree interface {intf} detail` shows `Bpdu guard is enabled` and `Portfast enabled`. `show spanning-tree summary` shows `PortFast BPDU Guard Default is enabled` if the global default was set. A port receiving a BPDU appears in `show interfaces status err-disabled`.
CTRL-007.rollback: `interface {intf}` -> `no spanning-tree bpduguard enable` (and `no spanning-tree portfast bpduguard default` globally). Caveat: reverting re-exposes the edge port to rogue-switch insertion and STP root-hijack / topology-manipulation attacks.
CTRL-007.impact: Non-disruptive to legitimate hosts — a normal PC/printer/AP sends no BPDUs and is unaffected. If a switch or bridging device is (or gets) connected, the port err-disables, which is the intended protective action but will drop that device until cleared. No reload.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 2.4.1 — Spanning-Tree BPDU Guard
Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — Layer 2 Security (BPDU Guard)

CTRL-008.risk:
The scanner checked whether the config contains a global `no cdp run` line (anchored regex `^no cdp run`). It did not, so CDP is running globally and CTRL-008 fired at MEDIUM. Runs on IOS/IOS-XE/WLC; NX-OS/ASA/FTD skipped by this module.

Cisco Discovery Protocol is a Cisco proprietary L2 protocol that periodically multicasts device details to directly connected neighbors: device ID/hostname, hardware platform, software version, management IP addresses, native VLAN, port ID, and power/VTP data. This is high-value reconnaissance handed out for free.

Concrete attack: an adversary with L2 access (rogue device on an access port, or a compromised host) passively captures CDP frames and learns the exact IOS/IOS-XE version and platform (ATT&CK T1590/T1592 reconnaissance, T1046 network service discovery). Knowing the precise software version lets them select a matching CVE/exploit; management IPs reveal the OOB/mgmt network to target next; native VLAN disclosure aids VLAN-hopping. Historically, CDP parsing bugs (e.g. CDPwn) also allowed RCE/DoS via crafted CDP frames, so enabled CDP is also an attack surface, not just an information leak.

Blast radius: reconnaissance data accelerates lateral movement and targeted exploitation across the campus/edge; a CDP-parsing vulnerability could compromise the device itself.

Platform nuance: disable everywhere with `no cdp run`, or selectively with `no cdp enable` on untrusted/edge interfaces while keeping it on infrastructure links. Caution: CDP is used by Cisco IP phones (voice VLAN assignment, PoE negotiation) and some management tools — disabling globally can break phone provisioning, so on access layers with Cisco phones prefer per-interface `no cdp enable` on non-phone ports. On Catalyst 9800 WLC (IOS-XE) CDP toward APs may be expected. Note the detection only clears on the global `no cdp run`.

Compliance: CIS CDP guidance, NIST 800-53 CM-7 (least functionality), CWE-200 information exposure.
steps: Determine where CDP is needed: `show cdp neighbors detail` to see connected phones/APs/switches.
Confirm current global state: `show cdp` (reports whether CDP is globally enabled and the timers).
Decide global-off vs per-interface-off based on Cisco phone/AP dependencies.
Enter config mode: `configure terminal`.
To disable everywhere: `no cdp run`.
To disable selectively instead: on each untrusted/edge port `interface {intf}` -> `no cdp enable`, leaving infrastructure/phone ports enabled.
On access layers with Cisco IP phones, keep CDP on phone-facing ports (voice VLAN/PoE) and disable on data-only ports.
Coordinate with voice/collaboration teams before disabling if phones rely on CDP for provisioning.
Apply and confirm neighbor tables reflect the change: `show cdp neighbors`.
Verify management/monitoring tools that used CDP topology still function or have LLDP as an alternative.
Validate CDP state (see verify).
Repeat the per-interface approach across all edge ports if not disabling globally.
Save: `copy running-config startup-config`.
Add the CDP policy to the golden template (edge ports `no cdp enable`).
Document any ports that intentionally retain CDP and why.
Re-run the audit to confirm CTRL-008 clears (requires the global `no cdp run` for this check).
CTRL-008.cli:
no cdp run
!
! -- OR, selective per-interface on untrusted edge ports:
! interface range GigabitEthernet1/0/1 - 24
!  no cdp enable
!
end
copy running-config startup-config
CTRL-008.verify: `show cdp` returns `CDP is not enabled` after `no cdp run`. `show run | include cdp run` shows `no cdp run`. For per-interface, `show cdp interface {intf}` no longer lists the port and `show cdp neighbors` no longer lists edge devices on those ports.
CTRL-008.rollback: `cdp run` globally (or `cdp enable` on the interface). Caveat: re-enabling CDP resumes broadcasting device model/version/IP/VLAN details to neighbors, restoring the reconnaissance exposure and CDP-parsing attack surface.
CTRL-008.impact: Global `no cdp run` can break Cisco IP phone voice-VLAN/PoE negotiation and any CDP-based network-management/topology discovery — mind voice and NMS dependencies. It does not affect data-plane forwarding and needs no reload. Per-interface disable on data-only ports is the least-disruptive approach.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 2.5.1 — Cisco Discovery Protocol
Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — Disabling CDP
CWE-200 Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor

CTRL-009.risk:
The scanner checked for a global `lldp run` line (anchored regex `^lldp run`) and found it, so LLDP is enabled globally and CTRL-009 fired at LOW. Runs on IOS/IOS-XE/WLC; NX-OS/ASA/FTD skipped. Note the asymmetry with CDP: CDP is on by default (flagged unless `no cdp run` is present), whereas LLDP is off by default and is only flagged when explicitly turned on with `lldp run`.

LLDP (IEEE 802.1AB) is the vendor-neutral equivalent of CDP: it advertises system name, description (often including OS/version), management address, port ID/description, and via LLDP-MED voice VLAN and PoE capabilities to directly attached neighbors.

Concrete attack: like CDP, an adversary with L2 access passively harvests LLDP TLVs to fingerprint the device platform, software, and management addressing for targeted exploitation and lateral-movement planning (ATT&CK T1590/T1592 reconnaissance). Because LLDP is multi-vendor, it also leaks details of non-Cisco neighbors an attacker could pivot to. Severity is LOW because LLDP is opt-in and typically deployed intentionally (e.g. for LLDP-MED phone/AP support or DCIM topology), so the finding is a prompt to confirm it is actually needed and scoped to trusted ports.

Blast radius: information exposure that eases reconnaissance across the L2 domain; limited direct impact on its own.

Platform nuance: disable globally with `no lldp run`, or scope it per-interface with `no lldp transmit` / `no lldp receive` on untrusted edge ports while retaining it on infrastructure or phone/AP ports that need LLDP-MED. IOS, IOS-XE, and Catalyst 9800 WLC share this syntax. If LLDP-MED is providing voice VLAN/PoE to IP phones, do not blanket-disable — restrict to phone-facing ports instead.

Compliance: CIS LLDP guidance, NIST 800-53 CM-7 (least functionality), CWE-200.
steps: Determine LLDP dependencies: `show lldp neighbors detail` (phones, APs, DCIM, other vendors).
Confirm global state: `show lldp` (reports whether LLDP is running and the timers).
Decide global-off vs per-interface-off based on LLDP-MED phone/AP needs.
Enter config mode: `configure terminal`.
To disable everywhere: `no lldp run`.
To scope instead: on untrusted edge ports `interface {intf}` -> `no lldp transmit` and `no lldp receive`.
Keep LLDP/LLDP-MED enabled only on phone/AP-facing ports if required for voice VLAN/PoE.
Coordinate with voice/collaboration and DCIM teams if they consume LLDP topology.
Apply and confirm neighbor tables reflect the change: `show lldp neighbors`.
Verify monitoring/topology tools still work or use an alternative source.
Validate LLDP state (see verify).
Repeat per-interface across all edge ports if not disabling globally.
Save: `copy running-config startup-config`.
Add the LLDP policy to the golden template.
Document any ports intentionally retaining LLDP and why.
Re-run the audit to confirm CTRL-009 clears after `no lldp run`.
CTRL-009.cli:
no lldp run
!
! -- OR, selective per-interface on untrusted edge ports:
! interface range GigabitEthernet1/0/1 - 24
!  no lldp transmit
!  no lldp receive
!
end
copy running-config startup-config
CTRL-009.verify: `show lldp` indicates LLDP is not enabled after `no lldp run`. `show run | include lldp run` no longer shows `lldp run`. For per-interface scoping, `show lldp interface {intf}` shows Tx/Rx disabled and `show lldp neighbors` no longer lists edge devices on those ports.
CTRL-009.rollback: `lldp run` globally (or `lldp transmit` / `lldp receive` on the interface). Caveat: re-enabling LLDP resumes advertising system/version/management-address TLVs to neighbors, restoring the reconnaissance exposure — and may be required for LLDP-MED phone/AP operation.
CTRL-009.impact: Low disruption in general, but global `no lldp run` can break LLDP-MED voice-VLAN/PoE negotiation for IP phones and any LLDP-based topology/DCIM discovery. No effect on data-plane forwarding and no reload required. Prefer per-interface scoping where LLDP-MED is in use.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 2.5.2 — Link Layer Discovery Protocol
Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — Disabling LLDP
CWE-200 Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor

CTRL-010.risk:
The scanner checked for a global `no ip source-route` line (anchored regex `^no ip source-route`) and did not find it, so IP source routing is left at its (historically enabled) default and CTRL-010 fired at HIGH. Runs on IOS/IOS-XE/WLC; NX-OS/ASA/FTD skipped by this module.

IP source routing is an IP option (Loose Source Route / Strict Source Route, LSRR/SSRR) that lets the sender dictate the path a packet takes through the network, overriding the routing table's normal next-hop decisions. It is a legacy feature with essentially no legitimate modern use and a long history of abuse.

Concrete attack: an adversary crafts packets with source-route options to (1) spoof a trusted source address and still receive return traffic by forcing the reply back along the specified path, defeating IP-based access controls and enabling session/trust hijacking; (2) reach hosts on segments that should be unreachable by steering packets through intermediate routers, bypassing segmentation and ACLs; and (3) map internal topology by observing which routes are honored (ATT&CK T1090 proxy/pivot, T1599 network boundary bridging, T1557 AiTM). Source routing effectively lets an attacker use the router as a routing oracle and pivot.

Blast radius: broken assumptions about reachability and source-address trust can expose otherwise isolated internal networks and let spoofed traffic bypass perimeter/segmentation ACLs — potentially network-wide.

Platform nuance: the fix is the single global command `no ip source-route` on IOS/IOS-XE (Catalyst 9800 WLC inherits IOS-XE). This is a long-standing Cisco hardening baseline. On some recent IOS-XE trains source routing is already disabled by default, but explicitly configuring `no ip source-route` makes the posture auditable and satisfies this check. (NX-OS/ASA handle IP options differently and are out of scope for this module.)

Compliance: CIS IP source-route guidance, NIST 800-53 SC-7 (boundary protection) / CM-7, CWE-290 authentication bypass by spoofing.
steps: Confirm current state: `show run | include ip source-route` (absence of `no ip source-route` means it is enabled).
Confirm no legitimate application relies on source routing (virtually none do).
Enter config mode: `configure terminal`.
Disable it globally: `no ip source-route`.
Optionally also drop IP options at ingress with `ip options drop` (IOS-XE) for defense in depth.
Because this only affects source-routed packets (which normal traffic never sets), it is safe to apply outside a window, but note it in change control.
Apply and confirm the command is present in the running config.
Validate that source-routed packets are now dropped (see verify).
On multi-device fleets, push the command to all IOS/IOS-XE routers and L3 switches.
Confirm no monitoring/legacy tool depended on source routing (extremely unlikely).
Verify normal routing/reachability is unaffected: spot-check `ping`/`traceroute` to known destinations.
Check interface counters/logs for dropped IP-options packets if `ip options drop` was added.
Confirm the change is consistent across all L3 devices in the segment.
Save: `copy running-config startup-config`.
Add `no ip source-route` to the golden template so all devices inherit it.
Re-run the audit to confirm CTRL-010 clears.
CTRL-010.cli:
no ip source-route
!
! Optional defense-in-depth on IOS-XE:
! ip options drop
!
end
copy running-config startup-config
CTRL-010.verify: `show run | include source-route` shows `no ip source-route`. A test packet crafted with an LSRR/SSRR option (e.g. via an extended `ping` with the loose/strict source-route option, or a packet-crafting tool from a test host) is now dropped/ignored rather than forwarded along the specified path. If `ip options drop` was added, `show ip traffic` shows options being dropped.
CTRL-010.rollback: `ip source-route` (re-enables the feature). Caveat: reverting re-exposes the device to source-route spoofing, ACL/segmentation bypass, and pivoting through the router via attacker-specified paths.
CTRL-010.impact: Non-disruptive. Only packets carrying source-route IP options are affected, and legitimate traffic does not set them. No admin/VPN session drop, no traffic impact on normal flows, and no reload required.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 2.6.1 — Disable IP Source Routing
Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — IP Source Routing

CTRL-011.risk:
The scanner checked for a global `no ip gratuitous-arps` line (anchored regex `^no ip gratuitous-arps`) and did not find it, so the sending of gratuitous ARP (GARP) by the device is left enabled and CTRL-011 fired at LOW. Runs on IOS/IOS-XE/WLC; NX-OS/ASA/FTD skipped.

Gratuitous ARP is an unsolicited ARP reply/announcement a device sends to advertise its own IP-to-MAC binding (used for duplicate-address detection, HSRP/failover MAC updates, and refreshing neighbor caches). The global `no ip gratuitous-arps` command stops the device from originating these unsolicited announcements.

Security concern / attack context: gratuitous ARP is the mechanism underlying ARP cache poisoning / ARP spoofing. While disabling the router from SENDING gratuitous ARPs does not by itself stop an attacker from poisoning caches, unnecessary GARP traffic reduces the clarity of the ARP environment; the hardening intent is to suppress unsolicited ARP the box does not need to send (ATT&CK T1557.002 ARP Cache Poisoning is the related adversary technique). This is a LOW-severity, defense-in-depth item — the primary anti-spoofing controls are Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) with DHCP snooping and port security, which are the real mitigations for ARP poisoning; disabling gratuitous ARP is a complementary reduction of unsolicited ARP chatter.

Blast radius: on its own, limited — this is a hygiene/hardening item rather than a direct exploit vector; the meaningful ARP-poisoning defense is DAI.

Platform nuance: `no ip gratuitous-arps` is a global IOS command. IMPORTANT caveat: on IOS-XE (including Catalyst 9800 WLC) this global knob may not exist or may be deprecated, and gratuitous-ARP behavior there is handled per-interface or by the FHRP; on such platforms this check can be a false positive and the correct control is DAI plus per-interface ARP handling. Do NOT disable gratuitous ARP where HSRP/VRRP/GLBP or a first-hop redundancy failover relies on GARP to update downstream MAC tables, or failover convergence will suffer.

Compliance: CIS ARP hardening guidance, NIST 800-53 SC-7, CWE-300/CWE-345.
steps: Confirm current state: `show run | include gratuitous` (absence of `no ip gratuitous-arps` means it is enabled).
Check for FHRP dependence: `show standby brief` / `show vrrp brief` / `show glbp brief` — GARP is used to update MAC tables on failover.
If HSRP/VRRP/GLBP failover relies on GARP, do NOT globally disable it; rely on DAI instead.
Verify the platform actually supports the global command (IOS classic yes; some IOS-XE trains no).
Enter config mode: `configure terminal`.
On supported IOS platforms with no FHRP GARP dependency: `no ip gratuitous-arps`.
Implement the real ARP-poisoning defenses: enable DHCP snooping `ip dhcp snooping` and per-VLAN `ip dhcp snooping vlan <list>`.
Enable Dynamic ARP Inspection: `ip arp inspection vlan <list>` and trust uplinks with `ip arp inspection trust` on infrastructure ports.
Add port security on access ports where appropriate to limit MAC spoofing.
Apply during a maintenance window if FHRP is present, to observe failover behavior.
Confirm the command is present (see verify).
Validate FHRP failover still converges acceptably if GARP was disabled (force a failover test).
Confirm DAI is dropping spoofed ARP: `show ip arp inspection statistics`.
On IOS-XE/WLC where the global command is unavailable, document that DAI is the compensating control and treat CTRL-011 as N/A.
Save: `copy running-config startup-config`.
Add DAI + DHCP snooping (and, where valid, `no ip gratuitous-arps`) to the golden template.
Re-run the audit to confirm CTRL-011 clears where the command applies.
CTRL-011.cli:
no ip gratuitous-arps
!
! Primary ARP-poisoning defense (recommended regardless):
ip dhcp snooping
ip dhcp snooping vlan 10,20,30
ip arp inspection vlan 10,20,30
!
interface GigabitEthernet1/0/48
 ip dhcp snooping trust
 ip arp inspection trust
!
end
copy running-config startup-config
CTRL-011.verify: `show run | include gratuitous` shows `no ip gratuitous-arps` on platforms that support it. `show ip arp inspection vlan <list>` shows DAI active on the protected VLANs, and `show ip arp inspection statistics` shows forwarded/dropped ARP counts, confirming the real anti-poisoning control is enforcing.
CTRL-011.rollback: `ip gratuitous-arps` (re-enables device-originated gratuitous ARP). Caveat: this only re-enables the device sending unsolicited ARP; it does not undo DAI. Removing DAI (`no ip arp inspection vlan ...`) is what would actually re-expose the network to ARP cache poisoning.
CTRL-011.impact: Disabling gratuitous ARP is generally low-impact, BUT it can slow or break first-hop redundancy (HSRP/VRRP/GLBP) failover convergence, which relies on GARP to update downstream MAC tables — test failover if FHRP is in use. No reload required. DAI/DHCP-snooping rollout should be staged (trust uplinks first) to avoid dropping legitimate ARP.
references: Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — ARP Security
CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark — Dynamic ARP Inspection / ARP Hardening

DATA-001.risk:
The scanner enumerated routed interfaces matching `Serial|GigabitEthernet|TenGig|Ethernet`, identified those carrying an `ip address` line (Layer-3 interfaces), and flagged every one that lacks Unicast Reverse Path Forwarding. Concretely, the finding fires when neither `ip verify unicast source reachable-via {rx|any}` (modern CEF-based syntax) nor the legacy `ip verify unicast reverse-path` appears in the interface stanza. uRPF is a per-interface anti-spoofing control, so absence on a given interface leaves that interface accepting packets whose source IP could not legitimately arrive there. The check is rated MEDIUM.

Without uRPF the router forwards packets regardless of whether the source address is routable back out the receiving interface. This is the enabling condition for source-address spoofing (MITRE ATT&CK T1498 Network Denial of Service and T1557 spoofing/adversary-in-the-middle). An attacker on or transiting a customer/edge interface can forge arbitrary source IPs to launch reflection/amplification DDoS (DNS, NTP, memcached), evade ACLs that trust source addresses, and hide the true origin of scans and attacks.

Blast radius is significant on internet edge and inter-AS/customer-facing links: a spoofing host can direct reflected floods at third parties (making your ASN a participant in an attack), bypass source-based security policy, and defeat traceback. On internal L3 boundaries it undermines segmentation because spoofed intra-campus sources can impersonate trusted subnets.

Platform nuances: IOS and IOS-XE both support CEF-based `ip verify unicast source reachable-via rx` (strict) or `any` (loose); strict mode is correct on single-homed/stub interfaces, loose mode on asymmetric/multi-homed links to avoid dropping valid asymmetric traffic. uRPF requires CEF (`ip cef`) to be enabled globally. NX-OS uses `ip verify unicast source reachable-via {rx|any}` as well but requires the urpf/CEF feature context and is not evaluated by this module (module scope is ios/iosxe/wlc). ASA/FTD are not in scope for this check.

Standards mapping: this aligns with BCP38/RFC2827 ingress filtering, CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark data-plane guidance, and NIST 800-53 SC-5 (denial-of-service protection). Enabling uRPF is the lowest-touch, industry-standard anti-spoofing baseline for routed edges.
steps: Inventory L3 interfaces and their role (edge/customer vs. core/asymmetric): `show ip interface brief` and `show running-config | section interface`.

Confirm CEF is enabled globally, since uRPF depends on it: `show ip cef summary` and `show running-config | include ip cef`; if absent, plan `ip cef` (or `ip cef distributed` on distributed platforms).
For each interface decide mode: use strict `rx` on single-homed/stub links and loose `any` on multi-homed or asymmetrically-routed links to avoid dropping legitimate return-path traffic.
Schedule a change window for edge/transit interfaces because misclassifying an asymmetric link as strict will drop production traffic.
Enter global config: `configure terminal`.
If CEF is off, enable it first: `ip cef`.
Select the interface: `interface GigabitEthernet0/1`.
Apply strict uRPF on a stub interface: `ip verify unicast source reachable-via rx`.
Or apply loose uRPF on a multi-homed interface: `ip verify unicast source reachable-via any`.
Optionally allow default-route matches on loose mode with the `allow-default` keyword, and permit self-pings with `allow-self-ping` only if operationally required.
Optionally attach an ACL to log/permit exceptions: `ip verify unicast source reachable-via rx <acl-number>` where the ACL logs spoofed drops.
Repeat per flagged interface; do NOT blanket-apply strict mode to core interfaces carrying asymmetric flows.
Exit interface and global config: `end`.
Validate drop counters against a baseline before saving to ensure legitimate traffic is not being dropped: `show cef drop` and `show ip interface GigabitEthernet0/1 | include drop`.
Monitor for several minutes during the window; if strict mode drops valid traffic on an asymmetric link, switch that interface to `any`.
Save the configuration: `copy running-config startup-config` (or `write memory`).
Document per-interface mode choice in change records for future audits.
Post-change, re-run the NSS scan to confirm DATA-001 clears on the remediated interfaces.
DATA-001.cli:
configure terminal
ip cef
!
interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 description WAN/edge - single-homed
 ip verify unicast source reachable-via rx
!
interface GigabitEthernet0/2
 description Multi-homed/asymmetric uplink
 ip verify unicast source reachable-via any allow-default
!
end
copy running-config startup-config
DATA-001.verify: Run `show ip interface GigabitEthernet0/1 | include verify|RPF|reachable`. Expected: a line such as `IP verify source reachable-via RX` (strict) or `... reachable-via ANY` (loose), followed by the drop/suppressed-drop counters. Also `show cef interface GigabitEthernet0/1 | include RPF` should show uRPF enabled, and `show cef drop` reports the RPF-drop counter (0 or only spoofed drops).
DATA-001.rollback: On the interface run `no ip verify unicast source reachable-via` (or `no ip verify unicast reverse-path` for legacy syntax), then `copy running-config startup-config`. Caveat: rolling back re-exposes the interface to source-IP spoofing, reflection/amplification DDoS participation, and ACL-bypass attacks.
DATA-001.impact: Enabling loose (`any`) mode is essentially non-disruptive. Strict (`rx`) mode is potentially disruptive: on asymmetrically-routed or multi-homed interfaces it silently drops packets whose source is not reachable via the ingress interface, which can black-hole legitimate flows. No reload is required and admin/VPN sessions are not dropped by the command itself, but misclassified interfaces can drop transit/user traffic, so apply strict mode only to verified single-homed links.
references: Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices - Unicast Reverse Path Forwarding

DATA-002.risk:
This check inspects the global configuration for DHCP Snooping. It fires when the config contains neither a global `ip dhcp snooping` line (matched as `^ip dhcp snooping$`) nor any `ip dhcp snooping vlan ...` statement, AND the device_type is `ios` or `iosxe`. In other words, the Layer-2 DHCP integrity feature is entirely absent, meaning the switch trusts DHCP offers/acks arriving on any port. The check is rated HIGH.

DHCP Snooping builds a binding table (MAC, IP, lease, VLAN, port) by watching DHCP exchanges and, critically, only permits DHCP server messages (OFFER/ACK/NAK) to enter from ports explicitly marked trusted. Without it, an attacker plugged into any access port can stand up a rogue DHCP server (MITRE ATT&CK T1557.003 DHCP Spoofing) and hand out a malicious default gateway and DNS server, silently redirecting all victim traffic through the attacker for adversary-in-the-middle interception, credential theft, and TLS-stripping. A rogue server can also lease-exhaust the legitimate scope (DHCP starvation) causing denial of service.

Blast radius spans the entire VLAN/broadcast domain: every host that requests an address can be pointed at attacker-controlled infrastructure, and the snooping binding table is a prerequisite for Dynamic ARP Inspection (DATA-003) and IP Source Guard, so its absence also disables those downstream protections. This is why the check is rated HIGH.

Platform nuances: `ip dhcp snooping` is IOS/IOS-XE Catalyst switching syntax; access-layer Catalyst 9000 (IOS-XE) uses the same commands. NX-OS requires `feature dhcp` first, then `ip dhcp snooping` and `ip dhcp snooping vlan`, which this module does not evaluate (it gates on ios/iosxe). On a Catalyst 9800 WLC (also IOS-XE) DHCP snooping is generally handled on the upstream wired switch, so it may legitimately not apply on a pure controller. Trusted ports (uplinks toward the real DHCP server/router) MUST be marked `ip dhcp snooping trust` or DHCP will break. Optionally enable Option-82 (`ip dhcp snooping information option`) awareness and per-port `ip dhcp snooping limit rate`.

Standards mapping: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark data-plane L2 hardening and NIST 800-53 SC-7 boundary protection. DHCP snooping is a foundational campus access-layer control.
steps: Identify all VLANs that carry client endpoints and the uplink/trunk ports that face the legitimate DHCP server or router: `show vlan brief` and `show interfaces trunk`.
Locate the real DHCP server path so you know which ports must be trusted BEFORE enabling snooping (untrusted uplinks will block DHCP).
Enter global config: `configure terminal`.
Enable the feature globally: `ip dhcp snooping`.
Enable it on the specific client VLANs: `ip dhcp snooping vlan 10,20,30` (or a range `ip dhcp snooping vlan 100-110`).
On an L2-only access switch where Option-82 relay insertion is not desired and can cause the upstream relay to drop packets (giaddr=0), disable it: `no ip dhcp snooping information option`.
Mark every uplink/trunk toward the DHCP server or distribution as trusted: `interface GigabitEthernet1/0/48` then `ip dhcp snooping trust`.
On access ports facing clients, optionally rate-limit to blunt starvation attacks: `ip dhcp snooping limit rate 15`.
Leave client access ports untrusted (default) so rogue servers are blocked.
If using DHCP relay, ensure the relay path and giaddr still function after the Option-82 policy is set.
Schedule a maintenance window if the switch is in production, because a mis-marked trust boundary will break address assignment for a whole VLAN.
Exit config: `end`.
Verify the feature and trust boundary before saving: `show ip dhcp snooping`.
Confirm bindings populate as clients renew: `show ip dhcp snooping binding`.
Watch for legitimate DHCP being dropped (rate limit/errdisable) during the window: `show ip dhcp snooping statistics`.
Save the configuration: `copy running-config startup-config`.
After stabilizing, layer on DATA-003 (DAI) and IP Source Guard which consume the snooping table.
Re-run the NSS scan to confirm DATA-002 clears.
DATA-002.cli:
configure terminal
ip dhcp snooping
ip dhcp snooping vlan 10,20,30
no ip dhcp snooping information option
!
interface GigabitEthernet1/0/48
 description Uplink to distribution / DHCP server
 ip dhcp snooping trust
!
interface range GigabitEthernet1/0/1 - 47
 description Client access ports
 ip dhcp snooping limit rate 15
!
end
copy running-config startup-config
DATA-002.verify: Run `show ip dhcp snooping`. Expected output shows `Switch DHCP snooping is enabled`, the configured VLANs under `DHCP snooping is configured on the following VLANs`, and the uplink listed as `yes` under the Trusted column while access ports show `no`. Then `show ip dhcp snooping binding` should list live MAC/IP/lease entries as clients acquire addresses.
DATA-002.rollback: Remove per-port trust with `no ip dhcp snooping trust`, disable per-VLAN with `no ip dhcp snooping vlan 10,20,30`, and globally with `no ip dhcp snooping`; then `copy running-config startup-config`. Caveat: reverting re-exposes the VLAN to rogue DHCP servers (AiTM via malicious gateway/DNS) and DHCP starvation, and tears down the binding table that DAI and IP Source Guard depend on.
DATA-002.impact: Disruptive if the trust boundary is wrong: if the uplink toward the real DHCP server is not marked trusted, all DHCP DISCOVER/OFFER traffic on the snooped VLANs is dropped and clients fail to get leases. Correctly configured (uplinks trusted), it is transparent to users. No reload required; no admin session impact. Aggressive `limit rate` values can errdisable a port under legitimate bursts, so size conservatively.
references: Cisco Catalyst Configuration Guide - Configuring DHCP Snooping

DATA-003.risk:
This check looks for Dynamic ARP Inspection and fires when the running config has no `ip arp inspection vlan ...` line AND the device_type is `ios` or `iosxe`. DAI being absent means the switch performs no validation of ARP replies against the DHCP snooping binding table, so any host can send unsolicited/gratuitous ARP claiming to own any IP in the subnet. The check is rated HIGH.

Unvalidated ARP is the direct enabler of ARP cache poisoning / ARP spoofing (MITRE ATT&CK T1557.002). An attacker on an access port sends forged ARP replies mapping the default-gateway IP (or a peer host IP) to the attacker's MAC. Victims and the gateway then send frames to the attacker, giving a full adversary-in-the-middle position: traffic sniffing, session hijacking, credential capture, TLS downgrade/strip, and selective packet manipulation or blackholing. This is one of the most common lateral-movement and interception techniques on flat access VLANs.

Blast radius is the whole VLAN broadcast domain - a single compromised or malicious endpoint can intercept traffic for every other host on that VLAN, including the path to the default gateway. Because interception is passive from the victim's perspective, it is hard to detect without DAI logging.

Platform nuances and dependencies: DAI is IOS/IOS-XE Catalyst syntax and requires the DHCP Snooping binding table (DATA-002) to be functional first - DAI validates ARP against those bindings on untrusted ports. Uplinks/trunks toward the distribution layer and the DHCP server must be marked `ip arp inspection trust` (trust follows the same topology as DHCP-snooping trust). For hosts with static IPs (servers, printers) that never DHCP, add an ARP ACL (`arp access-list`) and bind it with `ip arp inspection filter`. NX-OS implements DAI with `ip arp inspection vlan` after `feature dhcp` but is not evaluated by this module. ASA/FTD/pure WLC are out of scope.

Standards mapping: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark L2 hardening, NIST 800-53 SC-7, and it directly mitigates CWE-300 (channel accessible by non-endpoint / MITM). Rated HIGH because it stops a high-impact, low-effort interception attack.
steps: Confirm DHCP Snooping (DATA-002) is enabled and its binding table is populated first - DAI depends on it: `show ip dhcp snooping binding`.
List the VLANs to protect and the trusted uplink topology (mirror the DHCP-snooping trust boundary): `show vlan brief` and `show interfaces trunk`.
Inventory statically-addressed hosts (servers/printers/appliances) that never use DHCP so you can build ARP ACLs for them.
Enter global config: `configure terminal`.
Enable DAI on the client VLANs: `ip arp inspection vlan 10,20,30`.
For static hosts, create an ARP ACL: `arp access-list STATIC-HOSTS` then `permit ip host 10.10.10.5 mac host aabb.ccdd.eeff`.
Bind the ARP ACL so static entries are not dropped: `ip arp inspection filter STATIC-HOSTS vlan 10`.
Mark uplinks/trunks toward distribution and the DHCP server as trusted: `interface GigabitEthernet1/0/48` then `ip arp inspection trust`.
Optionally enable additional integrity validation: `ip arp inspection validate src-mac dst-mac ip`.
Rate-limit ARP on untrusted access ports to prevent CPU/DoS via ARP floods (default on untrusted ports is 15 pps; tune as needed): `ip arp inspection limit rate 15`.
Schedule a maintenance window; a wrong trust boundary or missing ARP ACL will drop ARP and break connectivity for affected hosts.
Exit config: `end`.
Verify configuration and trust state before saving: `show ip arp inspection`.
Confirm per-interface trust and drop counters: `show ip arp inspection interfaces`.
Monitor for legitimate ARP drops during the window: `show ip arp inspection statistics` and syslog for `%SW_DAI` messages.
If static hosts are being dropped, extend the ARP ACL rather than trusting the access port.
Save the configuration: `copy running-config startup-config`.
Re-run the NSS scan to confirm DATA-003 clears.
DATA-003.cli:
configure terminal
! DAI depends on DHCP snooping being enabled first
ip arp inspection vlan 10,20,30
ip arp inspection validate src-mac dst-mac ip
!
arp access-list STATIC-HOSTS
 permit ip host 10.10.10.5 mac host aabb.ccdd.eeff
!
ip arp inspection filter STATIC-HOSTS vlan 10
!
interface GigabitEthernet1/0/48
 description Uplink to distribution - trusted
 ip arp inspection trust
!
interface range GigabitEthernet1/0/1 - 47
 ip arp inspection limit rate 15
!
end
copy running-config startup-config
DATA-003.verify: Run `show ip arp inspection`. Expected: the protected VLANs listed with `Enabled` status and the configured ACL/validation shown. `show ip arp inspection interfaces` should show uplinks as `Trusted` and access ports as `Untrusted` with a rate limit. `show ip arp inspection statistics` shows ARP forwarded vs. dropped; drops should correspond only to spoofed/invalid ARP.
DATA-003.rollback: Remove per-port trust (`no ip arp inspection trust`), the filter (`no ip arp inspection filter STATIC-HOSTS vlan 10`), and disable per-VLAN (`no ip arp inspection vlan 10,20,30`); then save. Caveat: reverting re-opens the VLAN to ARP cache poisoning and full adversary-in-the-middle interception of intra-VLAN and gateway-bound traffic.
DATA-003.impact: Disruptive if misconfigured: statically-addressed hosts without an ARP ACL, or a mistrusted uplink, will have their ARP dropped and lose connectivity; aggressive ARP rate limits can errdisable ports. With DHCP snooping working, correct trust boundaries, and ARP ACLs for static hosts, it is transparent. No reload required; no admin-session drop from the command. Recovery from errdisable needs `errdisable recovery cause arp-inspection` or a manual `shutdown`/`no shutdown`.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 3.2.2 (Dynamic ARP Inspection)
Cisco Catalyst Configuration Guide - Configuring Dynamic ARP Inspection

DATA-004.risk:
This check enumerates interfaces matching `GigabitEthernet|FastEthernet|TenGig|Ethernet`, treats any interface containing a `switchport` line as a switched port, and flags those with no `storm-control` command in the stanza. The finding is aggregated: it reports a count of switchports lacking storm control and lists up to 20 of the affected interface short-names. Storm control is the per-port rate limiter for broadcast, multicast, and unknown-unicast traffic. The check is rated MEDIUM.

Without storm control, a broadcast/multicast storm - from a Layer-2 loop (STP failure, miswired patch, or a device bridging two ports), a malfunctioning NIC, or a deliberate broadcast flood - can saturate the switching fabric and uplinks. This maps to MITRE ATT&CK T1498/T1499 (network/endpoint denial of service) and, just as often, is an availability incident triggered by human error. Once a storm builds, CPU and buffers are consumed replicating broadcast frames to every port in the VLAN, control-plane protocols (STP BPDUs, routing hellos) starve, and the switch or entire access domain can go dark.

Blast radius is the whole broadcast domain and any switch sharing the affected VLAN trunks; a single unprotected access port bridged into a loop can take down a wing or floor. Because the failure is self-amplifying, recovery frequently requires physically locating and unplugging the offending port.

Platform nuances: `storm-control` is IOS/IOS-XE Catalyst switchport syntax and is applied per interface with a threshold expressed as a percentage (`level`), packets-per-second (`level pps`), or bits-per-second (`level bps`), plus an optional `storm-control action {shutdown|trap}`. Catalyst 9000 (IOS-XE) uses the same commands. NX-OS uses `storm-control broadcast level <x>` too but is not evaluated here. Trunk/uplink ports are usually excluded or set higher because legitimate aggregate broadcast is higher there. Loopback/routed (`no switchport`) interfaces are not flagged because they have no `switchport` line.

Standards mapping: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark L2 availability hardening and NIST 800-53 SC-5 (DoS protection). Rated MEDIUM as an availability control.
steps: Identify access switchports vs. uplinks/trunks; uplinks may need higher thresholds or exclusion: `show interfaces status` and `show interfaces trunk`.
Baseline current broadcast/multicast rates so thresholds are set above normal peaks: `show interfaces counters broadcast` and `show interfaces counters`.
Decide a threshold model - percentage of bandwidth (simple), pps, or bps - and a level (e.g. 10% broadcast/multicast on access ports).
Choose an action: `shutdown` (errdisable the port on storm) for strict access ports, or `trap` (SNMP notify, keep forwarding) for less disruptive alerting.
Enter global config: `configure terminal`.
Select an access port or range: `interface range GigabitEthernet1/0/1 - 47`.
Apply broadcast storm control: `storm-control broadcast level 10.00`.
Apply multicast storm control: `storm-control multicast level 10.00`.
Optionally apply unknown-unicast: `storm-control unicast level 30.00`.
Set the action: `storm-control action shutdown` (or `storm-control action trap`).
For uplinks/trunks, either omit storm control or set higher thresholds to avoid clipping legitimate aggregate broadcast.
If using `shutdown`, enable automatic recovery so a transient storm does not require manual intervention: `errdisable recovery cause storm-control` and `errdisable recovery interval 300`.
Exit config: `end`.
Verify thresholds and current levels before saving: `show storm-control`.
Verify the broadcast-specific view: `show storm-control broadcast`.
Monitor for false-positive suppression on legitimate bursts; adjust `level` upward if needed.
Save the configuration: `copy running-config startup-config`.
Re-run the NSS scan to confirm the DATA-004 count drops to zero.
DATA-004.cli:
configure terminal
errdisable recovery cause storm-control
errdisable recovery interval 300
!
interface range GigabitEthernet1/0/1 - 47
 storm-control broadcast level 10.00
 storm-control multicast level 10.00
 storm-control unicast level 30.00
 storm-control action shutdown
!
end
copy running-config startup-config
DATA-004.verify: Run `show storm-control`. Expected: each configured port lists Broadcast/Multicast/Unicast with the Upper/Lower thresholds and current rate, and a Filter State of `Forwarding` under normal load (or `Blocking`/errdisabled only during a storm). `show storm-control broadcast` confirms the per-port broadcast level. `show interfaces status` should show ports `connected`, not `err-disabled`, under normal traffic.
DATA-004.rollback: On the affected interfaces run `no storm-control broadcast level`, `no storm-control multicast level`, `no storm-control unicast level`, and `no storm-control action shutdown`; then save. Caveat: reverting removes the rate limiter, re-exposing the switch to broadcast/multicast storms from L2 loops or floods that can black-hole the entire VLAN/broadcast domain.
DATA-004.impact: Low disruption when thresholds are set above normal peaks. The `action shutdown` variant will errdisable a port during a storm (intended), which can drop that endpoint until errdisable recovery or a manual bounce - so configure `errdisable recovery cause storm-control`. If thresholds are set too low, legitimate broadcast bursts (imaging, discovery) may be clipped or ports flapped. No reload required; no admin-session impact.
references: Cisco Catalyst Configuration Guide - Configuring Port-Based Traffic Control (Storm Control)
MITRE ATT&CK T1499 (Endpoint Denial of Service)

DATA-005.risk:
This check searches the entire running configuration for any interface line that explicitly reads `ip directed-broadcast` (regex `^\s*ip directed-broadcast$`, i.e. the feature turned ON, not the `no` form). If found, it reports every matching line/interface. Directed broadcast means the router will convert a unicast packet addressed to a subnet's broadcast address into a Layer-2 broadcast onto the destination LAN. The check is rated HIGH.

This is the classic enabler of the Smurf amplification attack (a form of MITRE ATT&CK T1498.001 Reflection Amplification / Direct Network Flood). An attacker sends ICMP echo requests with a spoofed source (the victim's IP) to your subnet's directed-broadcast address; every host on that subnet replies to the victim, multiplying the attacker's traffic by the host count and reflecting it off your network. Directed broadcast can also be abused to reach and flood internal hosts, to map subnets, and as a stepping stone for broadcast-based attacks against services that trust broadcast.

Blast radius: your network becomes an unwitting DDoS amplifier aimed at third parties (reputational and possibly abuse-report consequences for your ASN), and internal subnets with directed-broadcast enabled can be flooded from outside. Because amplification factor scales with hosts-per-subnet, a single enabled interface on a populated VLAN is a potent reflector. This is why the check is HIGH severity.

Platform nuances: On IOS 12.0 and later and all IOS-XE, `no ip directed-broadcast` is the DEFAULT, so a finding here means someone deliberately re-enabled it - treat it as intentional and investigate why. The remediation is simply to set `no ip directed-broadcast` on the interface(s). NX-OS does not forward directed broadcasts by default; ASA/FTD do not route directed broadcasts. The module runs on ios/iosxe/wlc; on a Catalyst 9800 WLC routed SVI this command also applies.

Standards mapping: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark data-plane hardening, NIST 800-53 SC-5 (DoS protection), and it directly addresses CWE-406 (insufficient control of network message volume / amplification). RFC 2644 made disabled the default precisely because of Smurf.
steps: Find every interface where directed-broadcast is explicitly enabled: `show running-config | include directed-broadcast`.
For each hit, review the interface stanza and determine why it was enabled (rare legitimate cases: some WoL relay designs): `show running-config interface Vlan10`.
If a legitimate WoL/relay need exists, plan to replace directed-broadcast with a targeted `ip helper-address` / UDP-forward design instead of leaving amplification open.
Enter global config: `configure terminal`.
Select the affected interface: `interface Vlan10` (or the physical/SVI reported).
Disable directed broadcast: `no ip directed-broadcast`.
Repeat for every interface returned by the grep, including SVIs and physical L3 interfaces.
As defense in depth, ensure edge ACLs deny inbound packets destined to subnet broadcast addresses.
Confirm no interface still carries the enabled form: `show running-config | include directed-broadcast` should return nothing (or only `no ip directed-broadcast`).
No maintenance window is strictly required, but note any application (e.g. legacy WoL) that relied on it and validate an alternative first.
Exit config: `end`.
Validate the interface no longer accepts directed broadcasts: `show ip interface Vlan10 | include directed`.
Test from an external host that a ping to the subnet broadcast address is not amplified/answered.
Save the configuration: `copy running-config startup-config`.
Document the reason the feature had been enabled and the approved alternative in change records.
Add a config-compliance guard so `ip directed-broadcast` re-enablement is alerted on in future.
Re-run the NSS scan to confirm DATA-005 clears.
DATA-005.cli:
configure terminal
interface Vlan10
 no ip directed-broadcast
!
interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 no ip directed-broadcast
!
end
copy running-config startup-config
DATA-005.verify: Run `show ip interface Vlan10 | include directed`. Expected: `Directed broadcast forwarding is disabled`. Also `show running-config | include directed-broadcast` should return only `no ip directed-broadcast` lines (the bare enabled form is gone). Externally, an ICMP echo to the subnet's directed-broadcast address should elicit no host replies.
DATA-005.rollback: Re-enable with `ip directed-broadcast` on the interface (optionally with an ACL argument to restrict which sources are translated), then save. Caveat: reverting re-exposes the subnet as a Smurf/amplification reflector and allows external directed-broadcast floods against internal hosts.
DATA-005.impact: Non-disruptive in virtually all environments because disabled is the modern default. The only traffic effect is that unicast-to-directed-broadcast translation stops, which breaks legacy applications that (rarely) relied on it, such as some subnet-wide Wake-on-LAN relay schemes - validate an `ip helper-address`-based alternative before disabling in those niche cases. No reload; no session drop.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 3.4.1 (IP Directed Broadcast)
RFC 2644 (Changing the Default for Directed Broadcasts)
Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices - Disable IP Directed Broadcasts
CWE-406 (Insufficient Control of Network Message Volume)

DATA-006.risk:
This check iterates all interfaces, identifies Layer-3 interfaces (those with an `ip address` line), and flags the first one that does not contain `no ip redirects`. By design it reports once per scan (it breaks after the first finding) to avoid interface-by-interface noise, but the remediation must be applied to ALL routed interfaces. ICMP redirects are messages a router sends to a host telling it that a better next-hop gateway exists for a destination on the same subnet. The check is rated MEDIUM.

With redirects enabled (the IOS default on routed interfaces), an on-link attacker can abuse the trust hosts place in ICMP redirects, or the router can be induced to emit redirects, to steer a victim's traffic toward a malicious gateway - an adversary-in-the-middle vector (MITRE ATT&CK T1557). Even without direct abuse, redirects leak topology hints and can be used to manipulate host routing tables. On the router itself, generating redirects consumes CPU and can be leveraged in DoS-style ICMP floods.

Blast radius is per-subnet: hosts on a segment whose gateway emits redirects can have individual route entries poisoned, enabling selective interception or blackholing of their traffic. On internet- or DMZ-facing interfaces this is more serious because it aids external reconnaissance and manipulation.

Platform nuances: `no ip redirects` is IOS/IOS-XE interface syntax and is the recommended hardening baseline on all routed interfaces (physical, subinterface, and SVI). Note that enabling HSRP on an interface automatically disables ICMP redirects for that interface unless `standby redirects` is configured. NX-OS uses `no ip redirects` as well but is out of module scope; ASA/FTD manage ICMP redirects differently and are not evaluated. On Catalyst 9800 WLC routed interfaces the IOS-XE command applies.

Standards mapping: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark data-plane ICMP hardening, NIST 800-53 SC-7 boundary protection, and it mitigates CWE-300 (MITM). Rated MEDIUM. Because the scanner reports only the first offending interface, treat the finding as a signal to audit and remediate every L3 interface.
steps: Enumerate all routed interfaces (physical, subinterfaces, SVIs, tunnels) that carry an IP address: `show ip interface brief` and `show running-config | section interface`.
Note interfaces already running HSRP/VRRP, where redirects may already be auto-disabled: `show standby brief`.
Decide to apply `no ip redirects` uniformly to all L3 interfaces as the hardening baseline.
Enter global config: `configure terminal`.
Select an interface: `interface GigabitEthernet0/1`.
Disable ICMP redirects: `no ip redirects`.
Repeat for every routed interface, including SVIs (`interface Vlan10`) and subinterfaces (`interface GigabitEthernet0/1.100`).
Use an interface range where possible to apply consistently: `interface range GigabitEthernet0/1 - 4`.
Prioritize internet-facing, DMZ, and user-facing gateway interfaces first.
Confirm no dynamic feature depends on redirects (rare); hosts should be using proper default gateways/DHCP-provided routes.
Exit config: `end`.
Validate per interface: `show ip interface GigabitEthernet0/1 | include redirect`.
Sweep the whole config to confirm coverage: `show running-config | include ip redirects`.
No maintenance window is needed; this is non-disruptive to correctly-configured hosts.
Save the configuration: `copy running-config startup-config`.
Add a compliance guard so new interfaces inherit `no ip redirects` (interface template/config macro).
Re-run the NSS scan to confirm DATA-006 clears across interfaces.
DATA-006.cli:
configure terminal
interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 no ip redirects
!
interface Vlan10
 no ip redirects
!
interface GigabitEthernet0/1.100
 no ip redirects
!
end
copy running-config startup-config
DATA-006.verify: Run `show ip interface GigabitEthernet0/1 | include redirect`. Expected: `ICMP redirects are never sent`. A config sweep `show running-config | include ip redirects` should show `no ip redirects` on each routed interface and no bare `ip redirects` lines.
DATA-006.rollback: Re-enable with `ip redirects` on the interface, then save. Caveat: reverting lets the router emit ICMP redirects again, re-opening host-routing manipulation / redirect-based adversary-in-the-middle and giving attackers a topology-leak and traffic-steering primitive on that segment.
DATA-006.impact: Non-disruptive. Properly configured hosts use their configured default gateway and do not rely on ICMP redirects for reachability, so disabling has no traffic effect in normal designs. No reload, no session drop. The only theoretical effect is in unusual multi-gateway-per-subnet topologies that leaned on redirects for optimal path selection; those should use correct static/dynamic routing instead.
references: Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices - Disable ICMP Redirects

DATA-007.risk:
This check iterates interfaces, identifies Layer-3 interfaces (those with an `ip address`), and flags the first one lacking `no ip unreachables`. Like DATA-006 it reports once (breaks after the first finding) to reduce noise, but remediation should target all externally- or untrusted-facing routed interfaces. ICMP unreachable messages are generated when the router drops a packet because no route exists, the port/protocol is unreachable, or an ACL denies it, and the message tells the sender exactly why. The check is rated LOW.

Those messages are a reconnaissance goldmine (MITRE ATT&CK T1046 Network Service Discovery / T1595 Active Scanning). By observing which probes elicit an administratively-prohibited, port-unreachable, or host-unreachable reply versus a silent drop, an attacker can map your ACL boundaries, discover live hosts and closed vs. filtered ports, and fingerprint the topology - the same signal nmap and firewalking rely on. Generating unreachables also costs router CPU, so a flood of undeliverable packets can be turned into a control-plane DoS.

Blast radius is primarily information disclosure at the network edge: it accelerates an attacker's target selection and ACL-evasion planning against everything behind that interface. It is rated LOW because it aids rather than directly executes compromise, but on internet-facing interfaces it meaningfully reduces attacker effort.

Platform nuances: `no ip unreachables` is IOS/IOS-XE interface syntax; it is especially recommended on untrusted/internet-facing interfaces and on null-routing interfaces (`interface Null0`) used for blackholing, since Null0 by default generates unreachables and can be abused for CPU-based DoS. Note that disabling unreachables also disables the ICMP 'fragmentation needed' (type 3 code 4) message that Path MTU Discovery relies on, so be deliberate on interfaces where PMTUD matters. NX-OS uses `no ip unreachables`; ASA/FTD handle unreachable generation via the global `icmp unreachable rate-limit <rate> burst-size <n>` command and per-interface `icmp` control, and are out of scope. Module scope is ios/iosxe/wlc.

Standards mapping: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark ICMP hardening, NIST 800-53 SC-7, and it addresses CWE-200 (exposure of sensitive information).
steps: Identify external/untrusted-facing and Null0 interfaces where unreachables should be suppressed: `show ip interface brief` and `show running-config | section interface`.
Distinguish internal interfaces where PMTUD/traceroute is operationally needed from edge interfaces where recon-hardening wins - apply selectively.
Enter global config: `configure terminal`.
Select the external-facing interface: `interface GigabitEthernet0/0`.
Disable ICMP unreachables: `no ip unreachables`.
Apply to Null0 to prevent unreachable-generation DoS from blackholed traffic: `interface Null0` then `no ip unreachables`.
Repeat for all internet-/DMZ-facing routed interfaces and subinterfaces.
Consider leaving unreachables enabled on internal interfaces if PMTUD for large-MTU/tunnel traffic depends on them, to avoid black-hole MTU issues.
As complementary hardening, apply CoPP/control-plane policing to cap ICMP-generation CPU even where unreachables remain.
Exit config: `end`.
Validate per interface: `show ip interface GigabitEthernet0/0 | include unreachable`.
Sweep config for coverage: `show running-config | include ip unreachables`.
Test from outside that denied/undeliverable probes now yield silence rather than ICMP type-3 replies (nmap shows filtered instead of closed).
Confirm PMTUD-dependent internal apps are unaffected.
Save the configuration: `copy running-config startup-config`.
Re-run the NSS scan to confirm DATA-007 clears.
DATA-007.cli:
configure terminal
interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 description Internet-facing
 no ip unreachables
!
interface Null0
 no ip unreachables
!
end
copy running-config startup-config
DATA-007.verify: Run `show ip interface GigabitEthernet0/0 | include unreachable`. Expected: `ICMP unreachables are never sent`. A sweep `show running-config | include ip unreachables` shows `no ip unreachables` on the intended interfaces. Externally, a port scan against filtered ports should return no ICMP type-3 (port/host unreachable) messages.
DATA-007.rollback: Re-enable with `ip unreachables` on the interface, then save. Caveat: reverting restores ICMP unreachable generation, re-enabling network reconnaissance (ACL/topology mapping, open-vs-filtered port discovery) and the associated control-plane DoS surface.
DATA-007.impact: Low disruption but requires judgment: disabling unreachables also suppresses the ICMP 'fragmentation needed' and other type-3 messages that Path MTU Discovery depends on. On internal interfaces carrying tunneled/large-MTU traffic this can cause MTU black-holing, so apply primarily to external/untrusted and Null0 interfaces. No reload; no session drop.
references: Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices - Disable ICMP Unreachables

DATA-008.risk:
This check iterates all interfaces, identifies Layer-3 interfaces (those with an `ip address` line), and flags every one that does NOT contain `no ip proxy-arp`. It aggregates the count and lists up to 10 affected interface short-names. Proxy ARP is enabled by default on IOS/IOS-XE routed interfaces: the router answers ARP requests on behalf of hosts it can reach on other subnets, effectively pretending to be the target so the requester sends frames to the router for forwarding. The check is rated MEDIUM.

Proxy ARP undermines the security boundary a subnet is supposed to provide. Because the router will answer ARP for addresses outside the local segment, a host can reach destinations it should not, masking misconfiguration and defeating the assumption that L2 adjacency equals same-subnet. Attackers exploit this for MAC/ARP-based spoofing and interception across subnets (MITRE ATT&CK T1557 adversary-in-the-middle), and it enlarges the router's ARP table, opening an ARP-table-exhaustion denial-of-service vector where a flood of ARP-for-remote-hosts consumes memory and CPU. It also hides host misconfiguration (wrong mask/gateway) that should fail loudly.

Blast radius is per-subnet but systemic: every routed interface with proxy ARP left on is an implicit cross-subnet forwarding helper, weakening segmentation across the device and enabling spoofing/DoS against the router's ARP process. Because default-on means most untouched configs are affected, the count is often large.

Platform nuances: `no ip proxy-arp` is the IOS/IOS-XE per-interface hardening command (Catalyst 9000 identical). NX-OS disables proxy ARP by default and uses `ip proxy-arp` only when explicitly enabled (out of scope here). ASA/FTD manage proxy ARP per-NAT/interface with `sysopt noproxyarp <interface>` and are not evaluated. Caveat: some legacy designs rely on proxy ARP (hosts with no/incorrect default gateway, certain NAT/DMVPN or dial scenarios) - verify before disabling on those interfaces. Module scope is ios/iosxe/wlc.

Standards mapping: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark data-plane hardening, NIST 800-53 SC-7, and it mitigates CWE-300 (MITM). Rated MEDIUM.
steps: List all routed interfaces so you know the full remediation set: `show ip interface brief` and `show running-config | section interface`.
Confirm which interfaces currently have proxy ARP active: `show ip interface | include Proxy` (look for `Proxy ARP is enabled`).
Verify no legitimate dependency exists (hosts relying on proxy ARP for off-subnet reachability, certain NAT/DMVPN/dial designs) before disabling on a given interface.
Ensure endpoints have correct default gateways/masks, since proxy ARP often masks host misconfiguration that will surface once disabled.
Enter global config: `configure terminal`.
Select an interface: `interface GigabitEthernet0/1`.
Disable proxy ARP: `no ip proxy-arp`.
Repeat across all routed interfaces, including SVIs (`interface Vlan10`) and subinterfaces, using ranges where possible: `interface range GigabitEthernet0/1 - 4`.
Skip (and document) any interface with a validated proxy-ARP dependency.
Schedule a short window on user-facing gateways in case any host silently depended on proxy ARP and needs its gateway/mask corrected.
Exit config: `end`.
Validate per interface: `show ip interface GigabitEthernet0/1 | include Proxy`.
Sweep for coverage: `show running-config | include proxy-arp`.
Monitor for hosts that lose off-subnet reachability after the change and fix their default gateway rather than re-enabling proxy ARP.
Save the configuration: `copy running-config startup-config`.
Create an interface template/config guard so new L3 interfaces get `no ip proxy-arp` by default.
Re-run the NSS scan to confirm the DATA-008 count reaches zero.
DATA-008.cli:
configure terminal
interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 no ip proxy-arp
!
interface Vlan10
 no ip proxy-arp
!
interface range GigabitEthernet0/2 - 4
 no ip proxy-arp
!
end
copy running-config startup-config
DATA-008.verify: Run `show ip interface GigabitEthernet0/1 | include Proxy`. Expected: `Proxy ARP is disabled`. A config sweep `show running-config | include proxy-arp` should show `no ip proxy-arp` on each routed interface. Optionally confirm hosts still reach off-subnet destinations via their real default gateway.
DATA-008.rollback: Re-enable with `ip proxy-arp` on the interface, then save. Caveat: reverting restores cross-subnet ARP answering, re-weakening segmentation and re-opening ARP-table-exhaustion DoS and cross-subnet spoofing/MITM opportunities; it also re-masks host gateway misconfigurations.
DATA-008.impact: Usually non-disruptive because well-configured hosts use a correct default gateway and never need proxy ARP. The risk is hosts that silently relied on it (missing/incorrect gateway or mask) will lose off-subnet connectivity when it is disabled - the correct fix is to repair the host's gateway, not re-enable proxy ARP. No reload; no admin/VPN session drop. Apply on user gateways within a window so any exposed host misconfig can be corrected immediately.
references: Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices - Disable Proxy ARP

DATA-009.risk:
This check iterates all interfaces and flags any whose stanza contains `ip mask-reply` in the enabled form (the parser explicitly excludes lines containing `no ip mask-reply`). It reports once per scan (breaks after the first match). IP mask-reply controls whether the router will answer ICMP Address Mask Request messages (ICMP type 17) with an Address Mask Reply (type 18) that discloses the subnet mask configured on that interface. The check is rated LOW.

Answering mask requests is an information-disclosure weakness (MITRE ATT&CK T1590 Gather Victim Network Information / T1595 Active Scanning).
