By sending a single ICMP type-17 probe, a remote attacker learns the exact subnet mask, which reveals subnet size, likely broadcast and gateway addresses, and how the address space is carved up - dramatically narrowing the host-enumeration search space and informing follow-on attacks such as directed-broadcast/Smurf targeting and precise scanning. Legitimate hosts obtain masks via DHCP or static configuration, so there is no operational need to answer these requests.

Blast radius is reconnaissance/information disclosure rather than direct compromise, which is why it is rated LOW. However, on internet- or DMZ-facing interfaces it hands an attacker free topology intelligence and should be closed as part of ICMP hardening. It compounds with DATA-007 (unreachables) and DATA-005 (directed broadcast) to make an interface much easier to map and abuse.

Platform nuances: `ip mask-reply` is IOS/IOS-XE interface syntax and is OFF by default, so a finding means it was explicitly enabled - investigate why and disable it with `no ip mask-reply`. NX-OS does not send mask replies and uses different ICMP handling; ASA/FTD do not answer ICMP mask requests by default and are out of scope. On Catalyst 9800 WLC routed interfaces the IOS-XE command applies. Module scope is ios/iosxe/wlc.

Standards mapping: Cisco IOS device-hardening guidance on ICMP message suppression, NIST 800-53 SC-7 boundary protection, and CWE-200 (exposure of sensitive information to an unauthorized actor).
steps: Find every interface where mask-reply is explicitly enabled: `show running-config | include mask-reply`.
Review each hit's interface stanza and determine why it was enabled - it is off by default, so treat as an intentional change to investigate: `show running-config interface GigabitEthernet0/0`.
Confirm no host relies on ICMP mask replies (they should get masks via DHCP/static config).
Enter global config: `configure terminal`.
Select the affected interface: `interface GigabitEthernet0/0`.
Disable mask replies: `no ip mask-reply`.
Repeat for every interface returned by the grep, prioritizing internet-/DMZ-facing interfaces.
As defense in depth, ensure edge ACLs deny inbound ICMP type 17 (address-mask-request).
Combine with other ICMP hardening on the same interfaces (`no ip unreachables`, `no ip redirects`) for a consistent baseline.
Exit config: `end`.
Validate the enabled form is gone: `show running-config | include mask-reply` should return nothing or only `no ip mask-reply`.
Test externally that an ICMP address-mask request now receives no type-18 reply.
No maintenance window is required; this is non-disruptive.
Save the configuration: `copy running-config startup-config`.
Document why mask-reply had been enabled in change records.
Add a config-compliance guard to alert if `ip mask-reply` is re-added.
Re-run the NSS scan to confirm DATA-009 clears.
DATA-009.cli:
configure terminal
interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 no ip mask-reply
!
end
copy running-config startup-config
DATA-009.verify: Run `show running-config | include mask-reply`. Expected: no bare `ip mask-reply` lines remain (only `no ip mask-reply` if shown at all). From an external host, `nmap -PM <ip>` (an ICMP netmask/type-17 probe) should receive no ICMP type-18 Address Mask Reply.
DATA-009.rollback: Re-enable with `ip mask-reply` on the interface, then save. Caveat: reverting causes the router to disclose interface subnet masks to any ICMP address-mask requester, restoring the reconnaissance/topology-disclosure exposure.
DATA-009.impact: Non-disruptive. Mask reply is off by default and no standard host depends on it for operation (masks come from DHCP/static config), so suppressing it has no traffic or session effect. No reload, no admin/VPN session drop.
references: Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices - Disable ICMP Mask Reply
MITRE ATT&CK T1590 (Gather Victim Network Information)
CWE-200 (Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor)

LOG-001.risk:
The scanner calls get_logging_config() (every top-level `logging ...` line) and searches for a remote collector using two regexes: `logging\s+(host\s+)?\d+\.` (IOS / IOS-XE syntax, including the older `logging <ip>` form) and `logging\s+server\s+` (NX-OS syntax). If neither pattern matches on any line, LOG-001 fires at HIGH severity. In other words, the device forwards logs nowhere off-box.

With no external syslog target, all event records live only in the local RAM buffer (and possibly the console/monitor). That buffer is volatile: a reload, power event, crash, or simple buffer wrap silently destroys the evidence. An attacker who gains privileged access can also `clear logging` to erase the only record of their activity — this maps to MITRE ATT&CK T1070 (Indicator Removal) and T1562.008 (Impair Defenses: Disable/Modify Cloud/Host Logs). Because the logs never leave the device, there is no tamper-evident copy on a separate trust boundary.

Concrete scenario: an adversary brute-forces or phishes VTY/SSH credentials (T1110 / T1078), escalates to enable, alters ACLs or adds a rogue admin, then reboots the device to clear volatile state. Without remote syslog the SOC has zero telemetry of the login, the config change, or the reboot cause. Incident responders cannot build a timeline and cannot prove scope.

Blast radius is fleet-wide for detection: a core router or firewall with no log export is a blind spot that also breaks cross-device correlation in the SIEM. Platform nuances: IOS/IOS-XE and Catalyst 9800 WLC use `logging host`; NX-OS uses `logging server` (with a per-server severity and optional `use-vrf`); ASA/FTD are excluded from this module (skip notice) because ASA uses `logging host <nameif> <ip>` on a different parser path and FTD logging is driven from FMC.

Compliance: remote logging to an independent system is required by the CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark (section 6.1.1, remote syslog host), NIST 800-53 AU-2/AU-4/AU-6/AU-9(2), and PCI-DSS Req. 10. Absence is effectively a CWE-778 Insufficient Logging condition.
steps: Inventory current logging with `show running-config | include logging` and `show logging` to confirm no `logging host` / `logging server` line and to capture existing buffer settings before changing anything.
Confirm reachability and time sync first: `show ntp status` (accurate timestamps are worthless without NTP) and `ping <syslog-server-ip>` sourced from the intended management interface.
Identify the management source interface (usually a Loopback or dedicated OOB interface): `show ip interface brief | include Loopback`.
Enter global config: `configure terminal`.
(IOS / IOS-XE / 9800 WLC) Add the collector: `logging host 10.10.20.5` — repeat for a second collector for redundancy, e.g. `logging host 10.10.20.6`.
(NX-OS) Instead use `logging server 10.10.20.5 6 use-vrf management facility local7` (severity 6 = informational; keyword order is host, severity, then use-vrf/facility).
Set the forwarding severity so security events are captured: `logging trap informational` (IOS/IOS-XE). On NX-OS the level is the numeric severity field in the `logging server` line itself.
Pin the origin so logs correlate cleanly: `logging source-interface Loopback0` (IOS/IOS-XE) and `logging origin-id hostname` (IOS-XE).
Enable a healthy local buffer as a fallback so events survive brief collector outages: `logging buffered 64000 informational`.
Ensure timestamps are on every message: `service timestamps log datetime msec show-timezone` (IOS/IOS-XE) so records are forensically usable.
If the collector is reached over a routed VRF (common on NX-OS/OOB designs), add `use-vrf <vrf>` (NX-OS) or configure `logging host <ip> vrf <name>` on IOS-XE.
Review transport preference now — if the collector supports it, prefer TCP/TLS (see LOG-002) rather than the default UDP.
Exit and validate on-box: `end` then `show logging` — confirm the host appears under 'Trap logging' and the message counter increments.
Generate a test event to prove end-to-end delivery, e.g. toggle an unused loopback (`interface Loopback99` / `shutdown` / `no shutdown`), then confirm arrival on the SIEM.
Coordinate a brief change window only if adding logging to a heavily loaded control plane; adding a syslog host itself is non-disruptive to traffic.
Persist the change: `copy running-config startup-config` (IOS/IOS-XE/NX-OS) or `write memory`.
Confirm the SIEM/collector is parsing the device (correct facility, hostname, and severity) and add the device to any log-source coverage dashboard.
Document the collector IPs and source interface in the change record and the running baseline for future audits.
LOG-001.cli:
configure terminal
 logging source-interface Loopback0
 logging origin-id hostname
 logging host 10.10.20.5
 logging host 10.10.20.6
 logging trap informational
 logging buffered 64000 informational
 service timestamps log datetime msec show-timezone
end
copy running-config startup-config

! --- NX-OS equivalent ---
! logging server 10.10.20.5 6 use-vrf management facility local7
! logging source-interface loopback0
! copy running-config startup-config
LOG-001.verify: `show logging` — under the header expect: 'Trap logging: level informational ... Logging to 10.10.20.5 ... Logging to 10.10.20.6' with a non-zero 'xxx message lines logged' counter. `show running-config | include logging host|logging server|logging trap` should echo the configured collectors. On NX-OS: `show logging server` lists each server, VRF, and severity.
LOG-001.rollback: Remove with `no logging host 10.10.20.5` (IOS/IOS-XE) or `no logging server 10.10.20.5` (NX-OS). Caveat: reverting re-blinds the SOC — all event history returns to the volatile local buffer and can be lost on reload or erased with `clear logging`, defeating forensics.
LOG-001.impact: Non-disruptive. Adding a syslog destination does not drop admin, SSH, or VPN sessions and does not affect data-plane traffic; no reload required. Only caution is a very high message rate at debug level (avoid — use informational) which can add minor CPU/mgmt-link load.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 6.1.1 — Set 'logging host' to a remote syslog server
Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — 'Logging Best Practices'
NIST SP 800-53 Rev.5 AU-2, AU-4, AU-6, AU-9(2)

LOG-002.risk:
This check only runs when at least one syslog collector was found. For each configured host line the scanner tests whether the string contains 'tcp' or 'tls'; if a host line has neither token it is treated as the default UDP transport and LOG-002 fires at MEDIUM (it reports the first offending host, attaches up to five affected host lines via affected_items, then breaks). The detection is literal: `logging host 10.10.20.5` with no `transport` keyword is UDP/514 and is flagged.

UDP syslog (RFC 3164 style) has two structural weaknesses. First, it is connectionless and unacknowledged: under congestion, an oversubscribed management link, or a collector restart, log datagrams are silently dropped with no retransmission — precisely when volume spikes during an incident. Second, it is plaintext, so anyone on the transit path can read message contents and, worse, spoof them. UDP source addresses are trivially forged, enabling ATT&CK T1565.002 (Transmitted Data Manipulation) / log injection: an attacker floods the collector with forged events to bury real indicators or frame another host, and can also passively harvest topology, ACL hits, and usernames from the cleartext stream (T1040 Network Sniffing).

Concrete scenario: an on-path adversary in the management segment sniffs UDP/514 to map the network and read failed-auth messages, then, during their intrusion, spoofs thousands of benign-looking events to the collector so the true 'config changed' and 'login success' records are lost in the noise or dropped outright. The SOC's timeline is now unreliable.

Blast radius: unreliable/forgeable logs undermine every downstream detection and any legal/forensic use of the data. TCP restores guaranteed, ordered delivery; TLS (syslog over TLS, typically TCP/6514) adds confidentiality, integrity, and server/mutual authentication via PKI. Platform nuances: IOS-XE and Catalyst 9800 support `transport tcp` and, on 17.x, TLS via `logging host ... transport tls port 6514 profile <name>` where the profile references a `crypto pki trustpoint`; NX-OS has NO plain-TCP keyword — encrypted/secure transport is TLS via `logging server <ip> <sev> secure trustpoint client-identity <tp>`. Older IOS may support only UDP/TCP.

Compliance: NIST 800-53 SC-8 (transmission confidentiality/integrity) and AU-9 (protect audit information); CWE-319 Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information; RFC 6587 (syslog over TCP), RFC 5425 (syslog over TLS).
steps: Enumerate offending collectors: `show running-config | include logging host|logging server` and note which lines lack `transport tcp`/`tls` (IOS-XE) or `secure` (NX-OS).
Confirm the collector actually listens on TCP (or TLS/6514) BEFORE switching — a TCP switch to a UDP-only listener will drop all logs. Verify with the SIEM team.

For TLS, first provision PKI: create/import a trustpoint with the collector's CA, e.g. `crypto pki trustpoint SYSLOG-CA` / `enrollment terminal` / `revocation-check none`, then `crypto pki authenticate SYSLOG-CA`.
(IOS-XE 17.x TLS) Define the TLS profile that binds the trustpoint: `logging tls-profile SYSLOG-TLS` then set `tls-version tlsv1.2` and `trustpoint SYSLOG-CA` under it.
Enter config mode: `configure terminal`.
(IOS/IOS-XE, TCP) Replace the UDP host: `no logging host 10.10.20.5` then `logging host 10.10.20.5 transport tcp port 514`.
(IOS-XE 17.x, TLS) Use `logging host 10.10.20.5 transport tls port 6514 profile SYSLOG-TLS` (references the profile/trustpoint defined above).

(NX-OS) NX-OS has no `transport tcp` keyword; reliability/encryption is provided by TLS: `logging server 10.10.20.5 6 secure trustpoint client-identity SYSLOG-TP port 6514 use-vrf management` (requires a `crypto ca trustpoint` with the client identity).
Keep the forwarding level correct: `logging trap informational` (IOS/IOS-XE) so the transport change does not alter what is sent.
If you run dual collectors, migrate them one at a time so a misconfiguration does not sever all logging simultaneously.
Because a wrong collector port silently drops logs, do this in a short maintenance window and keep a session open to watch counters.
Exit config: `end`.
Validate the session state: `show logging` should show the host with TCP/TLS and an increasing counter; on NX-OS `show logging server` shows the secure/trustpoint fields.
Generate a test event and confirm arrival AND an established TCP/TLS session on the collector (connection/netstat view).
For TLS, confirm the certificate chain validates and the connection is not failing — check `show crypto pki certificates` and collector logs.
Save: `copy running-config startup-config` / `write memory`, then update the logging baseline/runbook with the transport, port, and trustpoint per collector.
LOG-002.cli:
configure terminal
 no logging host 10.10.20.5
 logging host 10.10.20.5 transport tcp port 514
 logging trap informational
end
copy running-config startup-config

! --- TLS variant (IOS-XE 17.x): PKI + tls-profile first ---
! crypto pki trustpoint SYSLOG-CA
!  enrollment terminal
!  revocation-check none
! logging tls-profile SYSLOG-TLS
!  tls-version tlsv1.2
!  trustpoint SYSLOG-CA
! logging host 10.10.20.5 transport tls port 6514 profile SYSLOG-TLS

! --- NX-OS secure (TLS) variant ---
! logging server 10.10.20.5 6 secure trustpoint client-identity SYSLOG-TP port 6514 use-vrf management
LOG-002.verify: `show logging` — the host entry should read 'Logging to 10.10.20.5 (tcp port 514 ...)' or '(tls port 6514 ...)' with a live/UP session and increasing message count. On NX-OS: `show logging server` displays the secure/trustpoint fields for the server. A packet capture on the mgmt link should show a TCP handshake, not UDP/514 datagrams.
LOG-002.rollback: Revert with `no logging host 10.10.20.5 transport tcp port 514` then `logging host 10.10.20.5` (default UDP); on NX-OS drop the `secure ...` keywords from the `logging server` line. Caveat: reverting re-exposes logs to silent loss under congestion and to on-path sniffing/spoofing (cleartext, unauthenticated), reopening CWE-319.
LOG-002.impact: Low but non-zero risk of disruption to the log stream (not to production traffic). If the collector's TCP/TLS listener or port is wrong, logs are dropped until fixed — hence the maintenance window and one-collector-at-a-time approach. No admin/VPN session drop, no reload. TLS adds minor CPU for the crypto session.
references: Cisco IOS-XE Hardening Guide — Secure/Encrypted Logging
Cisco IOS-XE Configuration Guide — Reliable Delivery and Syslog over TLS
RFC 6587 (Syslog over TCP), RFC 5425 (Syslog over TLS)
CWE-319: Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information

LOG-003.risk:
After confirming a `logging trap <level>` line exists, the scanner maps the keyword (or numeric value) to a syslog severity number 0-7 using the standard table (emergencies=0 ... debugging=7). If the resolved level is < 5 (i.e. emergencies, alerts, critical, errors, or warnings) LOG-003 fires at MEDIUM with the offending level interpolated into the title. The finding means the device forwards only relatively severe messages and suppresses everything at notifications (5) and informational (6).

The risk is a silent detection gap. Many security-relevant events are emitted precisely at notifications/informational: interface state changes, successful and failed logins, %SYS-5-CONFIG_I 'configured from' notices, LINK/LINEPROTO transitions, AAA events, ACL hit logging, and 'running config changed' notifications. If the trap level is 'warnings' or stricter, a legitimate but malicious `configure terminal` session, a new admin account, or an interface being enabled for exfiltration never reaches the SIEM. This directly enables ATT&CK T1562.006 (Impair Defenses: Indicator Blocking) — the attacker does not even need to disable logging; the too-restrictive threshold does it for them.

Concrete scenario: an operator sets `logging trap errors` (level 3) to reduce noise. An adversary with stolen credentials logs in (severity 5/6 event, suppressed), edits the config (severity 5 %SYS-5-CONFIG_I, suppressed), and leaves. The SOC sees nothing because only errors-and-above were exported. The intrusion is discovered only by out-of-band means, if at all.

Blast radius: every device set below informational is a partial blind spot; because the volume of level-6 messages is modest on a stable network, the noise argument rarely justifies the coverage loss. Platform nuances: IOS/IOS-XE use `logging trap <level>`; NX-OS controls this via the per-server severity field in `logging server <ip> <sev>` and per-feature `logging level <feature> <sev>`, so the fix differs. The recommended target is informational (6).

Compliance: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 6.1.2 (logging trap severity), NIST 800-53 AU-2 (event selection) and AU-12 (audit generation); a CWE-778-adjacent insufficient-logging condition.
steps: Capture the current setting: `show running-config | include logging trap` (IOS/IOS-XE) or `show logging server` (NX-OS) and record the level for the change ticket.
Understand what you will gain: level 6 adds login/config-change/interface events. Confirm the SIEM can absorb the additional (modest) volume.
Baseline current message rate with `show logging` (note the 'message lines logged' rate) so you can compare after the change.
Enter config: `configure terminal`.
(IOS/IOS-XE/9800 WLC) Set the recommended level: `logging trap informational` (equivalently `logging trap 6`).

(NX-OS) Raise the per-server severity, e.g. `logging server 10.10.20.5 6 use-vrf management`; if a specific feature was lowered, restore it with `logging level <feature> 6` (feature name, e.g. `logging level aaa 6` — not a syslog facility keyword).
Do NOT set debugging (7) — that is the opposite failure (see LOG-004). Stop at informational unless actively troubleshooting.
Ensure timestamps remain enabled so the extra events are usable: `service timestamps log datetime msec show-timezone`.
If message volume is a genuine concern, keep informational to the SIEM but rate-limit console/monitor separately (`logging rate-limit`) rather than lowering the trap level.
Exit: `end`.
Validate: `show logging` should report 'Trap logging: level informational'.
Trigger a level-5/6 event to confirm forwarding, e.g. enter and exit `configure terminal` (produces %SYS-5-CONFIG_I) and confirm it reaches the SIEM.
Confirm on the SIEM side that notifications/informational events for this device now appear where previously they did not.
Watch the collector for ~15 minutes to confirm the added volume is acceptable and not saturating the mgmt link.
Save: `copy running-config startup-config` / `write memory`.
Record the standardized trap level in the fleet logging baseline so future drift is caught.
LOG-003.cli:
configure terminal
 logging trap informational
 service timestamps log datetime msec show-timezone
end
copy running-config startup-config
LOG-003.verify: `show logging` — expect 'Trap logging: level informational (6), xx message lines logged'. `show running-config | include logging trap` returns `logging trap informational`. NX-OS: `show logging server` shows severity 6 for each server.
LOG-003.rollback: Restore the prior level, e.g. `logging trap warnings` (IOS/IOS-XE) or lower the severity in the `logging server` line (NX-OS). Caveat: reverting re-suppresses notifications/informational events — logins, config changes, and interface flaps stop reaching the SIEM, recreating the detection gap.
LOG-003.impact: Non-disruptive to traffic and sessions; no reload. Only side effect is increased syslog volume (login/config/link events) to collectors and management link — normally modest, but validate collector capacity on very large or flapping networks.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 6.1.2 — Set 'logging trap' severity to at least informational
Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — 'Logging Levels'

LOG-004.risk:
Using the same severity-mapping logic as LOG-003, the scanner also tests the upper bound: if the resolved `logging trap` level is >= 7 (debugging) LOG-004 fires at LOW. This is the opposite fault to LOG-003 — the device is configured to forward EVERY message, including verbose debug output, to the syslog collector. The trigger is literally `logging trap debugging` or `logging trap 7`.

Debug-level trap is dangerous for availability and detectability, not confidentiality. Debug messages are extremely high volume and are generated in the fast path of many features; sending them all to syslog can spike control-plane CPU, saturate the management link, and, most importantly, drown genuine security events in noise. On a busy device an active `debug` combined with `logging trap debugging` has historically caused CPU exhaustion and even device instability. From a defender's view this is a self-inflicted denial of visibility: the SIEM either drops messages (UDP), backs up (TCP), or the analyst simply cannot find the signal — an inadvertent form of ATT&CK T1499-style resource exhaustion and T1562.006 indicator-blocking-by-noise.

Concrete scenario: an engineer leaves `logging trap debugging` set after troubleshooting and later runs `debug ip packet` during a P1. The device's CPU climbs, the syslog stream balloons, the collector's ingest queue backs up, and the real 'configuration changed by unknown user' event is buried under millions of packet-debug lines or dropped entirely. An attacker active at that time is effectively invisible.

Blast radius: primarily the affected device (CPU/mgmt link) and the shared collector (ingest cost, retention burn, license/EPS overage in commercial SIEMs). Platform nuances: IOS/IOS-XE `logging trap debugging`; NX-OS uses severity 7 in the `logging server` line and `logging level <feature> 7`. Debug output should be viewed via `terminal monitor` / `logging monitor` for a live session, never permanently exported at trap level. The correct steady-state is informational (6).

Compliance angle: NIST 800-53 AU-4 (audit storage capacity) and Cisco operational hardening guidance; excessive logging is CWE-779.
steps: Confirm the setting and check for active debugs that would amplify it: `show running-config | include logging trap` and `show debugging`.
If any debugs are running and not currently needed, turn them off first: `undebug all` (alias `no debug all`).
Check current control-plane health before changing, to establish a baseline: `show processes cpu sorted | exclude 0.00` and the `show logging` message rate.
Enter config: `configure terminal`.
(IOS/IOS-XE/9800 WLC) Lower to the recommended steady-state: `logging trap informational`.
(NX-OS) Set the server severity to 6, e.g. `logging server 10.10.20.5 6`; reset any feature left at 7 with `logging level <feature> 6`.

If live debug output is genuinely needed, use a session channel instead of trap: `terminal monitor` on the VTY, or `logging monitor debugging` (monitor, not trap), scoped to the troubleshooting window only.
Add a safety rate-limit so a future debug cannot flood syslog: `logging rate-limit 100 except errors` (IOS/IOS-XE).
Exit: `end`.
Validate the trap level dropped: `show logging` should read 'Trap logging: level informational'.
Watch CPU recover if it was elevated: `show processes cpu history`.
Confirm the collector ingest rate for this device returns to normal on the SIEM dashboard.
Establish an operational rule: debugs are session-scoped and must be removed after use — document it in the runbook.
Trigger a normal level-6 event and confirm it still reaches the SIEM (coverage preserved, just not debug noise).
Save: `copy running-config startup-config` / `write memory`.
Add `logging trap` level to fleet drift monitoring so a device reset to debugging is flagged automatically.
LOG-004.cli:
configure terminal
 logging trap informational
 logging rate-limit 100 except errors
end
copy running-config startup-config
LOG-004.verify: `show logging` — expect 'Trap logging: level informational (6)'. `show running-config | include logging trap` returns `logging trap informational` (not debugging/7). `show debugging` should show no active debugs. NX-OS: `show logging server` shows severity 6.
LOG-004.rollback: Re-set `logging trap debugging` only if you truly need full debug export during a controlled troubleshooting window. Caveat: reverting re-exposes the device to control-plane CPU spikes and buries/drops genuine security events under debug noise (availability and detectability loss).
LOG-004.impact: Lowering the level is non-disruptive and generally REDUCES load (less CPU, less mgmt-link and collector pressure). No session drop, no reload. If a live debug troubleshooting session depended on trap-level export, it must instead use terminal monitor / logging monitor.
references: Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — 'Logging Levels' and rate-limiting
Cisco IOS Troubleshooting — 'Using Debug Commands' (CPU impact)

LOG-005.risk:
The scanner checks every `logging ...` line for one matching `logging buffered`. If none is present, LOG-005 fires at MEDIUM: local buffered logging is not enabled. On IOS/IOS-XE this means the device is not retaining recent events in a dedicated RAM ring buffer (viewable with `show logging`); it is relying solely on whatever remote/console/monitor destinations exist.

Buffered logging is the on-box safety net. Even when remote syslog is configured, the buffer gives an operator immediate, first-response access to recent events directly on the device — critical during a syslog-collector outage, a management-network partition, or the exact moment of an incident when you are consoled in and the SIEM is unreachable. Without it, `show logging` returns little or nothing and the responder is blind on the very box they are logged into. In combination with a missing remote collector (LOG-001) it means the device retains essentially no event history at all.

Concrete scenario: the management path to the SIEM is down (or was deliberately cut by an attacker, ATT&CK T1562.001 / T1490-style interference). An engineer connects via console to investigate anomalous behaviour and runs `show logging` — with no buffer there is nothing to see: no login records, no interface transitions, no config-change notices. The local evidence needed to triage the event simply does not exist.

Blast radius: per-device loss of recent-event visibility and a weaker forensic position fleet-wide. The buffer is cheap (RAM only) and non-disruptive, so there is little reason to omit it; recommended is a reasonably large buffer at informational, e.g. `logging buffered 64000 informational`. Platform nuances: IOS/IOS-XE/9800 WLC use `logging buffered <bytes> <level>`; NX-OS uses `logging logfile <name> <severity> [size <bytes>]` which writes to a persistent logfile in bootflash — so the NX-OS remediation command is different, and this check firing on NX-OS should be satisfied by the logfile mechanism.

Compliance: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 6.1.3 (buffered logging), NIST 800-53 AU-4 (storage) and AU-9 (protect audit records via a second copy); CWE-778.
steps: Check the current state: `show running-config | include logging buffered` (IOS/IOS-XE) or `show logging logfile` (NX-OS) and note any existing value.
Confirm available memory so the buffer size is reasonable: `show memory statistics` (a 64 KB buffer is negligible on modern platforms).
Decide the size/level: informational (6) is recommended; size 32000-256000 bytes depending on event rate and RAM.
Enter config: `configure terminal`.
(IOS/IOS-XE/9800 WLC) Enable it: `logging buffered 64000 informational`.
(NX-OS) Use the persistent logfile instead: `logging logfile messages 6 size 4194304`.
Ensure timestamps are on so buffered entries are usable: `service timestamps log datetime msec show-timezone` (IOS/IOS-XE).
If the box previously had a tiny default buffer, increasing it is safe; changing the size re-initializes the buffer (clears current contents) — note this in the ticket.
Exit: `end`.
Validate the buffer is active: `show logging` header should read 'Buffer logging: level informational, xx messages logged'.
Generate a test event (e.g. enter/exit config mode) and confirm it appears at the bottom of `show logging`.
On NX-OS confirm with `show logging logfile` that entries are being written.
Cross-check that remote syslog (LOG-001) is also configured — the buffer complements, not replaces, remote export.
Confirm the buffer size does not pressure memory on smaller platforms: re-run `show processes memory sorted` / `show memory statistics`.
Save: `copy running-config startup-config` / `write memory`.
Record the standard buffer size/level in the fleet logging baseline for consistency.
LOG-005.cli:
configure terminal
 logging buffered 64000 informational
 service timestamps log datetime msec show-timezone
end
copy running-config startup-config
LOG-005.verify: `show logging` — header shows 'Buffer logging: level informational, NN messages logged' and the tail lists recent timestamped events. `show running-config | include logging buffered` returns `logging buffered 64000 informational`. NX-OS: `show logging logfile` displays stored messages.
LOG-005.rollback: Disable with `no logging buffered` (IOS/IOS-XE) or `no logging logfile` (NX-OS). Caveat: reverting removes the on-box event history, so a responder consoled into the device during a SIEM/management outage has no local record to triage from.
LOG-005.impact: Non-disruptive: RAM-only, no traffic effect, no session drop, no reload. Setting or resizing the buffer clears its current contents but does not affect the device or remote logs. Negligible memory footprint on modern hardware.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 6.1.3 — Set 'logging buffered' size and severity
Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — 'Logging Best Practices'

LOG-006.risk:
The scanner collects any line containing `logging console` and flags LOG-006 (LOW) if such a line contains the token 'debugging' or the digit '7' — i.e. console logging is set to debug severity (`logging console debugging` or `logging console 7`). Console logging sends messages to the physical/virtual console line.

The concern here is device stability and operator usability rather than confidentiality. Console output is processed at the priority of the console process and, on many platforms, can be effectively synchronous — a flood of debug-level messages to a slow console (9600 baud serial in particular) can consume CPU, block the CLI, and in severe cases contribute to responsiveness problems or watchdog events. At debug level this is exactly what happens during troubleshooting or under a message storm. Operationally, a console buried in debug spam is unusable precisely when an engineer needs it during an incident, and it can mask important higher-severity messages scrolling past.

Concrete scenario: an operator is consoled in to a router during a P1. Console logging is at debugging and someone (or an automated process) triggers verbose events; the console scrolls uncontrollably, the engineer cannot type commands cleanly, and critical alerts are lost in the stream. If an attacker can induce high event volume, a debug-level console amplifies the disruption (availability/usability degradation).

Blast radius: limited to the local device's console usability and CPU under load — hence LOW severity. Best practice is to keep console logging at a low severity such as critical (or disable it and rely on buffered/remote), and use `logging synchronous` on the console line so command echo is not interleaved with messages. Platform nuances: IOS/IOS-XE/9800 WLC use `logging console <level>`; NX-OS uses `logging console <severity>` (0-7) as well, so the fix is similar, though NX-OS defaults and behaviour differ slightly.

Compliance/hardening: Cisco IOS hardening guidance recommends restricting console logging severity; relates to NIST 800-53 AU-4 (avoid overwhelming the log path) and general availability hardening; CWE-779.
steps: Check the current setting: `show running-config | include logging console` and note the level.
Decide the target: `critical` (level 2) is a common hardened choice; some sites disable console logging entirely and rely on buffered + remote.
Enter config: `configure terminal`.
(IOS/IOS-XE/9800 WLC) Lower the level: `logging console critical` — or to disable: `no logging console`.
(NX-OS) Equivalent: `logging console 2` (critical) or `no logging console`.
Prevent message/command interleaving on the console line: `line console 0` then `logging synchronous`, then `exit`.
Make sure you are not losing needed events — confirm buffered (LOG-005) and remote (LOG-001) logging are configured to still capture informational events.
If you were actively watching debug on the console, switch to a session-scoped channel: `terminal monitor` with `logging monitor debugging` on the VTY instead.
Exit: `end`.
Validate: `show logging` header should read 'Console logging: level critical' (or 'disabled').
Confirm `show running-config | section line con` shows `logging synchronous` under the console line.
Test by generating a low-severity event and confirming it does NOT spam the console but DOES appear in the `show logging` buffer.
Confirm the console remains responsive under a burst (optional, in a lab or low-risk window).
Ensure remote/buffered capture of the events you removed from console is intact on the SIEM.
Save: `copy running-config startup-config` / `write memory`.
Standardize the console logging level across the fleet in the baseline.
LOG-006.cli:
configure terminal
 logging console critical
 line console 0
  logging synchronous
  exit
end
copy running-config startup-config
LOG-006.verify: `show logging` — header shows 'Console logging: level critical' (or 'disabled'). `show running-config | include logging console` returns `logging console critical`. `show running-config | section line con` shows `logging synchronous`. NX-OS: `show logging console` reports the severity.
LOG-006.rollback: Restore with `logging console debugging` (IOS/IOS-XE) or `logging console 7` (NX-OS). Caveat: reverting re-exposes the console to debug-level message floods that can render the CLI unusable and add CPU load under high event volume.
LOG-006.impact: Non-disruptive to traffic, sessions, or the data plane; no reload. It reduces console noise and CPU pressure. Only behavioural change: low-severity messages no longer print to the console (they remain in buffer/remote logs).
references: Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — 'Console and Monitor Logging'
Cisco IOS Configuration Fundamentals — 'logging console' / 'logging synchronous'

LOG-007.risk:
This check fires only when a syslog destination is present: it verifies there is no `logging source-interface` line while `find_lines(r"logging\s+(host\s+)?\d")` matches somewhere in the config. If a collector is configured but no source interface is pinned, LOG-007 is raised at LOW. By default the device sources syslog packets from the egress interface nearest the destination, so the source IP can vary as routing changes or as the device uses different uplinks.

The problem is log-correlation integrity and management-plane hygiene. When syslog messages arrive at the collector with different source IPs over time (or an unexpected interface IP), the SIEM may treat one device as several log sources, break per-device dashboards, and complicate incident timelines. It also makes collector-side access controls harder: if the source IP is unpredictable you cannot tightly ACL the syslog listener to known device management addresses, weakening protection against spoofed/injected log traffic. Pinning to a stable loopback gives every message a single, routable, always-up source identity.

Concrete scenario: a core switch with two uplinks fails over; syslog now egresses a different interface with a different IP. The SIEM sees a 'new' log source, correlation rules keyed on the old IP go quiet, and an analyst investigating an event cannot cleanly tie pre- and post-failover logs to the same device. If the collector's inbound ACL only permitted the old source IP, logs may even be dropped after failover.

Blast radius: mainly operational/forensic clarity, hence LOW; but it becomes more important where syslog ACLs, source-based filtering, or TLS certificate CN/SAN matching depend on a fixed source. Best practice is a dedicated Loopback used consistently for all management-plane services (syslog, SNMP, NTP, AAA) so they share one identity. Platform nuances: IOS/IOS-XE/9800 WLC use `logging source-interface <intf>`; NX-OS uses `logging source-interface <intf>` as well (commonly a loopback with the server's `use-vrf`).

Compliance: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 6.1.4 (logging source-interface), NIST 800-53 AU-3 (content of audit records) and AU-6 (correlation); CWE-778.
steps: Confirm the gap: `show running-config | include logging source-interface` returns nothing, while `show running-config | include logging host|logging server` shows a collector.
Choose/verify a stable management source, ideally an always-up loopback: `show ip interface brief | include Loopback`. If none exists, create one.
(If needed) Create the loopback: `configure terminal` then `interface Loopback0` / `ip address 10.255.0.1 255.255.255.255` / `exit`, and ensure it is advertised/reachable to the collector.
Confirm the collector permits/expects the loopback IP (update any syslog inbound ACL or SIEM source list accordingly).
Enter config: `configure terminal`.
(IOS/IOS-XE/9800 WLC) Pin the source: `logging source-interface Loopback0`.
(NX-OS) Use `logging source-interface loopback0`; if syslog rides a VRF ensure the `logging server` line has matching `use-vrf`.

For consistency, pin the other management services to the same loopback: `snmp-server source-interface traps Loopback0`, `ntp source Loopback0`, and `ip tacacs source-interface Loopback0` / `ip radius source-interface Loopback0` as applicable.
Exit: `end`.
Validate the source is set: `show running-config | include source-interface`.
Generate a test event and confirm on the collector that messages now arrive from the loopback IP.
Confirm the SIEM sees a single, stable source for the device (no duplicate/host-split).
If a syslog inbound ACL is in place, verify it now matches the loopback and is not dropping the traffic.
Test an uplink failover (in a controlled window, if feasible) to confirm the source IP no longer changes.
Save: `copy running-config startup-config` / `write memory`.
Document the management loopback as the standard source for all logging/SNMP/NTP/AAA in the baseline.
LOG-007.cli:
configure terminal
 interface Loopback0
  ip address 10.255.0.1 255.255.255.255
  exit
 logging source-interface Loopback0
 snmp-server source-interface traps Loopback0
 ntp source Loopback0
end
copy running-config startup-config
LOG-007.verify: `show running-config | include logging source-interface` returns `logging source-interface Loopback0`. On the collector/SIEM, incoming syslog for this device shows the loopback IP as source. `show logging` continues to show an increasing counter after the change.
LOG-007.rollback: Remove with `no logging source-interface Loopback0`. Caveat: reverting lets the syslog source IP vary with egress/routing again, which can split the device into multiple SIEM sources, break correlation, and defeat source-based collector ACLs.
LOG-007.impact: Non-disruptive to data traffic; no reload. There can be a brief change in the source IP the collector sees at cutover — coordinate collector ACL/SIEM source updates so logs are not momentarily dropped. Admin/VPN sessions are unaffected.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 6.1.4 — Set 'logging source-interface'
Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — 'Management Plane / Loopback Sourcing'

LOG-008.risk:
The scanner tests for a top-level `archive` section using `has_line(r"^archive")`. If no such block exists, LOG-008 fires at MEDIUM: the IOS/IOS-XE Configuration Archive and change-logging feature is not configured. That feature (the `archive` block with `log config` / `logging enable` / `notify syslog`) records who changed what in the running-config and can retain periodic archives for rollback via `configure replace`.

Without it, the device keeps no per-command change log and no local rollback points. There is no on-box answer to 'which user entered which command, and when' — the accountability that ties a configuration change to an operator (or an intruder). This is a serious gap for insider-threat and post-compromise investigation: an attacker who reaches enable can modify ACLs, add users, or weaken crypto, and absent config-change logging there is no record of the individual commands, only (at best) a coarse %SYS-5-CONFIG_I 'configured from' notice. This corresponds to ATT&CK T1562-style tampering where change accountability is exactly what defenders lose.

Concrete scenario: a config drift or outage is traced to a bad change, or a breach investigation needs to know when a rogue `username backdoor privilege 15` was added. With the archive + `log config` / `notify syslog`, each command is logged with the username and can be shipped to the SIEM in real time, and a known-good archive can be restored with `configure replace flash:archive`. Without it, the team is reduced to guesswork and manual diffs against stale backups.

Blast radius: weakens change control and forensic reconstruction on every device lacking it. Platform nuances are important here: the `archive` / `log config` construct is an IOS / IOS-XE (and Catalyst 9800 WLC) feature. NX-OS does NOT use `archive`; it provides equivalent capability through `checkpoint`/`rollback`, `show running-config diff`, and AAA command accounting (`aaa accounting`), so on NX-OS this finding should be satisfied by those mechanisms rather than by an `archive` block. ASA/FTD are out of scope for this module.

Compliance: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 6.2.1 (config change logging / archive), NIST 800-53 CM-3 (configuration change control), CM-6, AU-2; CWE-778.
steps: Confirm absence: `show running-config | section ^archive` returns nothing (IOS/IOS-XE). On NX-OS check `show checkpoint summary` and `show running-config | include aaa accounting` instead.
Decide an archive destination with adequate space: `show flash:` or `show bootflash:`; a local path or a reachable FTP/TFTP/SCP URL both work.
Enter config: `configure terminal`.
(IOS/IOS-XE/9800 WLC) Create the archive block: `archive`.
Set where archives are written and a versioning cap: `path flash:/archive/$h-config` and `maximum 14`.
Optionally auto-archive on save/interval: `write-memory` (archive on each `copy run start`) and/or `time-period 1440` (daily, in minutes).
Enable the command change-log: still under `archive`, `log config`, then `logging enable`.
Hide secrets and export change events: `hidekeys` and (recommended) `notify syslog` so change events reach the SIEM in real time.
Exit the sub-blocks: `end`.

(NX-OS equivalent, since `archive` is unsupported) Enable command accounting and checkpoints: `configure terminal` / `aaa accounting default group <tacacs-group>` and use `checkpoint <name>` for rollback; verify with `show accounting log`.
Validate the block exists: `show running-config | section archive` shows `log config` / `logging enable` / `notify syslog`.
Confirm the change log is capturing: enter config mode, make a trivial change, then `show archive log config all` — the command and username should be listed.
Confirm change events reach the SIEM (search for %PARSER-5-CFGLOG_LOGGEDCMD).
Verify at least one archive is written after a save: `copy running-config startup-config` then `show archive` lists a versioned file.
Test rollback in a lab/controlled window: `configure replace flash:/archive/<file>` and confirm it restores cleanly.
Persist and document: ensure `copy running-config startup-config` is done and record the archive path/retention in the baseline.
LOG-008.cli:
configure terminal
 archive
  path flash:/archive/$h-config
  maximum 14
  write-memory
  time-period 1440
  log config
   logging enable
   hidekeys
   notify syslog
end
copy running-config startup-config
LOG-008.verify: `show running-config | section archive` shows the `archive` block with `log config`, `logging enable`, and `notify syslog`. `show archive log config all` lists recent commands with the associated username. `show archive` lists versioned archive files after a save. NX-OS: `show accounting log` and `show checkpoint summary` demonstrate the equivalent capability.
LOG-008.rollback: Remove with `no archive` (IOS/IOS-XE). Caveat: reverting eliminates per-command change accountability and local rollback points, so unauthorized or erroneous config changes can no longer be attributed to a user or easily reverted.
LOG-008.impact: Non-disruptive to traffic and sessions; no reload. Archives consume some flash/bootflash space (bounded by `maximum`) — ensure free space. `notify syslog` adds low-volume change events to the log stream. `configure replace` (rollback) IS disruptive and should only be run in a maintenance window.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 6.2.1 — Configuration change logging (archive / log config)
Cisco IOS Configuration Fundamentals — 'Configuration Change Notification and Logging' / 'Configuration Replace and Rollback'
NIST SP 800-53 Rev.5 CM-3, CM-6, AU-2

LOG-009.risk:
This check runs only if the device already has SNMP configured (get_snmp_config() returns any `snmp-server` line). Given that SNMP is in use, the scanner looks for `snmp-server enable traps`; if that string is absent, LOG-009 fires at LOW. The meaning is that SNMP is set up (likely for polling/read access) but the device will not push asynchronous trap/inform notifications to the NMS — the monitoring system only sees what it polls, and only when it polls.

The security-relevant impact is delayed or missed detection of state changes. Traps deliver near-real-time notifications for events like interface up/down, authentication failures (including SNMP auth failures), config changes, environmental alarms, and BGP/OSPF neighbor changes. Relying on polling alone introduces a detection latency equal to the poll interval and can entirely miss transient events that occur between polls. An adversary can exploit that window: e.g. briefly bring up an interface to exfiltrate data and drop it again between five-minute polls, or trigger repeated SNMP auth failures (reconnaissance / attempted community brute force, ATT&CK T1078 / T1046) that a trap would surface immediately but polling would never catch.

Concrete scenario: someone connects to an unused port, the link comes up and down within the poll gap, and a small data transfer occurs. With traps enabled the NMS/SIEM receives an immediate linkUp/linkDown and the SOC investigates; with traps disabled the event is invisible until (or unless) a later poll happens to catch a counter delta.

Blast radius: reduced real-time visibility across whatever this device monitors — hence LOW, and it is a visibility gap rather than a direct compromise vector. Important caveat: traps should be paired with SNMPv3 (auth+priv); enabling traps over SNMPv1/v2c community strings sends notifications in cleartext and should be avoided. Platform nuances: IOS/IOS-XE/9800 WLC and NX-OS all use `snmp-server enable traps ...` and `snmp-server host <ip> ...`; NX-OS additionally requires `feature snmp` and gates notifications behind host config.

Compliance: NIST 800-53 SI-4 (system monitoring), AU-6; CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 6.3.1 (SNMP traps). Because the finding presupposes SNMP is already present, also review that SNMP itself is v3-hardened.
steps: Confirm the state: `show running-config | include snmp-server` shows SNMP configured but no `enable traps`, and no `snmp-server host`.
Verify SNMP is hardened first — prefer SNMPv3 auth+priv: `show snmp user` and `show snmp group`. If only v1/v2c communities exist, plan to migrate before exposing traps.
Identify the NMS/trap receiver IP and the SNMP credential (v3 user or, if unavoidable, community) it expects.
Enter config: `configure terminal`.
Define the trap receiver with SNMPv3 (recommended): `snmp-server host 10.10.30.9 version 3 priv NMSUSER`.
Enable the trap categories you need (enabling all is noisy — scope it): `snmp-server enable traps snmp linkdown linkup authentication coldstart warmstart`.
Add security-relevant categories as applicable: `snmp-server enable traps config`, `snmp-server enable traps syslog`, and routing/bfd/environment traps for the platform role.
Pin the source for consistent identity (ties into LOG-007): `snmp-server source-interface traps Loopback0`.
(NX-OS) Ensure `feature snmp` is on, then use the same `snmp-server enable traps ...` and `snmp-server host <ip> ...` syntax; confirm with `show snmp host`.
Avoid v2c/v1 traps if possible; if a community must be used, restrict it and ACL the NMS. Never reuse a read-write community for traps.
Exit: `end`.
Validate enablement: `show running-config | include enable traps` lists the categories, and `show snmp host` shows the receiver.
Generate a test trap (e.g. `shutdown`/`no shutdown` an unused loopback) and confirm the NMS receives linkDown/linkUp.
Confirm the NMS/SIEM parses and alerts on the trap and that auth-failure traps flow to security monitoring.
Save: `copy running-config startup-config` / `write memory`.
Document the enabled trap set, receiver, and SNMPv3 user in the monitoring baseline; schedule a follow-up to retire any v2c usage.
LOG-009.cli:
configure terminal
 snmp-server source-interface traps Loopback0
 snmp-server host 10.10.30.9 version 3 priv NMSUSER
 snmp-server enable traps snmp linkdown linkup authentication coldstart warmstart
 snmp-server enable traps config
 snmp-server enable traps syslog
end
copy running-config startup-config
LOG-009.verify: `show running-config | include enable traps` lists the enabled categories; `show snmp host` (IOS/IOS-XE/NX-OS) shows the configured trap receiver, version, and user. Trigger an interface flap and confirm the NMS logs the linkDown/linkUp trap and, ideally, an authentication-failure trap on a bad SNMP attempt.
LOG-009.rollback: Disable with `no snmp-server enable traps` (and `no snmp-server host 10.10.30.9 ...`). Caveat: reverting returns monitoring to poll-only, reintroducing detection latency and the risk of missing transient events (brief link/auth changes) that occur between polls.
LOG-009.impact: Non-disruptive to data traffic and sessions; no reload. Adds low-volume SNMP notification traffic to the NMS and mgmt link. Enabling overly broad trap categories can be noisy — scope them. Ensure traps ride SNMPv3 so notifications are not sent in cleartext.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 6.3.1 — Enable SNMP traps (with SNMPv3)
Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — 'SNMP' and 'Notification'

LOG-010.risk:
The scanner declares NetFlow/IPFIX present if any of three patterns appears anywhere in the config: `flow\s+exporter`, `ip flow`, or `flow record`. If none is found, LOG-010 fires at LOW: the device exports no traffic-flow telemetry to a collector. This covers both classic/legacy NetFlow (`ip flow ingress`, `ip flow-export ...`) and modern Flexible NetFlow / IPFIX (`flow record`, `flow exporter`, `flow monitor`).

Flow telemetry is a foundational network-security data source. Unlike syslog (which records device events), NetFlow records who-talked-to-whom: source/destination IP, ports, protocol, byte/packet counts, and timing for every conversation traversing the device. Its absence means the SOC has no visibility into lateral movement, data exfiltration volumes, beaconing/C2 patterns, or east-west traffic between segments. Many detections rely on it: ATT&CK T1041 (Exfiltration Over C2 Channel) and T1071 (Application Layer Protocol / beaconing) are commonly surfaced by flow analytics (long-lived low-and-slow flows, periodic small connections to an external host), and T1046 (Network Service Discovery / scanning) shows up as fan-out flow patterns. Without flow data these behaviours are effectively invisible at the network layer.

Concrete scenario: malware on an internal host beacons to a C2 server every 60 seconds and slowly exfiltrates data over HTTPS. Packet payloads are encrypted, so DPI helps little; but NetFlow reveals the tell-tale periodic, similarly-sized flows to a single external IP and the cumulative outbound byte count. With flow export the analytics platform (Secure Network Analytics/Stealthwatch, or any IPFIX collector) alerts; without it, nobody sees the pattern.

Blast radius: a network-wide detection blind spot for traffic-based threats and also a loss of capacity/troubleshooting insight — hence LOW as a compliance item but high analytical value. Platform nuances matter for the fix: IOS-XE and Catalyst 9800 WLC use Flexible NetFlow (`flow record` -> `flow exporter` -> `flow monitor` applied to an interface with `ip flow monitor <name> input`); NX-OS uses `feature netflow` then `flow record`/`flow exporter`/`flow monitor`; older IOS may use legacy `ip flow-export version 9` plus `ip flow ingress` on interfaces.

Compliance: NIST 800-53 SI-4 (system/traffic monitoring) and AU-6; Cisco traffic-visibility hardening guidance; RFC 7011 (IPFIX); CWE-778.
steps: Confirm the gap and platform capability: `show running-config | include flow` (empty) and `show flow monitor` / `show flow exporter` (none). Verify the box supports Flexible NetFlow for its image/hardware.
Identify the flow collector IP/port (commonly UDP/2055, or /4739 for IPFIX) and the management source interface (reuse the loopback from LOG-007).
Plan interface scope: which interfaces to monitor (typically WAN/uplinks and inter-VLAN boundaries) to balance visibility vs. resource use.
Enter config: `configure terminal`.
(NX-OS only) Enable the feature first: `feature netflow`.

Define what to collect — a flow record (5-tuple + counters): `flow record FR-SECURITY` then `match ipv4 source address` / `match ipv4 destination address` / `match ipv4 protocol` / `match transport source-port` / `match transport destination-port` / `collect counter bytes` / `collect counter packets` / `collect timestamp sys-uptime first` / `collect timestamp sys-uptime last`, then `exit`.
Define where to send it — the exporter: `flow exporter FE-COLLECTOR` / `destination 10.10.40.20` / `source Loopback0` / `transport udp 2055` / `export-protocol ipfix` (or `netflow-v9`), then `exit`.
Bind them into a monitor: `flow monitor FM-SECURITY` / `record FR-SECURITY` / `exporter FE-COLLECTOR` / `cache timeout active 60`, then `exit`.

Apply the monitor to the chosen interface(s) in the ingress direction: `interface GigabitEthernet0/0/1` / `ip flow monitor FM-SECURITY input`, and add `ip flow monitor FM-SECURITY output` if egress flows are also wanted.

(Legacy IOS alternative, if Flexible NetFlow is unsupported) Use `ip flow-export version 9`, `ip flow-export destination 10.10.40.20 2055`, `ip flow-export source Loopback0`, and per interface `ip flow ingress`.
Consider resource impact on high-throughput platforms — check `show processes cpu` before/after and prefer hardware/sampled flow where required.
Exit: `end`.
Validate the pipeline: `show flow monitor FM-SECURITY statistics` (non-zero flows) and `show flow exporter FE-COLLECTOR statistics` (packets sent incrementing).
Confirm the collector is receiving and decoding flows from this device's source IP.
Save: `copy running-config startup-config` / `write memory`.
Document the record/exporter/monitor names, collector, and monitored interfaces in the baseline; roll out consistently across the fleet.
LOG-010.cli:
configure terminal
 flow record FR-SECURITY
  match ipv4 source address
  match ipv4 destination address
  match ipv4 protocol
  match transport source-port
  match transport destination-port
  collect counter bytes
  collect counter packets
  collect timestamp sys-uptime first
  collect timestamp sys-uptime last
  exit
 flow exporter FE-COLLECTOR
  destination 10.10.40.20
  source Loopback0
  transport udp 2055
  export-protocol ipfix
  exit
 flow monitor FM-SECURITY
  record FR-SECURITY
  exporter FE-COLLECTOR
  cache timeout active 60
  exit
 interface GigabitEthernet0/0/1
  ip flow monitor FM-SECURITY input
  exit
end
copy running-config startup-config
LOG-010.verify: `show flow monitor FM-SECURITY statistics` shows a growing flow count; `show flow exporter FE-COLLECTOR statistics` shows 'packets added' / 'bytes exported' incrementing. `show flow interface GigabitEthernet0/0/1` confirms the monitor is attached input. The collector shows flows arriving from the device's source (loopback) IP. Legacy IOS: `show ip flow export` and `show ip cache flow`.
LOG-010.rollback: Remove the interface binding first (`interface GigabitEthernet0/0/1` / `no ip flow monitor FM-SECURITY input`), then `no flow monitor FM-SECURITY`, `no flow exporter FE-COLLECTOR`, `no flow record FR-SECURITY` (NX-OS also `no feature netflow`). Caveat: reverting removes traffic-level visibility, re-blinding the SOC to exfiltration, C2/beaconing, and lateral-movement patterns that only flow data reveals.
LOG-010.impact: Applying flow monitors is generally non-disruptive to traffic and requires no reload, but flow accounting consumes CPU/memory and TCAM proportional to flow volume — on high-throughput platforms enable in a change window, monitor CPU, and use sampling if needed. Export adds UDP telemetry to the collector and mgmt link. No admin/VPN session impact.
references: Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — 'Traffic Identification and Traceback (NetFlow)'
Cisco IOS-XE Flexible NetFlow Configuration Guide; Cisco NX-OS NetFlow Configuration Guide

MGMT-001.risk:
The scanner searched the running configuration for a line beginning with `enable secret` (regex `^enable secret`) and found none, so it raised MGMT-001 at CRITICAL severity. This check only executes on the platforms in the module's SUPPORTED_PLATFORMS set — IOS, IOS-XE, and the Catalyst 9800 WLC (which is IOS-XE based). NX-OS, ASA, and FTD are skipped because they use role-based AAA or a native `enable password` model, so absence of the finding on those platforms is expected, not a pass.

The `enable secret` command protects entry into privileged EXEC mode (level 15), the mode from which an operator can view and rewrite the entire device configuration. When it is absent, one of two dangerous states exists: either privileged EXEC is reachable with no password at all from a console/aux session, or it is gated only by the far weaker `enable password`, which is stored as clear text or trivially reversible Type 7.

The concrete attack path is credential/privilege escalation (ATT&CK T1552.001 Unsecured Credentials and T1078 Valid Accounts). An adversary who reaches user EXEC via the console, a captured VTY session, or a shared low-privilege account simply types `enable`; with no strong secret set they gain full level-15 control. From there they can dump `running-config`, harvest SNMP/TACACS/VPN keys, add covert accounts, alter ACLs and routing, or disable logging.

Blast radius is total device compromise and lateral pivot: a core router or 9800 WLC controller taken this way exposes every downstream segment, AP, and management credential it stores.

Platform nuance: classic IOS stores `enable secret` as Type 5 (salted MD5) by default; IOS-XE 16.x/17.x and the 9800 WLC also support the stronger `enable algorithm-type sha256|scrypt` producing Type 8/9 hashes, which should be preferred. This maps to the CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark password rules, NIST 800-53 IA-2/IA-5, and CWE-306 (Missing Authentication for a Critical Function).
steps: Pre-check current state with `show running-config | include ^enable` to confirm whether any `enable secret` or `enable password` line exists.
Confirm you have a working level-15 login path that does NOT depend on the enable prompt (a local user or console access) before changing enable credentials, so you cannot lock yourself out.
Identify the platform/version with `show version` to decide whether Type 8/9 (`algorithm-type`) hashing is available (IOS-XE 16.x+ / 9800 WLC).
Choose a strong, unique passphrase that meets your password policy (length, complexity) and is not reused elsewhere.
Enter global configuration with `configure terminal`.
On IOS-XE / 9800, prefer a modern hash: `enable algorithm-type scrypt secret <passphrase>` (produces Type 9).
On classic IOS lacking scrypt support, use `enable secret <passphrase>` (produces Type 5).
If a legacy `enable password` line exists, remove it in the same session with `no enable password` so only the secret governs privileged EXEC (this is also MGMT-002).
Do NOT paste a pre-computed hash unless migrating; typing a cleartext passphrase lets IOS hash it correctly.
Verify the new line is present and hashed with `do show running-config | include enable secret`.
Open a SECOND, independent session to the device and test `enable` with the new passphrase before ending your original session.
Exit config mode with `end`.
Save the change with `copy running-config startup-config` (or `write memory`).
On a redundant/HA pair (dual-RP router or 9800 SSO controller) confirm the secret synchronizes to the standby with `show redundancy` and re-save on the active.
Update your password vault / secrets manager and any automation (Ansible, Catalyst Center/DNAC templates) with the new credential.
Record the change in change management and schedule a brief window only if you must also remove `enable password` on a shared device.
Post-change, re-run the NSS scanner to confirm MGMT-001 no longer fires.
Rotate the passphrase on your normal credential-rotation cadence going forward.
MGMT-001.cli:
configure terminal
! IOS-XE 16.x+/Catalyst 9800 (strongest, Type 9):
enable algorithm-type scrypt secret <passphrase>
! Classic IOS fallback (Type 5):
! enable secret <passphrase>
no enable password
end
copy running-config startup-config
MGMT-001.verify: `show running-config | include enable secret` should return a line such as `enable secret 9 $9$...` (Type 9) or `enable secret 5 $1$...` (Type 5). From a fresh session, user EXEC `enable` must prompt for and accept only the new passphrase, and `show running-config | include enable password` must return nothing.
MGMT-001.rollback: Reset with `configure terminal` then `enable secret <old-passphrase>`, or `no enable secret` to remove it. Caveat: `no enable secret` re-exposes privileged EXEC to either the weak `enable password` or no password at all — do not leave the device in that state.
MGMT-001.impact: Non-disruptive. Adding or changing `enable secret` does not drop existing SSH/VTY, VPN, or data-plane traffic and requires no reload; it only changes what credential is required the next time someone enters privileged EXEC. The only risk is administrative lockout if you set a secret you do not record, so validate from a second session first.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark — Password Rules (enable secret)
Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — Enable Secret / Password Management
CWE-306: Missing Authentication for Critical Function

MGMT-002.risk:
MGMT-002 fires when the scanner finds a configuration line beginning with `enable password` (regex `^enable password`); the emitted title is "Weak 'enable password' command in use" and severity is HIGH. It is evaluated inside the same method (check_enable_secret) as MGMT-001 and runs only on IOS, IOS-XE, and the Catalyst 9800 WLC. The affected line(s) are attached to the finding so the operator can see exactly which entry to remove.

The `enable password` command is the legacy way to protect privileged EXEC and is fundamentally weak. Without `service password-encryption` it is stored in the config in clear text (Type 0); with that service enabled, the password is stored as Vigenere-based Type 7 encoding, which is not encryption at all — it is reversible in milliseconds by any of dozens of public tools and by Cisco's own decoders. Either way the level-15 credential is effectively exposed to anyone who can read the configuration.

The realistic attack is credential recovery from configuration exposure (ATT&CK T1552.001). Configs routinely leak through TFTP/FTP backups, RANCID/Oxidized repos, screen shares, ticket attachments, or a captured lower-privilege show session. A recovered `enable password` yields immediate full administrative control, and because operators frequently reuse it, the same string often unlocks other devices — a classic lateral-movement multiplier.

Blast radius equals full compromise of the device and any peer that shares the password, including routing tables, crypto keys, and downstream WLC-managed APs.

Platform nuance: on IOS/IOS-XE the correct control is to remove `enable password` entirely and rely on `enable secret` (Type 5/8/9), which is a one-way hash. If both `enable secret` and `enable password` are present, IOS uses the secret and the password line is dead weight that only leaks a credential — so it must be deleted. Note ASA uses `enable password` natively with a stronger PBKDF2/scrypt hash, but ASA is out of scope for this module. This maps to CIS password rules, NIST IA-5, CWE-261 (Weak Encoding for Password) and CWE-259.
steps: Run `show running-config | include ^enable` to see whether both `enable secret` and `enable password` exist, or only the password.
If NO `enable secret` exists yet, configure it FIRST (see MGMT-001) so privileged EXEC is never left unprotected when you delete the password line.
Confirm you have an independent level-15 access path (console or a local admin user) before making the change.
Note the current `enable password` value only long enough to detect password reuse elsewhere, then plan to rotate those too.
Enter configuration mode with `configure terminal`.
Remove the weak line with `no enable password`.
Ensure a strong secret remains: `do show running-config | include enable secret` should show a Type 5/8/9 hash.
If the secret is still Type 5 and the platform supports it, upgrade with `enable algorithm-type scrypt secret <passphrase>` (Type 9).
Exit with `end`.
From a second, independent session, run `enable` and confirm the secret works and the old password no longer does.
Save with `copy running-config startup-config` (or `write memory`).
On HA pairs, confirm the standby RP/controller reflects the change via `show redundancy` and re-save on the active.
Search your other devices for the same reused enable string and remediate them too.
Update secrets vault and automation templates to stop provisioning `enable password`.
Re-run the NSS scanner to confirm MGMT-002 (and MGMT-001 if applicable) clear.
Add a config-compliance guard (Catalyst Center template / CI lint) that rejects `enable password` in future pushes.
MGMT-002.cli:
configure terminal
! Ensure a strong secret exists first:
enable algorithm-type scrypt secret <passphrase>
! Then remove the weak reversible password:
no enable password
end
copy running-config startup-config
MGMT-002.verify: `show running-config | include ^enable` should show only `enable secret 9 $9$...` (or Type 5) and NO `enable password` line. A fresh `enable` attempt must succeed with the secret and fail with the old password.
MGMT-002.rollback: Re-add with `configure terminal` then `enable password <value>`. Caveat: doing so restores a clear-text/Type-7 privileged-EXEC credential that is trivially recoverable from any config copy — only do this if a strong `enable secret` is simultaneously present, and prefer never reverting.
MGMT-002.impact: Non-disruptive. Removing `enable password` while a valid `enable secret` is present has zero effect on active sessions or traffic and needs no reload. The only failure mode is lockout if no secret exists, which the ordered steps prevent.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark — Password Rules
Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — Use enable secret, not enable password

MGMT-003.risk:
MGMT-003 fires at HIGH severity when the running configuration does not contain a line matching `^service password-encryption`. The check runs on IOS, IOS-XE, and the Catalyst 9800 WLC. Its purpose is narrow: `service password-encryption` is the global switch that tells IOS to obfuscate otherwise clear-text passwords when they are written into and displayed from the configuration.

When the service is disabled, any password stored as a plain string — line passwords (`password <x>` under console/vty/aux), `username ... password <x>`, some routing/authentication keys, and legacy `enable password` — appears verbatim in `show running-config`, in NVRAM, and in every archived copy of the config. That is Plaintext Storage of a Password (CWE-256).

The concrete risk is credential exposure via configuration disclosure (ATT&CK T1552.001). Config files are constantly copied to TFTP servers, version-control backup systems (RANCID/Oxidized), monitoring tools, and support tickets; a screenshot during a WebEx or an over-the-shoulder `show run` is enough. Clear-text passwords lift straight out, and because they are frequently reused, one leaked device password often unlocks peers, giving an attacker lateral movement across the fleet.

Blast radius depends on what is stored: leaked line/user passwords can grant management access to the device and to any peer sharing them; leaked routing/authentication keys can enable route injection or neighbor spoofing.

A critical caveat to communicate: `service password-encryption` only applies weak, reversible Type 7 encoding. It stops shoulder-surfing and casual disclosure but is NOT cryptographic protection — Type 7 is decoded instantly (see MGMT-004). It is therefore a mandatory baseline, not a substitute for `secret`-based Type 5/8/9 hashing or centralized AAA. This maps to CIS password rules, NIST IA-5(1)/SC-28, and CWE-256.
steps: Check current state with `show running-config | include service password-encryption`.
Inventory which cleartext passwords exist today using `show running-config | include password|secret|key` so you know what will be obfuscated.
Understand the scope: enabling the service only affects how passwords are stored/displayed; it does not change any password value or authentication behavior.
Enter configuration mode with `configure terminal`.
Enable the service with `service password-encryption`.
Note that only passwords entered AFTER enabling (or re-entered) are converted; already-hashed `secret` entries are unaffected.
Force conversion of existing cleartext line passwords by re-entering them, e.g. under `line vty 0 4` retype `password <x>` so it is stored as Type 7.
Recognize that Type 7 is weak: plan to migrate line/user passwords to `secret` (Type 5/8/9) or AAA (see MGMT-004) rather than relying on this alone.
Exit with `end`.
Confirm cleartext no longer appears with `do show running-config | include password`.
Save with `copy running-config startup-config` (or `write memory`).
On HA/SSO pairs, verify the standby reflects the setting and re-save on the active.
Update backup/automation systems so archived configs no longer contain cleartext strings.
Restrict read access to config backups regardless, since Type 7 is reversible.
Re-run the NSS scanner to confirm MGMT-003 clears, then address MGMT-004 for the now-Type-7 entries.
Add `service password-encryption` to your golden/baseline template so new devices inherit it.
MGMT-003.cli:
configure terminal
service password-encryption
! Re-enter existing cleartext line passwords so they are stored obfuscated, e.g.:
line vty 0 4
 password <existing-line-password>
exit
end
copy running-config startup-config
MGMT-003.verify: `show running-config | include service password-encryption` returns the line. Previously cleartext line/user passwords now display as `password 7 <hex>` rather than as a plain string (or `password 0 <text>`); confirm with `show running-config | include password`.
MGMT-003.rollback: Disable with `configure terminal` then `no service password-encryption`. Caveat: reverting causes all newly entered passwords to be stored in clear text again, exposing them in every config copy — there is no operational reason to revert.
MGMT-003.impact: Non-disruptive and no reload. Passwords keep working unchanged; only their on-disk/on-screen representation changes. Enabling the service does not drop sessions or affect data-plane traffic.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark — Password Rules (service password-encryption)
Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — Password Management
CWE-256: Plaintext Storage of a Password

MGMT-004.risk:
MGMT-004 fires at HIGH severity when the scanner finds any line matching `password\s+7\s+` or `username\s+\S+\s+password\s+7\s+` — that is, credentials stored with Cisco Type 7 encoding. The finding title includes the count of matching lines, and the affected items are shown with the hash masked (`password 7 ****`) so the scanner report itself does not leak recoverable secrets. It runs on IOS, IOS-XE, and the Catalyst 9800 WLC.

Type 7 is the encoding produced by `service password-encryption`. It is a Vigenere cipher with a published key and is reversible in milliseconds — Cisco explicitly states it is intended only to deter shoulder-surfing, not to protect secrets. Any of numerous free decoders (or `key chain` tricks on the device itself) turns `password 7 <hex>` back into the original clear-text password. Effectively, a Type 7 password in a config is a clear-text password with a thin veneer.

The attack is straightforward credential recovery from config exposure (ATT&CK T1552.001, Unsecured Credentials). Once a config reaches a backup server, ticket, chat, or screen share, the attacker decodes every Type 7 string offline and reuses them — often against multiple devices because operators reuse line/user passwords. This directly enables T1078 Valid Accounts and lateral movement.

Blast radius covers management access to the device and to any peer sharing the recovered password, plus any service key stored as Type 7.

Remediation differs by credential type. For local users, replace `username <name> password <x>` with `username <name> secret <x>` (one-way Type 5) or, on IOS-XE 16.x+/9800, `username <name> algorithm-type scrypt secret <x>` (Type 9). For line access, stop using line passwords entirely and move to AAA/local `login local`. Type 8 (PBKDF2-SHA256) and Type 9 (scrypt) are the modern, non-reversible options; Type 5 (MD5) is acceptable where 8/9 are unavailable. This maps to CIS password rules, NIST IA-5(1), CWE-261 and CWE-326.
steps: Enumerate offending lines with `show running-config | include password 7` and `show running-config | include username`.
Classify each hit: local user password, line password, or a service/protocol key (e.g. routing auth).
For each local user, capture the intended cleartext (rotate to a NEW value where possible rather than reusing the recoverable one).
Confirm the platform supports Type 8/9 with `show version` (IOS-XE 16.x+/9800 do).
Enter configuration mode with `configure terminal`.
Recreate each local user with a one-way hash: `username <name> privilege <lvl> algorithm-type scrypt secret <newpass>` (Type 9), or `username <name> secret <newpass>` (Type 5) on older IOS.
Remove any lingering old form with `no username <name> password ...` if the username was duplicated.
For VTY/console line passwords, migrate to AAA/local: under the line configure `login local` (with local `secret` users) or `login authentication <list>` and delete the `password 7` line.
Do not simply toggle `no service password-encryption` — that reverts to clear text and does not fix reversibility.
Exit with `end`.
From a second session, log in with each rebuilt credential to confirm authentication still works.
Verify no Type 7 remains with `do show running-config | include password 7`.
Save with `copy running-config startup-config` (or `write memory`).
On HA/SSO pairs, confirm sync to standby and re-save on the active.
Rotate the recovered passwords anywhere they were reused across the fleet.
Re-run the NSS scanner to confirm MGMT-004 clears, and add a template guard rejecting `secret`-less `username ... password` and line `password` entries.
MGMT-004.cli:
configure terminal
! Local users -> one-way hash (Type 9 on IOS-XE/9800):
username netadmin privilege 15 algorithm-type scrypt secret <newpass>
no username netadmin password 7 <old>
! Move line auth off line-passwords to local AAA:
line vty 0 4
 login local
 no password 7 <old>
exit
end
copy running-config startup-config
MGMT-004.verify: `show running-config | include password 7` returns nothing. `show running-config | include secret` shows users as `secret 9 $9$...` (or `secret 5 $1$...`). Each migrated user/line authenticates successfully from a fresh login.
MGMT-004.rollback: Restore a saved pre-change config or re-add the prior `username ... secret`/line config from your backup. Caveat: reverting to `password 7` re-introduces trivially reversible credentials that any config leak exposes; only restore hashed (`secret`) equivalents, never the Type 7 form.
MGMT-004.impact: Low disruption if sequenced correctly. Rebuilding user credentials does not drop existing authenticated sessions, but anyone using the OLD password must use the new one at next login, and any external system/script authenticating with the old line/user password must be updated first to avoid lockout. No reload or data-plane impact.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark — Password Rules (no Type 7)
Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — Type 8/9 Password Hashing
CWE-261: Weak Encoding for Password; CWE-326: Inadequate Encryption Strength

MGMT-005.risk:
MGMT-005 fires at CRITICAL severity when the running configuration lacks a line matching `^aaa new-model`.
