The check runs on IOS, IOS-XE, and the Catalyst 9800 WLC. `aaa new-model` is the master switch that activates the IOS Authentication, Authorization, and Accounting subsystem; without it the device falls back to legacy per-line password authentication and cannot use method lists, TACACS+, or RADIUS in the modern way.

With AAA disabled, the device has no path to centralized authentication. Administrators authenticate against local line/enable passwords, there is no per-user accountability, no centralized password policy or rotation, no command authorization, and no accounting trail sent to a TACACS+/RADIUS server. Every downstream AAA control the scanner checks (MGMT-006 authentication login, MGMT-007 none-fallback, MGMT-008 authorization, MGMT-009 accounting) is gated on `aaa new-model` being present, so its absence is the root cause that suppresses an entire class of protections.

The risk aligns with ATT&CK T1078 Valid Accounts and weak Account Management. Shared line passwords mean compromised or departed-employee credentials cannot be individually revoked, brute-force lockout and MFA are unavailable, and there is no authoritative log of who logged in or what commands ran — crippling incident response and defeating non-repudiation.

Blast radius is fleet-wide governance: on a core router or 9800 controller the lack of centralized AAA means credential compromise is hard to detect and contain.

Critical operational nuance: enabling `aaa new-model` immediately changes login behavior. The instant it is applied, VTY and console logins begin using the AAA `default` method list; if no method list and no reachable/valid method exist, you can lock yourself out. The safe sequence is to define a local level-15 user AND an authentication method list with a `local` fallback BEFORE or in the same commit as enabling AAA. This maps to CIS AAA rules, NIST 800-53 AC-2/IA-2, and CWE-287.
steps: Check current state with `show running-config | include aaa new-model`.
CRITICAL PRE-STEP: ensure a working local admin exists — `show running-config | include username` — and if not, create `username <admin> privilege 15 algorithm-type scrypt secret <pass>`.
Verify console access is available as an out-of-band recovery path in case VTY auth breaks.
Plan a short maintenance window; enabling AAA changes live login behavior.
Enter configuration mode with `configure terminal`.
Create the local fallback user if missing (see step 2) BEFORE enabling AAA.
Enable the framework with `aaa new-model`.
Immediately define a default login method list with a local fallback so you are not locked out: `aaa authentication login default group tacacs+ local` (or `local` only if no TACACS yet).
Add an enable authentication list if using enable: `aaa authentication enable default group tacacs+ enable`.
If TACACS+ is intended, configure the server now: `tacacs server TAC1` then `address ipv4 <ip>` and `key <shared-secret>`, and group it with `aaa group server tacacs+ TAC-GRP` / `server name TAC1`.
Bind console to a safe list so physical access is not accidentally broken: under `line con 0` set `login authentication default` (or a dedicated console list).
Exit with `end`.
From a SECOND session, log in and confirm authentication succeeds via both TACACS (if configured) and the local fallback (simulate TACACS down).
Save with `copy running-config startup-config` (or `write memory`).
On HA/SSO pairs, confirm the standby has the same AAA config and re-save on the active.
Re-run the NSS scanner to confirm MGMT-005 clears and then address MGMT-006 through MGMT-009.
Roll `aaa new-model` and the method lists into your golden template for all new devices.
MGMT-005.cli:
configure terminal
! 1) Guarantee a local fallback admin FIRST:
username netadmin privilege 15 algorithm-type scrypt secret <pass>
! 2) Enable AAA:
aaa new-model
! 3) Define method lists WITH local fallback (prevents lockout):
aaa authentication login default group tacacs+ local
aaa authentication enable default group tacacs+ enable
! 4) (optional) TACACS+ server:
tacacs server TAC1
 address ipv4 10.0.0.10
 key <shared-secret>
exit
aaa group server tacacs+ TAC-GRP
 server name TAC1
exit
line con 0
 login authentication default
exit
end
copy running-config startup-config
MGMT-005.verify: `show running-config | include aaa new-model` returns the line, and `show running-config | section aaa` shows the method lists. `show aaa method-lists authentication` lists a `default` list. From a fresh session, both a TACACS user and the local fallback user authenticate successfully.
MGMT-005.rollback: Disable with `configure terminal` then `no aaa new-model`. Caveat: this instantly tears down all method lists and reverts to legacy line/enable password authentication, re-exposing the device to no per-user accountability, no centralized auth, and no command authorization/accounting — and can itself drop you if lines then have no password. Prefer fixing the method list over disabling AAA.
MGMT-005.impact: Disruptive/high-risk change. Enabling `aaa new-model` immediately alters how console and VTY logins are authenticated; a missing or wrong method list can lock out all remote administration. No reload or data-plane traffic impact, but perform it in a window with console access and a verified local fallback.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark — AAA Rules (Enable aaa new-model)
Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — AAA / TACACS+

MGMT-006.risk:
MGMT-006 fires at HIGH severity when `aaa new-model` IS present but the configuration contains no line matching `^aaa authentication login`. Because the check first returns early if `aaa new-model` is absent, this finding specifically means: AAA is turned on, yet no login authentication method list has been defined. It runs on IOS, IOS-XE, and the Catalyst 9800 WLC.

Enabling AAA without a login method list leaves authentication in an ambiguous and often broken state. On IOS, once `aaa new-model` is enabled, lines that reference no explicit method list use the `default` list; if the `default` list itself is undefined, the effective behavior depends on platform/version defaults (frequently the local database). The result is a device where the intended centralized authentication (TACACS+/RADIUS) is not actually being enforced, and access may silently fall through to whatever legacy credential still exists.

The risk is Improper Authentication (CWE-287) enabling T1078 Valid Accounts. Administrators believe TACACS+ is protecting the box while in reality logins may be governed by an unmanaged local/line password with no per-user accountability, no server-side lockout, and no MFA. An attacker who knows or recovers that legacy credential logs in unnoticed.

Blast radius is management access to the device with no reliable audit of who authenticated, undermining detection and non-repudiation across whatever the device controls (routing, WLC-managed APs, etc.).

Platform nuance: the method list should always include a `local` fallback so that TACACS+/RADIUS server unreachability does not lock out administration — but it must NOT include `none` (that is MGMT-007). On the 9800 WLC the same `aaa authentication login` construct applies for management/admin login and can also be referenced by wireless client auth lists, so scope the named lists carefully. This maps to CIS AAA rules, NIST IA-2, CWE-287.
steps: Confirm the gap with `show running-config | include aaa authentication login` (expect no output) while `aaa new-model` is present.
Verify a local level-15 fallback user exists (`show running-config | include username`); create one if not, so the `local` keyword has something to fall back to.
Decide the intended backend: TACACS+, RADIUS, or local-only for now.
If TACACS+/RADIUS is intended, confirm the server and key are configured (`show running-config | section tacacs|radius`).
Plan a maintenance window since login behavior will change on commit.
Enter configuration mode with `configure terminal`.
Define the default login method list WITH a local fallback: `aaa authentication login default group tacacs+ local`.
If using RADIUS instead: `aaa authentication login default group radius local`.
Add an enable-mode list where relevant: `aaa authentication enable default group tacacs+ enable`.
Bind the console to a known-good list under `line con 0`: `login authentication default` (or a dedicated console list) so physical access is preserved.
Ensure VTY lines reference a valid list (the `default` list is used automatically unless overridden with `login authentication <named>`).
Exit with `end`.
From a SECOND session, authenticate using a TACACS/RADIUS account and separately verify the `local` fallback works by simulating server unreachability.
Save with `copy running-config startup-config` (or `write memory`).
On HA/SSO pairs, confirm the standby has identical AAA config and re-save on the active.
Re-run the NSS scanner to confirm MGMT-006 clears; check that MGMT-007 (no `none`) also passes.
MGMT-006.cli:
configure terminal
! Login method list with safe local fallback (no 'none'):
aaa authentication login default group tacacs+ local
aaa authentication enable default group tacacs+ enable
! Keep console on a known-good list:
line con 0
 login authentication default
exit
end
copy running-config startup-config
MGMT-006.verify: `show running-config | include aaa authentication login` returns `aaa authentication login default group tacacs+ local`. `show aaa method-lists authentication` shows the `default` list. A TACACS/RADIUS login and a local-fallback login both succeed from fresh sessions.
MGMT-006.rollback: Remove with `configure terminal` then `no aaa authentication login default group tacacs+ local`. Caveat: removing the list while `aaa new-model` stays enabled returns login to undefined/implicit default behavior and removes enforced centralized authentication — re-exposing the device to unmanaged legacy-credential access. Prefer editing the list over deleting it.
MGMT-006.impact: Disruptive to login behavior. Defining/changing the default login list changes how every subsequent console/VTY authentication is evaluated; a wrong backend or missing local fallback can lock out administration. No reload and no data-plane traffic impact — perform with console access available.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark — AAA Rules (aaa authentication login)
Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — AAA Authentication

MGMT-007.risk:
MGMT-007 fires at CRITICAL severity when an `aaa authentication login` line exists and contains the keyword `none` (matched case-insensitively with a word boundary, `\bnone\b`). The offending line(s) are attached to the finding. It runs on IOS, IOS-XE, and the Catalyst 9800 WLC, and only after `aaa new-model` is confirmed present.

The `none` method in an authentication list means "authentication succeeds with no credential." It is typically added as a lab convenience or a mistaken lockout-avoidance measure — e.g. `aaa authentication login default group tacacs+ none`. The intent is usually "fall back if TACACS+ is down," but the actual behavior is catastrophic: if the preceding method (the TACACS+/RADIUS group) returns an ERROR (server unreachable), IOS moves to `none` and grants access with no password at all. An attacker only has to make the AAA server unreachable — trivial via a targeted DoS, a link cut, or simply choosing a time when the server is down — to walk straight in.

This is an Authentication Bypass (CWE-306 Missing Authentication for a Critical Function / CWE-305) and maps to ATT&CK T1078 Valid Accounts and T1556 Modify Authentication Process. The result is unauthenticated administrative access to the device: full config read/write, credential harvest, routing manipulation, and log tampering, with no accountability because there is no authenticated user.

Blast radius is total device compromise reachable by anyone who can either reach the VTY/console when the AAA server is unavailable or induce that unavailability — an especially severe exposure on internet-adjacent or shared-management devices.

The correct fix is to replace `none` with `local` as the final fallback, so that if TACACS+/RADIUS is unreachable the device requires a valid LOCAL credential instead of granting free access. Never use `none` on any production management line. This maps to CIS AAA rules, NIST 800-53 IA-2, and CWE-306.
steps: Identify every offending list with `show running-config | include aaa authentication .* none`.
Note which lists use `none` and whether they are `default` or named lists bound to specific lines.

Ensure a local level-15 fallback user exists (`show running-config | include username`); create `username <admin> privilege 15 algorithm-type scrypt secret <pass>` if not — this is the safe replacement for `none`.
Verify the TACACS+/RADIUS server config is valid so the primary method actually works (`show running-config | section tacacs|radius`).
Plan a brief maintenance window; you are changing live authentication.
Enter configuration mode with `configure terminal`.
Rewrite each offending list to end in `local` instead of `none`, e.g. `aaa authentication login default group tacacs+ local`.
For a named list, reissue it identically but with `local`, e.g. `aaa authentication login VTY-LIST group tacacs+ local`.
If a list used ONLY `none` (`aaa authentication login <x> none`), replace it entirely with `group tacacs+ local` or at minimum `local`.
Confirm no other line type (console, aux) still references a `none` list.
Exit with `end`.
From a SECOND session, verify a valid TACACS/RADIUS login works, then simulate server-down and confirm the LOCAL fallback prompts for and requires a real password (NOT free access).
Save with `copy running-config startup-config` (or `write memory`).
On HA/SSO pairs, confirm the standby is updated and re-save on the active.
Re-run the NSS scanner to confirm MGMT-007 clears.
Add a compliance guard/template lint that rejects the `none` keyword in any authentication list.
MGMT-007.cli:
configure terminal
! Replace 'none' with 'local' as the final fallback:
aaa authentication login default group tacacs+ local
! Repeat for any named list that had 'none', e.g.:
aaa authentication login VTY-LIST group tacacs+ local
end
copy running-config startup-config
MGMT-007.verify: `show running-config | include aaa authentication` shows NO `none` keyword on any login list; each list ends in `local`. Simulating AAA-server unreachability, a login now REQUIRES a valid local password and denies access when none is given.
MGMT-007.rollback: Re-add the prior list with `configure terminal` then `aaa authentication login default group tacacs+ none`. Caveat: reverting re-introduces a full authentication bypass — anyone can gain admin access whenever the AAA server is unreachable. This is a critical exposure; do not revert to a `none`-containing list under any circumstances.
MGMT-007.impact: Disruptive to authentication semantics but strictly hardening. After the change, administrators can no longer get in without a credential when TACACS/RADIUS is down — they must use the local account. Ensure the local fallback credential is known/vaulted first. No reload, no data-plane impact.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark — AAA Rules (no 'none' method)
Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — AAA Authentication Fallback
CWE-306: Missing Authentication for Critical Function

MGMT-008.risk:
MGMT-008 fires at MEDIUM severity when `aaa new-model` is present but the configuration contains no line matching `^aaa authorization`. It runs on IOS, IOS-XE, and the Catalyst 9800 WLC. The finding means the device authenticates users but performs no AAA authorization — there is no EXEC authorization deciding what privilege level a user starts at, and no command authorization constraining which commands a user may run.

Without authorization, every successfully authenticated user operates at whatever privilege their session grants (commonly level 15 if the local user or TACACS profile grants it), and there is no per-command gating. This defeats least-privilege and separation of duties: a junior operator, a monitoring account, or a contractor can execute configuration-changing and destructive commands (`reload`, `write erase`, `no` statements, crypto/ACL edits) with nothing to stop them. It is Missing Authorization (CWE-862) and enables Privilege Escalation / Abuse of Elevation (ATT&CK T1078 with excessive rights).

The concrete scenario: a compromised low-tier admin account, or an insider, uses an over-privileged session to alter routing, exfiltrate the config, disable logging, or brick the device — and because command authorization is absent, TACACS+ never had the chance to deny the command. On a 9800 WLC this also means no granular control over who can change RF/WLAN/security policy affecting the whole wireless estate.

Blast radius is the full command surface of the device for every authenticated identity, scaled by however many accounts exist.

Critical operational nuance: command authorization can lock you out if TACACS+ becomes unreachable and no local fallback is configured, or if the authorizing profile denies the very commands you need. Always pair `group tacacs+` with a `local` (or `if-authenticated`) fallback, and use `aaa authorization console` deliberately if you want authorization enforced on the console too (by default it is not). This maps to CIS AAA rules, NIST 800-53 AC-3/AC-6, and CWE-862.
steps: Confirm the gap with `show running-config | include aaa authorization` (expect no output) while `aaa new-model` is present.
Confirm authentication and a local level-15 fallback user already work (MGMT-006/007) before layering authorization on top.
Ensure the TACACS+ server is configured to return command/EXEC authorization results for your admin groups.
Decide scope: EXEC authorization (starting privilege) and/or command authorization at levels 1 and 15.
Plan a maintenance window — misconfigured command authorization can lock out administration.
Enter configuration mode with `configure terminal`.
Configure EXEC authorization with local fallback: `aaa authorization exec default group tacacs+ local`.
Configure level-15 command authorization with fallback: `aaa authorization commands 15 default group tacacs+ local`.
Optionally add level-1 command authorization: `aaa authorization commands 1 default group tacacs+ local`.
Decide on console enforcement: add `aaa authorization console` only if you want authorization applied to `line con 0` (otherwise console is exempt as a safety valve).
Add `aaa authorization config-commands` if you want config-mode commands authorized (this is the strict setting).
Exit with `end`.
From a SECOND session, verify an authorized admin can run needed commands, and confirm the `local` fallback still permits management when TACACS is simulated down.
Test a lower-privilege account to confirm restricted commands are denied as expected.
Save with `copy running-config startup-config` (or `write memory`).
On HA/SSO pairs, confirm the standby is updated and re-save on the active; then re-run the NSS scanner to confirm MGMT-008 clears.
MGMT-008.cli:
configure terminal
aaa authorization exec default group tacacs+ local
aaa authorization commands 1 default group tacacs+ local
aaa authorization commands 15 default group tacacs+ local
! Enforce on console only if intended:
! aaa authorization console
end
copy running-config startup-config
MGMT-008.verify: `show running-config | include aaa authorization` shows the exec and commands lists. `show aaa method-lists authorization` lists the `default` lists. A privileged account runs allowed commands; a restricted account is denied a level-15 command; the local fallback still grants management when TACACS is unreachable.
MGMT-008.rollback: Remove with `configure terminal` then `no aaa authorization exec default group tacacs+ local` and `no aaa authorization commands 15 default group tacacs+ local`. Caveat: removing authorization returns every authenticated user to unconstrained command execution at their privilege level, eliminating least-privilege enforcement. If a lockout occurs due to TACACS unreachability, use console access (exempt unless `aaa authorization console` is set) to remove the offending list.
MGMT-008.impact: Disruptive if misconfigured. Command authorization actively gates what admins can type; a wrong TACACS profile or missing local fallback can block legitimate work or lock out remote administration. No reload or data-plane impact. Always retain console as an out-of-band escape and a `local` fallback.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark — AAA Rules (aaa authorization)
Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — Command Authorization

MGMT-009.risk:
MGMT-009 fires at MEDIUM severity when `aaa new-model` is present but the configuration contains no line matching `^aaa accounting`. It is evaluated in the same method as MGMT-008 (check_aaa_authorization) and runs on IOS, IOS-XE, and the Catalyst 9800 WLC. The finding means the device performs no AAA accounting — administrative sessions and commands are not being recorded to a TACACS+/RADIUS server for an audit trail.

Without accounting there is no authoritative, off-box record of who logged in, when, from where, and which configuration or operational commands they executed. Local logging/syslog may capture some events, but AAA command accounting (`aaa accounting commands 15`) is the control that produces the per-command, per-user audit trail security and compliance teams rely on. Its absence is Insufficient Logging (CWE-778) and directly undermines detection, forensic reconstruction, and non-repudiation.

The concrete impact appears during and after an incident: an attacker or malicious insider who gains admin access can alter routing, disable services, or exfiltrate the config, and the organization has no reliable evidence of the commands run — especially damaging because an attacker who reaches the device can also clear local logs. Command accounting sent to an external TACACS+/RADIUS/SIEM server survives local log tampering (mitigating ATT&CK T1070 Indicator Removal) and enables alerting on suspicious command sequences.

Blast radius is the loss of accountability for every administrative action on the device — a governance and compliance gap rather than a direct compromise vector, but one that magnifies the cost of any other breach.

Platform nuance: use `start-stop` so records are sent both when a session/command begins and ends, giving complete coverage. On IOS-XE and the 9800 WLC the syntax is identical. Ensure the AAA server actually stores/forwards these records to your SIEM; accounting configured against an unreachable or non-logging server provides no benefit. This maps to CIS AAA rules, NIST 800-53 AU-2/AU-12 and AC-6(9), and CWE-778.
steps: Confirm the gap with `show running-config | include aaa accounting` (expect no output) while `aaa new-model` is present.
Confirm a reachable TACACS+/RADIUS server is configured and that it forwards accounting to your SIEM/log store.
Confirm authentication (MGMT-006) is already working so accounting has authenticated identities to attribute records to.
Decide scope: EXEC (login/logout) accounting plus command accounting at levels 1 and 15.
Enter configuration mode with `configure terminal`.
Enable EXEC session accounting: `aaa accounting exec default start-stop group tacacs+`.
Enable level-15 command accounting: `aaa accounting commands 15 default start-stop group tacacs+`.
Optionally add level-1 command accounting: `aaa accounting commands 1 default start-stop group tacacs+`.
Optionally capture system-level events (reloads, AAA restarts): `aaa accounting system default start-stop group tacacs+`.
Consider `aaa accounting connection default start-stop group tacacs+` for outbound connection records where relevant.
Exit with `end`.
Generate test activity from a second session (login and run a level-15 command) to produce accounting records.
Confirm the TACACS+/RADIUS server (or SIEM) received the accounting records for that session and command.
Save with `copy running-config startup-config` (or `write memory`).
On HA/SSO pairs, confirm the standby has identical accounting config and re-save on the active.
Re-run the NSS scanner to confirm MGMT-009 clears, and add these accounting lines to your golden template.
MGMT-009.cli:
configure terminal
aaa accounting exec default start-stop group tacacs+
aaa accounting commands 1 default start-stop group tacacs+
aaa accounting commands 15 default start-stop group tacacs+
aaa accounting system default start-stop group tacacs+
end
copy running-config startup-config
MGMT-009.verify: `show running-config | include aaa accounting` shows the exec/commands/system lists. `show aaa sessions` displays active AAA/accounting sessions, and the TACACS+/RADIUS server or SIEM shows new login and command records generated by a test session.
MGMT-009.rollback: Remove with `configure terminal` then `no aaa accounting commands 15 default start-stop group tacacs+` and the other accounting lines. Caveat: reverting eliminates the off-box administrative audit trail, so command-level activity again survives only in tamperable local logs — degrading forensic and compliance capability. There is no security downside to keeping accounting enabled.
MGMT-009.impact: Non-disruptive. Accounting only sends records to the AAA server; it does not gate authentication or command execution and cannot lock out administrators. No reload and no data-plane traffic impact. A very large fleet may add modest load on the TACACS+/RADIUS/SIEM infrastructure, which should be sized accordingly.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark — AAA Rules (aaa accounting)
Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — AAA Accounting
NIST SP 800-53 Rev5 AU-2, AU-12, AC-6(9)

MGMT-010.risk:
The scanner matched the global-config line `ip ssh version 1` in the running configuration (detection: `self.config.has_line(r"^ip ssh version 1")` in check_ssh_version). This module runs only on IOS, IOS-XE and Catalyst 9800 WLC (SUPPORTED_PLATFORMS = ios/iosxe/wlc); NX-OS and ASA/FTD are skipped because they negotiate SSHv2 differently, so this finding is always an IOS-family device that has been explicitly pinned to the legacy SSH transport protocol version 1.

SSH protocol version 1 is cryptographically broken. It uses a server key plus a host key in a construction vulnerable to the well-known CRC-32 integrity-check insertion attack and to session-key recovery, and it has no real MAC (it relies on a CRC that an attacker can manipulate). An on-path adversary performing MITM (ATT&CK T1557 Adversary-in-the-Middle) can downgrade, inject, or decrypt administrative traffic.

Concrete attack scenario: an attacker with a foothold on a transit VLAN or a compromised switch ARP-spoofs the management gateway, intercepts the SSHv1 handshake, exploits the CRC-32 weakness to recover the session key, and reads the operator's TACACS+/enable credentials in flight (T1040 Network Sniffing, T1110 for any brute attempts). Those credentials are then replayed for privileged EXEC access (T1078 Valid Accounts).

Blast radius: full administrative compromise of the device. On a core router or a 9800 WLC that means control over routing, ACLs, and — on the WLC — every access point and wireless client policy it manages. A compromised management plane is typically pivoted to the rest of the estate.

Platform nuance: `ip ssh version 1` and the ambiguous default behaviour differ across trains. Setting `ip ssh version 2` forces v2-only and disables the v1 and v1.99 fallback. IOS-XE and 9800 behave identically here. NX-OS does not honour this command (SSHv2 only, feature ssh), and ASA uses `ssh version 2` — but the scanner never emits MGMT-010 for those platforms.

Compliance: this violates CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 1.3.1 (require SSHv2), the Cisco IOS Hardening Guide management-plane guidance, NIST 800-53 controls AC-17 (Remote Access) and SC-8 (Transmission Confidentiality/Integrity), and maps to CWE-327 (Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm).
steps: Pre-check: from privileged EXEC run `show running-config | include ip ssh` and `show ip ssh` to confirm the current `SSH Enabled - version 1.99` or `version 1.5` state and see the active RSA key length.
Inventory who is connected before you change transport: `show users` and `show ssh` (lists active SSH sessions; works on IOS, IOS-XE and 9800) so you know which sessions could be affected.
Confirm a working out-of-band path (console or a second reachable admin) in case SSH becomes briefly unavailable while the key is regenerated.

Verify prerequisites for SSHv2 keys exist: `show running-config | include hostname` and `show running-config | include ip domain`. A non-default `hostname` and an `ip domain-name` (or `ip domain name` on newer IOS-XE) are REQUIRED before an RSA key can be generated.
Enter config mode: `configure terminal`.
If missing, set identity first: `hostname CORE-RTR-01` then `ip domain-name corp.example.com` (newer IOS-XE syntax: `ip domain name corp.example.com`).

Generate an adequately sized RSA modulus BEFORE forcing version 2 (v2 requires a >=768-bit key; use 2048): `crypto key generate rsa modulus 2048`. If a key already exists you will be prompted to replace it — regenerating briefly drops existing SSH sessions.
Force SSH version 2 only, which removes the v1/v1.99 fallback: `ip ssh version 2`.
Harden the handshake while you are here: `ip ssh time-out 60` and `ip ssh authentication-retries 3` (aligns with MGMT-012/013).
Ensure the VTY lines only accept SSH so no Telnet fallback exists: under `line vty 0 4` (and `line vty 5 15` if present) set `transport input ssh`.
Exit to privileged EXEC: `end`.
Open a NEW SSH session from a jump host to confirm v2 works before you disconnect your current session.
Validate the negotiated version: `show ip ssh` should now report `SSH Enabled - version 2.0`.
Confirm no residual v1 line remains: `show running-config | include ip ssh version` should show only `ip ssh version 2`.
Change control: schedule the RSA regeneration inside a maintenance window if any automation (e.g. Ansible/NSO) holds long-lived SSH sessions, since regen resets them.
Save the configuration: `copy running-config startup-config` (or `write memory`).
On a 9800 WLC, additionally verify management access to the WebUI/NETCONF still works, as they share the crypto stack.
Post-change: review syslog for SSH handshake errors and re-run the NSS scan to confirm MGMT-010 has cleared.
MGMT-010.cli:
configure terminal
hostname CORE-RTR-01
ip domain-name corp.example.com
crypto key generate rsa modulus 2048
ip ssh version 2
ip ssh time-out 60
ip ssh authentication-retries 3
line vty 0 4
 transport input ssh
line vty 5 15
 transport input ssh
end
copy running-config startup-config
MGMT-010.verify: `show ip ssh` must display `SSH Enabled - version 2.0` (not `1.99` or `1.5`). `show running-config | include ip ssh version` returns only `ip ssh version 2`. A fresh `ssh -1 <device>` attempt from a client should be refused.
MGMT-010.rollback: `configure terminal` then `ip ssh version 1` (or `no ip ssh version 2` to return to the v1.99 fallback), then `copy running-config startup-config`. Caveat: reverting re-enables the cryptographically broken SSHv1, re-exposing the device to MITM key-recovery and credential interception.
MGMT-010.impact: Low-to-moderate operational impact. Applying `ip ssh version 2` alone is non-disruptive to established sessions. However `crypto key generate rsa` (if the key is replaced/regenerated) tears down all current SSH sessions momentarily and forces reconnection — do it with a console fallback available. No reload required; no data-plane/traffic impact.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 1.3.1 (Set 'ip ssh version 2')
Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — Management Plane / Use SSH
CWE-327: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm

MGMT-011.risk:
The scanner reached the `elif not self.config.has_line(r"^ip ssh version 2")` branch of check_ssh_version: the config has neither `ip ssh version 1` nor an explicit `ip ssh version 2`. In other words SSH version enforcement is left at the platform default. On IOS/IOS-XE that default is the compatibility mode `1.99`, meaning the device advertises support for BOTH SSHv2 and legacy SSHv1 and lets the client choose — so a downgrade-capable or misconfigured client can negotiate the broken v1 transport.

This module runs only on IOS, IOS-XE and Catalyst 9800 WLC. The finding is HIGH (one notch below the explicit-v1 CRITICAL) because the exposure is latent: the device is not forced to v1, but nothing prevents a v1 negotiation either.

Why it is a real risk: SSHv1 has no true MAC and is vulnerable to the CRC-32 insertion attack and session-key recovery. An adversary on the management path (T1557 Adversary-in-the-Middle) can force the connection to fall back to v1 during negotiation and then intercept or tamper with the administrative session, harvesting TACACS+/enable credentials (T1040 Network Sniffing) for later reuse (T1078 Valid Accounts).

Concrete scenario: a monitoring or backup tool built on an old SSH library connects with `-1` fallback; because the router advertises 1.99, the handshake completes over v1, and an attacker who has ARP-poisoned the segment silently records and decrypts that session, obtaining a service account with config-read (and often config-write) rights.

Blast radius: the same as MGMT-010 if the v1 path is actually exercised — full management-plane compromise. Even if never exploited, leaving 1.99 enabled is a standing audit failure and an easy downgrade target.

Platform nuance: explicitly setting `ip ssh version 2` removes the 1.99 advertisement on IOS and IOS-XE/9800. NX-OS runs SSHv2 only by default (no equivalent knob), and ASA/FTD use `ssh version 2`; the scanner does not evaluate those here. Newer IOS-XE 17.x images default toward v2 but still accept the explicit command, which is what the benchmark requires.

Compliance: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 1.3.1, Cisco IOS Hardening Guide (require SSHv2), NIST 800-53 AC-17/SC-8, CWE-327.
steps: Pre-check the current mode: `show ip ssh`. If it reports `version 1.99` (or `version 1.5 to 2.0`), v1 fallback is live even though no `ip ssh version 1` line exists.
List any explicit ssh config: `show running-config | include ip ssh`.
Confirm SSH crypto prerequisites: `show crypto key mypubkey rsa` to check an RSA key exists and its modulus (>=768 required for v2, 2048 recommended).
Confirm identity prerequisites are present: `show running-config | include hostname` and `show running-config | include ip domain`.
Ensure you have console/OOB fallback before touching crypto, in case a key regeneration is needed.
Enter config mode: `configure terminal`.

If no adequate RSA key exists, set `hostname CORE-RTR-01` and `ip domain-name corp.example.com` (or `ip domain name corp.example.com` on newer IOS-XE) first, then `crypto key generate rsa modulus 2048` — generate the key BEFORE enforcing v2.
Explicitly enforce version 2 only: `ip ssh version 2`.
While here, align related hardening: `ip ssh time-out 60` and `ip ssh authentication-retries 3`.
Restrict VTY transport so there is no other legacy path: under `line vty 0 4` and `line vty 5 15` set `transport input ssh`.
Return to EXEC: `end`.
Open a fresh SSH v2 session from a management host to confirm connectivity before closing your existing session.
Validate: `show ip ssh` should now report `version 2.0` rather than `1.99`.
Confirm the running config now contains the explicit line: `show running-config | include ip ssh version`.
If this is a 9800 WLC, confirm WebUI/NETCONF and AP join are unaffected.
Save: `copy running-config startup-config`.
Re-run the NSS scanner to confirm MGMT-011 has cleared.
Optionally push the same setting fleet-wide via template so no device is left at the 1.99 default.
MGMT-011.cli:
configure terminal
hostname CORE-RTR-01
ip domain-name corp.example.com
crypto key generate rsa modulus 2048
ip ssh version 2
ip ssh time-out 60
ip ssh authentication-retries 3
line vty 0 4
 transport input ssh
line vty 5 15
 transport input ssh
end
copy running-config startup-config
MGMT-011.verify: `show ip ssh` shows `SSH Enabled - version 2.0` (no longer `1.99`). `show running-config | include ip ssh version` returns exactly `ip ssh version 2`. Legacy clients attempting `ssh -1` are refused.
MGMT-011.rollback: `configure terminal` then `no ip ssh version 2`, then `copy running-config startup-config`. Caveat: this returns the device to the default 1.99 mode, re-advertising SSHv1 support and re-opening the downgrade/MITM exposure this check flags.
MGMT-011.impact: Minimal. `ip ssh version 2` on a device that already negotiates v2 is transparent to current sessions. Only if you must first generate/replace the RSA key will existing SSH sessions reset. No reload, no data-plane impact.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 1.3.1 (Require SSH version 2)
Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — Use SSH
CWE-327: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm

MGMT-012.risk:
The scanner extracted the integer from `ip ssh time-out <N>` (detection: `get_value(r"^ip ssh time-out\s+(\d+)")`) and found N greater than 60 seconds, emitting MGMT-012 at LOW severity with the actual value interpolated into the title (`SSH timeout too long ({timeout}s)`). This runs on IOS, IOS-XE and Catalyst 9800 WLC only.

Important nuance about what this timer actually is: `ip ssh time-out` is the SSH negotiation/authentication phase timeout — the window the device allows for the SSH handshake and login to complete before it tears the incomplete connection down. It is NOT the idle-EXEC timeout (that is `exec-timeout`, covered by MGMT-017/018). A long value here means half-open, pre-authentication SSH connections linger.

Why it is a risk: a generous authentication window makes the SSH front end easier to abuse for resource exhaustion and slow brute forcing. An attacker can open many concurrent connections that each sit in the authentication phase for the full timeout, consuming the limited VTY/SSH connection slots and delaying or denying legitimate admin access (T1499 Endpoint Denial of Service). A longer window also gives online credential-guessing (T1110 Brute Force) more time per connection and reduces the churn that would otherwise force reconnection.

Blast radius: primarily availability of the management plane rather than confidentiality — but on a device where the VTY pool is small (default 0-4, i.e. five sessions), even a modest flood of slow authentications can lock out operators during an incident, which is exactly when access matters.

Severity is LOW because exploitation requires network reach to the SSH port and yields DoS/brute-assist rather than direct compromise; it is a hardening/best-practice item, not a break.

Platform nuance: IOS, IOS-XE and the 9800 WLC all honour `ip ssh time-out` (valid range 1-120 seconds). NX-OS has no direct equivalent; ASA uses connection/console timeouts differently — neither is scanned here. Pair this with `login block-for` and short VTY ACLs for defence in depth.

Compliance: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 1.3.2, Cisco IOS Hardening Guide (SSH timers), NIST 800-53 AC-17, CWE-400 (Uncontrolled Resource Consumption).
steps: Pre-check the current value: `show running-config | include ip ssh time-out` (or `show ip ssh`, which prints `Authentication timeout: <N> secs`).
Confirm the scope: this is the auth-phase timer, distinct from `exec-timeout`; verify you are not conflating it with idle timeout by also checking `show running-config | section line vty`.
Note the current VTY session capacity: `show running-config | section line vty` (default 0-4 = five slots) so you understand the DoS surface you are shrinking.
Decide the target value: CIS recommends <=60 seconds; 60 is a safe operational choice that still tolerates slow WAN handshakes.
Enter config mode: `configure terminal`.
Set the timer to the recommended maximum: `ip ssh time-out 60`.
While here, tighten retries too if not already done: `ip ssh authentication-retries 3` (MGMT-013).
Return to EXEC: `end`.
Open a fresh SSH session to confirm the shorter window still allows a normal login to complete comfortably.
If admins authenticate via TACACS+ over a high-latency path, confirm 60s is still sufficient; if truly necessary, no value above 60 should be used — instead investigate the latency.
Validate the running value: `show ip ssh` should report `Authentication timeout: 60 secs`.
Confirm the config line: `show running-config | include ip ssh time-out` returns `ip ssh time-out 60`.
On a 9800 WLC, confirm admin WebUI/SSH logins still complete within the window.
Save: `copy running-config startup-config`.
Consider layering brute-force protection: `login block-for 120 attempts 3 within 60` at global config.
Re-run the NSS scan to confirm MGMT-012 has cleared.
MGMT-012.cli:
configure terminal
ip ssh time-out 60
ip ssh authentication-retries 3
end
copy running-config startup-config
MGMT-012.verify: `show ip ssh` displays `Authentication timeout: 60 secs; Authentication retries: 3`. `show running-config | include ip ssh time-out` returns `ip ssh time-out 60`.
MGMT-012.rollback: `configure terminal` then `ip ssh time-out <previous-value>` (or `no ip ssh time-out` to return to the 120s default), then `copy running-config startup-config`. Caveat: a longer window re-widens the slow-brute-force / half-open-connection DoS surface on the SSH front end.
MGMT-012.impact: Negligible and non-disruptive. It only shortens how long an incomplete SSH login may stall; established authenticated sessions are unaffected. No reload, no traffic impact. Only risk is that an excessively short value on a very high-latency link could cut off a legitimate slow handshake — 60s avoids this.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 1.3.2 (Set 'ip ssh time-out' <= 60)
Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — SSH timers

MGMT-013.risk:
The scanner parsed `ip ssh authentication-retries <N>` (detection: `get_value(r"^ip ssh authentication-retries\s+(\d+)")`) and found N greater than 3, emitting MGMT-013 at LOW severity with the value interpolated (`SSH authentication retries too high ({retries})`). Applies to IOS, IOS-XE and Catalyst 9800 WLC only.

What the setting does: `ip ssh authentication-retries` bounds how many times a single SSH connection may present incorrect credentials before the device drops that connection. A high value means each connection grants the attacker more password guesses before being forced to reconnect.

Why it is a risk: this is a direct multiplier on online brute-force efficiency (ATT&CK T1110 Brute Force, specifically T1110.001 Password Guessing / T1110.003 Password Spraying). With the default of 3, an attacker gets three guesses per TCP/SSH connection and must pay the full handshake cost to try more; raising it to, say, 6 doubles the guesses per connection and halves the connection overhead of a campaign. Combined with a long `ip ssh time-out` (MGMT-012) it makes credential-guessing meaningfully cheaper and quieter (fewer connection-open events in logs per guess).

Blast radius: if a local or fallback password is weak, a higher retry ceiling shortens the time to crack it, leading to privileged EXEC access (T1078 Valid Accounts) and full device compromise. On a 9800 WLC that extends to wireless infrastructure control.

Severity is LOW because it is an efficiency multiplier, not a vulnerability by itself; strong credentials, TACACS+, and `login block-for` provide the real backstop. Nonetheless the benchmark expects the tighter ceiling.

Platform nuance: IOS/IOS-XE/9800 all accept `ip ssh authentication-retries` (valid range 0-5; note the practical/benchmark ceiling is 3). NX-OS and ASA/FTD handle login limits through AAA/lockout mechanisms and are not scanned by this module. Pair the retry limit with global brute-force protection (`login block-for`) and account lockout on the AAA server for defence in depth.

Compliance: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 1.3.3, Cisco IOS Hardening Guide, NIST 800-53 AC-7 (Unsuccessful Logon Attempts) and AC-17, CWE-307 (Improper Restriction of Excessive Authentication Attempts).
steps: Pre-check the current value: `show running-config | include ip ssh authentication-retries` and `show ip ssh` (prints `Authentication retries: <N>`).
Confirm the configured local/AAA authentication path so you know how many real login stages a legitimate user needs (usually one).
Confirm whether global brute-force protection exists: `show running-config | include login block-for`.
Decide target: CIS recommends <=3; 3 is the standard hardened value.
Enter config mode: `configure terminal`.
Set the retry ceiling: `ip ssh authentication-retries 3`.
While here, ensure the auth-phase timer is also bounded: `ip ssh time-out 60` (MGMT-012).
Add global brute-force rate limiting if absent: `login block-for 120 attempts 3 within 60`.
Enable failed-login visibility: `login on-failure log` and `login on-success log`.
Return to EXEC: `end`.
Open a fresh SSH session and deliberately mistype the password once to confirm a legitimate user still gets a reasonable number of attempts within a connection.
Validate the value: `show ip ssh` reports `Authentication retries: 3`.
Confirm the config line: `show running-config | include ip ssh authentication-retries` returns `ip ssh authentication-retries 3`.
Confirm brute-force protection is active: `show login` shows the quiet-mode / block parameters.
Save: `copy running-config startup-config`.
Re-run the NSS scan to confirm MGMT-013 has cleared.
MGMT-013.cli:
configure terminal
ip ssh authentication-retries 3
ip ssh time-out 60
login block-for 120 attempts 3 within 60
login on-failure log
login on-success log
end
copy running-config startup-config
MGMT-013.verify: `show ip ssh` displays `Authentication retries: 3`. `show running-config | include ip ssh authentication-retries` returns `ip ssh authentication-retries 3`. `show login` confirms any configured block-for parameters.
MGMT-013.rollback: `configure terminal` then `ip ssh authentication-retries <previous-value>` (or `no ip ssh authentication-retries` to return to the default of 3), then `copy running-config startup-config`. Caveat: raising the ceiling again gives online password-guessing more attempts per SSH connection, improving brute-force efficiency against local/fallback credentials.
MGMT-013.impact: Negligible and non-disruptive. Reducing retries to 3 does not affect authenticated sessions and rarely affects legitimate users (who normally authenticate on the first attempt). No reload, no traffic impact.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 1.3.3 (Set 'ip ssh authentication-retries' <= 3)
Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — SSH
CWE-307: Improper Restriction of Excessive Authentication Attempts

MGMT-014.risk:
For each VTY section returned by `get_vty_lines()` (every `line vty <range>` block, e.g. `line vty 0 4`, `line vty 5 15`), the scanner checked whether any line begins with `transport input` (detection: `l.lower().startswith("transport input")`). When none is present it emits MGMT-014 (HIGH), with `{section}` being the exact VTY block header from the config (check_vty_transport). Applies to IOS, IOS-XE and Catalyst 9800 WLC.

What the absence means: `transport input` controls which protocols may terminate an inbound session on that VTY. With no explicit `transport input`, the permitted set depends on the platform/train default, which historically included Telnet (and even `all`) on many IOS images — so the device may silently accept plaintext Telnet to the CLI even though SSH is also configured. The scanner flags the missing restriction rather than assuming a safe default.

Why it is a real risk: if Telnet is reachable, administrative credentials and the entire session travel in cleartext. An attacker on the path (T1040 Network Sniffing, T1557 Adversary-in-the-Middle) captures enable/TACACS+ passwords and session content with no cryptographic effort, then reuses them (T1078 Valid Accounts). Even if only SSH is used in practice, an unrestricted VTY leaves an unexpected Telnet listener as an unmonitored attack path and a downgrade opportunity.

Blast radius: full management-plane exposure across every session slot in that VTY range. On a core device this is control of routing/ACLs; on a 9800 WLC it is control of the wireless estate. Because VTY blocks are pooled, one unrestricted block undermines the hardening of the others.

Platform nuance: IOS/IOS-XE/9800 all use `transport input ssh` under the VTY block; you can also add `transport output none` to stop the device initiating outbound sessions. NX-OS does not use `line vty transport input` (SSH/Telnet are feature-gated: `feature ssh`, `no feature telnet`), and ASA controls access with the `ssh`/`telnet <ip> <mask> <intf>` commands — neither is scanned by this module. Remember VTY ranges are cumulative: configure BOTH `0 4` and `5 15` (or `0 15`) so no high-numbered lines are left open.

Compliance: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 1.3.4, Cisco IOS Hardening Guide (restrict VTY transport), NIST 800-53 AC-17/SC-8, CWE-319 (Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information).
steps: Pre-check every VTY block, not just 0-4: `show running-config | section line vty` to see all ranges and current transport settings.
Identify the full VTY count on this platform (many IOS-XE devices expose `line vty 0 4` and `line vty 5 15`; some go higher) so none are missed.
Confirm SSH is fully working first (`show ip ssh`, MGMT-010/011) — you must not remove Telnet before SSH is proven, or you can lock yourself out.
Ensure you have a console/OOB fallback session open during the change.
Enter config mode: `configure terminal`.
Restrict the first range to SSH only: `line vty 0 4` then `transport input ssh`.
Optionally prevent the device initiating outbound sessions: `transport output none` (or `transport output ssh`).
Repeat for every additional range, e.g. `line vty 5 15` then `transport input ssh`.
If additional high ranges exist (e.g. `line vty 16 31`), apply `transport input ssh` there too.
While in each VTY block, confirm companion hardening is present: `access-class MGMT-VTY in` (MGMT-016) and `exec-timeout 10 0` (MGMT-017).
Return to EXEC: `end`.
From a management host open a NEW SSH session to confirm access still works after the restriction.
Confirm Telnet is now refused: from a client, `telnet <device> 23` should be rejected/refused.
Validate config: `show running-config | section line vty` shows `transport input ssh` on every block.
On a 9800 WLC, confirm CLI/WebUI management is unaffected.
Save: `copy running-config startup-config`.
Re-run the NSS scan to confirm MGMT-014 has cleared for all VTY sections.
MGMT-014.cli:
configure terminal
line vty 0 4
 transport input ssh
 transport output none
line vty 5 15
 transport input ssh
 transport output none
end
copy running-config startup-config
MGMT-014.verify: `show running-config | section line vty` shows `transport input ssh` under every VTY block (no block without it). A Telnet attempt to TCP/23 is refused, while SSH still connects.
MGMT-014.rollback: `configure terminal`, then under the affected block `line vty 0 4` set `transport input telnet ssh` (or `transport input all`), then `copy running-config startup-config`. Caveat: restoring Telnet re-enables cleartext CLI access, re-exposing admin credentials to sniffing/MITM.
MGMT-014.impact: Low risk if sequenced correctly, but potentially lockout-inducing if done wrong. Restricting to `transport input ssh` only affects NEW sessions; existing sessions persist. It is non-disruptive to the data plane and requires no reload. Danger: if SSH is not actually working and you remove Telnet, you lose remote CLI — mitigated by testing SSH first and keeping console access.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 1.3.4 (Set 'transport input ssh' for VTY)
Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — Limit VTY Access
CWE-319: Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information

MGMT-015.risk:
Within the same VTY loop, when a `transport input` line IS present but contains the keyword `telnet` or `all` (detection: `if "telnet" in t.lower() or "all" in t.lower()`), the scanner emits MGMT-015 (CRITICAL) with `{section}` set to the VTY block header and the offending line captured as an affected item. Applies to IOS, IOS-XE and Catalyst 9800 WLC. This is the explicit, confirmed case of Telnet being permitted (as opposed to MGMT-014's missing restriction).

Telnet is a plaintext protocol: there is no encryption, no integrity protection, and no host authentication. Every keystroke — including the login username, password, `enable secret` entry, and all configuration typed in the session — traverses the network in the clear. `transport input all` is even worse because it also permits Telnet (plus other transports like rlogin where supported).

Concrete attack scenario: an adversary who can passively observe the management segment (a SPAN tap, a compromised switch, or an ARP-spoofing foothold — T1040 Network Sniffing / T1557 Adversary-in-the-Middle) captures the full Telnet session and extracts the privileged credentials verbatim. No cracking is required. Those credentials are then replayed over SSH or Telnet for persistent privileged access (T1078 Valid Accounts), and the captured running-config reveals SNMP strings, keys, and ACLs for lateral movement.

Blast radius: complete and immediate compromise of the device's management plane the moment one Telnet session is observed. On a core router that is control of routing and filtering; on a 9800 WLC it is control of every AP and wireless client. Because credentials are frequently reused across the fleet, one captured Telnet session often compromises many devices.

Severity is CRITICAL — this is an active, trivially exploitable cleartext administrative channel, the highest-value target on the device.

Platform nuance: on IOS/IOS-XE/9800 the fix is `transport input ssh` under each VTY block. NX-OS uses `no feature telnet` (and `feature ssh`); ASA disables it by simply not configuring `telnet <ip> <mask> <intf>` — but the scanner does not evaluate those platforms. Apply the fix to ALL VTY ranges since they are pooled.

Compliance: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 1.3.4, Cisco IOS Hardening Guide (disable Telnet, use SSH), NIST 800-53 AC-17/SC-8, CWE-319 (Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information).
steps: Identify every affected block: `show running-config | section line vty` and note which ranges show `transport input telnet`, `transport input telnet ssh`, or `transport input all`.
Confirm SSH is configured and functional BEFORE removing Telnet: `show ip ssh` (must show v2 enabled) and confirm an RSA key exists (`show crypto key mypubkey rsa`).
If SSH is not yet working, first complete MGMT-010/011 remediation (set hostname/domain, generate RSA key, `ip ssh version 2`).
Open and keep a console/OOB session as a fallback in case of lockout.
Announce/schedule the change if any tooling still uses Telnet to this device (it must be migrated to SSH).
Enter config mode: `configure terminal`.
On the first range, override to SSH only: `line vty 0 4` then `transport input ssh`.
Repeat for every other range, e.g. `line vty 5 15` then `transport input ssh` (and any higher ranges present).
Optionally set `transport output none` to prevent the device originating Telnet sessions outbound.
While in each block, verify the access-class ACL (MGMT-016) and exec-timeout (MGMT-017) are present.
Return to EXEC: `end`.
From a management host, open a NEW SSH session to confirm access before disconnecting.
Confirm Telnet is now refused: `telnet <device>` from a client should fail/refuse on TCP/23.
Validate: `show running-config | section line vty` shows `transport input ssh` and no `telnet`/`all` on any block.
On a 9800 WLC, confirm SSH/WebUI management still works and AP join is unaffected.
Save: `copy running-config startup-config`.
If credentials may have been exposed over Telnet historically, rotate the enable secret and all local/TACACS+ passwords for this device.
Re-run the NSS scan to confirm MGMT-015 has cleared on every VTY section.
MGMT-015.cli:
configure terminal
line vty 0 4
 transport input ssh
 transport output none
line vty 5 15
 transport input ssh
 transport output none
end
copy running-config startup-config
MGMT-015.verify: `show running-config | section line vty` shows only `transport input ssh` (no `telnet`, no `all`) on every VTY block. A Telnet connection to TCP/23 is refused; SSH connects normally. `show line vty 0` shows the SSH transport for that line.
MGMT-015.rollback: `configure terminal`, then under the block `line vty 0 4` set `transport input telnet ssh`, then `copy running-config startup-config`. Caveat: this reinstates the cleartext Telnet channel — any observer on the path can capture admin credentials and the full session, which is exactly the CRITICAL exposure this check flags.
MGMT-015.impact: Sequencing-sensitive. Switching to `transport input ssh` affects only new sessions; existing sessions stay up and there is no reload or data-plane impact. If any operational tooling still relies on Telnet it WILL break and must be migrated to SSH first. Keep console access to avoid self-lockout.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 1.3.4 (Set 'transport input ssh' / disable Telnet)
Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — Disable Telnet, Use SSH
CWE-319: Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information

MGMT-016.risk:
For each VTY block from `get_vty_lines()`, the scanner checked whether any line contains `access-class` (detection: `any("access-class" in l.lower() for l in lines)` in check_vty_access_class). When none is found it emits MGMT-016 (HIGH), with `{section}` set to the exact VTY block header. Applies to IOS, IOS-XE and Catalyst 9800 WLC.

What is missing: an `access-class <ACL> in` statement binds a standard/extended ACL to the VTY lines so that only approved source addresses (typically a dedicated management subnet or jump-host range) may even open a CLI session. Without it, the SSH/Telnet listener is reachable from every source that can route to any of the device's IP addresses — including data-plane and, in poorly segmented networks, external interfaces.

Why it is a real risk: an exposed management port is the primary target for credential attacks. With no source restriction, any host can attempt authentication, enabling password spraying and brute forcing at scale (ATT&CK T1110 Brute Force) and scanning/enumeration of the management service (T1046 Network Service Discovery). It also broadens exposure to any authentication-bypass or SSH vulnerability in the running image — the device is only as safe as its patch level and password strength, with no network-layer gate in front.

Concrete scenario: a compromised user workstation on a flat network discovers the router's SSH port, launches a spray using a common credential list against the shared local account, succeeds, and gains privileged EXEC (T1078 Valid Accounts). An access-class permitting only the 10.10.250.0/24 management range would have dropped every one of those connections before authentication.

Blast radius: the entire management plane is reachable from a far larger population of hosts than necessary, dramatically increasing the attack surface and the log noise that hides a real intrusion. On a 9800 WLC this exposes wireless-infrastructure control to any reachable host.

Platform nuance: IOS/IOS-XE/9800 use a named or numbered ACL applied with `access-class <name|number> in` under the VTY block; you can add `vrf-also` if management is in a VRF. For IPv6 use `ipv6 access-class <name> in`. NX-OS restricts management via `ip access-list` on the mgmt interface / CoPP and ASA uses the `ssh <ip> <mask> <intf>` command set — neither is scanned here. Apply the access-class to ALL VTY ranges.

Compliance: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 1.3.5, Cisco IOS Hardening Guide (restrict management access with ACLs), NIST 800-53 AC-3/AC-17/SC-7, CWE-284 (Improper Access Control).
steps: Pre-check every VTY block for an existing binding: `show running-config | section line vty` and `show running-config | include access-class`.
Define the legitimate management sources (jump hosts / NOC subnet) and confirm they are stable, routable addresses.
Confirm you will be connecting FROM an address inside the planned permit range, or you will lock yourself out.
Keep a console/OOB session open as a fallback during the change.
Enter config mode: `configure terminal`.
Create a named standard ACL for management sources: `ip access-list standard MGMT-VTY`.
Permit the approved ranges: `permit 10.10.250.0 0.0.0.255` (repeat for each admin subnet/host, e.g. `permit host 10.10.250.10`).
End the ACL with an explicit deny for logging: `deny any log` (an implicit deny already exists).
Exit the ACL: `exit`.
Apply the ACL inbound to the first VTY range: `line vty 0 4` then `access-class MGMT-VTY in`.
Repeat for every other range: `line vty 5 15` then `access-class MGMT-VTY in` (and any higher ranges).
If management runs in a VRF and you need the ACL to also cover VRF sessions, use `access-class MGMT-VTY in vrf-also`.
For IPv6 management, additionally build an IPv6 ACL (`ipv6 access-list MGMT-VTY-V6`) and apply `ipv6 access-class MGMT-VTY-V6 in`.
Return to EXEC: `end`.
From a permitted management host, open a NEW SSH session to confirm access still works.
From a non-permitted address (if safely possible), confirm the connection is denied.
Validate: `show running-config | section line vty` shows `access-class MGMT-VTY in` on every block; `show ip access-lists MGMT-VTY` shows the permit entries and hit counts.
Save: `copy running-config startup-config` and re-run the NSS scan to confirm MGMT-016 cleared.
MGMT-016.cli:
configure terminal
ip access-list standard MGMT-VTY
 permit 10.10.250.0 0.0.0.255
 permit host 10.10.250.10
 deny any log
exit
line vty 0 4
 access-class MGMT-VTY in
line vty 5 15
 access-class MGMT-VTY in
end
copy running-config startup-config
MGMT-016.verify: `show running-config | section line vty` shows `access-class MGMT-VTY in` under every VTY block. `show ip access-lists MGMT-VTY` shows the permit lines. An SSH attempt from a non-permitted source is dropped; permitted sources connect normally.
MGMT-016.rollback: `configure terminal`, then under each block `line vty 0 4` issue `no access-class MGMT-VTY in`, then `copy running-config startup-config`. Caveat: removing the access-class re-exposes the SSH/Telnet listener to every host that can route to the device, restoring the broad brute-force/scan surface.
MGMT-016.impact: Moderate lockout risk if the permit range is wrong; otherwise non-disruptive. The ACL affects only new inbound management sessions (existing sessions persist) and has no data-plane/traffic impact and no reload. Critical caution: ensure your own admin source is inside the permit range and keep console access, because an incorrect ACL will block all remote CLI.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 1.3.5 (Set 'access-class' for VTY)
Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — Restrict Management Access with ACLs

MGMT-017.risk:
The scanner iterated over both `line vty` and `line con` sections (detection: `check_vty_exec_timeout` loops `["line vty", "line con"]` via `get_section`) and, for each block, checked whether any line contains `exec-timeout`. Where none exists it emits MGMT-017 (MEDIUM), with `{section}` being the exact block header (e.g. `line vty 0 4` or `line con 0`). Applies to IOS, IOS-XE and Catalyst 9800 WLC.

What is missing: `exec-timeout <min> <sec>` sets the idle timer that automatically logs out an inactive EXEC session. With no explicit `exec-timeout`, the line uses the platform default (historically 10 minutes on IOS, but not guaranteed and easy to have been overridden), and the benchmark requires it to be set explicitly and conservatively so idle sessions do not linger.

Why it is a real risk: an idle, authenticated session left on a screen, a jump host, or a terminal server is a ready-made privileged foothold. An attacker with physical or logical access to that terminal (an unlocked workstation, a shared bastion, a hijacked terminal-server line) inherits an already-authenticated privileged session with no need for credentials — a session-hijacking / valid-access technique (ATT&CK T1078 Valid Accounts; opportunistic post-compromise access). The console case is especially relevant: an unattended console in a wiring closet with a live EXEC session grants full control to anyone with brief physical access.

Blast radius: unauthorized privileged access to the device for as long as the stale session survives, which without a timeout is indefinite. On a 9800 WLC that is control of the wireless plane; on a router it is routing/ACL control. It also complicates accountability, since actions taken on a hijacked session are attributed to the original operator.

Severity is MEDIUM: exploitation requires access to an already-authenticated terminal rather than breaking authentication, but the consequence is full privileged control.

Platform nuance: IOS/IOS-XE/9800 all use `exec-timeout <min> <sec>` (e.g. `exec-timeout 10 0` for 10 minutes). Note the scanner evaluates `line vty` and `line con` here; the AUX line is handled separately (MGMT-020). Complement `exec-timeout` with the line-level `session-timeout` for outbound connections. NX-OS uses `exec-timeout <min>` under `line vty/console`; ASA uses `console timeout`/`ssh timeout` — not scanned by this module.

Compliance: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 1.3.6, Cisco IOS Hardening Guide (session timeouts), NIST 800-53 AC-11 (Device Lock) and AC-12 (Session Termination), CWE-613 (Insufficient Session Expiration).
steps: Pre-check all relevant blocks: `show running-config | section line vty` and `show running-config | section line con`.
Confirm which sections lack `exec-timeout` (the finding names the exact block).
Decide the idle policy: CIS recommends a short timeout; 10 minutes (`exec-timeout 10 0`) is a common operational balance, tighter (e.g. `exec-timeout 5 0`) for high-security environments.
Note any automation that holds long-lived idle CLI sessions so it can tolerate the timeout (most should reconnect).
Enter config mode: `configure terminal`.
Set the timeout on the console: `line con 0` then `exec-timeout 10 0`.
Set the timeout on the first VTY range: `line vty 0 4` then `exec-timeout 10 0`.
Repeat for every other VTY range, e.g. `line vty 5 15` then `exec-timeout 10 0`.
Avoid `exec-timeout 0 0`, which disables the timeout entirely and would trigger MGMT-018.
Optionally add an absolute session cap for outbound connections: `session-timeout 15` under the same lines where appropriate.
While in each block, confirm companion hardening (access-class, transport input) is present.
Return to EXEC: `end`.
Test the console timeout: leave a console session idle past the interval and confirm it logs out (or verify via `show line con 0`).
Test a VTY session: idle an SSH session and confirm it disconnects after the interval.
Validate config: `show running-config | section line` shows `exec-timeout 10 0` on con and every VTY block.
On a 9800 WLC, confirm the change does not interfere with any WebUI-driven session handling.
Save: `copy running-config startup-config`.
Re-run the NSS scan to confirm MGMT-017 has cleared for all named sections.
MGMT-017.cli:
configure terminal
line con 0
 exec-timeout 10 0
line vty 0 4
 exec-timeout 10 0
line vty 5 15
 exec-timeout 10 0
end
copy running-config startup-config
MGMT-017.verify: `show running-config | section line` shows `exec-timeout 10 0` under `line con 0` and every VTY block. `show line con 0` / `show line vty 0` list the idle timeout. An idle session actually disconnects after the configured interval.
MGMT-017.rollback: `configure terminal`, then under the block set `no exec-timeout` (reverts to default) or a longer value, then `copy running-config startup-config`. Caveat: removing or lengthening the timeout lets idle authenticated sessions persist, restoring the stale-session hijack risk — and `exec-timeout 0 0` disables it entirely (a separate HIGH, MGMT-018).
MGMT-017.impact: Low, but user-visible. It does not affect the data plane and needs no reload. Operators with genuinely idle sessions will be logged out after the interval and must reconnect; this is expected behaviour. Set the interval long enough to avoid disrupting legitimate long-running operations (e.g. staged config work).
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 1.3.6 (Set 'exec-timeout' on lines)
Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — EXEC Timeout

MGMT-018.risk:
When an `exec-timeout` line IS present on a `line vty` or `line con` block but is set to `exec-timeout 0 0`, the scanner emits MGMT-018 (HIGH). Detection: it regex-matches `re.match(r"exec-timeout\s+(\d+)\s+(\d+)", l)` and fires only when BOTH the minutes and seconds fields are exactly 0 (`int(m.group(1)) == 0 and int(m.group(2)) == 0`), with `{section}` set to the block header. Applies to IOS, IOS-XE and Catalyst 9800 WLC.

What it means: `exec-timeout 0 0` explicitly disables the idle logout on that line — the session will NEVER time out. This is worse than a missing `exec-timeout` (MGMT-017, MEDIUM) because it is an affirmative configuration choice to keep sessions open forever, which is why the scanner rates it HIGH. It is a common convenience setting left behind after a long maintenance activity.

Why it is a real risk: any authenticated EXEC session — console or VTY — stays live indefinitely with no idle disconnect. An attacker who reaches an unattended terminal (an unlocked operator workstation, a shared jump host with a detached SSH/tmux session, or a physical console in a comms room) inherits full privileged access without presenting credentials (ATT&CK T1078 Valid Accounts; session hijack of an already-authenticated context). Because the session never expires, the window of opportunity is unbounded rather than the minutes an idle timeout would allow.

Concrete scenario: an engineer opens an SSH session to a core switch from a bastion, gets pulled away, and the tmux/screen session persists for days; another user (or an attacker who compromised the bastion) reattaches and is dropped straight into privileged EXEC on the switch. On a console, an unlocked terminal in a data-hall rack yields the same outcome to anyone with brief physical access.

Blast radius: indefinite unauthorized privileged control of the device, plus loss of accountability (actions attributed to the original operator). On a 9800 WLC this is standing access to the wireless control plane; on a router, to routing and filtering.

Platform nuance: the fix on IOS/IOS-XE/9800 is to replace `exec-timeout 0 0` with a finite value such as `exec-timeout 10 0`. Note that `0 0` truly means never — do not confuse it with a small non-zero value. NX-OS `exec-timeout 0` and ASA `console timeout 0` are the equivalents on those platforms but are not scanned by this module.

Compliance: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 1.3.6, Cisco IOS Hardening Guide (session timeouts), NIST 800-53 AC-11/AC-12, CWE-613 (Insufficient Session Expiration).
steps: Pre-check which blocks carry the disabling value: `show running-config | include exec-timeout` and `show running-config | section line` — look for `exec-timeout 0 0`.
Confirm the finding's named section (e.g. `line vty 0 4` or `line con 0`) matches the offending line.
Check for active long-lived sessions before changing: `show users` / `show line` so you understand who will be subject to the new timeout.
Decide the replacement value: `exec-timeout 10 0` (10 minutes) is the standard hardened choice; use `exec-timeout 5 0` for higher-security lines.
Warn any operators who deliberately rely on non-expiring sessions (e.g. long console captures) so they adapt their workflow.
Enter config mode: `configure terminal`.
On the affected console block, replace the value: `line con 0` then `exec-timeout 10 0` (this overwrites the `0 0`).
On each affected VTY range, do the same: `line vty 0 4` then `exec-timeout 10 0`.
Repeat for other ranges such as `line vty 5 15` then `exec-timeout 10 0`.
Do NOT use `no exec-timeout` as the fix if a specific finite policy is required — set the explicit value instead, since `no exec-timeout` reverts to the platform default which may differ.
Confirm you are not leaving any other line at `0 0`: re-check with `show running-config | include exec-timeout`.
Return to EXEC: `end`.
Test: idle a VTY session past the interval and confirm it now disconnects; check `show line vty 0` reflects the finite timeout.
Validate: `show running-config | include exec-timeout` shows no `exec-timeout 0 0` remaining, only finite values.
On a 9800 WLC, confirm management sessions behave as expected with the new timeout.
Save: `copy running-config startup-config`.
Re-run the NSS scan to confirm MGMT-018 has cleared.
MGMT-018.cli:
configure terminal
line con 0
 exec-timeout 10 0
line vty 0 4
 exec-timeout 10 0
line vty 5 15
 exec-timeout 10 0
end
copy running-config startup-config
MGMT-018.verify: `show running-config | include exec-timeout` shows only finite values (e.g. `exec-timeout 10 0`) with no `exec-timeout 0 0`. `show line vty 0` / `show line con 0` list a non-zero idle timeout, and an idle session actually disconnects after the interval.
MGMT-018.rollback: `configure terminal`, then under the block re-apply `exec-timeout 0 0`, then `copy running-config startup-config`. Caveat: restoring `0 0` disables the idle logout entirely, letting an authenticated privileged session persist forever — the exact stale-session hijack exposure this HIGH finding flags.
MGMT-018.impact: Low technical impact, user-visible behaviour change. No reload and no data-plane effect. Sessions that were previously immortal will now be logged out when idle past the interval; anyone relying on never-expiring console/VTY sessions must change workflow. Choose an interval that does not interrupt legitimate long operations.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 1.3.6 (Set finite 'exec-timeout', not 0 0)
Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — EXEC Timeout

MGMT-019.risk:
The scanner calls `get_console_lines()`, which returns every config section whose name begins with `line con` (on virtually all IOS/IOS-XE devices this is a single section, `line con 0`; the full spelled-out form is `line console 0`). For each such section it inspects the sub-commands and raises this HIGH finding when NOT A SINGLE sub-command under the console stanza contains the substring `login` — meaning there is neither a bare `login` (line-password auth), a `login local` (local username/password auth), nor a `login authentication <list>` (AAA method-list auth). In that state the console EXEC is reachable with no credential challenge whatsoever.

This is a real, physical-access risk. An adversary with a console cable, a compromised terminal server, or access to an out-of-band console concentrator lands directly in user or privileged EXEC with no username or password prompt. This maps to ATT&CK T1078 (Valid/Default Accounts abuse via physical access) and T1556 for downstream credential manipulation. Once at the console, the attacker can enter the ROMMON password-recovery path or simply `enable` (if the enable secret is weak or absent), then exfiltrate the running-config, add a backdoor local user, alter ACLs, or pivot into the management VLAN.

Blast radius: the console is the trust anchor of the device. Console access typically survives network ACLs, VTY access-classes, and control-plane policing, so it bypasses every network-layer protection you built. On a distribution/core switch or a Catalyst 9800 WLC, that is full control of routing, SVIs, or the entire wireless domain including CAPWAP tunnels and client authentication.

Platform nuances: the check runs on IOS, IOS-XE, and Catalyst 9800 WLC (all IOS-XE syntax per SUPPORTED_PLATFORMS = ios/iosxe/wlc). On these, an unconfigured console still allows access. NX-OS, ASA, and FTD are out of scope for this module (NX-OS console is role/AAA gated by default; ASA uses different line semantics). Note the classic IOS trap: configuring `login local` with NO local username, or `login` with no `password` on the line, can lock you out of the console — always create the credential first.

Compliance: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 1.3.7 requires console authentication; NIST 800-53 AC-3/IA-2 (access enforcement / identification & authentication); CWE-306 Missing Authentication for a Critical Function.
steps: Pre-check the current console state: `show running-config | section line con` and confirm no `login`, `login local`, or `login authentication` line is present.
Verify you have a working credential to authenticate WITH before enforcing login — check `show running-config | include ^username` and `show running-config | include aaa authentication login`.
If using local auth and no admin user exists, create one first: `configure terminal` then `username netadmin privilege 15 secret <StrongSecret>` (Type-9 scrypt is used automatically with `secret`).
Confirm an enable secret exists so privileged mode is protected after login: `show running-config | include enable secret`; if absent, `enable secret <StrongSecret>`.
Decide the auth model: AAA method-list (preferred, centralized TACACS+) or `login local` (standalone). If AAA, confirm the list exists: `show running-config | include aaa authentication login`.
Enter config mode: `configure terminal`.
Select the console line: `line con 0` (the equivalent full form is `line console 0`; there is no second console line).
Apply authentication. Local model: `login local`. AAA model: `login authentication default` (or your named list, e.g. `login authentication CONSOLE_AUTH`).
Add an idle timeout so an authenticated-but-abandoned session closes: `exec-timeout 10 0`.
(Optional hardening) Bound the transport: `transport input none` if you never use reverse-telnet into the console, or leave default for pure console access.
Do NOT close your existing session yet. Open a SECOND session (e.g. another SSH/console) and test login end-to-end to avoid lockout.
From the test session, physically or logically re-connect to the console and confirm you are challenged for credentials and can authenticate.
If AAA is used, confirm a fallback exists in case the TACACS+ server is unreachable: the method list should end in `local` or `enable` (e.g. `aaa authentication login default group tacacs+ local`).
Once validated, exit line config: `end`.
Re-run the scanner-equivalent check: `show running-config | section line con` and confirm a `login...` directive is present.
Save the configuration: `copy running-config startup-config` (or `write memory`).
On Catalyst 9800 WLC, the syntax is identical (IOS-XE); apply the same steps and save with `write memory`.
Record the change in change control; console-auth changes are low-traffic-risk but carry lockout risk if credentials are wrong.
Schedule a follow-up to migrate standalone `login local` devices to centralized AAA/TACACS+ for consistent credential management.
Document the console credential in your privileged-access vault and rotate per policy.
MGMT-019.cli:
! IOS / IOS-XE / Catalyst 9800
configure terminal
username netadmin privilege 15 secret <StrongSecret>
enable secret <StrongEnableSecret>
!
line con 0
 login local
 exec-timeout 10 0
 logging synchronous
!
! --- OR the AAA/TACACS+ model instead of 'login local' ---
! aaa new-model
! aaa authentication login CONSOLE_AUTH group tacacs+ local
! line con 0
!  login authentication CONSOLE_AUTH
!  exec-timeout 10 0
!
end
copy running-config startup-config
MGMT-019.verify: `show running-config | section line con` must show `login local` (or `login authentication <list>`) under `line con 0`.
