Test by disconnecting/reconnecting the console: you should now receive a `Username:`/`Password:` prompt. `show users` confirms the authenticated session. For AAA: `test aaa group tacacs+ netadmin <pw> legacy` should return a PASS if the server is reachable.
MGMT-019.rollback: `configure terminal` then `line con 0` then `no login local` (or `no login authentication <list>`). Caveat: reverting removes all console authentication, so anyone with physical or terminal-server access to the console again gains credential-free EXEC access to the device.
MGMT-019.impact: Low network/traffic impact — no forwarding change and no reload. It does NOT drop VPN or data-plane sessions. The only risk is administrative lockout: if you enforce `login local` without a valid local user (or `login authentication` pointing at an unreachable AAA server with no `local`/`enable` fallback), you can be locked out and forced into ROMMON password recovery. Always validate from a second session before saving.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 1.3.7 (require console authentication / set 'login')
Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — Management Plane / Line Authentication
CWE-306: Missing Authentication for Critical Function

MGMT-020.risk:
The scanner locates the auxiliary line via `get_section("line aux")`. If a `line aux` stanza exists, it inspects the sub-commands and raises this MEDIUM finding when the port is NOT disabled — specifically when neither `no exec` NOR `transport input none` is present under the line. In that condition the auxiliary EXEC is still spawnable, so a device (modem, terminal server RJ-45, or a misused cable) attached to the AUX port can obtain an EXEC session.

The AUX port is a legacy out-of-band access vector. Historically it was wired to a dial-up modem for emergency access; today it is almost never used but frequently left enabled. If a modem or a reverse-telnet terminal server is connected, an attacker who dials in or reaches the terminal-server TCP port lands in an EXEC prompt, bypassing your VTY access-class and network ACLs entirely. This aligns with ATT&CK T1133 (External Remote Services) and T1078 (Valid Accounts) — an unmonitored back door into the control plane.

Even with no modem attached, an enabled AUX line inflates the attack surface and violates least-functionality hardening. A stray connection or an insider with physical access to the AUX RJ-45 gets a shell. Because the AUX line often inherits weak or no authentication (a separate concern from MGMT-019), the exposure compounds.

Blast radius: identical to console — full device control if the attacker can also defeat enable/AAA. On a core router or aggregation switch this is a routing/switching compromise; on a WLC it is control of the wireless service.

Platform nuances: this check runs on IOS, IOS-XE, and Catalyst 9800 WLC. Many modern IOS-XE fixed platforms and the 9800 have no physical AUX port at all, in which case the stanza may not appear and the finding will not fire (the check is guarded by `if aux:`); where a virtual/aux line exists it should still be disabled. NX-OS, ASA, and FTD are out of scope. Both accepted remediation states are recognized: `no exec` (denies EXEC on the line) or `transport input none` (permits no inbound transport) — configuring both is the belt-and-suspenders standard.

Compliance: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 1.3.8 (disable AUX EXEC); NIST 800-53 CM-7 (least functionality); CWE-1188 / CWE-284 (improper access control on an enabled interface).
steps: Confirm the AUX line exists and its state: `show running-config | section line aux` and `show line` (look at the AUX line's status).
Check whether anything is actually connected to AUX: `show line aux 0` — review modem/uart state; confirm it is not part of a documented OOB dial/console-server design.
If AUX IS used for legitimate OOB management, STOP — do not disable it; instead secure it with authentication and access controls and document the exception.
If AUX is unused (the common case), plan to disable it. This is non-disruptive to data traffic.
Enter config mode: `configure terminal`.
Select the AUX line: `line aux 0`.
Disable the EXEC on the line: `no exec`.
Deny all inbound transports on the line: `transport input none`.
Also deny outbound to prevent reverse connections: `transport output none`.
Set an idle timeout as defense-in-depth: `exec-timeout 0 1` (very short) or rely on `no exec`.
(Optional) If a modem was previously configured, clear it: `no modem inout` (removes the modem inout/dial capability from the line).
Return to privileged mode: `end`.
Verify the line no longer offers EXEC: `show line` should show the AUX line without an active EXEC-capable state.
Re-check config: `show running-config | section line aux` and confirm both `no exec` and `transport input none` are present.
If the platform reports no AUX port (`show line` lists none), note that the finding is not applicable to that hardware and record it as N/A.
Save the configuration: `copy running-config startup-config` (or `write memory`).
On Catalyst 9800 WLC / IOS-XE use identical syntax where an AUX line exists.
Update the device baseline/hardening template so new deployments ship with AUX disabled.
Log the change in change control (low risk, non-disruptive).
Re-run the audit to confirm MGMT-020 no longer fires.
MGMT-020.cli:
! IOS / IOS-XE / Catalyst 9800 — disable unused AUX port
configure terminal
line aux 0
 no exec
 transport input none
 transport output none
 exec-timeout 0 1
end
copy running-config startup-config
MGMT-020.verify: `show running-config | section line aux` must display both `no exec` and `transport input none`. `show line` should show the AUX (TTY) line with no EXEC session available. Attempting to reverse-telnet to the AUX line's rotary/port should be refused.
MGMT-020.rollback: `configure terminal` then `line aux 0` then `exec` and `transport input all` (or the specific transport you need). Caveat: reverting re-enables the auxiliary EXEC, re-exposing an out-of-band back door that bypasses VTY access-classes if a modem or terminal-server port is attached.
MGMT-020.impact: No data-plane or forwarding impact and no reload. It does NOT affect the console, VTY/SSH sessions, VPN tunnels, or client traffic. The only effect is that the AUX port can no longer be used for EXEC access — which is the intended outcome. Safe to apply in production during business hours unless AUX is part of a live OOB dial design.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 1.3.8 (Disable the AUX port)
Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — Disabling Unused Services / AUX Line

MGMT-021.risk:
The scanner raises this HIGH finding when the running-config contains a line matching `^ip http server` AND does NOT contain `^no ip http server`. This means the built-in cleartext HTTP management server is active, exposing the device's web UI and, on IOS/IOS-XE, the embedded web services over unencrypted TCP/80. The check is a simple presence test against the global line, matching the exact state the CLI reports.

Cleartext HTTP management is a serious credential-exposure risk. Every login to the web UI transmits the administrator username and password (or the HTTP Basic/enable credential) in plaintext across the wire. An attacker positioned for on-path interception (ATT&CK T1040 Network Sniffing, T1557 Adversary-in-the-Middle) captures those credentials and replays them (T1078 Valid Accounts) to gain full device administration. The HTTP server has also historically been the delivery surface for critical RCE and auth-bypass CVEs in IOS-XE (for example the 2023 IOS-XE Web UI implant campaign exploiting CVE-2023-20198 with CVE-2023-20273), so leaving it on TCP/80 materially increases exploitability.

Blast radius: the web server typically runs with privileged access to device configuration. Compromise yields running-config disclosure, credential theft, and configuration tampering — on a core device that is a network-wide compromise; on a Catalyst 9800 WLC it exposes the entire wireless management plane and the GUI used to administer thousands of APs and clients.

Platform nuances: this check runs on IOS, IOS-XE, and Catalyst 9800 WLC. The remediation is to disable HTTP and use HTTPS: `no ip http server` plus `ip http secure-server`. The Catalyst 9800 WLC GUI REQUIRES the HTTP/HTTPS server for management, so you must keep `ip http secure-server` (HTTPS) enabled there rather than turning web management off entirely. On pure routers/switches with no need for a web UI, disable BOTH the HTTP and HTTPS servers. Always restrict access with `ip http access-class` and prefer local/AAA auth via `ip http authentication`. NX-OS/ASA/FTD are out of scope for this module.

Compliance: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 1.4.1 (disable HTTP server) and related HTTPS/access-class items; NIST 800-53 SC-8 (transmission confidentiality) and AC-17; CWE-319 Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information.
steps: Determine whether web management is needed at all. Routers/switches administered only by CLI/NETCONF should disable both HTTP and HTTPS. A Catalyst 9800 WLC needs HTTPS for its GUI.
Inventory current state: `show running-config | include ip http` and `show ip http server status` (shows HTTP/HTTPS enabled state, ports, and active sessions).
Check who is currently connected via web: `show ip http server connection` and `show ip http server session-module`.
If no web UI is required (CLI-only device): plan to remove both servers.
Enter config mode: `configure terminal`.
Disable the cleartext HTTP server: `no ip http server`.
If you need a web UI (e.g. 9800 WLC), enable the encrypted server instead: `ip http secure-server`.
Build a management ACL to restrict who may reach the web server: `ip access-list standard MGMT_HTTPS` then `permit 10.10.0.0 0.0.255.255`.

Apply the ACL to the HTTP/HTTPS server. On classic IOS: `ip http access-class MGMT_HTTPS`. On IOS-XE the current form is `ip http access-class ipv4 MGMT_HTTPS` (an IPv6 variant `ip http access-class ipv6 <name>` also exists).
Force strong authentication for the web server: `ip http authentication aaa` (or `ip http authentication local`) — never leave it on enable-password auth.
On IOS-XE, tighten TLS by pinning a modern version: `ip http tls-version tlsv1.2`, and review `ip http secure-ciphersuite` to remove weak ciphers where supported.
If HTTPS is enabled and no trustpoint exists, ensure a certificate is available: the device auto-generates a self-signed cert, or bind a CA-signed one with `ip http secure-trustpoint <name>`.
Return to privileged mode: `end`.
Validate HTTP is off and HTTPS (if kept) is on: `show ip http server status`.
Test that TCP/80 is refused and TCP/443 responds (from a management host): browse to https://<device> and confirm the GUI loads over TLS.
Confirm your management ACL is effective — attempt access from a non-management subnet and verify it is denied.
Save the configuration: `copy running-config startup-config` (or `write memory`).
On the 9800 WLC, confirm GUI login still works over HTTPS after the change (do not disable secure-server there).
Add HTTP-server-disabled / HTTPS-restricted settings to the golden config template and record in change control (disabling HTTP can interrupt tooling that scrapes the HTTP UI).
Re-run the scanner to confirm MGMT-021 is cleared.
MGMT-021.cli:
! IOS / IOS-XE — disable cleartext HTTP, use restricted HTTPS
configure terminal
no ip http server
ip http secure-server
ip http authentication aaa
!
ip access-list standard MGMT_HTTPS
 permit 10.10.0.0 0.0.255.255
 deny   any log
exit
! classic IOS: ip http access-class MGMT_HTTPS
ip http access-class ipv4 MGMT_HTTPS
! (optional TLS hardening on IOS-XE)
ip http tls-version tlsv1.2
end
copy running-config startup-config

! NOTE: On a Catalyst 9800 WLC keep 'ip http secure-server' enabled
! (the GUI requires it). On CLI-only routers/switches you may also
! run 'no ip http secure-server' to disable web management entirely.
MGMT-021.verify: `show ip http server status` should report `HTTP server status: Disabled` and (if kept) `HTTP secure server status: Enabled` on port 443. From a management host, `curl -k https://<device>` succeeds while `curl http://<device>` is refused/times out. From a non-management subnet, access should be denied by the `ip http access-class`.
MGMT-021.rollback: `configure terminal` then `no ip http secure-server` and `ip http server` to restore the original cleartext state. Caveat: reverting re-enables plaintext HTTP management on TCP/80, re-exposing admin credentials to on-path sniffing and reopening the historically CVE-prone Web UI attack surface.
MGMT-021.impact: Disabling HTTP is non-disruptive to data traffic and requires no reload, but it WILL break any browser session or automation that manages the device over cleartext HTTP — including the Catalyst 9800 WLC GUI if you disable the secure-server there. Existing SSH/CLI, VPN, and forwarding are unaffected. Apply during a maintenance window if web-based tooling depends on it.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 1.4.1 (Disable the HTTP server) and HTTPS/access-class items
Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — Management Plane / HTTP(S) Server
Cisco IOS-XE Web UI advisories (CVE-2023-20198 / CVE-2023-20273)
NIST SP 800-53 Rev.5 SC-8 (Transmission Confidentiality), AC-17
CWE-319: Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information

MGMT-022.risk:
The scanner raises this MEDIUM finding when NONE of the three banner directives are present in the running-config: it tests for `^banner motd`, `^banner login`, and `^banner exec` and fires only if all three are absent. In other words, the device presents no legal/authorized-use notice to anyone connecting via console, VTY/SSH, or the web/GUI login. The check is a straightforward global-config presence test across those three banner types.

While a banner does not technically prevent access, its absence is a genuine security and legal control gap. A properly worded warning banner establishes that access is restricted to authorized users, that activity may be monitored and logged, and that unauthorized use will be prosecuted. Many jurisdictions and prosecutorial standards require an explicit no-expectation-of-privacy / authorized-use notice before monitoring evidence or unauthorized-access charges can be reliably used against an intruder. Without it, an organization's ability to pursue legal action after a compromise (ATT&CK phases from Initial Access through Impact) is materially weakened, and monitoring/consent posture is ambiguous.

From a defensive-standards perspective, the banner also removes ambiguity for legitimate users and is a near-universal audit item. The finding therefore reflects both a compliance deficiency and a forensic-readiness gap rather than a direct exploit path.

Blast radius: indirect — the risk materializes after an incident (weaker legal footing, disputed monitoring consent) rather than as a technical breach. It applies to every management entry point on the device.

Platform nuances: this check runs on IOS, IOS-XE, and Catalyst 9800 WLC, all of which use identical `banner {motd|login|exec} <delimiter>...<delimiter>` syntax. `banner login` displays before the username/password prompt (ideal for the legal warning); `banner motd` shows at connection to all lines; `banner exec` shows after successful authentication. IMPORTANT: do NOT include hostname, model, software version, ownership, or contact details in the banner — that leaks reconnaissance data (ATT&CK T1592). NX-OS/ASA/FTD are out of scope here.

Compliance: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 1.5.1 (configure a login/MOTD banner); NIST 800-53 AC-8 (System Use Notification); DISA Cisco IOS/IOS-XE STIG logon-banner requirement.
steps: Obtain your organization's officially approved legal banner text from Legal/Security — do not improvise wording, as the exact language affects enforceability.
Confirm the banner is absent: `show running-config | include ^banner` (expect no output).
Choose a delimiter character that does NOT appear in the banner text (commonly `^`, `#`, or `~`) — the delimiter marks start and end.
Ensure the banner text contains NO sensitive info (no hostname, model, IOS version, location, or owner name) to avoid handing recon to attackers.
Enter config mode: `configure terminal`.
Configure the pre-authentication legal warning: `banner login ^ ... approved text ... ^` (login banner shows before the credential prompt).
Optionally add a post-login banner for authorized users: `banner exec ^ ... internal-use notice ... ^`.
Optionally set an MOTD if operational messages are needed: `banner motd ^ ... ^` (keep generic).
On IOS-XE, if you use SSH, the login banner is presented to SSH clients as well; confirm `ip ssh` is configured so the banner displays pre-auth.
Return to privileged mode: `end`.
Verify the banner is stored: `show running-config | begin banner`.
Test the console: disconnect/reconnect and confirm the banner appears before the login prompt.
Test SSH from a management host and confirm the banner is displayed to the client.
On the Catalyst 9800 WLC, also confirm the GUI/CLI login shows the banner (same IOS-XE syntax applies).
If deploying at scale, template the exact approved banner text and push via your config-management tool for consistency.
Save the configuration: `copy running-config startup-config` (or `write memory`).
Record the approved banner version/date in your compliance evidence store (for CIS/AC-8 audits).
Set a review reminder so the banner text stays aligned with current legal guidance.
Communicate to operations that a new banner will now appear at login (avoids confusion).
Re-run the scanner to confirm MGMT-022 is cleared.
MGMT-022.cli:
! IOS / IOS-XE / Catalyst 9800 — approved login warning banner
configure terminal
banner login ^
***************************************************************
* UNAUTHORIZED ACCESS PROHIBITED. This system is restricted   *
* to authorized users for legitimate business purposes only.  *
* All activity is monitored and logged. Unauthorized or       *
* improper use may result in disciplinary action and civil    *
* or criminal prosecution. By continuing you consent to       *
* monitoring. Disconnect immediately if you are not an        *
* authorized user.                                            *
***************************************************************
^
banner exec ^
Authorized session established. Activity is logged.
^
end
copy running-config startup-config
MGMT-022.verify: `show running-config | include ^banner` should list `banner login` (and any others configured). Reconnect via console and SSH: the login banner must appear BEFORE the `Username:`/`Password:` prompt. On the 9800 WLC the same banner appears at CLI/GUI login.
MGMT-022.rollback: `configure terminal` then `no banner login` (and `no banner exec` / `no banner motd` as applicable). Caveat: removing the banner re-exposes the legal/forensic gap — you lose the authorized-use and monitoring-consent notice that supports prosecution and demonstrates AC-8 compliance.
MGMT-022.impact: Zero traffic or forwarding impact and no reload. Purely cosmetic to the login flow — a warning message now displays at console/SSH/GUI login. No sessions are dropped. Safe to apply any time; the only operational note is that users and automation expecting a banner-free login should be informed.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 1.5.1 (Configure a login/MOTD banner)
Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — Legal Notification Banners
NIST SP 800-53 Rev.5 AC-8 (System Use Notification)
DISA Cisco IOS/IOS-XE STIG — logon banner requirement

MGMT-023.risk:
The scanner raises this MEDIUM finding when the running-config does not contain a line matching `^login block-for`. That command is the front end of Cisco IOS Login Enhancements (Login Block / quiet period): it lets the device detect repeated failed login attempts and, once a threshold is crossed, enter a 'quiet mode' during which further login attempts on VTY/console are refused (or restricted to a permit ACL). Its absence means there is no native rate-limiting or lockout for brute-force authentication attempts against the device's management interfaces.

Without login-block protection, an attacker can hammer SSH (or Telnet, if enabled) with high-rate credential guessing. This is textbook ATT&CK T1110 (Brute Force) and T1110.001 (Password Guessing) / T1110.003 (Password Spraying). Because IOS by default allows rapid successive login attempts, an automated tool (e.g. Hydra/Medusa against SSH) can test thousands of credentials, especially dangerous when combined with weak local passwords or reused credentials. It also enables a management-plane DoS: unrestricted attempts consume VTY slots and CPU on the login process.

Blast radius: successful credential guessing yields device administration (routing/switching or, on a WLC, the wireless control plane). Even unsuccessful, sustained brute force can exhaust VTY lines and degrade legitimate admin access.

Platform nuances: this check runs on IOS, IOS-XE, and Catalyst 9800 WLC — all support `login block-for <sec> attempts <n> within <sec>`. You SHOULD pair it with a `login quiet-mode access-class <ACL>` so your own management stations are never locked out during an attack, and with `login delay <sec>` to slow attempts. Note: login-block only engages after the failure threshold; it complements, but does not replace, a VTY `access-class` that restricts WHO can even reach SSH. NX-OS uses different native mechanisms and ASA/FTD are out of scope for this module. Best practice is also to disable Telnet (`transport input ssh`) so brute force is only possible over encrypted SSH.

Compliance: Cisco IOS Hardening Guide — Login Enhancements; CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark login-block section; NIST 800-53 AC-7 (Unsuccessful Logon Attempts); CWE-307 Improper Restriction of Excessive Authentication Attempts.
steps: Confirm the feature is absent: `show running-config | include login block-for` (expect no output) and `show login` (state should be normal / not configured).
Build a permit ACL of your legitimate management source subnets so admins are exempt during quiet mode: `ip access-list standard MGMT_HOSTS` then `permit 10.10.0.0 0.0.255.255`.
Decide thresholds. A sane starting policy: block for 120s after 3 failed attempts within 60s (`login block-for 120 attempts 3 within 60`).
Enter config mode: `configure terminal`.
Enable login-block with your thresholds: `login block-for 120 attempts 3 within 60`.
Exempt your management hosts from quiet mode so an attacker cannot lock YOU out: `login quiet-mode access-class MGMT_HOSTS`.
Add a per-attempt delay to slow automated guessing: `login delay 3`.
(Recommended companion) Log the events so lockouts are visible: `login on-failure log` and `login on-success log` (see MGMT-024).
Ensure SSH-only transport on VTY so brute force cannot occur over cleartext Telnet: `line vty 0 15` then `transport input ssh`.
Ensure a restrictive VTY access-class is also present (defense in depth): `access-class MGMT_HOSTS in` under the VTY lines.
Return to privileged mode: `end`.
Verify configuration and current state: `show login` should report the block-for parameters and whether it is in Normal or Quiet-Mode.
Test carefully from a NON-exempt host: make 3 rapid bad logins and confirm the device enters quiet mode and refuses further attempts for the block duration.
Confirm an exempt management host can STILL log in during quiet mode (validates the quiet-mode access-class).
Check logging output for `SEC_LOGIN` messages: `show logging | include SEC_LOGIN`.
Save the configuration: `copy running-config startup-config` (or `write memory`).
On the Catalyst 9800 WLC apply identical IOS-XE syntax and save with `write memory`.
Tune thresholds if legitimate users trip quiet mode too easily (e.g. raise attempts or shorten block-for).
Add the login-block policy to the golden config template.
Document in change control and re-run the scanner to confirm MGMT-023 is cleared.
MGMT-023.cli:
! IOS / IOS-XE / Catalyst 9800 — brute-force login protection
configure terminal
ip access-list standard MGMT_HOSTS
 permit 10.10.0.0 0.0.255.255
exit
!
login block-for 120 attempts 3 within 60
login quiet-mode access-class MGMT_HOSTS
login delay 3
login on-failure log
login on-success log
!
line vty 0 15
 transport input ssh
 access-class MGMT_HOSTS in
end
copy running-config startup-config
MGMT-023.verify: `show login` should display the configured watch period, block duration, and attempt threshold (e.g. 'If more than 3 login failures occur in 60 seconds, logins will be disabled for 120 seconds') and the current mode (Normal / Quiet). During a simulated attack, `show login` flips to Quiet-Mode. `show logging | include SEC_LOGIN` shows QUIET_START/QUIET_STOP events.
MGMT-023.rollback: `configure terminal` then `no login block-for` (which also removes the dependent `login quiet-mode access-class` and `login delay`). Caveat: reverting removes native brute-force rate-limiting, re-exposing the management plane to unthrottled password guessing/spraying and VTY-exhaustion DoS.
MGMT-023.impact: No data-plane impact and no reload. It affects only the management login path. Risk to be aware of: if the quiet-mode access-class is misconfigured or omitted, an attacker triggering quiet mode can also lock out legitimate admins for the block duration — hence always configure `login quiet-mode access-class`. Existing established SSH sessions are not dropped; only NEW logins are throttled once the threshold trips.
references: Cisco IOS Login Enhancements (Login Block / Quiet Period) configuration guide
CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark — Login block-for / failed-login rate limiting
NIST SP 800-53 Rev.5 AC-7 (Unsuccessful Logon Attempts)
CWE-307: Improper Restriction of Excessive Authentication Attempts

MGMT-024.risk:
The scanner raises this LOW finding when the running-config does not contain a line matching `^login on-failure log`. That command instructs IOS/IOS-XE to generate a syslog message (`%SEC_LOGIN-4-LOGIN_FAILED`) every time an authentication attempt on the console/VTY fails. Its absence means failed administrative logins are not being recorded, creating a detection and forensic blind spot on the management plane. The remediation the scanner recommends also adds `login on-success log` so successful logins are captured for a complete audit trail. Note the check is evaluated inside `check_login_failure_rate`, alongside MGMT-023.

Failed-login logging is a foundational detection control. Without it, brute-force and password-spraying activity (ATT&CK T1110) proceeds invisibly — your SIEM never sees the tell-tale burst of failed logins, and post-incident investigators cannot reconstruct who attempted access, from where, and when. Pairing failure and success logging lets analysts correlate a spray followed by an eventual successful compromise (T1078 Valid Accounts), which is one of the highest-signal indicators of account takeover on network gear.

Blast radius: indirect but important. The gap does not itself grant access, but it removes the primary evidence source used to detect and respond to management-plane attacks. Combined with missing brute-force protection (MGMT-023), an attacker can guess credentials both unthrottled AND unobserved.

Platform nuances: this check runs on IOS, IOS-XE, and Catalyst 9800 WLC, all of which support `login on-failure log` and `login on-success log`. To be useful, the messages must actually reach a collector, so ensure `logging host <syslog-ip>`, an appropriate `logging trap` level (informational/debugging captures SEC_LOGIN), timestamps (`service timestamps log datetime msec localtime show-timezone`), and accurate NTP-synced time are configured. You can throttle noise with the `every <n>` option (e.g. `login on-success log every 10`). NX-OS/ASA/FTD are out of scope for this module.

Compliance: Cisco IOS Hardening Guide — Login logging; CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark logging section; NIST 800-53 AU-2 / AU-3 / AC-7 (audit events, content, and unsuccessful-logon handling); CWE-778 Insufficient Logging.
steps: Confirm the feature is absent: `show running-config | include login on-` (expect no output).
Verify a syslog destination and time source already exist so logs are actually collected: `show running-config | include logging host` and `show ntp status`.
If no syslog server is configured, add one first: `logging host <syslog-ip>` and set `logging trap informational`.
Ensure accurate timestamps for forensic correlation: `service timestamps log datetime msec localtime show-timezone`.
Enter config mode: `configure terminal`.
Enable failed-login logging: `login on-failure log`.
Enable successful-login logging for a full audit trail: `login on-success log`.
(Optional) Reduce success-log volume on busy devices: `login on-success log every 10` to log every 10th success while still logging every failure.
Confirm buffered logging is on to retain events locally: `logging buffered 64000 informational`.
Return to privileged mode: `end`.
Verify configuration: `show running-config | include login on-`.
Generate a test failure: attempt a login with a wrong password from a test host.
Confirm the event was logged locally: `show logging | include SEC_LOGIN` (expect a `LOGIN_FAILED` message with source IP and timestamp).
Confirm the message reached the syslog server / SIEM and is parsed correctly.
Generate a successful login and confirm a `LOGIN_SUCCESS` message is recorded (unless suppressed by the `every` option).
Save the configuration: `copy running-config startup-config` (or `write memory`).
On the Catalyst 9800 WLC apply the same IOS-XE syntax and save with `write memory`.
Build/verify a SIEM detection rule that alerts on failed-login bursts (ties into brute-force detection).
Add login-logging directives to the golden config template.
Record in change control (non-disruptive) and re-run the scanner to confirm MGMT-024 is cleared.
MGMT-024.cli:
! IOS / IOS-XE / Catalyst 9800 — failed & successful login logging
configure terminal
service timestamps log datetime msec localtime show-timezone
logging buffered 64000 informational
logging host 10.10.20.5
logging trap informational
!
login on-failure log
login on-success log
! (optional) login on-success log every 10
end
copy running-config startup-config
MGMT-024.verify: `show running-config | include login on-` shows `login on-failure log` and `login on-success log`. After a deliberate bad login, `show logging | include SEC_LOGIN` displays a `%SEC_LOGIN-4-LOGIN_FAILED` entry with the source IP, line, and timestamp; a good login produces `%SEC_LOGIN-5-LOGIN_SUCCESS`. Confirm the same events appear on the syslog/SIEM collector.
MGMT-024.rollback: `configure terminal` then `no login on-failure log` and `no login on-success log`. Caveat: reverting stops recording authentication attempts, restoring the management-plane detection/forensic blind spot — brute-force and successful-compromise events will no longer be logged or alertable.
MGMT-024.impact: Negligible operational impact and no reload. Data-plane and existing sessions are unaffected. The only consideration is log volume: on devices with very frequent administrative logins, `login on-success log` can add syslog traffic — use the `every <n>` qualifier to throttle. Failed-login logging volume is normally low and highly valuable.
references: Cisco IOS Login Enhancements — 'login on-failure' / 'login on-success' logging
CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark — Logging of failed login attempts
NIST SP 800-53 Rev.5 AU-2, AU-3, AC-7

MGMT-025.risk:
The scanner uses `find_lines(r"username\s+\S+\s+privilege\s+15")` to count local user accounts defined with full administrative rights, and raises this MEDIUM finding when MORE THAN THREE such accounts exist (the finding title includes the actual count, and up to ten sanitized matching lines are attached as affected_items). Passwords/secrets in the matched lines are sanitized with a regex before being reported. A large population of privilege-15 local accounts indicates that day-to-day administration relies on many individually provisioned superuser identities stored on the device itself, rather than on centralized, role-limited authorization.

This is an excessive-privilege and credential-sprawl problem. Every privilege-15 local account is a full-control credential that (a) lives in the device config, (b) must be individually rotated/deprovisioned, and (c) grants unrestricted command access with no per-command authorization. It violates least privilege (ATT&CK-relevant: T1078 Valid Accounts, T1136 Create Account for persistence, T1098 Account Manipulation). Local accounts are a favorite persistence mechanism — an attacker who gains one config write can quietly add another `privilege 15` user that survives AAA outages, and the more legitimate level-15 users exist, the easier such a rogue account hides. Orphaned accounts for departed staff are also common in this pattern.

Blast radius: each account is a full compromise vector for the device; on a core/distribution switch or a Catalyst 9800 WLC that is control of the entire routing/switching or wireless domain. Local-account sprawl also breaks accountability — shared or numerous local logins make attributing actions to an individual difficult during incident response.

Platform nuances: this check runs on IOS, IOS-XE, and Catalyst 9800 WLC. The strategic fix is to move authentication and, crucially, command AUTHORIZATION to TACACS+ (AAA), which supports per-command and per-privilege-level authorization tied to individual identities and central deprovisioning — reducing local level-15 accounts to a small break-glass set (typically one or two). Configure `aaa new-model`, `aaa authentication login`, and `aaa authorization exec`/`commands`, keep a single emergency `local` fallback account, and use TACACS+ command sets for granular roles. NX-OS (role-based RBAC) and ASA/FTD are out of scope for this module.

Compliance: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 1.2.6 (privilege-level / least-privilege) and AAA sections; NIST 800-53 AC-6 (Least Privilege), AC-2 (Account Management), IA-2; CWE-250 Execution with Unnecessary Privileges / CWE-269 Improper Privilege Management.
steps: Inventory all level-15 local accounts: `show running-config | include privilege 15` and note how many and who they belong to.
Cross-reference each account against HR/asset records to identify orphaned or shared accounts for removal.
Confirm whether centralized AAA/TACACS+ (e.g. Cisco ISE) is available; if so, plan migration of authentication and authorization to it.
Verify TACACS+ reachability and shared-secret before cutover: `test aaa group tacacs+ <user> <pw> legacy`.
Enter config mode: `configure terminal`.
Enable AAA if not already: `aaa new-model`.
Define the TACACS+ server (IOS-XE): `tacacs server ISE1` then `address ipv4 10.10.20.10` then `key 0 <shared-secret>`; group them with `aaa group server tacacs+ ISE_GRP` then `server name ISE1`.
Configure login authentication with local fallback: `aaa authentication login default group ISE_GRP local`.
Configure EXEC and command authorization for granular control: `aaa authorization exec default group ISE_GRP local` and `aaa authorization commands 15 default group ISE_GRP local`.
Configure accounting for attribution: `aaa accounting commands 15 default start-stop group ISE_GRP`.
Create/keep exactly ONE local break-glass level-15 account with a vaulted strong secret: `username breakglass privilege 15 secret <StrongSecret>`.
Validate from a SECOND session that TACACS+ login works and that command authorization behaves as expected BEFORE removing any local accounts (avoid lockout).
Remove surplus/orphaned level-15 local accounts one at a time: `no username <olduser>` — verifying continued access after each.
For local users that must remain but need less power, lower their privilege or scope them via TACACS+ command sets rather than level 15.
Ensure a fallback path exists if TACACS+ is unreachable — confirm the `local` keyword is on the method lists and the break-glass account works.
Return to privileged mode: `end`.
Re-count level-15 local users: `show running-config | include privilege 15` — target is 3 or fewer (ideally 1 break-glass).
Save the configuration: `copy running-config startup-config` (or `write memory`).
On the Catalyst 9800 WLC apply identical IOS-XE AAA syntax and save with `write memory`.
Document the account-reduction and AAA cutover in change control; store the break-glass credential in the privileged-access vault and re-run the scanner to confirm MGMT-025 is cleared.
MGMT-025.cli:
! IOS-XE — centralize auth/authz on TACACS+, keep one break-glass
configure terminal
aaa new-model
tacacs server ISE1
 address ipv4 10.10.20.10
 key 0 <SharedSecret>
exit
aaa group server tacacs+ ISE_GRP
 server name ISE1
exit
aaa authentication login default group ISE_GRP local
aaa authorization exec default group ISE_GRP local
aaa authorization commands 15 default group ISE_GRP local
aaa accounting commands 15 default start-stop group ISE_GRP
!
! single vaulted break-glass account
username breakglass privilege 15 secret <StrongSecret>
!
! remove surplus level-15 locals (one at a time, after testing)
no username olduser1
no username olduser2
end
copy running-config startup-config
MGMT-025.verify: `show running-config | include privilege 15` should return 3 or fewer local users (ideally just the break-glass account). `test aaa group tacacs+ <user> <pw> legacy` returns a PASS. Log in as a TACACS+ user and confirm command authorization is enforced per role; `show tacacs` confirms server reachability and `show aaa sessions` shows AAA-authenticated logins.
MGMT-025.rollback: Re-add the removed local accounts (`username <name> privilege 15 secret <pw>`) and, if needed, `no aaa authorization commands 15 default group ISE_GRP local` / revert to `aaa authentication login default local` (or `no aaa new-model` to disable AAA entirely). Caveat: reverting restores local privilege-15 sprawl and removes centralized per-command authorization/accountability, re-creating the persistence and least-privilege exposure.
MGMT-025.impact: Potentially disruptive if mishandled — enabling AAA and command authorization changes how EVERY admin login is validated, and a wrong method list or unreachable TACACS+ with no `local` fallback can lock out all administrators. No data-plane/forwarding impact and no reload. Perform during a maintenance window, always keep a validated `local` fallback and a working break-glass account, and test from a second session before removing any local users. Existing sessions are not dropped by the config change itself.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 1.2.6 (privilege levels) and AAA authorization sections
Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — AAA / TACACS+ Command Authorization
NIST SP 800-53 Rev.5 AC-6 (Least Privilege), AC-2 (Account Management), IA-2
CWE-250 / CWE-269: Excessive / Improper Privilege Management

NGFW-001.risk:
The scanner matched a line resembling `default ... action ... allow` in the supplied configuration and raised NGFW-001 at CRITICAL. In `NgfwCoreAuditor.check_default_action()` this maps to `has_allow_default = self.config.has_line(r"default.*action.*allow")`. On Cisco Secure Firewall Threat Defense (FTD) the Access Control Policy has exactly one default action that governs every packet that does not match an explicit rule; the offending values are "Trust All Traffic" and "Permit"/"Allow All Traffic". This check runs on the ftd and asa families (class SUPPORTED_PLATFORMS = {"ftd","asa"}).

A default action of Allow inverts the fundamental firewall security model. Instead of default-deny (deny-by-default, allow-by-exception), the device forwards anything an administrator forgot to explicitly deny. Every future service, host, or subnet is reachable the moment it appears on the wire, with no change-control gate.

The concrete attack scenario: an adversary who has gained a foothold (or is scanning from the outside) performs lateral movement and C2 egress over ports/protocols nobody wrote a rule for. This aligns with MITRE ATT&CK T1071 (Application Layer Protocol) and T1048 (Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol) — the traffic simply rides the permissive default. "Trust All Traffic" is worse still: trusted flows bypass Snort entirely, so IPS, AMP/file, and URL inspection never run on them even if configured.

Blast radius is the entire trust zone pair the policy governs — typically inside-to-outside and, in flat designs, inside-to-inside. A single permissive default can expose the whole enterprise egress and any inter-zone path the appliance sits on.

Platform nuance: on FTD this is an FMC/FDM policy object, not visible in the LINA `show running-config`; it is confirmed with `show access-control-config`. On classic ASA there is no literal "default action allow" directive — each ACL ends in an implicit deny — so a NGFW-001 hit on an ASA text export is almost always a false-positive from a comment/description or an FTD export mislabeled; still worth verifying. The standards angle: default-deny is required by NIST 800-53 AC-4/SC-7 and is the core intent of the NSA/CISA Network Infrastructure Security Guide boundary-protection guidance.
steps: Identify the platform and manager: on FTD CLI run `show managers` to confirm FMC vs FDM (on-box) vs cdFMC management; the remediation surface differs.
Inventory the current default action from the device data plane: `show access-control-config | begin Default Action` and record whether it reads Allow/Trust or Block.
Confirm which access control policy is deployed to this device in FMC: Policies > Access Control, note the policy name mapped to the device under Devices > Device Management.
Open a change window — flipping the default from Allow to Block is potentially service-affecting because any traffic that relied on the permissive default will drop.

Before flipping, build a baseline of what the default was silently allowing: temporarily enable logging on the default action (Access Control Policy > Default Action gearwheel/Logging > `Log at End of Connection`, send to FMC/syslog) and let it run to capture flows.
Review the connection events (Analysis > Connections) filtered on `Action: Allow` / rule = Default Action to enumerate legitimate flows that need explicit permit rules.
Create explicit allow rules for each required flow with least-privilege source/destination/port before changing the default, so approved traffic survives the cutover.
In FMC set the default action: Policies > Access Control > [policy] > Default Action drop-down > choose `Block All Traffic` (or `Block with reset` for TCP RST behavior). Avoid `Trust All Traffic`.
Attach an intrusion policy to the default action where supported (Default Action gearwheel) so residual traffic is still inspected during tuning — pairs with NGFW-003.
Ensure the default action logging remains enabled (`Log at Beginning`/`Log at End of Connection`) so denied-by-default traffic is auditable — pairs with NGFW-008.
For FDM-managed (on-box) FTD: Policies > Access Control > default action gear icon at the bottom of the rule table > set to Block; there is no FMC.

For an ASA false-positive: verify with `show run access-group` and `show access-list` that each interface ACL ends in the intended `deny ip any any log` and add an explicit `deny ip any any log` as the last ACE if you want denies logged.

Save/deploy the change: in FMC click Deploy > select the device > Deploy; on FDM click the Deploy icon > Deploy Now. (FMC-managed devices persist the deployed config automatically; on ASA save with `write memory`.)
On the FTD data plane confirm the deployed policy: `show access-control-config | include Default Action` should now show Block.
Monitor Analysis > Connections for a spike of newly-blocked flows and reconcile against your explicit allow rules; add any missed legitimate flow as a scoped rule.
Update the deployment/rollback record and, if using the FMC REST API for IaC, capture the policy `defaultAction` object via `GET /api/fmc_config/v1/domain/{domainUUID}/policy/accesspolicies/{id}`.
Schedule a follow-up review to remove the temporary broad rules created during migration and tighten to final least-privilege.
Document the change in change-control and confirm startup persistence is automatic for FMC-managed devices (deployed config is persisted on the device).
NGFW-001.cli:
! ==== Cisco Secure Firewall Threat Defense (FMC-managed) ====
! Default action is an FMC policy object, NOT in LINA running-config.
! GUI: Policies > Access Control > <policy> > Default Action > "Block All Traffic"
!      Default Action gear > Logging > Log at End of Connection (FMC + syslog)
!      Default Action gear > Intrusion Policy > Balanced Security and Connectivity
! Then: Deploy > <device> > Deploy Now
!
! Representative FMC REST API (set default action to BLOCK):
! curl -k -X PUT \
!   https://<fmc>/api/fmc_config/v1/domain/{domainUUID}/policy/accesspolicies/{policyId} \
!   -H "X-auth-access-token: <token>" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
!   -d '{ "id":"{policyId}", "name":"<policy>",
!         "defaultAction": { "action": "BLOCK",
!           "logBegin": false, "logEnd": true, "sendEventsToFMC": true } }'
!
! ==== Classic ASA (NGFW-001 usually a false positive here) ====
! ASA has an implicit deny; make it explicit + logged as the last ACE:
access-list OUTSIDE_IN extended deny ip any any log 6 interval 300
access-group OUTSIDE_IN in interface outside
write memory
NGFW-001.verify: FTD: `show access-control-config | begin Default Action` must show `Default Action: Block All Traffic` (not Allow/Trust) and, if configured, an Intrusion Policy and `Logging Configuration: ... Log at End`. ASA: `show run access-group` plus `show access-list OUTSIDE_IN | include deny ip any any` shows the explicit trailing deny with hit counts.
NGFW-001.rollback: FTD: in FMC set Default Action back to Allow/Trust and redeploy (or PUT `"action":"PERMIT"`); ASA: `no access-list OUTSIDE_IN extended deny ip any any log`. Caveat: reverting restores default-permit, re-exposing every service that lacks an explicit allow rule and (for Trust) bypassing IPS/AMP/URL inspection on that traffic.
NGFW-001.impact: Disruptive/service-affecting. Changing the default from Allow to Block drops any traffic not matched by an explicit rule at the moment of deployment; sessions relying on the permissive default are cut. FMC deployment briefly re-evaluates the policy (Snort restart is only triggered by certain changes, not a plain default-action edit) and does not require a reload. No admin/VPN session impact from the change itself, but egress/inter-zone user traffic can break if allow rules are incomplete — do it in a window.
references: NSA/CISA Network Infrastructure Security Guide — Filtering and boundary protection (default-deny)
Cisco Secure Firewall Management Center Device Configuration Guide — Access Control Policy Default Action
NIST SP 800-53 Rev.5 AC-4 (Information Flow Enforcement), SC-7 (Boundary Protection)
CWE-1188 Insecure Default Initialization; CWE-276 Incorrect Default Permissions

NGFW-002.risk:
NGFW-002 (HIGH) fires only for FTD (`self.config.device_type == "ftd"`) and only when the scanner can find NEITHER a deny/block default NOR an allow default in the text — i.e. `has_deny_default` and `has_allow_default` are both false. The logic is a deliberate fail-safe: rather than assume the firewall is secure, the auditor flags that the single most important control (the Access Control Policy default action) could not be positively verified from the provided configuration and demands manual confirmation in FMC.

This condition is common and expected for FTD because the Access Control Policy — including its default action — is authored and stored in Firepower Management Center (or FDM for on-box management) and is NOT present in the LINA `show running-config`. An operator who exports only `show running-config` will legitimately trigger NGFW-002 even on a well-configured firewall. The finding therefore reflects insufficient evidence, not a proven misconfiguration.

The security risk of leaving it unverified is real: if the default action actually is Allow or Trust (see NGFW-001), the firewall silently forwards unmatched traffic, enabling C2 egress and lateral movement (MITRE ATT&CK T1071/T1048) with no rule ever logging it. An unknown default is an unmanaged risk — you cannot attest to boundary protection you have not confirmed.

Blast radius: the entire zone-pair the policy governs, identical to NGFW-001, because the default action is the catch-all for every non-matching packet.

Platform nuance: on ASA the default is the implicit per-ACL deny and is provable from `show run access-group`/`show access-list`; NGFW-002 is scoped to FTD precisely because FTD's default lives off-box. The remediation is to obtain authoritative evidence — either the FTD data-plane view via `show access-control-config`, or the FMC GUI/REST API — and to set the default to Block. Standards: NIST 800-53 AC-4/SC-7 and CA-2/CA-7 (assessment/continuous monitoring) require that boundary controls be verifiable, not merely assumed.
steps: Determine management mode first: `show managers` on the FTD CLI (FMC, FDM/local, or cdFMC) — this dictates where the default action is authored and how you verify it.
Pull authoritative evidence from the data plane: `show access-control-config | begin Default Action` — this reflects what is actually deployed regardless of manager.
If the export you scanned was only `show running-config`, re-collect with `show access-control-config` (and optionally `show running-config all`) so the audit input actually contains the policy.
In FMC confirm the deployed policy name: Devices > Device Management > [device] > note the Access Control Policy assigned.
Open that policy: Policies > Access Control > [policy] and read the Default Action drop-down at the bottom of the rule table.
If the default reads Allow or Trust, treat as NGFW-001 and change it to `Block All Traffic` (schedule a change window as it is service-affecting).
If the default is already Block, capture the evidence (screenshot or REST GET) and record it against NGFW-002 to close the finding as verified-compliant.
Enable logging on the default action: Default Action gear icon > Logging > `Log at End of Connection`, send to FMC and syslog, so catch-all traffic is auditable.
Attach an intrusion policy to the default action so residual traffic is still inspected (links to NGFW-003).
For FDM-managed FTD: Policies > Access Control > default action gear at the bottom of the table > confirm/ set Block.
For programmatic verification, call the FMC REST API `GET /api/fmc_config/v1/domain/{domainUUID}/policy/accesspolicies/{id}` and read the `defaultAction.action` field (expect `BLOCK`).
If any change was made, deploy it: FMC Deploy > [device] > Deploy Now (FDM: Deploy icon > Deploy Now).
Re-run `show access-control-config | include Default Action` on the FTD and confirm it now shows Block.
Establish a repeatable evidence-collection step so future audits ingest `show access-control-config`, not just `show running-config`, eliminating the false-unknown.
Record the verified state and evidence source in your assessment artifact for CA-2/CA-7 traceability.
Add the default-action check to your deployment/IaC validation (REST assertion `defaultAction.action == BLOCK`) so drift is caught automatically.
NGFW-002.cli:
! FTD default action lives in FMC/FDM, not in LINA running-config.
! Step 1 - collect authoritative data-plane evidence:
!   show access-control-config | begin Default Action
!
! Step 2 - if unverified/incorrect, set BLOCK in FMC:
!   GUI: Policies > Access Control > <policy> > Default Action = "Block All Traffic"
!   Deploy > <device> > Deploy Now
!
! Representative FMC REST API read/verify:
! curl -k -H "X-auth-access-token: <token>" \
!   https://<fmc>/api/fmc_config/v1/domain/{domainUUID}/policy/accesspolicies/{policyId} \
!   | grep -i defaultAction    # expect "action":"BLOCK"
NGFW-002.verify: `show access-control-config | begin Default Action` returns a definitive `Default Action: Block All Traffic` line (evidence now present, finding resolvable). Programmatically, FMC REST GET on the access policy returns `"defaultAction": { "action": "BLOCK" }`.
NGFW-002.rollback: No security change is required to verify; if you changed the default to Block and must revert, set it back in FMC and redeploy. Caveat: reverting to Allow/Trust re-exposes all unmatched traffic exactly as in NGFW-001, and reverting to 'unknown' (removing evidence) simply reinstates the unverifiable state the finding warns about.
NGFW-002.impact: Verification itself is non-disruptive (read-only show/REST). If verification reveals an Allow/Trust default and you correct it to Block, that correction is service-affecting exactly as described for NGFW-001 (unmatched traffic drops on deploy) and should be done in a maintenance window. No reload required.
references: Cisco Secure Firewall Management Center Device Configuration Guide — Access Control Policy Default Action
Cisco Secure Firewall Threat Defense Command Reference — show access-control-config
NIST SP 800-53 Rev.5 AC-4, SC-7, CA-2/CA-7 (Control Assessment / Continuous Monitoring)

NGFW-003.risk:
NGFW-003 (HIGH, FTD-only) is raised when the scanner finds no evidence of intrusion prevention: `check_ips_policy()` looks for `(intrusion|ips)\s*(policy|rule)`, `snort`, or `inspect.*policy` and finds none. It signals that the Snort intrusion detection/prevention engine either has no intrusion policy bound to the traffic or the evidence of one is absent from the config export.

FTD's threat value comes almost entirely from Snort. An Access Control rule (or the default action) with no Intrusion Policy attached performs only stateful L3/L4 firewalling — it does not detect or block exploitation of known CVEs, buffer overflows, SQLi, deserialization, web-shell traffic, or protocol abuse. Without IPS the appliance is a glorified stateful ACL.

Concrete attack scenario: an internet-facing service is reachable through an allow rule; an attacker sends an exploit for a known vulnerability (MITRE ATT&CK T1190, Exploit Public-Facing Application). With a bound intrusion policy and current Snort SRU/LSP rules, the matching signature drops the packet and logs an intrusion event; without it, the exploit reaches the server and the only trace is a benign 'allow' connection event. Post-compromise, IPS also catches C2 patterns and known-bad payloads (T1071).

Blast radius: every flow permitted by rules that lack an intrusion policy — potentially all inspected traffic. A single un-inspected allow rule is a hole an attacker will find.

Platform nuance: IPS requires the Threat (IPS) Smart License entitlement on FTD; on classic ASA the analogous feature was the legacy IPS/CX module, which is EOL, so this check is correctly scoped to FTD. Base intrusion policies ship as Connectivity Over Security, Balanced Security and Connectivity (Cisco's recommended default), Security Over Connectivity, and Maximum Detection. The policy must be attached per-rule (Inspection tab) AND to the default action to be effective. Standards: NIST 800-53 SI-3 (malicious code protection) and SI-4 (system monitoring); the NSA/CISA guide calls for IPS at the boundary.
steps: Confirm the Threat (IPS) license is registered: FMC System > Licenses > Smart Licenses shows Threat as In-Use/Authorized (or FTD `show version` / FMC device page).

Check what is actually deployed: FTD `show access-control-config` — each rule block lists its intrusion policy (field shown as `IPS Policy:`/`Intrusion Policy:` depending on version) or shows none; also `show snort instances` and `show snort statistics` confirm Snort is running.
In FMC review or create the base intrusion policy: Policies > Intrusion; use Cisco's recommended `Balanced Security and Connectivity` base policy for most environments.
Ensure Snort rules are current: System > Updates > Rule Updates — download/schedule the latest LSP (SRU) so signatures cover recent CVEs.
Enable Cisco Recommendations on the intrusion policy (Firepower Recommendations) to tune enabled rules to the protected hosts, then generate and use recommendations.
Open the Access Control Policy: Policies > Access Control > [policy].

For each allow rule that carries risk (especially any permitting inbound to servers or outbound user traffic), open the rule > Inspection tab > set Intrusion Policy to your base policy and select a Variable Set.
Attach an intrusion policy to the Default Action as well (Default Action gear > Intrusion Policy) so catch-all traffic is inspected.
Confirm a Variable Set with correct HOME_NET/EXTERNAL_NET is applied (Objects > Variable Set) so signatures fire with correct directionality.
Set intrusion event logging so events reach FMC (rule Inspection/Logging tabs) for visibility and correlation.
Consider inline vs passive: ensure the device/interfaces are in inline (routed/transparent) mode so IPS can DROP, not just alert; verify `Drop when Inline` is enabled in the intrusion policy base.
Schedule deployment in a change window — enabling Snort inspection increases CPU and can affect throughput/latency on undersized platforms.
Deploy: FMC Deploy > [device] > Deploy Now (a Snort restart may occur for some policy changes, briefly interrupting inspection with configured bypass behavior).
Validate the engine: FTD `show snort statistics` shows non-zero inspected/analyzed counters; `show access-control-config` now lists the intrusion policy on the rules.
Test with a benign trigger (e.g., a controlled Talos/curl test string against a lab target) and confirm an intrusion event appears under Analysis > Intrusions > Events.
Monitor CPU/throughput after enabling (`show cpu`, FMC Health Monitor) to confirm the platform is sized for inspection.
Document base policy, LSP version, and which rules carry inspection; schedule recurring rule-update automation.
NGFW-003.cli:
! FTD IPS is an FMC/FDM policy construct + Snort engine; not set via LINA CLI.
! GUI remediation:
!   Policies > Intrusion > base policy "Balanced Security and Connectivity"
!   System > Updates > Rule Updates > download latest LSP/SRU
!   Policies > Access Control > <policy> > <rule> > Inspection >
!        Intrusion Policy = Balanced..., Variable Set = Default-Set
!   Default Action gear > Intrusion Policy = Balanced...
!   Deploy > <device> > Deploy Now
!
! Data-plane verification commands (run on FTD CLI):
show snort instances
show snort statistics
show access-control-config | include Policy
NGFW-003.verify: `show access-control-config | include Policy` lists an intrusion policy name on the intended rules (not blank). `show snort statistics` shows increasing 'Packets analyzed' counters, confirming Snort is inspecting. A controlled signature test produces an event under FMC Analysis > Intrusions > Events.
NGFW-003.rollback: Remove the Intrusion Policy from the rule's Inspection tab (and Default Action) in FMC and redeploy. Caveat: reverting disables exploit/CVE detection on that traffic, re-exposing permitted flows to T1190 exploitation with only benign connection logs as evidence.
NGFW-003.impact: Enabling IPS is CPU/throughput-intensive and can add latency; on undersized appliances it may reduce max throughput. Some intrusion-policy deployments trigger a Snort restart, causing a brief inspection interruption governed by the configured Snort Fail Open / bypass setting (traffic either drops or passes uninspected momentarily). No device reload. Plan for a maintenance window on production egress.
references: NSA/CISA Network Infrastructure Security Guide — Intrusion detection/prevention at the boundary
Cisco Secure Firewall Management Center Snort 3 Configuration Guide — Intrusion Policies
NIST SP 800-53 Rev.5 SI-3 (Malicious Code Protection), SI-4 (System Monitoring)

NGFW-004.risk:
NGFW-004 (HIGH, FTD-only) fires when `check_malware_protection()` finds no evidence of file/malware inspection: it searches for `(malware|amp|file\s*policy|threat\s*defense)` or `file.*inspect` and matches nothing. It indicates the absence of a File Policy (Cisco's malware defense / AMP for Networks capability) bound to inspected traffic.

File Policies give FTD three linked functions: file-type control (block risky types), local malware analysis and SHA-256 disposition lookups against Cisco Secure Malware Analytics/Talos cloud (Spero/local analysis), and dynamic analysis (sandboxing). Without a File Policy, the firewall passes executables, Office documents, PDFs, and archives without any malware verdict — even when IPS and URL filtering are active, because malware disposition is a separate inspection.

Concrete attack scenario: a user is lured to download a weaponized document or a trojanized installer (MITRE ATT&CK T1105 Ingress Tool Transfer / T1566 Phishing payload delivery). A File Policy set to Block Malware would compute the file's SHA-256, get a Malicious disposition from Talos, and block the transfer while logging a file/malware event. With no File Policy, the payload lands on the endpoint and the firewall records only a benign allow.

Blast radius: any endpoint reachable over the inspected application protocols (HTTP, HTTPS-if-decrypted, SMTP, FTP, SMB, NFS). One un-inspected download path is enough for initial access.

Platform nuance: malware defense requires the Malware Defense (formerly AMP/Threat) Smart License on FTD; classic ASA has no equivalent native file engine, so the check is correctly FTD-scoped. Note that malware over HTTPS is only inspected if SSL/TLS decryption is enabled (see NGFW-007) — a File Policy without decryption misses encrypted downloads. Standards: NIST 800-53 SI-3 (malicious code protection) and the NSA/CISA guide's malware-defense recommendation.
steps: Verify the Malware Defense (AMP) license: FMC System > Licenses > Smart Licenses shows Malware/Malware Defense In-Use.
Check current state on the device: FTD `show access-control-config` — rule blocks show `File Policy: <name>` if one is attached; none indicates the gap.
In FMC create a File Policy: Policies > Malware & File > New File Policy (e.g., name it 'Block-Malware').
Add a rule to the File Policy with Action `Block Malware`, select applicable Application Protocols (HTTP, SMTP, FTP, SMB/NFS) and Directions (Download/Upload).
Select file categories/types to inspect (Office Documents, PDF, Executables, Archives) and enable `Spero Analysis`, `Dynamic Analysis`, and `Local Malware Analysis` as licensed.
Optionally add a `Block Files` rule for prohibited types (e.g., multimedia/executables) per policy.
Open the Access Control Policy: Policies > Access Control > [policy].
For each relevant allow rule (user egress, inbound to servers), open the rule > Inspection tab > set File Policy to 'Block-Malware'.
Enable file/malware event logging on those rules (Logging tab) so verdicts reach FMC and syslog.
Ensure SSL/TLS decryption (NGFW-007) is configured for HTTPS paths, otherwise malware in TLS is not inspected — coordinate the two changes.

Confirm cloud reachability for disposition lookups: the FTD/management path must reach the Cisco AMP cloud (FMC Health / connectivity); for air-gapped sites use the on-prem Secure Malware Analytics appliance.
Set the malware cloud/Secure Malware Analytics region appropriately (System > Integration) if not default.
Schedule deployment; file inspection buffers files and adds latency/CPU, so plan a change window for high-throughput links.
Deploy: FMC Deploy > [device] > Deploy Now.
Validate with a controlled EICAR test file download over an inspected protocol and confirm it is blocked and logged under Analysis > Files > Malware Events / File Events.
Confirm on the device: `show access-control-config | include File Policy` lists the policy on the intended rules.
Monitor performance and file-event volume; tune inspected types to balance coverage and throughput.
Document the File Policy, licensed features enabled, and cloud region; add to recurring review.
NGFW-004.cli:
! FTD File/Malware (AMP) policy is authored in FMC/FDM, not LINA CLI.
! GUI remediation:
!   Policies > Malware & File > New File Policy "Block-Malware"
!     Rule: Action = Block Malware; App Protocols = HTTP,SMTP,FTP,SMB;
!           enable Spero/Local Malware/Dynamic Analysis
!   Policies > Access Control > <policy> > <rule> > Inspection >
!           File Policy = Block-Malware ; Logging = on
!   (Pair with SSL decryption for HTTPS downloads - see NGFW-007)
!   Deploy > <device> > Deploy Now
!
! Data-plane verification:
show access-control-config | include File Policy
NGFW-004.verify: `show access-control-config | include File Policy` shows the File Policy name attached to the intended access rules. A controlled EICAR download over an inspected protocol is blocked and appears under FMC Analysis > Files > Malware Events with disposition Malicious.
NGFW-004.rollback: Remove the File Policy from the rule Inspection tab in FMC and redeploy (or disable the Block Malware rule). Caveat: reverting stops SHA-256/disposition inspection, re-exposing endpoints to malware delivery (T1105/T1566) with only benign connection logs.
NGFW-004.impact: File inspection buffers files for analysis, adding latency and CPU load and possibly reducing throughput on large transfers; misconfigured file-type blocking can break legitimate downloads. Dynamic analysis introduces additional delay for unknown files. No reload; a Snort restart may occur on some deployments. Use a maintenance window on production egress.
references: NSA/CISA Network Infrastructure Security Guide — Malware defense
Cisco Secure Firewall Management Center Device Configuration Guide — File and Malware Policies (Malware Defense)
NIST SP 800-53 Rev.5 SI-3 (Malicious Code Protection)
CWE-494 Download of Code Without Integrity Check; CWE-693 Protection Mechanism Failure

NGFW-005.risk:
NGFW-005 (HIGH, FTD-only) is raised when `check_security_intelligence()` finds neither `security.intelligence` nor `(blacklist|blocklist|threat\s*feed)` in the config. It means Security Intelligence (SI) — FTD's earliest, cheapest line of defense — is not evidenced.

Security Intelligence blocks (or monitors) traffic to/from known-bad IP addresses, URLs, and DNS domains BEFORE the access control rules and Snort inspection run. It draws on the Cisco Talos-curated feeds (auto-updated), plus custom block/do-not-block lists. Because SI drops matching flows at the front of the pipeline, it is extremely efficient: no rule evaluation, no deep inspection, no CPU spent on traffic already known to be malicious.

Concrete attack scenario: a compromised internal host beacons to a Talos-flagged C2 IP or resolves a known malware domain (MITRE ATT&CK T1071 C2, T1071.004 DNS). With SI enabled, the flow is blackholed and logged the instant it matches the feed — often before the malware operation succeeds. The DNS SI list additionally supports sinkholing so infected hosts are redirected and identified. Without SI, that C2 traffic is only stopped if some other layer happens to catch it.

Blast radius: all traffic the policy governs; SI is a global pre-filter, so its absence removes a broad, low-cost safety net across every flow.

Platform nuance: SI (Network, URL, and DNS lists) requires the Threat license and is configured in the Access Control Policy's Security Intelligence tab (Networks/URLs) and via a DNS Policy for domains; on classic ASA there is no equivalent, hence the FTD scope. URL SI overlaps with but is distinct from category URL filtering (NGFW-006): SI is reputation/feed-based blocking, URL filtering is category-based. Best practice is to run SI in Block mode (not just Monitor). Standards: NIST 800-53 SI-4 (system monitoring) and SC-7 (boundary protection); the NSA/CISA guide recommends threat-intelligence-driven blocking.
steps: Verify the Threat license is active (FMC System > Licenses > Smart Licenses) — SI feeds require it.
Confirm FMC/FTD can reach the Cisco Talos feed servers (management connectivity) so feeds auto-update; note the feed update interval.
Open the Access Control Policy: Policies > Access Control > [policy] > Security Intelligence tab.
Under Networks, add the Talos-curated Network feeds/lists (e.g., Attackers, Bogon, Bots, CnC, Malware, Phishing, Spam) to the Block list.
Set the action to Block (not Monitor-only) for production; use Monitor first only for a short baselining period to catch false positives.
Enable logging for SI events (the log icon on each SI list entry) so blocks are recorded and sent to FMC/syslog.
Under URLs on the same tab, add the Talos URL SI feeds to the Block list with logging enabled.
Create/assign a DNS Policy: Policies > DNS — add Talos DNS feeds and set action to Domain Not Found or Sinkhole; the DNS policy is enforced via the access control policy's SI DNS section.
Configure a Sinkhole object (Objects > Sinkhole) and apply it to the DNS SI so infected hosts are redirected and identifiable.
Optionally add custom block lists (Objects > Security Intelligence > Network/URL/DNS Lists and Feeds) for org-specific IOCs.
Add any required do-not-block (allow) list entries for known false positives to avoid outages.
Schedule deployment; SI blocking is low-overhead but can drop flows if a feed contains an entry your environment relies on.
Deploy: FMC Deploy > [device] > Deploy Now.
Validate SI is loaded on the device: FTD `show access-control-config` shows Security Intelligence sections; check feed freshness in FMC (Objects > Security Intelligence shows last-updated).
Generate a test hit against a known-listed IP/domain (carefully, in a lab) or review live Analysis > Connections filtered on Action=Block, Reason=IP/URL/DNS Block.
Confirm SI events appear under Analysis > Connections > Security Intelligence Events.
Set a recurring review of feed updates and the custom/do-not-block lists; document the enabled feeds and mode (Block).
NGFW-005.cli:
! FTD Security Intelligence is an FMC policy construct (feeds from Talos).
! GUI remediation:
!   Policies > Access Control > <policy> > Security Intelligence tab
!     Networks: add Talos feeds (Attackers,CnC,Malware,Bots,Phishing) to Block + log
!     URLs:     add Talos URL feeds to Block + log
!   Policies > DNS: add Talos DNS feeds, action = Sinkhole/Domain-Not-Found
!     (enforced via the access policy SI DNS section)
!   Objects > Sinkhole: define sinkhole for infected-host identification
!   Deploy > <device> > Deploy Now
!
! Data-plane verification:
show access-control-config | begin Security Intelligence
NGFW-005.verify: `show access-control-config | begin Security Intelligence` shows Network/URL/DNS SI lists in Block mode. FMC Objects > Security Intelligence shows recent feed update timestamps. Analysis > Connections > Security Intelligence Events records blocks against listed IOCs.
NGFW-005.rollback: Set the SI lists to Monitor-only or remove them from the Security Intelligence/DNS tabs and redeploy. Caveat: reverting removes the pre-filter that blackholes known-bad IPs/URLs/domains, re-exposing the network to C2 and known-malicious infrastructure (T1071) that other layers may not catch.
NGFW-005.impact: Low performance overhead (SI is a fast pre-filter and typically improves efficiency by short-circuiting known-bad traffic). Risk is functional, not capacity: an over-broad or stale feed entry could block a needed destination — mitigate with a do-not-block list and an initial Monitor period. No reload; deployment does not require Snort restart for SI-only changes.
references: NSA/CISA Network Infrastructure Security Guide — Threat intelligence / reputation filtering
Cisco Secure Firewall Management Center Device Configuration Guide — Security Intelligence (Network/URL/DNS)
NIST SP 800-53 Rev.5 SI-4 (System Monitoring), SC-7 (Boundary Protection)

NGFW-006.risk:
NGFW-006 (MEDIUM, FTD-only) fires when `check_url_filtering()` finds no `url.*(filter|category|reputation)` and no `http.*inspect.*url` evidence. It indicates category/reputation-based URL filtering is not applied to the access control policy.

FTD URL filtering classifies HTTP/HTTPS destinations into ~80 categories and assigns each a reputation (1–5 risk). Rules can then block by category (e.g., Malware Sites, Phishing, Botnets, Command and Control, Newly Registered Domains) and/or by reputation threshold. This enforces acceptable-use policy and, more importantly for security, blocks access to malicious web infrastructure at the request stage.

Concrete attack scenario: a phishing email lures a user to click a link to a credential-harvesting or drive-by-download site (MITRE ATT&CK T1566 Phishing, T1189 Drive-by Compromise). A rule blocking the Phishing/Malware Sites categories and low-reputation URLs stops the connection and logs it, breaking the delivery chain even if the email itself got through. It also curtails C2 over web categories and blocks user access to anonymizers/proxies used to evade other controls.

Blast radius: outbound user web traffic across the policy — a broad exposure for the most common initial-access vector (the web browser).

Platform nuance: URL filtering requires the URL Filtering Smart License (URLDB) on FTD and cloud/local category lookups; classic ASA lacks native category filtering (only the legacy Botnet Traffic Filter historically), so the check is FTD-scoped. Critically, for HTTPS sites the firewall can categorize using the TLS SNI/server-certificate CN even without decryption, but full URL-path filtering and reliable enforcement need SSL/TLS decryption (NGFW-007). URL filtering (category) is distinct from URL Security Intelligence (NGFW-005, feed/reputation blocklists) — deploy both. Standards: NIST 800-53 SC-7/AC-4 and the NSA/CISA boundary-filtering guidance.
steps: Verify the URL Filtering license: FMC System > Licenses > Smart Licenses shows URL Filtering In-Use.
Confirm cloud category lookup connectivity (or enable the on-box URL database): System > Integration > Cloud Services > enable URL lookups and category updates.
Check current state: FTD `show access-control-config` rule blocks show URL categories if present; absence confirms the gap.
Open the Access Control Policy: Policies > Access Control > [policy].

Create a high-priority BLOCK rule for malicious categories: add a rule, URLs tab > Categories > select Malware Sites, Phishing, Command and Control, Botnets/Bots, Spam URLs, Newly Registered Domains; Action = Block.
Add a reputation threshold: for the selected categories set 'Reputations' to block Questionable/High Risk (reputation 1–3) as policy dictates.
Optionally add acceptable-use block rules (e.g., Anonymizers/Proxy Avoidance, Illegal, Adult) per organizational policy.
Enable logging on the URL rules (Logging tab) so blocks and category events are recorded to FMC/syslog.
Order the URL block rules ABOVE broad allow rules so they take precedence (rule order matters).
Configure a custom block response/HTTP response page if you want users to see a notice (Access Control Policy > HTTP Responses).
Coordinate SSL/TLS decryption (NGFW-007) for reliable HTTPS URL enforcement; without it, only SNI/cert-CN based categorization applies.
Add a do-not-block/allow rule for business-critical sites that may be miscategorized to prevent outages.
Schedule deployment; new block rules can interrupt user access to affected categories.
Deploy: FMC Deploy > [device] > Deploy Now.
Validate on the device: `show access-control-config` shows the URL categories/reputations on the rule; test browsing to a known malicious-category test URL and confirm it is blocked.
Confirm URL events under Analysis > Connections filtered on URL Category / Action=Block.
Review category-update freshness and tune false positives via the allow list; document blocked categories and reputation threshold.
NGFW-006.cli:
! FTD URL filtering is an FMC policy construct (category+reputation, URLDB license).
! GUI remediation:
!   Policies > Access Control > <policy> > add BLOCK rule
!     URLs tab > Categories: Malware Sites, Phishing, C2, Botnets, Spam,
!                Newly Registered Domains
!     Reputations: block 1-3 (High Risk..Questionable)
!     Action = Block ; Logging = on ; place ABOVE broad allow rules
!   (Pair with SSL decryption for HTTPS path enforcement - NGFW-007)
!   Deploy > <device> > Deploy Now
!
! Data-plane verification:
show access-control-config
NGFW-006.verify: `show access-control-config` shows the intended rule with URL Categories and reputation criteria and Action Block. Browsing to a known malicious-category test URL is blocked (block page or reset), and the event appears under FMC Analysis > Connections with the URL Category and Action=Block.
NGFW-006.rollback: Disable or delete the URL block rule in FMC and redeploy. Caveat: reverting restores unrestricted web access, re-exposing users to phishing/malware/drive-by sites (T1566/T1189) and removing acceptable-use enforcement.
NGFW-006.impact: Moderately disruptive to users if categories are set too broadly — legitimate but miscategorized sites can be blocked; mitigate with allow-list exceptions and a monitored rollout. Category lookups add minor latency (mostly cached). HTTPS enforcement depth depends on decryption. No reload; a Snort restart may occur for some policy changes.
references: NSA/CISA Network Infrastructure Security Guide — Web/URL filtering
Cisco Secure Firewall Management Center Device Configuration Guide — URL Filtering (categories and reputation)
NIST SP 800-53 Rev.5 SC-7 (Boundary Protection), AC-4 (Information Flow Enforcement)

NGFW-007.risk:
NGFW-007 (MEDIUM, FTD-only) is raised when `check_ssl_inspection()` finds no `ssl.*(policy|inspection|decrypt)` and no `decrypt.*rule`. It indicates no SSL/TLS decryption (Decryption Policy) is configured, so encrypted traffic traverses the firewall opaque to inspection.

The overwhelming majority of web traffic is TLS. Without decryption, FTD's most valuable engines are blind: IPS (NGFW-003) cannot see exploits inside HTTPS, AMP/File (NGFW-004) cannot compute malware dispositions on encrypted downloads, and URL filtering (NGFW-006) is reduced to SNI/certificate-CN heuristics. In effect, an attacker who wraps activity in TLS bypasses the security stack.

Concrete attack scenario: malware is delivered over HTTPS or beacons to an HTTPS C2 endpoint (MITRE ATT&CK T1573 Encrypted Channel, T1071.001 Web Protocols). With a Decrypt-Resign rule on outbound traffic, FTD terminates and re-originates the TLS session, feeds the plaintext to Snort/AMP/URL, blocks the malicious content, then re-encrypts — the payload never reaches the endpoint. Without decryption, only encrypted-traffic heuristics (e.g., EVE/TLS fingerprinting where supported) apply, which are weaker than full inspection.

Blast radius: essentially all modern user and server traffic, since nearly everything is TLS — the absence of decryption undermines every other inspection control on encrypted flows.

Platform nuance: on FTD this is the SSL/Decryption Policy in FMC (renamed 'Decryption' in 7.x), configured with Decrypt-Resign (uses an internal CA cert trusted by clients) for outbound, and Decrypt-Known-Key (uses the server's own key) for inbound to owned servers. It leverages TLS crypto-acceleration hardware on supported appliances. Classic ASA has no equivalent, hence the FTD scope. Legal/privacy nuance: decryption must exclude privacy-sensitive categories (Health, Financial, Government) via Do-Not-Decrypt rules, and requires organizational/HR/legal approval — this is why it is MEDIUM, not automatic. Standards: NIST 800-53 SC-7/SI-4; the NSA/CISA guide notes TLS inspection is needed for effective boundary defense.
