steps: Confirm the platform supports and has TLS crypto acceleration: FTD `show crypto accelerator statistics` and `system support ssl-hw-status` (hardware offload avoids severe throughput loss).
Obtain organizational, HR, and legal sign-off for TLS interception and publish an acceptable-use notice — a prerequisite before enabling.
Create or import the internal CA for Decrypt-Resign: Objects > PKI > Internal CAs (generate a subordinate CA or import your enterprise sub-CA).
Distribute/trust the Resign CA certificate to client endpoints (via GPO/MDM) so browsers do not throw certificate warnings.
For inbound protection of owned servers, import the server certificate+key as an Internal Certificate object for Decrypt-Known-Key.
Create the Decryption/SSL Policy: Policies > SSL (or Decryption) > New Policy; set the default action (typically Do Not Decrypt) conservatively.
Add a Decrypt-Resign rule for outbound user traffic (Action = Decrypt - Resign, select the Internal CA).
Add a Decrypt-Known-Key rule for inbound traffic to your published servers (Action = Decrypt - Known Key, select the server cert).

Add Do-Not-Decrypt rules ABOVE the decrypt rules for privacy-sensitive URL categories (Health and Medicine, Financial Services, Government, Legal) and for applications that pin certificates (banking apps, software updaters) to avoid breakage.
Set TLS version/cipher handling and undecryptable-actions (e.g., block or pass for unsupported ciphers, certificate errors, session-not-cached).
Associate the SSL/Decryption policy with the Access Control Policy (Access Control Policy > Advanced > SSL Policy Settings) so it is enforced.
Enable logging on decryption rules for visibility (Logging tab).
Schedule a maintenance window — decryption is CPU-intensive and can break pinned/legacy applications; pilot on a small user group first.
Deploy: FMC Deploy > [device] > Deploy Now.
Validate: browse an HTTPS site and confirm the presented certificate is signed by your Resign CA; on the device check `show ssl-policy-config` and TLS/decryption counters.
Test that a HTTPS-delivered EICAR/malicious-category resource is now inspected and blocked (confirms decryption feeds AMP/URL/IPS).
Monitor CPU/throughput (`show cpu`, FMC Health Monitor) and the do-not-decrypt exceptions; expand rollout gradually.
Document the CA, decrypt scope, exclusions, and legal approval; schedule review of pinned-app exceptions.
NGFW-007.cli:
! FTD SSL/Decryption is an FMC policy construct; leverages TLS crypto HW.
! GUI remediation:
!   Objects > PKI > Internal CAs: create/import Resign sub-CA (push to clients)
!   Policies > SSL (Decryption) > New Policy, default = Do Not Decrypt
!     Rule1 (above): Do Not Decrypt = Health,Financial,Government + pinned apps
!     Rule2: Decrypt - Resign (outbound) using Internal CA
!     Rule3: Decrypt - Known Key (inbound) using server cert/key
!   Access Control Policy > Advanced > associate SSL/Decryption Policy
!   Deploy > <device> > Deploy Now
!
! Data-plane verification (FTD CLI):
show ssl-policy-config
system support ssl-hw-status
NGFW-007.verify: `show ssl-policy-config` displays the deployed decryption rules (Decrypt-Resign / Do-Not-Decrypt). On a client, an inspected HTTPS site presents a certificate issued by the internal Resign CA. A HTTPS-delivered test malware/URL is now blocked, proving inspection engines see plaintext. `system support ssl-hw-status` confirms hardware offload is active.
NGFW-007.rollback: Disassociate the SSL/Decryption policy from the Access Control Policy (or set rules to Do Not Decrypt) and redeploy. Caveat: reverting makes all TLS traffic opaque again, re-blinding IPS/AMP/URL to encrypted threats and C2 (T1573/T1071.001).
NGFW-007.impact: Highly resource-intensive and the most operationally sensitive change here: decryption significantly increases CPU and can cut throughput on platforms without/with limited TLS acceleration; it breaks certificate-pinned and some legacy applications unless excluded, and it has legal/privacy obligations. No device reload, but a Snort restart may occur on deployment. Pilot before broad rollout and use do-not-decrypt exceptions.
references: NSA/CISA Network Infrastructure Security Guide — TLS/SSL inspection considerations
Cisco Secure Firewall Management Center Device Configuration Guide — Decryption (SSL) Policy, Decrypt-Resign/Known-Key
NIST SP 800-53 Rev.5 SC-7 (Boundary Protection), SI-4 (System Monitoring)

NGFW-008.risk:
NGFW-008 (MEDIUM) fires from `check_rule_logging()` when more than half of the matched rule lines lack logging: the scanner collects lines matching `(access-list|access-control|rule)\s`, keeps those without the substring `log`, and raises the finding when `len(no_log) > total * 0.5`. The finding title embeds the ratio (e.g., 'Many firewall rules without logging (37/50)'). It runs on both ftd and asa families.

Logging is the evidentiary backbone of a firewall. A rule with no logging silently permits or denies traffic, leaving no record of what matched it. When an incident occurs, responders cannot answer the essential questions: did the firewall see this flow, which rule handled it, was it allowed or dropped, and when. This is precisely the insufficient-logging weakness (CWE-778) and it defeats detection, forensics, and compliance attestation alike.

Concrete attack scenario: an attacker performs reconnaissance and lateral movement (MITRE ATT&CK T1046 Network Service Scanning, T1021 Remote Services). If the rules governing that traffic have no logging, the activity generates no connection events; the SOC has no telemetry to alert on, and post-incident the timeline has a gap exactly where the firewall sat. Equally, an allow rule with no logging means data exfiltration (T1048) rides through invisibly.

Blast radius: observability across a majority of the rule base — the finding indicates a systemic gap, not a single missed rule, so most of the traffic the device handles is unlogged.

Platform nuance is significant. On classic ASA, logging is per-ACE with the `log` keyword: `access-list NAME extended permit/deny ... log [level] [interval secs]`, and it depends on `logging enable` plus a `logging trap`/`logging host` destination. On FTD, per-rule logging is set in FMC on each access rule's Logging tab (Log at Beginning and/or End of Connection, and Send events to FMC/Syslog) — it is not a `log` keyword in the LINA config, so an FTD `show running-config` export will legitimately look unlogged and can inflate this finding. Standards: NIST 800-53 AU-2 (event logging) and AU-12 (audit record generation); the NSA/CISA guide requires firewall logging to a central collector.
steps: Determine platform: for ASA proceed with per-ACE `log`; for FTD the fix is in FMC per-rule Logging tabs (re-collect `show access-control-config`, not just `show running-config`, so evidence is accurate).
ASA inventory: `show run access-list` and `show run access-group` to list ACLs and their interface bindings; identify ACEs lacking the `log` keyword.
ASA: confirm a logging pipeline exists — `show run logging` should show `logging enable`, a `logging trap <level>`, and at least one `logging host <intf> <syslog-ip>` (or FMC/SAL collector).
ASA: if missing, configure the destination first: `logging enable`, `logging trap informational`, `logging host inside 10.0.0.50`, `logging timestamp`.

ASA: add per-ACE logging to the meaningful rules, e.g. `access-list OUTSIDE_IN extended permit tcp any host 10.1.1.10 eq 443 log 6 interval 300` (level 6=informational, interval throttles duplicate deny logs).

ASA: ensure the explicit deny is logged — add `access-list OUTSIDE_IN extended deny ip any any log 6 interval 300` as the last ACE if you want denied traffic recorded (the implicit deny is logged as %ASA-4-106023 only when hit against a defined ACL).
ASA: avoid log-flooding — set a sensible `interval` and level; do not enable `log` at debugging level on high-hit rules.
ASA: save config with `write memory` (or `copy running-config startup-config`).
FTD: in FMC open Policies > Access Control > [policy]; for each rule open the Logging tab.
FTD: enable `Log at Beginning of Connection` and/or `Log at End of Connection` (End is preferred for allow rules to capture byte/duration; Begin+End for deny/monitor as needed).
FTD: set `Send connection events to: FMC` and, if using external SIEM, also to `Syslog` (select a Syslog Alert / configure the syslog server under the policy or platform settings).
FTD: enable logging on the Default Action as well (Default Action gear > Logging) so catch-all traffic is recorded — ties to NGFW-001/002.
FTD: for high-volume allow rules, prefer Log at End only to control event volume; ensure the FMC/SIEM can handle the rate.
FTD: deploy the change — FMC Deploy > [device] > Deploy Now (logging-only changes typically do not force a Snort restart).
Validate telemetry: generate test traffic and confirm events appear — ASA in syslog/SAL, FTD under Analysis > Connections and on the SIEM.
ASA verify: `show access-list OUTSIDE_IN` shows the `log` flag and hit counts on the ACEs; `show logging` shows messages flowing.
Reconcile the ratio: re-audit to confirm the majority of rules now log; document any intentionally-unlogged rules with justification.
Tune log levels/intervals to balance coverage vs. collector capacity and cost; set a recurring review.
NGFW-008.cli:
! ==== Classic ASA (per-ACE 'log' keyword) ====
logging enable
logging timestamp
logging trap informational
logging host inside 10.0.0.50
!
access-list OUTSIDE_IN extended permit tcp any host 10.1.1.10 eq 443 log 6 interval 300
access-list OUTSIDE_IN extended deny   ip  any any log 6 interval 300
access-group OUTSIDE_IN in interface outside
write memory
!
! ==== Cisco Secure Firewall Threat Defense (FMC) ====
! Per-rule logging is set in FMC, not the LINA config:
!   Policies > Access Control > <policy> > <rule> > Logging tab:
!        [x] Log at End of Connection   (Begin+End for deny/monitor)
!        Send events to: FMC  (+ Syslog for SIEM)
!   Default Action gear > Logging = Log at End of Connection
!   Deploy > <device> > Deploy Now
NGFW-008.verify: ASA: `show access-list OUTSIDE_IN` shows the `log` flag on ACEs with hit counts, and `show logging` shows connection messages (e.g., %ASA-6-302013/302014 build/teardown) reaching the collector. FTD: `show access-control-config` shows `Log Begin`/`Log End` on rules, and Analysis > Connections in FMC (plus the SIEM) receives events for test traffic.
NGFW-008.rollback: ASA: remove per-ACE logging with `no access-list OUTSIDE_IN extended permit tcp any host 10.1.1.10 eq 443 log` (or disable globally via `no logging trap`); FTD: uncheck Log at Beginning/End on the rule and redeploy. Caveat: reverting reinstates blind rules — traffic matching them becomes invisible, defeating detection and forensics (CWE-778) and breaking audit compliance.
NGFW-008.impact: Low traffic impact — logging does not change forwarding. The real risk is event volume: over-broad logging (especially at low intervals or on high-hit rules) can overload the syslog/FMC/SIEM pipeline and CPU. On ASA the `log` keyword change is non-disruptive and applied immediately; on FTD it requires a deploy but typically no Snort restart and no reload. No admin/VPN session impact.
references: NSA/CISA Network Infrastructure Security Guide — Logging and monitoring to a central collector
Cisco ASA Series CLI Configuration Guide — Access Rule / ACL logging (log keyword, interval, level)
Cisco Secure Firewall Management Center Device Configuration Guide — Access Control Rule Logging
NIST SP 800-53 Rev.5 AU-2 (Event Logging), AU-12 (Audit Record Generation); CWE-778 Insufficient Logging

NGFW-009.risk:
The NgfwCoreAuditor.check_any_any_rules() method scans every configuration line with the regex `(permit|allow)\s+(ip|tcp|udp)?\s*any\s+any` (case-insensitive). Any line that permits a protocol from source `any` to destination `any` is collected; if one or more match, a HIGH finding fires titled "Overly permissive 'any any' rules (N)", listing up to the first 10 matches in affected_items. This module's SUPPORTED_PLATFORMS is {"ftd","asa"}, so the check runs against both classic ASA ACL syntax (`access-list OUTSIDE_IN extended permit ip any any`) and the LINA/prefilter ACLs that FTD generates.

An any-source-to-any-destination permit rule is the archetypal firewall hygiene failure: for the traffic it matches, the firewall degrades to a routed hop and performs no segmentation. Because ACL evaluation is first-match, a broad `permit ip any any` placed above more specific rules silently shadows them, so tighter controls below it never execute.

Concretely, an adversary who lands on any host inside a zone governed by such a rule can reach any service in the destination zone. This directly enables ATT&CK Lateral Movement (T1021 Remote Services) and Command-and-Control egress (T1071) — an outbound `permit ip any any` lets a beacon reach arbitrary internet C2 over any port, defeating egress filtering. An inbound one exposes every internal service to reconnaissance and exploitation (T1046 Network Service Discovery).

Blast radius is the entire zone pair the ACL is bound to. On a perimeter interface this can mean the whole internal network is reachable from the internet; on an internal segmentation firewall it collapses east-west controls and voids micro-segmentation assumptions used for PCI/CDE scoping.

Platform nuance: on ASA the rule appears literally in `show running-config access-list` and is enforced by LINA. On FTD the offending construct usually originates from an Access Control Policy rule with Source/Destination = Any (or a permissive Prefilter fastpath rule) that FMC compiles into LINA; you remediate it in FMC, not by editing device CLI, because a direct CLI edit is overwritten on the next deployment. The compliance angle: CIS Cisco Firewall/FTD benchmarks require least-privilege rule bases, and PCI-DSS explicitly prohibits any-any permit rules on segmentation and perimeter firewalls.
steps: Inventory the offending rules and where they are bound: on ASA run `show running-config access-list | include any any` and `show running-config access-group`; on FTD identify the source ACP/Prefilter rule in FMC (Policies > Access Control) rather than the compiled LINA line.
For each match, determine the interface/zone pair and direction with `show running-config access-group` (ASA) so you know the real traffic the rule carries before you touch it.

Baseline actual usage so you can scope the replacement: `show access-list OUTSIDE_IN` shows per-ACE hit counts; on ASA add `log` to the ACE temporarily and review `show logging` / syslog for the real 5-tuples traversing the any-any ACE.
Open a change window — tightening a live any-any rule can drop production flows, so schedule maintenance and stage a rollback config.
On ASA, enter config mode with `configure terminal` and build a named object-group for the legitimate sources: `object-group network SRC_ALLOWED` then `network-object host 10.10.5.20`.
Build the destination/service scope similarly: `object-group network DST_SERVERS` and `object-group service SVC_WEB tcp` with `port-object eq 443`.

Insert the specific replacement ACE ABOVE the any-any line using a line number so ordering is preserved: `access-list OUTSIDE_IN line 1 extended permit tcp object-group SRC_ALLOWED object-group DST_SERVERS object-group SVC_WEB log`.
Add any additional explicit flows the traffic analysis proved are needed, each as its own least-privilege ACE.
Remove the permissive rule only after the specific ACEs are in place: `no access-list OUTSIDE_IN extended permit ip any any`.
Confirm an explicit terminal deny with logging exists (ASA has an implicit deny, but an explicit `access-list OUTSIDE_IN extended deny ip any any log` gives you drop visibility).
Re-apply/confirm binding: `access-group OUTSIDE_IN in interface outside` (only if it was not already bound).

On FTD instead: in FMC edit the ACP rule, set Source Networks and Destination Networks to specific objects, set Ports to required services, set Action appropriately, and enable logging (Logging tab > Log at End of Connection).
On FTD, also review the Prefilter policy for any Fastpath/Analyze rule with Any/Any that bypasses Snort, and scope or delete it.
Deploy on FTD via FMC (Deploy > Deployment) — never edit the LINA any-any line directly on the device, as deployment overwrites it.
Validate hit counts move to the new specific ACEs and not to the deny: `clear access-list OUTSIDE_IN counters` then `show access-list OUTSIDE_IN` after traffic resumes.
Save configuration on ASA with `write memory` (equivalently `copy running-config startup-config`); on FTD the deploy persists automatically.
Monitor syslog/connection events for legitimate drops for at least one business cycle and add narrowly-scoped ACEs for any false denies.
Record the change and set a review date; if the any-any was intentionally temporary, document an expiry.
NGFW-009.cli:
! === ASA — replace 'permit ip any any' with least-privilege ACEs ===
configure terminal
!
object-group network SRC_ALLOWED
 network-object host 10.10.5.20
 network-object 10.10.6.0 255.255.255.0
object-group network DST_SERVERS
 network-object host 172.16.1.10
object-group service SVC_WEB tcp
 port-object eq 443
!
! Insert specific permits ABOVE the broad rule (first-match ordering)
access-list OUTSIDE_IN line 1 extended permit tcp object-group SRC_ALLOWED object-group DST_SERVERS object-group SVC_WEB log
!
! Remove the overly permissive rule
no access-list OUTSIDE_IN extended permit ip any any
!
! Explicit logged deny for drop visibility
access-list OUTSIDE_IN extended deny ip any any log
!
access-group OUTSIDE_IN in interface outside
end
write memory
NGFW-009.verify: ASA: `show running-config access-list OUTSIDE_IN` must show the specific permit ACEs and NO `permit ip any any`; `show running-config access-list OUTSIDE_IN | include any any` should return only the intended terminal `deny ip any any log`. Confirm traffic lands on the new ACEs with `show access-list OUTSIDE_IN` (rising hitcnt on specific lines, deny counter not spiking). FTD: in FMC the ACP rule shows scoped Source/Destination and `show access-control-config` in the FTD CLI reflects the compiled specific rule.
NGFW-009.rollback: Re-add the broad rule and remove the specifics: `access-list OUTSIDE_IN line 1 extended permit ip any any` then `no access-list OUTSIDE_IN extended deny ip any any log`, `write memory` (FTD: revert the ACP rule in FMC and redeploy). Caveat: reverting re-opens unrestricted any-source-to-any-destination traffic on that zone pair, restoring the full lateral-movement/egress exposure.
NGFW-009.impact: Potentially disruptive. Tightening a live any-any ACE will drop any flow you did not enumerate in the replacement ACEs — do it in a maintenance window after hit-count/log analysis. Existing connections already in the ASA conn table survive until they time out (new matching flows are what get denied); use `clear conn` cautiously if you must force re-evaluation. No reload is required and admin/VPN sessions are unaffected. On FTD the FMC deploy briefly recompiles Snort/LINA policy but does not drop the box.
references: CIS Cisco Secure Firewall Threat Defense Benchmark — firewall rule-base hygiene / least privilege
PCI-DSS v4.0 Req 1.2.1 & 1.4.x — restrict traffic to that which is necessary; prohibit any-any
NIST SP 800-53 Rev5 AC-4 (Information Flow Enforcement), SC-7 (Boundary Protection), CM-7 (Least Functionality)
CWE-284 Improper Access Control; CWE-1327 Binding to an Unrestricted IP Address
Cisco Secure Firewall ASA/FTD Configuration Guide — Access Rules and Access Control Policy

NGFW-PLAT-001.risk:
NgfwPlatformAuditor.check_management_access() runs only when device_type == "ftd". It sets has_ssh_restrict = has_line(`ssh.*access-list`) and has_https_restrict = has_line(`(http|https).*access-list`). If either is absent it raises a HIGH finding "FTD management access not restricted", enumerating the missing controls ("SSH access: not restricted by ACL" and/or "HTTPS management: not restricted by ACL"). In practice the finding fires whenever the running-config does not contain a line tying SSH or HTTP(S) management to an access-list, which is the common case because FMC-deployed LINA config expresses SSH/HTTP access as `ssh <net> <mask> <intf>` / `http <net> <mask> <intf>` referencing networks rather than a named ACL.

Unrestricted management access means the device's SSH (TCP/22) and HTTPS (TCP/443, used by FDM and by the FMC on-box manager) listeners accept connection attempts from any host that can route to a management-enabled interface. This is a direct enabler of ATT&CK Credential Access via brute force (T1110) and Exploitation of Remote Services (T1210) against the management plane.

The concrete attack: an adversary who reaches the mgmt interface (a flat management VLAN, a jump host, or a data interface that inadvertently has SSH/HTTP enabled) can hammer admin credentials, exploit an SSH/web-server CVE, or capture a misconfigured management session. Compromise of the firewall's control plane is worst-case — the attacker can rewrite ACPs, disable IPS/AMP, create tunnels, and pivot anywhere the firewall can reach.

Blast radius is the entire security posture enforced by the device plus every network it fronts. A perimeter FTD with an exposed management listener is effectively an internet-facing admin portal.

Platform nuance: on the dedicated FTD management interface, SSH source restriction is set with the device CLI `configure ssh-access-list <ip>/<prefix>` and HTTPS with `configure https-access-list <ip>/<prefix>`; note both commands OVERWRITE the entire list, so all authorized subnets must be given in one comma-separated command. For SSH/HTTP on data interfaces used for management, restriction is enforced through FMC Platform Settings (Secure Shell and HTTP Access lists) which compile to LINA `ssh`/`http` statements. Because FMC owns the config, edits made directly on the device are overwritten on the next deployment. Compliance frameworks (CIS FTD, NIST 800-53 AC-17/SC-7) require management planes to be restricted to a defined administrative network.
steps: Confirm the platform and current listeners: on the FTD CLI run `show ssh-access-list` and `show https-access-list` for the dedicated management interface, and `show running-config ssh` / `show running-config http` for LINA statements bound to data interfaces.
Identify the interface used for management (dedicated Management0/0 vs a data interface with `nameif management`) with `show running-config interface` and note the addressing.
Define the authorized administrative source network(s) — ideally a small jump-host subnet or bastion range, never `0.0.0.0/0`.
Restrict SSH to the dedicated management interface at the device CLI in ONE command (it overwrites the whole list): `configure ssh-access-list 10.20.0.0/24,10.30.0.0/24`.
Restrict HTTPS/FDM on the dedicated management interface the same way: `configure https-access-list 10.20.0.0/24`.

For SSH/HTTP on DATA interfaces used for management, drive the change from FMC: Devices > Platform Settings > (policy) > Secure Shell — add Host/Network objects for admin subnets bound to the correct security zone/interface.
In the same Platform Settings policy, open HTTP Access, enable it only if required, add the admin Host/Network + interface, and remove any `0.0.0.0/0` entry.
Where an FMC-managed ACL is the right control, create a Host/Network object group in FMC for the admin sources so the compiled LINA `ssh`/`http` lines are specific.
Ensure SSH is hardened alongside restriction: confirm SSHv2 and strong ciphers/HMACs in Platform Settings (Secure Shell > cipher/HMAC lists) so the restricted service is also cryptographically sound.
Schedule a change window and keep a console/serial or out-of-band session open, because tightening management ACLs can lock out your current SSH/HTTPS session.
Deploy the Platform Settings changes from FMC: Deploy > Deployment, select the device, and deploy.

If a direct device-CLI `configure ssh-access-list`/`configure https-access-list` change was made on an FMC-managed data-interface path, confirm FMC settings match so the next deploy does not overwrite it.
Validate SSH restriction: from an authorized host confirm access works, then from a non-authorized host confirm the connection is refused/times out.
Validate HTTPS restriction the same way against TCP/443 (FDM/on-box or FMC access as applicable).
Review AAA so that even authorized sources authenticate against TACACS+/RADIUS rather than only local accounts (Platform Settings > External Authentication).
Confirm the change persisted with `show ssh-access-list` / `show https-access-list` and `show running-config ssh` / `show running-config http`.
Document the authorized management subnets, update the network diagram, and set a periodic review.
Enable/verify management-plane logging so future access attempts are auditable (Platform Settings > Syslog).
NGFW-PLAT-001.cli:
! === FTD device CLI — restrict SSH/HTTPS on the dedicated management interface ===
! NOTE: each command overwrites the entire list; list ALL admin subnets, comma-separated.
configure ssh-access-list 10.20.0.0/24,10.30.0.0/24
configure https-access-list 10.20.0.0/24
show ssh-access-list
show https-access-list

! === For DATA interfaces used for management, fix in FMC (compiles to LINA) ===
! Devices > Platform Settings > <policy> > Secure Shell:
!   add Network object ADMIN_NET (10.20.0.0/24) bound to the mgmt zone/interface
! Devices > Platform Settings > <policy> > HTTP Access:
!   enable, add ADMIN_NET on the mgmt interface, remove any 0.0.0.0/0
! Deploy > Deployment > select device > Deploy

! Resulting LINA statements after deploy (verify only):
!   ssh 10.20.0.0 255.255.255.0 management
!   http 10.20.0.0 255.255.255.0 management
NGFW-PLAT-001.verify: On the dedicated management interface: `show ssh-access-list` and `show https-access-list` list only the authorized admin subnets (ACCEPT entries) and NO 0.0.0.0/0. For data-interface management: `show running-config ssh` lists specific `ssh <net> <mask> <intf>` entries with NO `ssh 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0`; `show running-config http` shows only admin networks. Functional test: SSH/HTTPS from an authorized admin host succeeds; the same from an out-of-scope host is refused. In FMC, Platform Settings > Secure Shell / HTTP Access shows the scoped network objects.
NGFW-PLAT-001.rollback: Widen the source back if you are locked out: `configure ssh-access-list 0.0.0.0/0` (and `configure https-access-list 0.0.0.0/0`) at the console, or in FMC re-add a broad network to Secure Shell/HTTP Access and redeploy. Caveat: reverting re-exposes the SSH (22) and HTTPS (443) management services to any reachable host, restoring the brute-force / remote-exploit exposure of the control plane.
NGFW-PLAT-001.impact: Disruptive to the management plane only, not to data traffic. Applying a restrictive SSH/HTTPS access list can immediately terminate your own management session if your source IP is not in the allowed set — always retain console/OOB access during the change. No reload is required; VPN and transit traffic are unaffected. The FMC deploy causes a brief policy recompile but does not interrupt forwarding.
references: CIS Cisco Secure Firewall Threat Defense Benchmark — Management Access / Secure Shell / HTTP Access
Cisco Secure Firewall Threat Defense Command Reference — configure ssh-access-list / configure https-access-list
NIST SP 800-53 Rev5 AC-17 (Remote Access), SC-7 (Boundary Protection), AC-3 (Access Enforcement)
CWE-284 Improper Access Control; CWE-306 Missing Authentication for Critical Function
Cisco Secure Firewall Management Center Device Configuration Guide — Platform Settings (SSH/HTTP access lists)

NGFW-PLAT-002.risk:
NgfwPlatformAuditor.check_user_accounts() runs only for device_type == "ftd". It collects all lines matching `^username\s+\S+`, then filters those containing "privilege 15" or "admin" (case-insensitive) into admin_users. If len(admin_users) > 3 it raises a MEDIUM finding titled "Excessive admin accounts on FTD (N)", listing up to 10 sanitized username lines (the secret/password token is masked to `**** ` before display). The trigger is therefore strictly more than three local accounts that are either privilege-15 or have "admin" in the username string.

A large population of local, fully-privileged accounts violates least privilege and defeats individual accountability. Local accounts typically bypass centralized password policy, MFA, and rapid deprovisioning, so each one is a standing credential an attacker can target or that a departing admin may retain.

Concrete risk: local admin accounts are prime targets for ATT&CK Brute Force (T1110) and Valid Accounts (T1078) — shared or stale local admins ("admin", "backup", "cisco") are frequently reused and rarely rotated, and their use is hard to attribute in logs. If AAA to FMC/TACACS+ fails open to local auth, these accounts become the path of least resistance.

Blast radius: any of these accounts grants full control of the firewall's policy, so compromise of one equals compromise of the device and the segmentation it enforces. More accounts means a larger attack surface and more secrets to rotate during an incident.

Platform nuance: FTD user management is normally centralized in FMC (System > Users) with external authentication (RADIUS/TACACS+); the local `admin` account is the built-in break-glass identity and should generally be the only local admin. On a data-plane/LINA view these appear as `username ... privilege 15`. The compliance angle: CIS FTD and NIST 800-53 AC-2/AC-6/IA-2 require least privilege, individual accounts, and centralized identity — a proliferation of local admins is a direct control gap.
steps: Enumerate local accounts and privilege levels on the device: `show running-config username` (LINA view) and, in FMC, System > Users > Users for the management-plane account list.
Classify each account: which are real individuals, which are shared/functional ("backup","monitor","vendor"), and which is the built-in break-glass `admin`.
Map each human admin to an external identity in TACACS+/RADIUS so their local counterpart can be retired.
Stand up external AAA first if not present: in FMC Devices > Platform Settings > External Authentication, add the RADIUS/TACACS+ server(s) and enable it for management access.
Validate external auth works end-to-end by logging in as an AAA-backed user BEFORE removing local accounts, to avoid lockout.
Reduce privilege where full admin is not needed: assign FMC user roles (e.g. Security Analyst, read-only) instead of Administrator in System > Users > User Roles.
Remove each unnecessary local admin on the device: `configure terminal` then `no username backup_admin` (repeat per account) — or delete via FMC System > Users for management accounts.
Keep exactly one local break-glass account (the built-in `admin`), give it a long unique passphrase, and store it in a vault.
Rotate the retained local admin secret: `username admin password <strong-secret> privilege 15` (or reset via FMC), documenting it as break-glass only.
Confirm AAA fallback behavior is intentional (fall back to local only when the AAA server is unreachable) in Platform Settings > External Authentication.
Enable MFA on the AAA/identity provider side so interactive admin logins require a second factor.
Schedule the account removals in a change window in case a service integration silently depends on one of the accounts.
Deploy the Platform Settings/AAA changes from FMC (Deploy > Deployment).
Save the LINA-side change if made directly: `write memory` on the device.
Re-run the inventory to confirm admin_users count is now <= 3 (ideally 1 local + AAA).
Enable login/auth logging (Platform Settings > Syslog) so future account use is attributable, and set a recurring access review.
NGFW-PLAT-002.cli:
! === Reduce local admin accounts on FTD (device LINA view) ===
configure terminal
! Remove surplus local admins (keep only break-glass 'admin')
no username backup_admin
no username vendor_svc
no username monitor1
! Harden the retained break-glass account
username admin password <STRONG-UNIQUE-SECRET> privilege 15
end
write memory

! === Authoritative management-plane fix in FMC ===
! Devices > Platform Settings > External Authentication:
!   add TACACS+/RADIUS server, enable for management, test login
! System > Users > Users: delete surplus local admins; assign least-priv roles
! Deploy > Deployment > select device > Deploy
NGFW-PLAT-002.verify: `show running-config username` should return at most the break-glass admin (and any strictly required, non-admin service account) — no more than three privilege-15/admin entries, ideally one. In FMC System > Users the local user list is minimized and remaining humans authenticate via External Authentication (confirm by logging in as an AAA user and checking the audit log shows the external identity, not a shared local name).
NGFW-PLAT-002.rollback: Recreate a removed account if a dependency breaks: `username backup_admin password <secret> privilege 15` then `write memory` (or re-add in FMC). Caveat: restoring extra local admin accounts re-introduces standing, centrally-unmanaged privileged credentials that bypass MFA/central policy and dilute accountability.
NGFW-PLAT-002.impact: Low data-plane impact; this is an administrative change. Risk is operational lockout — never delete accounts before confirming external AAA (or the retained break-glass account) works, and keep a console session open. No reload, no traffic impact, no VPN disruption. Removing an account that an automation/monitoring integration silently uses could break that integration, hence the change-control step.
references: CIS Cisco Secure Firewall Threat Defense Benchmark — User Accounts / Local Users
Cisco Secure Firewall Management Center Administration Guide — Users and External Authentication
NIST SP 800-53 Rev5 AC-2 (Account Management), AC-6 (Least Privilege), IA-2 (Identification & Authentication)
CWE-269 Improper Privilege Management; CWE-1268 Policy Privileges Not Assigned Consistently
Cisco Secure Firewall Threat Defense Command Reference — username / configure user

NGFW-PLAT-003.risk:
NgfwPlatformAuditor.check_fxos_version() runs only for device_type == "ftd". It extracts a version string with get_value(`(FXOS|FTD|Firepower).*version\s+(\S+)`, group=2), splits on ".", and parses the first component as the major integer. If major < 7 it raises a HIGH finding titled "FTD/FXOS version may be outdated ({version})", with the detected version in affected_items. So any config whose version line reports a major of 6 or lower (e.g. 6.4.0, 6.6.5) triggers; 7.x and above pass. If no version line is present or it is unparseable, the check stays silent (the ValueError/IndexError is swallowed).

Running an FTD/FXOS train below 7.0 means the platform is very likely past its software maintenance and exposed to publicly disclosed CVEs. FTD 6.x trains have reached end of software maintenance/end of support, and Cisco has issued numerous advisories affecting Snort, the web/management services, SSL VPN, and FXOS on these releases.

Concrete risk: outdated firewall firmware is a favored target for ATT&CK Exploit Public-Facing Application (T1190) — network-edge devices with known, unpatched CVEs are exploited for initial access and persistence; several state-actor campaigns specifically target unpatched Cisco edge firewalls to implant on the device itself. A pre-auth RCE or auth-bypass on a perimeter FTD hands the attacker the control plane.

Blast radius: the firewall is the security boundary, so its compromise nullifies every policy it enforces and provides a persistent foothold that survives host reimaging behind it. Missing patches also often carry the fixes for DoS bugs that can take the firewall (and thus the whole segment) offline.

Platform nuance: on Firepower 4100/9300 the chassis runs a separate FXOS supervisor that is versioned and patched independently of the FTD application; on 1000/2100/3100 appliances FXOS is bundled and upgraded together with FTD. Upgrades are orchestrated from FMC (System > Updates) for the FTD app, while chassis FXOS on 4100/9300 is upgraded via Firepower Chassis Manager / FXOS CLI. Compliance frameworks (CIS FTD, NIST 800-53 SI-2 Flaw Remediation) require running supported, patched software.
steps: Confirm the exact running versions: on the FTD device run `show version` (FTD app + LINA), and on 4100/9300 chassis check FXOS with `show version` under the FXOS CLI (`connect fxos`) or Firepower Chassis Manager > Overview.
Record the model, current FTD version, and (where separate) the FXOS bundle version so you pick a compatible target.
Check the Cisco Secure Firewall / FMC compatibility matrix to select a supported target FTD release and the matching FXOS version for your hardware.
Review open Cisco Security Advisories for your current train at the Cisco Security Advisories portal to gauge urgency (KEV-listed CVEs = expedite).
Verify FMC itself is on a version equal to or newer than the target FTD version (FMC must be upgraded first): FMC Help > About or `show version`.
Download the required upgrade and readiness packages into FMC: System > Updates > Product Updates.
Run the FTD upgrade readiness check from FMC before scheduling: System > Updates > select package > Readiness Check.
Take a device backup: FMC System > Tools > Backup/Restore, and export the device configuration.
Schedule a maintenance window — FTD upgrades reboot the data plane and interrupt traffic (plan HA failover if clustered/HA).
For HA pairs, upgrade the standby first; FMC's upgrade wizard sequences this and performs the switchover to minimize outage.
On 4100/9300, upgrade chassis FXOS to the supported version first via Firepower Chassis Manager or FXOS CLI (`scope firmware` / `scope auto-install`), then upgrade the FTD logical device.
Initiate the FTD upgrade from FMC (System > Updates > Upgrade) and monitor progress; do not deploy other changes during the upgrade.
After reboot, confirm the device re-registers and reaches the healthy state in FMC (Devices > Device Management).
Redeploy policy if prompted (Deploy > Deployment) to resync configuration to the new version.
Validate traffic and services (VPN tunnels up, ACP enforcing, IPS/AMP active) before closing the window.
Remove obsolete upgrade packages and update your asset/patch records with the new baseline.
Establish/verify a recurring process to track advisories and schedule future patches (SI-2 program).
NGFW-PLAT-003.cli:
! === Check versions (FTD device CLI) ===
show version

! === FXOS chassis (Firepower 4100/9300) CLI ===
connect fxos
scope system
show version
! Upgrade path (chassis):
top
scope firmware
  show download-task
  scope auto-install
    install security-pack version <target-fxos-version> force

! === FTD application upgrade is driven from FMC (no device CLI) ===
! FMC: System > Updates > Product Updates > (download target + readiness)
! FMC: System > Tools > Backup/Restore > device backup
! FMC: System > Updates > Upgrade > select device (standby first for HA) > Upgrade
! FMC: Deploy > Deployment after upgrade completes
NGFW-PLAT-003.verify: After upgrade, `show version` on the FTD reports a major of 7 or higher (e.g. 7.2.x / 7.4.x) and, on 4100/9300, `show version` under FXOS reports the matching supported FXOS bundle. In FMC, Devices > Device Management shows the device Online/healthy at the new version and Deploy shows in-sync. Cross-check that the running version is no longer listed in any applicable open Cisco advisory.
NGFW-PLAT-003.rollback: FTD supports a revert to the pre-upgrade state for a limited window: FMC System > Updates > (device) > Revert Upgrade, or device CLI `configure revert`. Restore from the pre-upgrade backup if needed. Caveat: reverting returns the device to the outdated, vulnerable train — it re-exposes every CVE fixed in the newer release and should be temporary only.
NGFW-PLAT-003.impact: Highly disruptive. FTD upgrades reboot the appliance/data plane, dropping all transit traffic, VPN tunnels, and management sessions for the reboot duration; plan for an outage (minutes to tens of minutes) or use HA/clustering to fail over. FXOS chassis upgrades on 4100/9300 are similarly service-affecting. A reload is inherent to the process — this is a maintenance-window activity, not a hot change.
references: Cisco Secure Firewall Threat Defense / FMC Compatibility Guide and Upgrade Guide
Cisco Security Advisories portal (Cisco PSIRT) — FTD/FXOS advisories
CIS Cisco Secure Firewall Threat Defense Benchmark — Software Maintenance / Supported Versions
NIST SP 800-53 Rev5 SI-2 (Flaw Remediation), CM-8 (System Component Inventory)
CWE-1104 Use of Unmaintained Third Party Components; CWE-1035 Using Components with Known Vulnerabilities

NGFW-PLAT-004.risk:
NgfwPlatformAuditor.check_dns_security() runs only for device_type == "ftd". It sets has_dns_inspect = has_line(`(dns|domain).*inspect`) and, if no such line exists, raises a MEDIUM finding "DNS inspection not configured on FTD". The trigger is simply the absence of any config line where a DNS/domain token is followed by an inspect reference — i.e. no `inspect dns` policy-map/service-policy construct is present in the parsed configuration.

Without DNS inspection and DNS-layer security, the firewall treats DNS (UDP/TCP 53) as opaque pass-through. That leaves two gaps: protocol-level enforcement (malformed DNS, oversized messages, protocol abuse) and, more importantly, no DNS Security Intelligence / sinkholing to block resolution of known-malicious domains.

Concrete risk: DNS is a primary covert channel. Adversaries use ATT&CK Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol (T1048) and C2 over DNS (T1071.004) to tunnel data and command traffic inside DNS queries that flat firewalls happily forward. Malware also relies on DNS to resolve C2 and DGA domains; without DNS-layer blocking/sinkholing, that first resolution succeeds and the infection proceeds. Absence of inspection also permits DNS-based amplification participation and cache-poisoning-adjacent abuse.

Blast radius: any host behind the firewall that can send DNS can potentially exfiltrate data or reach C2 regardless of how tight the port-based rules are, because 53 is almost always open outbound. This undermines data-loss and C2-containment assumptions across the entire protected estate.

Platform nuance: FTD provides two complementary controls. LINA-level protocol inspection (`policy-map type inspect dns` bound via `service-policy`) enforces DNS message hygiene and is present by default in the base global policy but can be missing/removed. The higher-value control is the FMC DNS Policy (Policies > Access Control > DNS) which applies Security Intelligence DNS feeds and sinkholing, plus Umbrella/DNS category enforcement — this is configured in FMC, not device CLI. Compliance frameworks (CIS FTD DNS inspection, NIST 800-53 SC-7/SI-4) call for DNS-layer inspection and monitoring.
steps: Confirm current state on the device: `show service-policy | include dns` and `show running-config policy-map` to see whether an `inspect dns` map is applied to the global policy.
Check FMC for a DNS Policy and its assignment: Policies > Access Control > DNS, and whether the Access Control Policy references it.
Decide scope: enable LINA DNS protocol inspection (hygiene) AND an FMC DNS Policy with Security Intelligence/sinkhole (threat blocking) — you want both.
If LINA DNS inspection is missing, restore it via FlexConfig (FMC Objects > FlexConfig) since FMC owns the LINA config; use the built-in Default DNS inspection object.
Alternatively (lab/ASA-style) build the inspect map directly: `policy-map type inspect dns PRESET_DNS_MAP` with a `parameters` block and `message-length maximum 512`.
Attach it to the global policy: under `policy-map global_policy` / `class inspection_default` add `inspect dns PRESET_DNS_MAP`.
Ensure the service policy is applied globally: `service-policy global_policy global`.
In FMC, create/enable a DNS Policy: Policies > Access Control > DNS > add rules that Block or Sinkhole the Cisco Talos DNS Security Intelligence feeds (DNS Malware, C2, Phishing).
Configure a sinkhole object (Objects > Object Management > Sinkhole) so blocked lookups resolve to a controlled address, giving you infected-host visibility.
Add custom DNS block lists for any domains from local threat intel, and safe-list legitimate internal domains to avoid false positives.
Associate the DNS Policy with the Access Control Policy (ACP > Security Intelligence > DNS tab) so it is enforced.
Ensure the Talos/Security Intelligence feeds auto-update: System > Updates > enable recurring Security Intelligence feed updates.
Schedule a brief change window; DNS inspection/sinkholing rarely disrupts, but message-length limits and blocking can affect edge cases (large TXT/DNSSEC responses).
Deploy from FMC (Deploy > Deployment) and, if you edited LINA directly in a lab, `write memory`.
Validate protocol inspection is counting: `show service-policy inspect dns` shows hit counters incrementing.
Validate threat blocking: from a test host, resolve a known test/sinkholed domain and confirm it is redirected/blocked and logged in FMC Analysis > Connections / Security Intelligence Events.
Tune out false positives over a monitoring period and document the DNS policy baseline.
NGFW-PLAT-004.cli:
! === LINA DNS protocol inspection (apply via FMC FlexConfig on FTD) ===
policy-map type inspect dns PRESET_DNS_MAP
 parameters
  message-length maximum client auto
  message-length maximum 512
  no tcp-inspection
policy-map global_policy
 class inspection_default
  inspect dns PRESET_DNS_MAP
service-policy global_policy global

! === Threat-layer DNS control (authoritative, in FMC) ===
! Policies > Access Control > DNS:
!   Rule: Action=Sinkhole (or Block), DNS Lists = Talos 'DNS Malware/C2/Phishing'
! Objects > Object Management > Sinkhole: define sinkhole target for visibility
! ACP > Security Intelligence > DNS tab: attach the DNS policy
! System > Updates: enable recurring Security Intelligence feed updates
! Deploy > Deployment > select device > Deploy
NGFW-PLAT-004.verify: Device: `show service-policy inspect dns` shows the map applied with incrementing packet counters, and `show running-config policy-map global_policy` lists `inspect dns`. FMC: the DNS Policy is attached to the ACP and Analysis > Connections shows DNS Security Intelligence block/sinkhole events. Functional test: a lookup of a Talos-flagged/sinkhole test domain returns the sinkhole address (or is blocked) and appears in the event log.
NGFW-PLAT-004.rollback: Remove the inspect map (`no inspect dns PRESET_DNS_MAP` under the class, via FlexConfig) and/or unassign the DNS Policy from the ACP in FMC, then redeploy. Caveat: reverting restores opaque DNS pass-through, re-enabling DNS tunneling/exfiltration (T1048) and unblocked resolution of known-malicious C2/DGA domains.
NGFW-PLAT-004.impact: Low disruption. DNS protocol inspection and Security Intelligence sinkholing operate inline but are generally transparent to legitimate traffic; the main risk is over-tight message-length limits or over-broad block lists affecting unusual responses (large TXT records, DNSSEC, split-horizon internal domains) — mitigate with safe-lists. No reload; no VPN/session impact. The FMC deploy is a brief policy recompile.
references: CIS Cisco Secure Firewall Threat Defense Benchmark — DNS Inspection / DNS Policy
Cisco Secure Firewall Management Center Configuration Guide — DNS Policies and Sinkhole (Security Intelligence)
NIST SP 800-53 Rev5 SC-7 (Boundary Protection), SI-4 (System Monitoring), SI-3 (Malicious Code Protection)
CWE-406 Insufficient Control of Network Message Volume in a Session; MITRE ATT&CK T1048, T1071.004
NSA Network Infrastructure Security Guidance — DNS monitoring/filtering

NGFW-PLAT-005.risk:
NgfwPlatformAuditor.check_geolocation_updates() runs only for device_type == "ftd". It sets has_geo = has_line(`geo.*location|country.*rule`) and, if absent, raises a LOW finding "Geolocation-based rules not detected". The trigger is the absence of any config line matching a geolocation/country-rule token — i.e. no evidence in the parsed configuration that geolocation-based access control is in use. Because geolocation is defined inside FMC Access Control Policy rules (and applied at the Snort/ACP layer rather than as a distinct LINA statement), this check is a best-effort heuristic and is expected to fire on most device-only config exports.

Geolocation controls let you allow/deny/log traffic by source or destination country/continent using Cisco's GeoDB. Their absence is not itself a breach, but it forgoes a cheap, high-leverage reduction of attack surface: blocking or alerting on connections to/from countries where you have no business presence.

Concrete value: geolocation rules shrink exposure to opportunistic scanning and brute forcing (ATT&CK T1595 Active Scanning, T1110 Brute Force) that disproportionately originate from a handful of geographies, and they help contain C2/exfiltration to sanctioned regions. They are also a practical guardrail for sanctions/embargo compliance (blocking OFAC-embargoed countries).

Blast radius consideration is inverse here — this is an opportunity cost / defense-in-depth gap rather than an active vulnerability, hence the LOW severity. Over-reliance on geo is itself a pitfall (attackers proxy through allowed countries), so it complements, not replaces, IPS/SI/URL controls.

Platform nuance: this is purely an FMC-side control — there is no meaningful device-CLI equivalent, and the GeoDB must be kept current (System > Updates > Geolocation Updates) or country mappings drift and become inaccurate. On FTD the country condition is set in the ACP rule's Networks tab (Geolocation); ASA has no native equivalent. Compliance angle: CIS/NSA hardening guidance recommends geolocation filtering as boundary protection (NIST 800-53 SC-7, AC-4) and it supports sanctions compliance.
steps: Confirm the GeoDB is installed and current in FMC: System > Updates > Geolocation Updates — note the database date.
Enable recurring GeoDB updates so country mappings stay accurate: System > Updates > Geolocation Updates > enable a weekly recurring task.
Gather business input on which countries you legitimately transact with, and which are candidates for block or monitor-only (start with monitor to avoid outages).
Decide policy posture: default is usually allow-with-selective-block for high-risk/embargoed countries on internet-facing zones, plus alerting on unexpected geos to sensitive zones.
In FMC open the relevant Access Control Policy: Policies > Access Control > (policy).
Create a geolocation block rule: add a rule, Networks tab > Geolocation, select the target countries/continents as Source (for inbound) and/or Destination (for outbound), Action = Block.
Position the geo rule appropriately in the ACP order (early enough to take effect, but after any needed allow exceptions for legitimate partners).
Add allow exceptions above the block rule for known-good sources in otherwise-blocked countries (partner/VPN endpoints), using network objects.
Enable logging on the geo rule (Logging tab > Log at Beginning/End of Connection) so blocked/allowed geos are visible for tuning.
For a lower-risk rollout, first deploy the rule with Action = Monitor to observe volume before switching to Block.
Consider a separate high-severity alert rule for connections from sanctioned/embargoed countries to your crown-jewel zones.
Schedule the change; geo blocking can disrupt legitimate roaming users or CDN/cloud endpoints that resolve to unexpected regions — review before enforcing.
Deploy from FMC (Deploy > Deployment > select device > Deploy).
Validate enforcement: generate/observe a test connection from an in-scope country (or review Analysis > Connections) and confirm the geo rule matches and blocks/logs.
Review Analysis > Connection Events filtered by the geo rule for false positives (e.g. cloud providers) and add exceptions as needed.
Document the allowed/blocked country baseline and set a periodic review tied to business/sanctions changes.
Confirm the GeoDB recurring update task is succeeding on its schedule (System > Updates task history).
NGFW-PLAT-005.cli:
! === Geolocation is configured in FMC only (no FTD device CLI) ===
! 1) Keep the database current:
!    System > Updates > Geolocation Updates > Enable weekly recurring update
!
! 2) Build the rule in the Access Control Policy:
!    Policies > Access Control > <ACP> > Add Rule
!      Name:   BLOCK-HIGH-RISK-GEO
!      Networks tab > Geolocation: add Source countries/continents
!      Action: Monitor (initial rollout) -> Block (enforce)
!      Logging: Log at End of Connection
!    Add ALLOW exceptions above it for legitimate partner networks
!
! 3) Deploy > Deployment > select device > Deploy
! ASA has no native geolocation equivalent — n/a on ASA.
NGFW-PLAT-005.verify: In FMC the ACP shows the geolocation rule with the intended countries and Action, and the GeoDB date under System > Updates > Geolocation Updates is recent. Enforcement is proven in Analysis > Connections / Intrusion Events: connections from the blocked countries show the geo rule name with Action=Block (or logged under Monitor). The recurring geolocation update task shows recent successful runs in the task history.
NGFW-PLAT-005.rollback: Set the geo rule Action back to Monitor, or disable/delete the rule in the ACP, and redeploy; disable the recurring GeoDB task only if truly desired. Caveat: reverting removes country-based boundary filtering, re-widening exposure to scanning/brute-force from high-risk geographies and dropping the sanctions/embargo guardrail.
NGFW-PLAT-005.impact: Low-to-moderate and fully controllable. Because the control lives in the ACP, deploying it only recompiles Snort/policy with no reload and no impact to existing sessions beyond new-connection matching. The real operational risk is over-broad country blocks denying legitimate users, roaming staff, or cloud/CDN endpoints — mitigated by an initial Monitor phase and allow exceptions. No effect on VPN/management sessions.
references: Cisco Secure Firewall Management Center Configuration Guide — Geolocation in Access Control Rules; Geolocation Database (GeoDB) Updates
NSA/CISA Network Infrastructure Security Guidance — boundary filtering
CIS Cisco Secure Firewall Threat Defense Benchmark — Access Control / boundary filtering
NIST SP 800-53 Rev5 SC-7 (Boundary Protection), AC-4 (Information Flow Enforcement)
MITRE ATT&CK T1595 (Active Scanning), T1110 (Brute Force) — geo reduces exposure surface

NGFW-PLAT-006.risk:
NgfwPlatformAuditor.check_snmp_on_ftd() runs only for device_type == "ftd". It calls get_snmp_config() which returns all lines matching `^snmp-server\s`. Only if SNMP is configured (the list is non-empty) does it evaluate has_v3 = any line containing "v3" or "version 3"; when SNMP exists but no v3 indicator is found, it raises a MEDIUM finding "SNMP on FTD not using SNMPv3". So the trigger is: SNMP is enabled AND it appears to use only SNMPv1/v2c (community-string based) rather than SNMPv3. A device with no SNMP at all does not fire this check.

SNMPv1/v2c has no cryptographic protection: the community string is a shared, static, cleartext password sent in every packet, and payloads are unencrypted and unauthenticated. That is fundamentally unsafe on any monitored management network.

Concrete attack: an on-path adversary sniffs the community string (ATT&CK T1040 Network Sniffing) or simply guesses common values ("public"/"private") and then performs SNMP reconnaissance (T1046) to read the full running interface table, routing, ARP/CAM, and often the device configuration. With a writable community, they can push changes (T1565 Data Manipulation / config tampering). SNMPv2c is also spoofable, enabling forged traps or set operations. Because SNMP is UDP, source addresses are trivially spoofed absent host restrictions.

Blast radius: read access leaks the network topology and device internals that fuel further attacks; write access is effectively device control. Even read-only SNMPv2c across many devices gives an attacker an authoritative map of the environment.

Platform nuance: on FTD, SNMP is configured through FMC Platform Settings (SNMP) and compiled into LINA `snmp-server` statements. SNMPv3 requires an `snmp-server group ... v3 priv`, an `snmp-server user` with an auth protocol (SHA) and a privacy protocol (AES-128/192/256), and `snmp-server host ... version 3 <user>` — configured in FMC's SNMP page with an SNMPv3 user (auth SHA, privacy AES). Avoid MD5/DES, which are weak. Compliance frameworks (CIS FTD SNMP, NIST 800-53 IA-2/SC-8, PCI-DSS) require authenticated, encrypted SNMP or its removal.
steps: Inventory current SNMP config on the device: `show running-config snmp-server` (LINA view) to see community strings, hosts, and whether any v3 users/groups exist.
Confirm the management source(s) that must poll SNMP (the NMS/monitoring server IPs) so you can scope host statements.
In FMC open the SNMP platform settings: Devices > Platform Settings > (policy) > SNMP; enable SNMP and select Version 3.
Create an SNMPv3 group/user with strong crypto: authentication = SHA, privacy (encryption) = AES-128 or higher; set a strong, unique auth and priv passphrase.
Bind the SNMPv3 user to the polling host with the correct interface and security level (Priv/authPriv): add the NMS as an SNMP host using the v3 user.
Restrict SNMP to the specific NMS host(s) only — never leave it open to any source.
Remove all SNMPv1/v2c community configuration once v3 is proven working: delete the community entries in FMC SNMP settings (compiles to `no snmp-server community ...`).

If editing LINA directly in a lab, create the v3 objects: `snmp-server group NMSGRP v3 priv`, `snmp-server user nmsuser NMSGRP v3 auth sha <auth-pass> priv aes 256 <priv-pass>`, `snmp-server host management 10.20.0.10 version 3 nmsuser`.
Remove weak/legacy entries in the lab case: `no snmp-server community <string>` and any `snmp-server host ... version 2c ...` lines.
Ensure trap destinations also use v3 if traps are enabled, so notifications are authenticated/encrypted too.
Update the NMS/monitoring platform side to poll with the same SNMPv3 credentials (SHA auth + AES priv, authPriv).
Schedule a brief change window; changing SNMP will interrupt polling until the NMS is reconfigured with v3 credentials.
Deploy from FMC (Deploy > Deployment); if a LINA lab edit was made, `write memory`.
Validate v3 is active and v2c is gone: `show running-config snmp-server` shows `snmp-server user ... v3 auth sha ... priv aes ...` and no `community` lines.
From the NMS, perform an SNMPv3 authPriv poll and confirm it succeeds; confirm a v2c poll now fails.
Confirm the community string is fully removed and rotate it as a secret if it was ever shared/known; document the SNMPv3 credentials in a vault and enable syslog for SNMP auth failures.
NGFW-PLAT-006.cli:
! === FTD SNMPv3 (authoritative config in FMC Platform Settings > SNMP) ===
! FMC: Devices > Platform Settings > <policy> > SNMP
!   Enable SNMP, Version = 3
!   Add SNMPv3 user: Auth=SHA, Encryption(Privacy)=AES-128+, strong passphrases
!   Add SNMP Host = NMS IP on 'management' zone, bind to the v3 user
!   Remove any v1/v2c community entries
! Deploy > Deployment

! === Equivalent LINA statements (verify after deploy / lab-only edit) ===
snmp-server group NMSGRP v3 priv
snmp-server user nmsuser NMSGRP v3 auth sha <AUTH-PASS> priv aes 256 <PRIV-PASS>
snmp-server host management 10.20.0.10 version 3 nmsuser
no snmp-server community <old-community>
! (write memory only if edited directly on device)
NGFW-PLAT-006.verify: `show running-config snmp-server` shows an `snmp-server user ... v3 auth sha ... priv aes ...` line and an `snmp-server host ... version 3 ...` entry, with NO `snmp-server community` and no `version 2c` hosts. Functional proof: an SNMPv3 authPriv poll from the authorized NMS returns data, while an SNMPv2c poll with the old community fails/times out. In FMC, Platform Settings > SNMP shows Version 3 with the AES/SHA user.
NGFW-PLAT-006.rollback: Re-enable v2c temporarily by re-adding a community in FMC SNMP settings (or `snmp-server community <string>` + `snmp-server host ... version 2c <string>` in a lab) and redeploy. Caveat: reverting to SNMPv1/v2c restores cleartext, unauthenticated community-string SNMP — the string is sniffable and the device becomes readable (and possibly writable) to anyone on the management path.
NGFW-PLAT-006.impact: Low data-plane impact, but it interrupts monitoring: SNMP polling and traps stop working until the NMS is reconfigured with the SNMPv3 credentials, so coordinate the change with the monitoring team. No reload, no effect on transit traffic, VPN, or admin SSH sessions. The FMC deploy is a brief policy recompile.
references: CIS Cisco Secure Firewall Threat Defense Benchmark — SNMP Configuration (require SNMPv3 authPriv)
Cisco Secure Firewall Management Center Configuration Guide — Platform Settings: SNMP
NIST SP 800-53 Rev5 IA-2/IA-5 (Authentication), SC-8 (Transmission Confidentiality/Integrity), CM-6
CWE-319 Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information; CWE-798 Use of Hard-coded Credentials
MITRE ATT&CK T1040 (Network Sniffing), T1046 (Network Service Discovery)

NGFW-PLAT-007.risk:
NgfwPlatformAuditor.check_management_interface() runs only for device_type == "ftd". It calls get_interfaces(`management|mgmt`), which returns every parsed interface section whose header line matches "management" or "mgmt" (case-insensitive) — e.g. `interface Management0/0` or an interface with a mgmt-style name. For each such interface it checks whether any line in the section contains "access-group" or "access-list"; if none does, it raises a MEDIUM finding titled "Management interface without ACL: {intf}", where {intf} is the interface section header. The trigger is therefore a management-named interface whose config block has no ACL/access-group binding.

A management interface with no control-plane ACL means the services bound to it (SSH, HTTPS/FDM, SNMP, sftunnel to FMC, ping) accept traffic from any host that can route to that interface's subnet. On a shared or poorly-segmented management VLAN this is a broad, unauthenticated reconnaissance and attack surface.

Concrete risk: an attacker on the management segment can scan and reach the firewall's control plane, enabling ATT&CK Brute Force (T1110) against SSH/web logins, Exploitation of Remote Services (T1210) against management daemons, and Network Service Discovery (T1046). Because this is the plane that programs the firewall, a successful hit yields full policy control and a pivot point into every zone the device fronts.

Blast radius: the whole device and, transitively, all networks it protects. Management-plane exposure is consistently among the highest-value targets in edge-device intrusions.

Platform nuance matters here. On FTD there are two distinct cases: (1) the dedicated Management/diagnostic interface (Management0/0) is not governed by a LINA `access-group` — its SSH/HTTPS access is restricted with the device CLI `configure ssh-access-list` / `configure https-access-list` (each command overwrites the whole list, so list all admin subnets comma-separated) and FMC Platform Settings, so for that interface the correct control is the SSH/HTTP access list, not a data ACL; (2) a data interface repurposed for management is governed by a control-plane ACL applied with `access-group <acl> in interface <name> control-plane`. The scanner keys on the literal presence of access-group/access-list text, so the remediation is to add the appropriate restriction for whichever interface type {intf} is. Compliance frameworks (CIS FTD, NIST 800-53 SC-7/AC-17) require restricting management-plane access to defined admin networks.
steps: Identify exactly which interface {intf} is and its role: `show running-config interface {intf}` and `show interface {intf}` to see nameif, IP, and whether it is the dedicated Management interface or a data interface.

Determine which management services are bound to it (SSH/HTTPS/SNMP): `show running-config ssh`, `show running-config http`, `show running-config snmp-server`, plus `show ssh-access-list` / `show https-access-list` for the dedicated mgmt interface.
Define the authorized administrative source subnet(s) — the NMS/jump-host range — that legitimately need management access.

If {intf} is the DEDICATED management interface: restrict SSH at the device CLI in one command `configure ssh-access-list 10.20.0.0/24` and HTTPS `configure https-access-list 10.20.0.0/24` (each overwrites its whole list — comma-separate multiple subnets).
If {intf} is a DATA interface used for management: build a restrictive ACL. `configure terminal` then `access-list MGMT_CPL extended permit tcp 10.20.0.0 255.255.255.0 any eq 22`.

Add the other required management ports to the ACL: `access-list MGMT_CPL extended permit tcp 10.20.0.0 255.255.255.0 any eq 443` and SNMP `access-list MGMT_CPL extended permit udp 10.20.0.0 255.255.255.0 any eq 161` as needed.
Terminate the ACL with a logged deny for visibility: `access-list MGMT_CPL extended deny ip any any log`.
Apply the ACL to the interface control plane: `access-group MGMT_CPL in interface <nameif> control-plane`.
On FTD, do this authoritatively in FMC so it is not overwritten: Devices > Platform Settings for SSH/HTTP access lists, and/or an Extended ACL applied to the interface, then deploy.
Verify management services themselves are hardened (SSHv2, strong ciphers, HTTPS only) alongside the ACL so the restricted surface is also robust.
Confirm the sftunnel/management path to FMC is not broken by the new restriction — include the FMC IP in the allowed set if it traverses this interface.
Open a change window and keep console/OOB access, since a control-plane ACL or access-list can drop your current management session if your source is excluded.
Deploy from FMC (Deploy > Deployment); if a LINA edit was made directly, `write memory`.
Validate the binding exists (data interface): `show running-config access-group` shows MGMT_CPL applied to <nameif>, and `show access-list MGMT_CPL` shows the ACEs.
Functional test: management from an authorized host succeeds; from a non-authorized host it is refused/timed out and the deny ACE hit count increments.
Confirm the finding's condition is cleared: the {intf} section now contains an access-group (or the dedicated mgmt interface now has an ssh/https access-list).
Document the authorized management subnets, update the diagram, and enable syslog on the deny for ongoing monitoring.
NGFW-PLAT-007.cli:
! === Case A: DEDICATED FTD management interface (device CLI) ===
! Each command OVERWRITES the entire list; list all admin subnets comma-separated.
configure ssh-access-list 10.20.0.0/24
configure https-access-list 10.20.0.0/24
show ssh-access-list
show https-access-list

! === Case B: DATA interface used for management (control-plane ACL, LINA) ===
configure terminal
access-list MGMT_CPL extended permit tcp 10.20.0.0 255.255.255.0 any eq 22
access-list MGMT_CPL extended permit tcp 10.20.0.0 255.255.255.0 any eq 443
access-list MGMT_CPL extended permit udp 10.20.0.0 255.255.255.0 any eq 161
access-list MGMT_CPL extended deny ip any any log
access-group MGMT_CPL in interface mgmt control-plane
end
write memory
! On FMC-managed FTD, configure the equivalent in FMC (Platform Settings / interface ACL) and Deploy.
NGFW-PLAT-007.verify: For a data interface: `show running-config access-group` shows `access-group MGMT_CPL in interface <nameif> control-plane` and `show access-list MGMT_CPL` lists the scoped ACEs with the terminal `deny ip any any log`. For the dedicated management interface: `show ssh-access-list` and `show https-access-list` show the restricted admin network(s) with no 0.0.0.0/0. Functional proof: management from an authorized host works; from an unauthorized host it is denied and the deny ACE / access-list counter increments.
NGFW-PLAT-007.rollback: Remove the binding to restore open access: `no access-group MGMT_CPL in interface <nameif> control-plane` (data interface) or widen `configure ssh-access-list 0.0.0.0/0` / `configure https-access-list 0.0.0.0/0` (dedicated mgmt), then `write memory`/redeploy. Caveat: reverting re-exposes the firewall's management services (SSH/HTTPS/SNMP/sftunnel) to any host that can reach the interface, restoring the control-plane brute-force and remote-exploit risk.
NGFW-PLAT-007.impact: Disruptive to the management plane only. A control-plane ACL or SSH/HTTPS access-list that omits your current source IP will drop your active management session and can lock you out — always keep console/OOB access and include the FMC and NMS addresses. Data/transit traffic, VPN tunnels, and forwarding are unaffected; no reload is required. On FMC-managed FTD the deploy briefly recompiles policy without dropping the box.
references: CIS Cisco Secure Firewall Threat Defense Benchmark — Management Interface / Control-Plane Access
Cisco Secure Firewall Threat Defense Command Reference — configure ssh-access-list / configure https-access-list; access-group control-plane
NIST SP 800-53 Rev5 SC-7 (Boundary Protection), AC-17 (Remote Access), AC-3 (Access Enforcement)
CWE-284 Improper Access Control; CWE-306 Missing Authentication for Critical Function
Cisco Secure Firewall ASA/FTD Configuration Guide — Control-Plane ACLs (access-group ... control-plane) and management access

SVC-001.risk:
The scanner iterates its DANGEROUS_SERVICES table and flags any entry whose enabling line is present in the running-config and is NOT negated by a matching `no` line (the code compares `^<service>` against `^no\s+<service>`). The table is IOS / IOS-XE (and Catalyst 9800 WLC) oriented and covers: `ip finger`, `ip bootp server`, `ip identd`, `service pad`, `ip http server`, `service tcp-small-servers`, `service udp-small-servers`, `service config`, and `ip domain-lookup`. Each service found enabled increments the count and the finding is raised at HIGH severity listing every offender.

These legacy services expand the attack surface with little or no operational value. `service tcp-small-servers` / `service udp-small-servers` expose echo/discard/daytime/chargen; the UDP chargen and echo services are classic reflection/amplification sources abused for volumetric DDoS (ATT&CK T1498 Network Denial of Service) and for looping traffic between two devices.

`ip finger` and `ip identd` enable remote user/session enumeration (T1592/T1590 reconnaissance) that helps an attacker profile logged-in admins before a credential attack. `ip http server` stands up an unauthenticated-by-default, cleartext HTTP management plane where credentials and session data can be sniffed (T1040) and which has historically carried critical auth-bypass CVEs.

`service config` is the most dangerous: it makes the device attempt to load its configuration from the network at boot, which an on-path attacker can hijack to inject a malicious config or IOS image (T1601 Modify System Image / boot-time supply-chain compromise). `ip bootp server` and `service pad` (X.25 PAD) are obsolete in nearly all modern deployments.

`ip domain-lookup` is lower-risk but causes mistyped EXEC commands to be treated as hostnames and broadcast for DNS resolution, hanging the CLI and leaking internal command strings. Note the detection keys on the hyphenated `ip domain-lookup` form; modern IOS-XE writes `ip domain lookup` (space-separated), so a spaced form will not trip this specific entry.

Blast radius: an enabled HTTP server or writable config vector can lead to full device compromise; small-servers turn the router into a DDoS reflector implicating the whole organisation. Platform nuance: this list is IOS/IOS-XE specific — the class SUPPORTED_PLATFORMS is {ios, iosxe, wlc}. NX-OS uses the `feature` model (e.g. `no feature telnet`) and ASA/FTD ship a fixed service set, so the check is correctly scoped away from them. On the Catalyst 9800 (IOS-XE) most small-servers are absent by default, so a hit there usually means a legacy carry-over config.

Standards angle: this maps directly to least-functionality hardening (NIST 800-53 CM-7) and the CIS Cisco IOS services-disable recommendations.
steps: Take a config snapshot for change control: `show running-config | include finger|bootp|identd|service pad|http server|small-servers|service config|domain`.
Confirm the platform is IOS/IOS-XE (or Cat 9800): `show version` — the check does not apply to NX-OS/ASA/FTD which use different service models.
Verify the HTTP server is not actively used for a legitimate GUI (e.g. WebUI, PnP, guest portal) before disabling: `show ip http server status`.
Schedule a brief maintenance window only if `ip http server`/`ip domain-lookup` removal could affect an in-use tool; the small-servers/finger/pad removals are non-disruptive.
Enter global config: `configure terminal`.
Disable finger: `no ip finger` (and legacy `no service finger` if that older form is present).
Disable BOOTP server: `no ip bootp server`.
Disable ident: `no ip identd`.
Disable X.25 PAD: `no service pad`.
Disable TCP small servers: `no service tcp-small-servers`.
Disable UDP small servers: `no service udp-small-servers`.
Disable network config auto-load: `no service config`.

Disable the cleartext HTTP server: `no ip http server`. If a web UI is required, instead run HTTPS only: `ip http secure-server`, `ip http authentication local` (or `aaa`), and restrict it with `ip http access-class <acl>`.
Disable broadcast DNS lookups on mistyped commands: `no ip domain lookup` (older IOS uses `no ip domain-lookup`).
If DNS resolution is genuinely needed, keep lookups but pin a resolver: `ip name-server <dns-ip>` and set `ip domain lookup source-interface <mgmt-int>` rather than broadcasting.
Exit config: `end`.
Re-run the inventory command from step 1 to confirm each line now shows the `no` form or is absent.
Save the configuration: `copy running-config startup-config` (or `write memory`).
Document the change and update the golden/baseline config template so the services stay disabled on future deployments.
SVC-001.cli:
configure terminal
no ip finger
no ip bootp server
no ip identd
no service pad
no service tcp-small-servers
no service udp-small-servers
no service config
no ip http server
no ip domain lookup
end
copy running-config startup-config
SVC-001.verify: Run `show running-config | include finger|bootp|identd|service pad|http server|small-servers|service config|domain`. Expected: the disruptive services no longer appear, or appear as `no ...` (IOS omits default-off services entirely, and shows `no service config`, `no ip domain lookup`, `no ip bootp server` where they were explicitly negated). `show ip http server status` should report HTTP server status: Disabled.
SVC-001.rollback: Re-enable a specific service with its positive form, e.g. `ip http server` or `service config`. Caveat: restoring `ip http server` re-exposes an unencrypted management plane and any web-UI auth-bypass CVEs; restoring `service config` re-opens the boot-time network config-injection vector.
SVC-001.impact: Low operational impact and non-disruptive for finger/identd/pad/small-servers/bootp. Disabling `ip http server` will break any tool relying on plain HTTP to the device (migrate to `ip http secure-server`). `no ip domain lookup` only stops name resolution of typed hostnames. No reload is required; no admin/VPN sessions are dropped.
references: Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — Disable Unused Services (finger, BOOTP, ident, PAD, TCP/UDP small servers, service config, HTTP)
CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark — Disable unnecessary services
CWE-1188: Insecure Default Initialization of Resource; CWE-16: Configuration

SVC-002.risk:
The scanner pulls all SNMP lines via get_snmp_config() and detects the condition where SNMPv1/v2c community strings exist (a line matching `snmp-server community <string> RO|RW`) while NO SNMPv3 group is defined (no line matching `snmp-server group <name> v3`). When community strings are present without any v3 group, the finding fires at HIGH severity.

SNMPv1 and v2c have no cryptographic protection whatsoever. The community string acts as a shared plaintext password carried in every request/response and, on v1/v2c, is transmitted in the clear across the network. An on-path attacker or anyone able to sniff a management segment can capture the community string (ATT&CK T1040 Network Sniffing) and immediately gain the same access the NMS has.

Because there is no message authentication, v1/v2c is also trivially spoofable: an attacker who learns or guesses the community can forge requests from any source address the ACL permits (T1078/T1557). With a read community they can walk the entire MIB — interfaces, routing tables, ARP/CAM, IP inventory, running-config fragments — providing a complete network map for lateral movement.

Blast radius: read access alone is a serious reconnaissance and data-exposure problem across the managed estate; if the community is reused across devices (common), one captured string compromises the whole fleet. SNMPv3 solves this with per-user authentication (HMAC-SHA) and privacy (AES) so credentials are never sent in the clear and messages are integrity-protected and optionally encrypted.

Platform nuance: the detection is written for IOS-syntax `snmp-server community`/`snmp-server group` lines, so it targets IOS, IOS-XE and Catalyst 9800 WLC (SUPPORTED_PLATFORMS = {ios, iosxe, wlc}). NX-OS and ASA render SNMP differently and are out of scope here. On AireOS WLCs SNMPv3 is configured via `config snmp v3user` commands rather than IOS syntax; the Catalyst 9800 uses the IOS-XE commands shown below.

Standards angle: cleartext SNMP violates transmission-confidentiality controls (NIST 800-53 SC-8) and the CIS requirement to use SNMPv3 with auth+priv. Migrating removes CWE-319 (Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information) exposure.
steps: Inventory current SNMP config: `show running-config | include snmp-server` and `show snmp community`.
Identify every NMS/poller and its source IP so you can build an ACL and pre-stage the v3 user on the management platform.
Confirm the image supports strong crypto (k9): `show version | include image|K9` — AES/SHA privacy requires a crypto-capable image.
Enter config mode: `configure terminal`.
Create a restrictive management ACL first: `ip access-list standard SNMP-NMS` then `permit host <nms-ip>` and `deny any log`.
Define an SNMP view scoping what can be read: `snmp-server view SNMP-VIEW iso included` (tighten to specific OIDs if desired).
Create the SNMPv3 group with privacy (auth + encryption): `snmp-server group SNMP-SEC v3 priv read SNMP-VIEW access SNMP-NMS`.
Create the SNMPv3 user bound to that group: `snmp-server user SNMPADMIN SNMP-SEC v3 auth sha <AUTH-PASSPHRASE> priv aes 256 <PRIV-PASSPHRASE>`.
If the platform/image only supports 128-bit privacy, use `priv aes 128 <key>` instead of `aes 256`.
Update the NMS (SolarWinds/PRTG/etc.) with the same v3 user, SHA auth and AES priv passphrases and test a poll from the NMS.
Once v3 polling is confirmed working, remove each legacy community: `no snmp-server community <string> RO` and `no snmp-server community <string> RW`.
Remove any lingering v1/v2c trap hosts and re-add them as v3 (see SVC-006).
Verify no community lines remain: `show running-config | include snmp-server community`.
Exit config: `end`.
Confirm the v3 user and group: `show snmp user` and `show snmp group`.
