Save the configuration: `copy running-config startup-config`.
Roll the same v3 user/group/ACL into the device baseline template and rotate the passphrases per your credential-management policy.
SVC-002.cli:
configure terminal
ip access-list standard SNMP-NMS
 permit host 10.10.10.5
 deny any log
exit
snmp-server view SNMP-VIEW iso included
snmp-server group SNMP-SEC v3 priv read SNMP-VIEW access SNMP-NMS
snmp-server user SNMPADMIN SNMP-SEC v3 auth sha S0me-Auth-Passphrase priv aes 256 S0me-Priv-Passphrase
! after confirming v3 polling works, remove legacy communities:
no snmp-server community public RO
end
copy running-config startup-config
SVC-002.verify: Run `show snmp group` — expected: group SNMP-SEC with security model v3 and 'privacy'. Run `show snmp user` — expected: SNMPADMIN with Authentication Protocol SHA and Privacy Protocol AES256. Run `show running-config | include snmp-server community` — expected: no output (no v1/v2c communities remain).
SVC-002.rollback: Temporarily re-add a community with an ACL: `snmp-server community <complex-string> RO SNMP-NMS`. Caveat: reverting to v1/v2c re-exposes the community as cleartext on the wire and removes message authentication, allowing sniffing and request spoofing.
SVC-002.impact: Non-disruptive to the data plane and to admin/VPN sessions; no reload. Monitoring visibility WILL break if the NMS is not reconfigured for SNMPv3 before the communities are removed — stage the v3 user on the NMS and confirm a successful poll before deleting communities.
references: Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — Use SNMPv3 / SNMP hardening
CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark — SNMP: require SNMPv3 with auth and privacy
NIST SP 800-53 Rev.5 SC-8 (Transmission Confidentiality and Integrity), IA-2/IA-5
CWE-319: Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information

SVC-003.risk:
This CRITICAL check parses each `snmp-server community <string> RO|RW` line, lowercases the community string and compares it against a well-known-default set: public, private, community, cisco, default. Any match raises SVC-003, reporting the exact community and its permission (RO/RW). The offending line is included verbatim in affected_items. It only runs when v1/v2c communities are present (has_v1v2 is true).

Default and dictionary community strings are the very first thing an attacker or automated scanner tries. Mass-scan tools and services like Shodan probe UDP/161 with `public`/`private` constantly; this is credential-based access using known defaults (ATT&CK T1078 Valid Accounts / default credentials, aided by T1046 Network Service Discovery).

With a default RO community (e.g. `public`), an attacker can walk the entire MIB and harvest a full picture of the device and network — interface list, IP addressing, routing/ARP/CAM tables, and configuration details — enabling targeted lateral movement. If the default community has RW permission (e.g. `private`), the consequences escalate to full device takeover: via the Cisco CISCO-CONFIG-COPY-MIB an attacker can push a modified configuration or TFTP the running-config off the box (see SVC-004).

Blast radius: because default strings are so often reused across an entire fleet, one guessed default can unlock every device that shares it. This is a high-likelihood, high-impact exposure — hence CRITICAL — and is routinely the initial foothold in real intrusions.

Platform nuance: the check keys on IOS-format `snmp-server community` lines, so it applies to IOS, IOS-XE and Catalyst 9800 WLC (SUPPORTED_PLATFORMS = {ios, iosxe, wlc}). NX-OS/ASA are out of scope for this parser. The best long-term fix is to eliminate community-based SNMP entirely and move to SNMPv3 (see SVC-002); if v1/v2c must remain temporarily, the string must be replaced with a long, random, non-guessable value AND ACL-restricted.

Standards angle: default credentials map to CWE-798 (Use of Hard-coded/Default Credentials) and CWE-1392 (Use of Default Credentials), and violate NIST 800-53 IA-5 (Authenticator Management) and the CIS prohibition on default community strings.
steps: List all communities and permissions: `show running-config | include snmp-server community` and `show snmp community`.
Flag every string matching a default (public, private, community, cisco, default) and note whether RO or RW.
Prefer the strategic fix — migrate to SNMPv3 and remove communities entirely (see SVC-002 steps).
If v1/v2c must stay temporarily, generate a long random community string (>= 16 chars, mixed) from a password manager — do not reuse across devices.
Build/confirm a management ACL: `ip access-list standard SNMP-NMS` with `permit host <nms-ip>` and `deny any log`.
Enter config mode: `configure terminal`.
Add the new non-default, ACL-bound community BEFORE removing the old one to avoid a monitoring gap: `snmp-server community <RANDOM-STRING> RO SNMP-NMS`.
Update the NMS with the new community string and confirm a successful poll.
Remove the default community: `no snmp-server community public RO` (repeat for each default found, matching its exact permission).
If a default RW community existed, treat it as a possible compromise: also review `show running-config` for unexpected changes and rotate other credentials.
Confirm no default strings remain: `show running-config | include snmp-server community`.
Where possible, replace the community entirely with an SNMPv3 user/group per SVC-002 and then remove all communities.
Exit config: `end`.
Re-run `show snmp community` to validate only the intended, non-default, ACL-bound strings exist.
Save: `copy running-config startup-config`.
Search the rest of the fleet for the same default string and remediate everywhere it appears.
Update the golden config/baseline so default communities can never be re-introduced by templates.
SVC-003.cli:
configure terminal
ip access-list standard SNMP-NMS
 permit host 10.10.10.5
 deny any log
exit
! add replacement first, then remove the default:
snmp-server community 9x!Kd3RvQ2pLm7Tz RO SNMP-NMS
no snmp-server community public RO
end
copy running-config startup-config
SVC-003.verify: Run `show running-config | include snmp-server community`. Expected: no line containing public/private/community/cisco/default; only the new random, ACL-bound community (or, ideally, none at all after SNMPv3 migration). `show snmp community` should list only the intended string with its access list name.
SVC-003.rollback: Re-add the previous community with `snmp-server community <string> RO <acl>`. Caveat: restoring a default string like `public` re-opens the device to the very first credential an internet/inside scanner will try, effectively unauthenticated SNMP read access.
SVC-003.impact: Non-disruptive; no reload and no session loss. The only risk is a monitoring blind spot if the NMS is not updated with the new string first — always add-new-then-remove-old and confirm a poll succeeds before deleting the default.
references: Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — SNMP Community Strings (avoid defaults)
CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark — do not use default SNMP community strings
CWE-798: Use of Hard-coded Credentials; CWE-1392: Use of Default Credentials

SVC-004.risk:
The scanner examines each `snmp-server community <string> RO|RW` line and, whenever the permission field equals RW (read-write), raises SVC-004 at HIGH severity. The community string itself is masked in the finding output (the code substitutes `community ****`) so the report does not leak the secret, but the presence of write access is flagged. It runs only when v1/v2c communities are present.

An SNMP read-write community is effectively a remote administrative credential. Anyone who possesses it can issue SNMP SET operations against writable MIB objects. On Cisco IOS/IOS-XE the CISCO-CONFIG-COPY-MIB (ccCopyEntry) lets an SNMP RW client instruct the device to copy its running-config to an attacker-controlled TFTP/FTP server, or to copy a new config from that server onto the device. That is full configuration exfiltration and modification over UDP/161 with only the community string (ATT&CK T1602 Data from Configuration Repository, T1078 Valid Accounts, and effectively remote config tampering).

Because v1/v2c carries the community in cleartext and cannot authenticate the sender, an RW community is doubly dangerous: it is both easy to capture and immediately powerful once captured. An attacker can add a rogue admin user, alter ACLs/routing, disable logging, or pivot deeper — a single sniffed RW string can lead to complete device compromise and onward lateral movement.

Blast radius: device-level total compromise, and fleet-wide if the RW string is reused. This is why write access via community-based SNMP is discouraged outright.

Platform nuance: detection keys on IOS-syntax community lines (IOS/IOS-XE/Cat9800 WLC per SUPPORTED_PLATFORMS). Most monitoring only requires read access, so RW is rarely justified; where write operations are genuinely needed (some automation), they should be done via SNMPv3 with a dedicated, tightly ACL-scoped write view, never via a v1/v2c RW community.

Standards angle: excessive privilege via SNMP RW maps to NIST 800-53 AC-6 (Least Privilege) and CM-5 (Access Restrictions for Change), and to CWE-269 (Improper Privilege Management). CIS recommends against RW communities.
steps: Identify all RW communities: `show running-config | include snmp-server community`.
Determine whether any tool legitimately performs SNMP writes; the vast majority of monitoring is read-only, so RW is usually removable.
If RW is not required, plan straight removal; if it is required, plan an SNMPv3 write-view replacement instead.
Build/confirm a strict management ACL limited to the exact write-authorised host: `ip access-list standard SNMP-RW` then `permit host <automation-ip>` and `deny any log`.
Enter config mode: `configure terminal`.
For the strategic fix, create an SNMPv3 write path: `snmp-server view RW-VIEW iso included`, then `snmp-server group SNMP-RW-SEC v3 priv read RW-VIEW write RW-VIEW access SNMP-RW`.
Create the v3 write user: `snmp-server user RWUSER SNMP-RW-SEC v3 auth sha <AUTH> priv aes 256 <PRIV>`.
Update the automation platform to use SNMPv3 and confirm a test SET succeeds.
Remove the RW community: `no snmp-server community <string> RW`.
If the RW community used a default string, treat the device as potentially compromised and audit `show running-config` for unauthorised changes and rogue users.
If RW is simply not needed, skip the v3 write path and just remove the community line.
Confirm no RW community remains: `show running-config | include snmp-server community`.
Exit config: `end`.
Validate the write path (if kept): `show snmp group` shows the group with a write view; `show snmp user` shows the v3 user.
Save: `copy running-config startup-config`.
Search the fleet for the same RW string and remediate everywhere; rotate it if it was shared.
Update baseline templates to prohibit v1/v2c RW communities.
SVC-004.cli:
configure terminal
! remove the read-write community outright if not needed:
no snmp-server community MyRwString RW
! OR, if write access is genuinely required, use SNMPv3 with a scoped write view:
ip access-list standard SNMP-RW
 permit host 10.10.10.9
 deny any log
exit
snmp-server view RW-VIEW iso included
snmp-server group SNMP-RW-SEC v3 priv read RW-VIEW write RW-VIEW access SNMP-RW
snmp-server user RWUSER SNMP-RW-SEC v3 auth sha Auth-Pass-Here priv aes 256 Priv-Pass-Here
end
copy running-config startup-config
SVC-004.verify: Run `show running-config | include snmp-server community`. Expected: no line ending in RW. If an SNMPv3 write group was created, `show snmp group` shows SNMP-RW-SEC with a writeview of RW-VIEW and security model v3/privacy. `show snmp user` lists RWUSER with SHA/AES.
SVC-004.rollback: Re-add with `snmp-server community <string> RW <acl>`. Caveat: restoring an RW community re-enables remote configuration change and running-config exfiltration over SNMP (config-copy MIB) to anyone who holds or sniffs the string.
SVC-004.impact: Non-disruptive to traffic; no reload or session drop. Removing RW breaks only tools that actually perform SNMP writes (rare) — confirm none do, or stage the SNMPv3 write path first.
references: Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — SNMP (avoid RW community access)
CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark — SNMP: do not configure read-write communities
NIST SP 800-53 Rev.5 AC-6 (Least Privilege), CM-5 (Access Restrictions for Change)

SVC-005.risk:
This check inspects the SNMP configuration for lines containing the word 'community' and tests whether an access-list is bound. It splits each such line on whitespace; the canonical form is `snmp-server community <string> <RO|RW> [ACL]`, which is 4 tokens without an ACL and 5 with one. When a community line has 4 or fewer tokens (i.e. no trailing ACL name/number), it raises SVC-005 at HIGH severity, masking the community in output, and it reports on the FIRST such unrestricted community found (the loop breaks after the first hit).

Without an ACL, the device will answer SNMP queries carrying the correct community from ANY source IP that can reach UDP/161. Combined with cleartext v1/v2c community transport, this means any host on a reachable network — or any attacker who has sniffed or guessed the string — can poll the device (ATT&CK T1046 Network Service Discovery, T1590/T1592 reconnaissance). The ACL is the single most important compensating control when community-based SNMP must exist: it constrains who can even attempt to use the string.

Blast radius: an unrestricted RO community exposes full MIB reconnaissance to the entire reachable network; an unrestricted RW community (see SVC-004) exposes remote config change/exfiltration to the same broad audience. If the device management interface is inadvertently reachable from user VLANs, partner links, or the internet, unrestricted SNMP becomes a direct, low-effort attack path.

Platform nuance: detection targets IOS-syntax community lines (IOS/IOS-XE/Cat9800 WLC per SUPPORTED_PLATFORMS). On IOS the ACL can be a standard numbered ACL (1-99), a named standard ACL, and can be combined with `snmp-server community <str> RO ipv6 <acl6>` for v6. The best fix is still SNMPv3 (which uses `access <acl>` on the group), but where communities remain they must always carry an ACL. Note the parser only tokenises whitespace, so a community that uses a named ACL still needs that fifth token present.

Standards angle: missing source restriction maps to NIST 800-53 AC-3/AC-4 and SC-7 (Boundary Protection), and CWE-284 (Improper Access Control). CIS requires SNMP access to be restricted by ACL.
steps: List community lines and check for a trailing ACL token: `show running-config | include snmp-server community`.
Enumerate the legitimate NMS/poller source IPs (and management subnet) that must reach SNMP.
Enter config mode: `configure terminal`.
Create a standard ACL naming the permitted managers: `ip access-list standard SNMP-NMS`.
Add explicit permits: `permit host 10.10.10.5` (repeat per NMS) or `permit 10.10.10.0 0.0.0.255` for a management subnet.
Add an explicit deny with logging for visibility: `deny any log`, then `exit`.
Re-apply each community WITH the ACL bound (this overwrites the existing line): `snmp-server community <string> RO SNMP-NMS`.
For any RW community that must persist, bind an even tighter ACL: `snmp-server community <string> RW SNMP-RW`.
If IPv6 SNMP is used, also bind an IPv6 ACL: `snmp-server community <string> RO ipv6 SNMP-NMS-V6`.
Prefer replacing communities with SNMPv3 groups that use `access <acl>` (see SVC-002) as the durable fix.
Bind SNMP to a specific management interface/VRF where the platform supports it (e.g. under the SNMPv3 group or via VRF-aware polling).
Confirm each community line now ends with an ACL: `show running-config | include snmp-server community`.
Validate the ACL contents: `show ip access-lists SNMP-NMS`.
Test a poll from an allowed NMS (should succeed) and, if feasible, from a non-listed host (should be dropped).
Exit config: `end`.
Save: `copy running-config startup-config`.
Sweep the fleet for other unrestricted community lines and apply the same ACL pattern.
SVC-005.cli:
configure terminal
ip access-list standard SNMP-NMS
 permit host 10.10.10.5
 permit 10.10.10.0 0.0.0.255
 deny any log
exit
! re-apply the community with the ACL bound (overwrites the unrestricted line):
snmp-server community 9x!Kd3RvQ2pLm7Tz RO SNMP-NMS
end
copy running-config startup-config
SVC-005.verify: Run `show running-config | include snmp-server community`. Expected: every community line ends with an ACL token (e.g. `... RO SNMP-NMS`). Run `show ip access-lists SNMP-NMS` and confirm it lists the intended manager hosts/subnet with a trailing `deny any`.
SVC-005.rollback: Re-enter the community without the ACL: `snmp-server community <string> RO`. Caveat: removing the ACL re-opens SNMP to every host that can reach UDP/161, so any sniffed or guessed community becomes usable from anywhere on the reachable network.
SVC-005.impact: Non-disruptive if the ACL correctly includes all real pollers; no reload or session drop. Risk is a monitoring outage if an NMS source IP (or NAT address) is omitted from the ACL — enumerate all pollers first and include a `deny any log` to spot missed sources in logs.
references: Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — Restrict SNMP Access with ACLs
CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark — restrict SNMP with an access-list
NIST SP 800-53 Rev.5 AC-3, SC-7 (Boundary Protection)

SVC-006.risk:
This check looks for SNMP notification/trap destinations — lines containing `snmp-server host`. If any trap host is configured, it examines each destination and, when a host line does not specify `version 3` (or `v3`), raises SVC-006 at MEDIUM severity, listing the trap targets with the community masked. In other words it flags trap/inform destinations still using v1 or v2c, and it reports once (the loop breaks after the first non-v3 host).

SNMP traps and informs carry operational and sometimes sensitive state to the NMS: interface up/down, authentication failures, config-change notifications, environmental alarms, and more. Under v1/v2c these notifications are sent with a cleartext community and no authentication, so an on-path attacker can read them (ATT&CK T1040 Network Sniffing) to learn device state and topology, and can also forge or suppress traps. Forged traps can mislead operators or trigger unwanted automation; suppression/spoofing supports defense evasion (T1562 Impair Defenses) by hiding the symptoms of an attack from the monitoring platform.

Blast radius: loss of confidentiality and integrity of the monitoring/alerting channel. If an attacker can inject false traps or hide real ones, they undermine incident detection across every device pointing at that NMS. SNMPv3 traps/informs provide authentication and (with priv) encryption of the notification stream, closing this gap.

Platform nuance: detection targets IOS-syntax `snmp-server host` lines (IOS/IOS-XE/Cat9800 WLC per SUPPORTED_PLATFORMS). SNMPv3 informs (`informs version 3`) are acknowledged and preferred over traps for reliability but require the engine-ID of the receiver to be known; traps are unacknowledged. The trap host must reference an SNMPv3 user that exists on the device and is provisioned on the NMS with matching auth/priv. On IOS-XE the enabling of specific trap types uses `snmp-server enable traps <type>` independently of the host security level.

Standards angle: unauthenticated/cleartext notifications map to NIST 800-53 SC-8 (Transmission Confidentiality/Integrity) and AU-9 (Protection of Audit Information), and to CWE-319. CIS recommends SNMPv3 for trap destinations.
steps: List current trap destinations and their versions: `show running-config | include snmp-server host`.
Confirm which SNMP trap types are enabled: `show running-config | include snmp-server enable traps`.
Ensure an SNMPv3 user suitable for notifications exists (create per SVC-002 if not): `show snmp user`.
On the receiving NMS, provision the same v3 user (SHA auth + AES priv) so it can validate/decrypt notifications.
Enter config mode: `configure terminal`.
Remove each legacy v1/v2c trap host: `no snmp-server host <ip> <community>` (match the exact existing line).
Add the destination back as SNMPv3 with privacy: `snmp-server host <ip> version 3 priv <v3user>`.
Prefer acknowledged informs where reliability matters: `snmp-server host <ip> informs version 3 priv <v3user>` (requires the receiver engine-ID to be learned/configured).
Optionally restrict which notifications are sent by specifying trap types after the user, e.g. `snmp-server host <ip> version 3 priv <v3user> config`.
Enable the desired trap categories (separate commands per category): `snmp-server enable traps snmp` and `snmp-server enable traps config`.
Set the source interface so traps originate from the management address: `snmp-server trap-source <mgmt-interface>` (and `snmp-server source-interface informs <mgmt-interface>` for informs).
Confirm all trap hosts now show version 3: `show running-config | include snmp-server host`.
Trigger a test notification (e.g. shut/no-shut a lab interface) and confirm the NMS receives it under v3.
Exit config: `end`.
Validate configured notification targets where supported: `show snmp host`.
Save: `copy running-config startup-config`.
Repeat across the fleet and bake v3 trap hosts into the baseline template.
SVC-006.cli:
configure terminal
! remove the legacy v2c trap host:
no snmp-server host 10.10.10.20 TrapComm
! add it back as SNMPv3 with authentication + encryption:
snmp-server host 10.10.10.20 version 3 priv SNMPADMIN
snmp-server trap-source Loopback0
snmp-server enable traps snmp
snmp-server enable traps config
end
copy running-config startup-config
SVC-006.verify: Run `show running-config | include snmp-server host`. Expected: every destination line contains `version 3 priv` (or `informs version 3 priv`) and references a valid v3 user — no bare community/v2c host lines remain. Generate a test event and confirm the NMS logs the notification as SNMPv3.
SVC-006.rollback: Re-add the v2c host: `snmp-server host <ip> version 2c <community>`. Caveat: reverting sends traps/informs in cleartext with no authentication, allowing an attacker to read, forge, or suppress the device's notifications to the NMS.
SVC-006.impact: Non-disruptive to data traffic; no reload or session loss. Notifications will fail to be validated at the NMS if the matching SNMPv3 user is not provisioned there first, so stage the receiver before switching the host lines.
references: Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — SNMP notifications / traps hardening
CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark — configure SNMP trap destinations with SNMPv3
NIST SP 800-53 Rev.5 SC-8, AU-9 (Protection of Audit Information)
CWE-319: Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information

SVC-007.risk:
This LOW-severity check tests for the presence of `service tcp-keepalives-in` in the running-config (a line matching `^service tcp-keepalives-in`). If it is absent, SVC-007 is raised. The scanner's remediation also recommends the companion `service tcp-keepalives-out`.

TCP keepalives-in tells the device to send periodic keepalive probes on inbound TCP connections (VTY sessions to Telnet/SSH, and other TCP services the device terminates). Without keepalives, a management session whose client disappears abruptly — the admin's laptop crashes, a jump host is powered off, or an intermediate NAT/firewall silently drops the flow — can leave an orphaned, half-open TCP session bound to a VTY line on the device. IOS has a finite number of VTY lines (typically 0-4 or 0-15); a build-up of stale sessions can exhaust the VTY pool and lock legitimate admins out (a low-grade denial of management access).

From a security standpoint, an orphaned but still-authenticated session is also a small hijack/reuse risk: the session remains in an established state that could, in theory, be reattached to or that keeps an authenticated context alive longer than intended. Enabling keepalives lets the device detect the dead peer and reclaim the resource, improving availability and hygiene of the management plane. This is a defense-in-depth control rather than a direct exploit mitigation, which is why it is rated LOW.

Blast radius: limited — primarily management-plane resource exhaustion and lingering sessions; it does not affect transit traffic. Platform nuance: this is an IOS/IOS-XE (and Cat9800 WLC) global service, matching SUPPORTED_PLATFORMS = {ios, iosxe, wlc}. `service tcp-keepalives-in` protects device-terminated inbound sessions; `service tcp-keepalives-out` covers device-originated outbound TCP. It pairs well with `exec-timeout` and `session-timeout` on the VTY/console lines, which enforce idle disconnection independently.

Standards angle: aligns with NIST 800-53 AC-12 (Session Termination) and SC-10 (Network Disconnect), and CIS guidance to enable TCP keepalives for incoming and outgoing connections.
steps: Check current state: `show running-config | include service tcp-keepalives`.
Confirm platform is IOS/IOS-XE/Cat9800 (the global `service` command applies here, not to ASA/NX-OS).
Enter config mode: `configure terminal`.
Enable keepalives for inbound connections: `service tcp-keepalives-in`.
Enable keepalives for outbound connections: `service tcp-keepalives-out`.
Complement with session hygiene on VTY lines: `line vty 0 15` then `exec-timeout 10 0` and `session-timeout 15`.
Apply the same idle timeout to the console: `line con 0` then `exec-timeout 10 0`.
Exit line config: `exit`.
Confirm the services are present: `show running-config | include service tcp-keepalives`.
Optionally review currently established management sessions: `show tcp brief` and `show users`.
Clear any obviously stale VTY session if needed: `clear line vty <n>` (use with care — it disconnects that line).
Exit config: `end`.
Validate keepalive behaviour over time by observing that dead sessions are reaped (`show users` no longer lists ghost sessions).
Save the configuration: `copy running-config startup-config`.
Add both `service tcp-keepalives-in` and `-out` to the device baseline/golden template.
Roll the change across the fleet during normal operations (no window required).
SVC-007.cli:
configure terminal
service tcp-keepalives-in
service tcp-keepalives-out
line vty 0 15
 exec-timeout 10 0
 session-timeout 15
exit
line con 0
 exec-timeout 10 0
end
copy running-config startup-config
SVC-007.verify: Run `show running-config | include service tcp-keepalives`. Expected output includes both `service tcp-keepalives-in` and `service tcp-keepalives-out`. Over time `show users` / `show tcp brief` should no longer accumulate orphaned established management sessions.
SVC-007.rollback: Disable with `no service tcp-keepalives-in` (and `no service tcp-keepalives-out`). Caveat: reverting re-allows orphaned/half-open management TCP sessions to persist and can lead to VTY exhaustion locking admins out.
SVC-007.impact: Negligible operational impact and non-disruptive — no reload, and active healthy sessions are unaffected. Keepalive probes are tiny and infrequent. This is safe to apply during production hours.
references: Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — TCP Keepalives for Management Sessions
CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark — enable service tcp-keepalives-in / -out
NIST SP 800-53 Rev.5 AC-12 (Session Termination), SC-10 (Network Disconnect)
CWE-772: Missing Release of Resource after Effective Lifetime

SVC-008.risk:
This MEDIUM check tests whether Cisco Express Forwarding is explicitly enabled by looking for either `ip cef` or `ipv6 cef` in the running-config (patterns `^ip cef` / `^ipv6 cef`). If NEITHER is present, SVC-008 is raised, noting that CEF underpins several security features.

CEF is Cisco's topology-driven forwarding architecture: it builds a Forwarding Information Base (FIB) and adjacency table so packets are switched in hardware/optimised paths rather than by slower process/fast switching. Beyond performance, CEF is a prerequisite for important data-plane security controls — most notably Unicast Reverse Path Forwarding (uRPF, `ip verify unicast source reachable-via ...`), which relies on the CEF FIB to drop spoofed source addresses. Without CEF, uRPF cannot function, weakening anti-spoofing defenses that mitigate source-address forgery used in reflection/amplification DDoS and spoofed-source attacks (ATT&CK T1498 Network DoS). CEF also improves resilience to high packet-per-second attacks by keeping forwarding off the route processor.

Blast radius: absence of CEF (and therefore uRPF) removes an anti-spoofing layer at network edges and can leave the device more exposed to CPU-punting DoS. On legacy process/fast-switching platforms, forwarding performance and feature availability are also reduced.

Platform nuance is important and can create false positives: on modern IOS-XE (ISR/ASR/Catalyst) and Catalyst 9800 WLC, CEF is enabled by default and frequently CANNOT be disabled, so `ip cef` may simply not appear in the running-config even though CEF is active. On classic IOS, `ip cef` is a real global toggle and its absence is meaningful. Therefore treat a hit on IOS-XE as 'confirm via show command' rather than an outright defect, and on classic IOS as a genuine setting to add. NX-OS and ASA are out of scope for this parser (SUPPORTED_PLATFORMS = {ios, iosxe, wlc}).

Standards angle: enabling CEF supports the data-plane hardening baseline and anti-spoofing recommendations (NIST 800-53 SC-5 Denial-of-Service Protection, SC-7 Boundary Protection) and Cisco's hardening guidance around uRPF.
steps: Check whether CEF appears in config: `show running-config | include ip cef|ipv6 cef`.
Confirm actual CEF operational state (authoritative, especially on IOS-XE): `show ip cef summary` and `show cef state`.
On IOS-XE/Cat9800, if `show cef state` reports CEF enabled, the missing config line is expected — document as a false positive rather than changing anything.
On classic IOS where CEF is off, plan the change; enabling CEF changes the switching path and is best done in a maintenance window on production forwarding devices.
Enter config mode: `configure terminal`.
Enable IPv4 CEF globally: `ip cef`.
If IPv6 is routed, enable IPv6 CEF: `ipv6 cef`.
On distributed-forwarding platforms, use `ip cef distributed` (and `ipv6 cef distributed`) instead where supported.
Ensure no interface has CEF disabled locally: check for `no ip route-cache cef` under interfaces and re-enable path switching with `ip route-cache cef` if found.
Now that CEF is active, add anti-spoofing uRPF on edge/downstream interfaces: `interface <intf>` then `ip verify unicast source reachable-via rx` (or `any` for multihomed/asymmetric links).
Exit interface config: `exit`.
Verify CEF is operational: `show ip cef summary` and confirm the FIB is populated with `show ip cef`.
Confirm uRPF is applied where configured: `show ip interface <intf> | include verify`.
Exit config: `end`.
Monitor CPU and forwarding after enabling on classic IOS: `show processes cpu history`.
Save the configuration: `copy running-config startup-config`.
Add `ip cef` (and `ipv6 cef` where relevant) to the baseline template for classic IOS devices.
SVC-008.cli:
configure terminal
ip cef
ipv6 cef
! enable anti-spoofing on an edge interface now that CEF is active:
interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 ip verify unicast source reachable-via rx
end
copy running-config startup-config
SVC-008.verify: Run `show ip cef summary` — expected: CEF is enabled and the FIB shows populated prefixes (not 'CEF disabled'). `show cef state` should report CEF Status: enabled. On interfaces with uRPF, `show ip interface <intf>` should show 'IP verify source reachable-via RX' with drop counters.
SVC-008.rollback: Disable with `no ip cef` (and `no ipv6 cef`) on classic IOS. Note this is often rejected on IOS-XE where CEF is mandatory. Caveat: disabling CEF also disables uRPF, removing anti-spoofing protection and reverting to slower process/fast switching that is more susceptible to CPU-punt DoS.
SVC-008.impact: On modern IOS-XE, typically a no-op (CEF already on). On classic IOS, enabling CEF changes the switching path and can briefly perturb forwarding/CPU as the FIB builds — treat as potentially disruptive and use a maintenance window on production routers. No reload is required; management sessions are not dropped.
references: Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — CEF and Unicast RPF (uRPF)
Cisco IOS Express Forwarding Configuration Guide
NIST SP 800-53 Rev.5 SC-5 (Denial-of-Service Protection), SC-7

SVC-009.risk:
This MEDIUM check validates that log and debug output are timestamped with real date/time. It looks for `service timestamps log datetime` and `service timestamps debug datetime` (patterns `^service timestamps log datetime` and `^service timestamps debug datetime`). If either is missing, SVC-009 is raised, itemising which timestamp type is not configured. The recommended form adds `msec localtime show-timezone` for millisecond precision and timezone context.

By default, IOS may timestamp messages with system uptime (`service timestamps ... uptime`) rather than wall-clock time, or the setting may be inconsistent. Uptime-based or absent timestamps make it very hard to correlate device events with logs from other systems (firewalls, servers, SIEM) during an investigation. Accurate, timezone-qualified datetime stamps are foundational to security operations: they enable event correlation, establish sequence of actions during an intrusion, and support the integrity and admissibility of logs as evidence.

From an adversary perspective, poor timestamping aids anti-forensics and slows detection — it becomes difficult to reconstruct a timeline of ATT&CK behaviours (e.g. T1078 valid-account logins, T1562 impairing defenses, config changes). If two devices report events in uptime seconds, an analyst cannot reliably order events across them. Millisecond precision additionally helps disambiguate high-frequency events, and `show-timezone` prevents ambiguity when devices span regions.

Blast radius: this is an observability/forensic-readiness control; its absence doesn't itself grant access but materially degrades incident response and audit across the estate. It must be paired with reliable time: `service timestamps` is only as trustworthy as the clock, so NTP (authenticated where possible) and a configured `clock timezone` are prerequisites for meaningful localtime stamps.

Platform nuance: `service timestamps` is IOS/IOS-XE/Cat9800 WLC syntax (SUPPORTED_PLATFORMS = {ios, iosxe, wlc}). NX-OS handles logging timestamps via `logging timestamp <units>`, and ASA uses `logging timestamp` — those are out of scope for this parser.

Standards angle: this maps directly to NIST 800-53 AU-8 (Time Stamps) and supports AU-3/AU-6/AU-12, plus CIS guidance to configure service timestamps for log and debug with datetime.
steps: Check current timestamp configuration: `show running-config | include service timestamps`.
Verify the clock/time source is trustworthy first — timestamps are only meaningful with correct time: `show ntp status` and `show clock detail`.
If NTP is not set, configure it (ideally authenticated): `ntp server <ntp-ip>` (and `ntp authenticate` / `ntp authentication-key` where used).
Set the local timezone so localtime stamps are correct: `configure terminal` then `clock timezone <NAME> <hours-offset> <minutes-offset>` (e.g. `clock timezone EST -5 0`).
Configure daylight-saving if applicable: `clock summer-time EDT recurring`.
Enable datetime timestamps for log messages with precision and timezone: `service timestamps log datetime msec localtime show-timezone`.
Enable datetime timestamps for debug output likewise: `service timestamps debug datetime msec localtime show-timezone`.
Ensure logging is actually capturing events to a durable target: `logging buffered <size>` and `logging host <syslog-ip>` (or `logging <ip>` on older IOS).
Optionally enable `logging synchronous` on the VTY/console lines to keep interactive output readable.
Exit config: `end`.
Confirm both timestamp lines are present: `show running-config | include service timestamps`.
Generate a test log event (e.g. a benign interface flap in lab) and check the stamp format: `show logging`.
Confirm the displayed timestamps show full date, time, msec and timezone (e.g. `Jul 11 14:05:22.123 EST`).
Cross-check the device clock against the SIEM/NTP source to ensure alignment: `show clock`.
Save the configuration: `copy running-config startup-config`.
Apply the same `service timestamps` and NTP/timezone settings across the fleet via the baseline template.
Confirm the SIEM now correlates these device logs with other sources on a common timeline.
SVC-009.cli:
configure terminal
clock timezone EST -5 0
clock summer-time EDT recurring
service timestamps log datetime msec localtime show-timezone
service timestamps debug datetime msec localtime show-timezone
ntp server 10.10.10.30
logging host 10.10.10.40
end
copy running-config startup-config
SVC-009.verify: Run `show running-config | include service timestamps` — expected: both `service timestamps log datetime msec localtime show-timezone` and `service timestamps debug datetime msec localtime show-timezone` are present. Then `show logging` should display entries stamped with full date/time, milliseconds and timezone (e.g. `Jul 11 14:05:22.123 EST:`), and `show clock detail` should show time sourced from NTP.
SVC-009.rollback: Revert with `no service timestamps log datetime` / `no service timestamps debug datetime` (IOS then falls back to uptime-based stamps). Caveat: reverting reintroduces uptime or unstamped log/debug output, breaking cross-device time correlation and weakening forensic timelines.
SVC-009.impact: Non-disruptive; no reload and no session drop. It only changes how future log/debug lines are formatted. The one dependency is accurate time — set NTP and the timezone so localtime stamps are correct; otherwise stamps will be precise but wrong.
references: Cisco Guide to Harden Cisco IOS Devices — Use service timestamps / logging best practices
CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark — configure service timestamps (log and debug) with datetime
NIST SP 800-53 Rev.5 AU-8 (Time Stamps), AU-3 (Content of Audit Records)

SW-001.risk:
The scanner enumerated all interfaces matching `(GigabitEthernet|FastEthernet|Ethernet)` via `get_interfaces()`, and for every interface whose config contains `switchport mode access` it checked for the presence of any line containing `switchport port-security`. Access ports with no port-security stanza are reported under SW-001 (MEDIUM severity). The check is scoped to IOS / IOS-XE Catalyst only (class SUPPORTED_PLATFORMS = {ios, iosxe}); NX-OS, ASA/FTD and WLC are not evaluated because they are not L2 access switches in this model.

An access port with no MAC restriction accepts frames from any device that plugs in. This enables the classic Layer-2 access-control bypass: an attacker who reaches a wall jack, an unsecured conference-room port, or a port behind an unmanaged desk hub can attach a rogue laptop, dropbox implant (e.g. a Raspberry Pi / LAN Turtle), or a rogue AP and immediately gain link-layer presence in the VLAN. This maps to MITRE ATT&CK Hardware Additions (T1200) and Network Sniffing (T1040), and it is the launch pad for ARP spoofing / adversary-in-the-middle (T1557) and CAM-table flooding (macof) that turns the switch into a hub.

Without port-security there is no cap on learned MAC addresses per port, so a CAM-overflow attack can exhaust the MAC address table and force the switch to flood all frames out every port in the VLAN, exposing traffic to a passive sniffer.

Blast radius: any host in the same VLAN/broadcast domain as the compromised port is exposed to interception or spoofing, and the rogue device gets a DHCP lease and a path toward whatever the VLAN can reach. In flat networks this can mean the entire user population.

Platform nuance: syntax is identical on IOS and IOS-XE Catalyst. Sticky learning (`switchport port-security mac-address sticky`) writes learned MACs into running-config and must be saved to persist. Voice deployments need `maximum` raised (phone data MAC + PC MAC + phone voice MAC, so at least 3). Where 802.1X/MAB is deployed that is the preferred edge control and port-security may be redundant; on some older trains it interacts with 802.1X, so validate coexistence.

Compliance: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 5.1 (port-security on access ports), NIST 800-53 AC-3 / SC-7 boundary protection, and the Layer-2 hardening guidance in Cisco's switch security best-practices.
steps: Inventory access ports missing the feature: `show running-config | include ^interface|switchport mode access|switchport port-security` and cross-reference against the scanner's flagged list.
Confirm which ports are true user/access ports vs. uplinks/trunks so you do not apply port-security to a trunk: `show interfaces status` and `show interfaces trunk`.
For voice ports, note the phone + PC topology so you size `maximum` correctly (typically 3: data MAC, phone data MAC, phone voice MAC).
Schedule the change during a maintenance window if using `violation shutdown`, because a mistaken MAC cap can err-disable a live port.
Enter global config: `configure terminal`.
Select the access port: `interface GigabitEthernet1/0/5`.
Ensure the port is a static access port (prerequisite): `switchport mode access`.
Enable the feature: `switchport port-security`.
Set a sane MAC cap for a data-only port: `switchport port-security maximum 2` (raise to 3 on voice ports).
Choose a violation mode: `switchport port-security violation restrict` (drops offending frames + logs/SNMP, keeps port up) — prefer this over `shutdown` for production access ports.
Enable sticky learning so approved MACs persist: `switchport port-security mac-address sticky`.
Optionally add aging for transient devices: `switchport port-security aging time 120` and `switchport port-security aging type inactivity`.
For voice ports, keep `switchport voice vlan <id>` and confirm the maximum accounts for the phone's voice-VLAN MAC.
Apply the same block to remaining flagged ports, using `interface range GigabitEthernet1/0/5 - 24` to batch-configure identical access ports.
Exit interface and global config: `end`.
Validate learned addresses and status: `show port-security` and `show port-security interface GigabitEthernet1/0/5`.
Confirm no ports fell into err-disable: `show interfaces status err-disabled`.
Save the config so sticky MACs and settings persist a reload: `copy running-config startup-config` (or `write memory`).
Monitor for violations over the next business day: `show port-security` and check logs/SNMP traps for `PSECURE_VIOLATION`.
SW-001.cli:
configure terminal
interface range GigabitEthernet1/0/5 - 24
 switchport mode access
 switchport port-security
 switchport port-security maximum 2
 switchport port-security violation restrict
 switchport port-security mac-address sticky
 switchport port-security aging time 120
 switchport port-security aging type inactivity
end
copy running-config startup-config
SW-001.verify: `show port-security interface GigabitEthernet1/0/5` should show `Port Security : Enabled`, `Port Status : Secure-up`, the configured `Maximum MAC Addresses`, and `Violation Mode : Restrict`. `show port-security address` lists the sticky-learned MACs. `show running-config interface GigabitEthernet1/0/5` should contain the `switchport port-security ...` lines and `switchport port-security mac-address sticky <mac>` entries.
SW-001.rollback: Per interface: `interface GigabitEthernet1/0/5` then `no switchport port-security` (and `no switchport port-security mac-address sticky` to purge learned MACs), then `copy running-config startup-config`. Caveat: reverting re-opens the port to any device MAC, restoring the rogue-device / CAM-flooding exposure.
SW-001.impact: Low-to-moderate. Enabling the feature with `violation restrict` is non-disruptive to already-connected, approved devices and does not drop the link. Risk is operational: with `violation shutdown` a device swap or a downstream hub can err-disable the port until recovered; a too-low `maximum` can lock out legitimate phone+PC setups. No reload required; no impact on uplinks/trunks (they are excluded).
references: Cisco Catalyst Switch Security Best Practices — Port Security

SW-002.risk:
The scanner walked every interface section (`get_interfaces()` with no filter) and, for those containing `switchport mode trunk`, inspected the native-VLAN configuration. A trunk is flagged for SW-002 in two cases: (1) there is no `switchport trunk native vlan` line at all (so the native VLAN defaults to VLAN 1), or (2) the line explicitly sets `switchport trunk native vlan 1`. The finding is HIGH severity and runs on IOS / IOS-XE Catalyst only.

The native VLAN on an 802.1Q trunk is the one VLAN whose frames traverse the trunk UNTAGGED. When the native VLAN is VLAN 1 — which is also the default VLAN every access port lands in and the VLAN carrying control-plane chatter (CDP, VTP, PAgP, DTP, STP) — an attacker positioned on an access port can perform the double-tagging VLAN-hopping attack (network boundary bypass). The adversary crafts a frame with two 802.1Q tags: the outer tag matches the native VLAN and is stripped by the first switch on egress to the trunk, leaving the inner tag intact so the frame is delivered into a target VLAN the attacker was never supposed to reach. This is a one-way injection primitive useful for spoofing, DoS, or reaching a segmented server VLAN.

Blast radius: crossing a VLAN boundary defeats the primary segmentation control; a native/inner combination lets an attacker on a low-trust user VLAN push frames into a high-trust VLAN (server, management, PCI, OT). Even without a full bidirectional session, unidirectional injection enables spoofed DHCP, ARP, or attack payloads across the boundary.

Platform nuance: IOS and IOS-XE syntax is identical (`switchport trunk native vlan <id>`). The chosen native VLAN must exist and must NOT be used by any access host — it should be a dedicated black-hole/parking VLAN pruned from where it is not needed. Both ends of the trunk must agree on the native VLAN or STP will log a native-VLAN mismatch (PVST+ `%SPANTREE-2-RECV_PVID_ERR`). Hardening can be reinforced with the global `vlan dot1q tag native`, which forces even native-VLAN frames to be tagged, closing the double-tag vector entirely.

Compliance: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 5.2 (change native VLAN on trunks), NIST 800-53 SC-7 boundary protection / SC-32 information-flow segmentation, CWE-290-class trust bypass.
steps: Identify all trunk links and their current native VLAN: `show interfaces trunk` (the Native vlan column) and `show running-config | section interface`.
Pick or create a dedicated unused parking VLAN for native use (e.g. 999) that carries no host traffic: `configure terminal` then `vlan 999` and `name NATIVE-UNUSED`.
Confirm the parking VLAN is not assigned to any access port so it truly carries no user data: `show vlan brief`.
Plan a maintenance note: native-VLAN change must be applied to BOTH ends of each trunk to avoid an STP native-VLAN mismatch.
Enter config for the first trunk interface: `interface GigabitEthernet1/0/1`.
Set the new native VLAN: `switchport trunk native vlan 999`.
Restrict the trunk's allowed VLAN list so unneeded VLANs are pruned: `switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,20,30`.
Repeat on the far-end switch trunk interface with the identical native VLAN 999 to keep both ends consistent.
For defense-in-depth, force tagging of native-VLAN traffic globally: `vlan dot1q tag native` (verify downstream devices tolerate tagged native frames before enabling widely).
If any trunk was implicitly VLAN 1 (no native line), add the explicit `switchport trunk native vlan 999` rather than relying on defaults.

Set encapsulation to dot1q only on platforms that still expose ISL selection (e.g. some older 3560/3750): `switchport trunk encapsulation dot1q`. On modern dot1q-only Catalyst (3850/9300) this command does not exist — omit it there.
Exit config: `end`.
Verify both ends agree and no mismatch is logged: `show interfaces trunk` and `show logging` for `%SPANTREE-2-RECV_PVID_ERR` or native-VLAN mismatch messages.
Confirm the parking VLAN carries no MACs: `show mac address-table vlan 999` (should be empty).
Save configuration on both switches: `copy running-config startup-config`.
Post-change, validate end-to-end reachability on production VLANs is unaffected with a ping/traffic test across the trunk.
Document the new native-VLAN standard so future trunks are provisioned consistently.
SW-002.cli:
configure terminal
vlan 999
 name NATIVE-UNUSED
 exit
interface GigabitEthernet1/0/1
 switchport trunk encapsulation dot1q
 switchport mode trunk
 switchport trunk native vlan 999
 switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,20,30
 exit
vlan dot1q tag native
end
copy running-config startup-config
SW-002.verify: `show interfaces trunk` must show `999` in the Native vlan column for each trunk (not 1). `show running-config interface GigabitEthernet1/0/1` must contain `switchport trunk native vlan 999`. With `vlan dot1q tag native` set, `show vlan dot1q tag native` reports `dot1q native vlan tagging is enabled`. `show logging` should be free of native-VLAN mismatch errors after both ends are changed.
SW-002.rollback: `interface GigabitEthernet1/0/1` then `no switchport trunk native vlan` (reverts to VLAN 1) and, if set, `no vlan dot1q tag native`; `copy running-config startup-config`. Caveat: reverting restores VLAN 1 as native and re-exposes the trunk to 802.1Q double-tagging VLAN-hopping.
SW-002.impact: Moderate and potentially disruptive if done one-sided. Changing the native VLAN on only one end of a trunk causes an STP native-VLAN mismatch and can inconsistency-log/block the port; always change both ends in the same window. `vlan dot1q tag native` is global and changes how native frames are tagged — misconfigured downstream gear that expects untagged native frames can lose connectivity. No reload required; correctly applied it is transparent to user traffic.
references: Cisco Layer 2 Security Best Practices — VLAN Hopping / Native VLAN

SW-003.risk:
The scanner examined interfaces matching `(GigabitEthernet|FastEthernet|TenGig)` and flagged any port that is configured `switchport mode access` but does NOT also carry `switchport nonegotiate`. The finding is HIGH severity and applies to IOS / IOS-XE Catalyst switches only. It targets the Dynamic Trunking Protocol: even a port set to access mode can, on many default configs, still send/respond to DTP frames unless negotiation is explicitly disabled.

DTP is Cisco's proprietary protocol that lets two switch ports auto-negotiate whether the link becomes a trunk. The danger is switch-spoofing VLAN hopping: an attacker connects a device that emits DTP frames advertising itself as a switch willing to trunk (using tools like Yersinia). If the victim port will negotiate, it forms an 802.1Q trunk to the attacker, and the attacker's port then receives traffic for — and can inject into — ALL VLANs allowed on that trunk. This is the single-encapsulation VLAN-hopping attack (network-boundary bypass), and unlike double-tagging it is bidirectional, giving full cross-VLAN reachability.

Blast radius: a successful DTP spoof collapses VLAN segmentation for that switch — the attacker gains visibility and injection into every VLAN carried by the negotiated trunk, potentially including management, server, voice, and restricted VLANs. Combined with ARP/DHCP spoofing this becomes full MITM across segments (ATT&CK T1557).

Platform nuance: `switchport nonegotiate` is valid IOS/IOS-XE syntax and disables DTP frame transmission on the port; it can only be applied when the port is in a static mode (`switchport mode access` or `switchport mode trunk`) — you cannot apply it while the port is `dynamic auto`/`dynamic desirable`. Setting `switchport mode access` alone stops the port from becoming a trunk, but the explicit `nonegotiate` is what the benchmark and this check require. NX-OS does not run DTP and ASA/FTD/WLC are out of scope, which is why the check is limited to IOS/IOS-XE.

Compliance: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 5.3 (disable DTP/auto-trunking), NIST 800-53 SC-7 boundary protection, CWE-290 trust-by-spoofing.
steps: List access ports and their negotiation state: `show interfaces switchport | include Name:|Administrative Mode|Negotiation` and match against the scanner's flagged ports.
Confirm current DTP posture per port: `show dtp interface GigabitEthernet1/0/5` (Negotiation of Trunking line).
Verify each flagged port is genuinely an access/edge port, not an uplink that must trunk.
This change is non-disruptive to end hosts, so a formal window is usually unnecessary, but note it for uplink candidates.
Enter global config: `configure terminal`.
Select the access port (or a range for efficiency): `interface range GigabitEthernet1/0/5 - 24`.
Pin the port statically to access mode (prerequisite for nonegotiate): `switchport mode access`.
Assign the intended data VLAN so the port is not left in a negotiated/default state: `switchport access vlan 10`.
Disable DTP negotiation: `switchport nonegotiate`.
For trunk uplinks that must stay trunks, also hardcode them and disable DTP: `switchport mode trunk` then `switchport nonegotiate` (never leave uplinks in dynamic auto/desirable).
Exit interface config: `exit`, then `end`.
Verify DTP is now off on the port: `show dtp interface GigabitEthernet1/0/5` should report negotiation disabled.
Confirm the mode is static and not dynamic: `show interfaces GigabitEthernet1/0/5 switchport | include Mode|Negotiation`.
Ensure no port unexpectedly formed/dropped a trunk: `show interfaces trunk`.
Save the configuration: `copy running-config startup-config`.
Re-run the audit / spot check remaining ports to confirm zero SW-003 findings remain.
Standardize an access-port template (mode access + nonegotiate + port-security) for future provisioning.
SW-003.cli:
configure terminal
interface range GigabitEthernet1/0/5 - 24
 switchport mode access
 switchport access vlan 10
 switchport nonegotiate
end
copy running-config startup-config
SW-003.verify: `show interfaces GigabitEthernet1/0/5 switchport` should show `Administrative Mode: static access` and `Negotiation of Trunking: Off`. `show dtp interface GigabitEthernet1/0/5` shows the TOS/TAS/TNS fields with negotiation off and `Neighbor is not present`. `show running-config interface GigabitEthernet1/0/5` contains `switchport nonegotiate`.
SW-003.rollback: `interface GigabitEthernet1/0/5` then `no switchport nonegotiate`; `copy running-config startup-config`. Caveat: reverting re-enables DTP frame processing, restoring the switch-spoofing / auto-trunk VLAN-hopping attack surface on that port.
SW-003.impact: Very low / non-disruptive. `switchport nonegotiate` only stops DTP frames; it does not change forwarding for an already-static access port and does not drop end-host links. No reload. The only caution: applying `switchport mode access` to a port that was serving as a dynamic trunk uplink would tear down that trunk — verify a flagged port is truly an edge port first.
references: Cisco Layer 2 Security Best Practices — Disable DTP

SW-004.risk:
The scanner scanned interfaces matching `(GigabitEthernet|FastEthernet|Ethernet)` and flagged any port that is (a) NOT administratively shut down (no standalone `shutdown` line, matched as an exact trimmed line) AND (b) has no meaningful configuration — specifically no line starting with `switchport` and no line starting with `ip address`. Such ports are treated as unused-but-live and reported under SW-004 (MEDIUM). The check runs on IOS / IOS-XE Catalyst only.

An enabled port with no configuration typically falls into the default VLAN (VLAN 1) in dynamic mode, meaning anyone who plugs into it gets an immediate Layer-2 foothold with a DHCP path into the default segment. This is the physical-access initial-access vector (MITRE ATT&CK Hardware Additions, T1200): unattended ports in lobbies, meeting rooms, printer alcoves, or unlocked wiring closets become unauthenticated network entry points for rogue laptops, implants, or rogue access points. Because the port has no port-security, no 802.1X, and potentially default DTP behaviour, it also inherits the DTP/VLAN-hopping and MAC-flooding exposures of an unhardened access port.

Blast radius: every unused live port is an independent unmanaged doorway into the network; in environments with many idle ports this multiplies the attack surface substantially. An attacker needs only physical proximity, not credentials, to begin reconnaissance, DHCP starvation, ARP poisoning, or lateral movement within whatever VLAN the port defaults to.

Platform nuance: IOS/IOS-XE syntax is identical. Best practice is defense-in-depth: administratively `shutdown` the port AND move it to a dedicated black-hole/unused VLAN so that if it is ever accidentally re-enabled it lands nowhere useful. Note that Layer-3 routed interfaces or ports intended to be brought up on demand may legitimately be unconfigured; treat the list as candidates and confirm operational intent before disabling. On stacks/chassis, use `interface range` to disable large blocks efficiently.

Compliance: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 5.4 (disable unused ports), NIST 800-53 AC-3 / PE-3 (physical access) / SC-7, CWE-1188/CWE-284 (insecure default / improper access control).
steps: Generate the candidate list of idle ports: `show interfaces status | include notconnect|disabled` and reconcile with the scanner output.
Confirm ports are truly unused (no recent link/traffic): `show interfaces status` and `show mac address-table interface GigabitEthernet1/0/30` (empty = no learned hosts).
Cross-check patch records / cable maps so you do not disable a port that is intentionally staged for a future device.
Create a dedicated black-hole VLAN for parked ports if one does not exist: `configure terminal`, `vlan 998`, `name UNUSED-PARKING`, `exit`.
Enter config mode: `configure terminal`.
Select the block of unused ports: `interface range GigabitEthernet1/0/30 - 48`.
Force them to static access so they cannot negotiate a trunk if re-enabled: `switchport mode access`.
Park them in the black-hole VLAN: `switchport access vlan 998`.
Add a clear description for operations: `description UNUSED - DISABLED PER HARDENING`.
Disable DTP on the parked ports: `switchport nonegotiate`.
Administratively disable the ports: `shutdown`.
Exit interface config: `exit`, then `end`.
Verify the ports now show administratively down: `show interfaces status | include disabled`.
Confirm they are in the parking VLAN, not VLAN 1: `show vlan brief` and `show interfaces GigabitEthernet1/0/30 switchport`.
Save configuration: `copy running-config startup-config`.
Establish an operational procedure: when a port is needed, `no shutdown` + assign the correct VLAN, and re-audit.
Optionally schedule periodic re-audit so newly-freed ports get parked automatically.
SW-004.cli:
configure terminal
vlan 998
 name UNUSED-PARKING
 exit
interface range GigabitEthernet1/0/30 - 48
 description UNUSED - DISABLED PER HARDENING
 switchport mode access
 switchport access vlan 998
 switchport nonegotiate
 shutdown
end
copy running-config startup-config
SW-004.verify: `show interfaces status` should list the parked ports as `disabled` (admin down). `show ip interface brief | include down` shows `administratively down`. `show running-config interface GigabitEthernet1/0/30` contains `shutdown` and `switchport access vlan 998`. `show vlan brief` shows the ports as members of VLAN 998, not VLAN 1.
SW-004.rollback: Per port or range: `interface range GigabitEthernet1/0/30 - 48` then `no shutdown` (and reassign the intended VLAN); `copy running-config startup-config`. Caveat: `no shutdown` re-enables the physical port — if left in the default VLAN it re-exposes an unauthenticated Layer-2 entry point for anyone with physical access.
SW-004.impact: Low, provided the ports are genuinely unused — shutting them down has no effect on existing traffic. The real risk is human error: disabling a port that was actually staged for imminent use, or one carrying an unlabeled device, causes an outage for that device. Verify idleness first. No reload required; the action is per-interface and instantly reversible with `no shutdown`.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 5.4 (Disable Unused Ports)
Cisco Catalyst Switch Hardening — Unused Ports
CWE-1188 Insecure Default Initialization of Resource

SW-005.risk:
The scanner searched every interface for the literal `switchport access vlan 1` (regex `switchport access vlan\s+1\b`, so it matches VLAN 1 exactly and not VLAN 10/100/etc.) and flagged each port explicitly pinned to VLAN 1. The finding is MEDIUM severity, IOS / IOS-XE Catalyst only. It is specifically about USER/access data traffic being placed in VLAN 1.

VLAN 1 is the switch's default and special-purpose VLAN: it is where unconfigured ports land and it carries Layer-2 control-plane protocols — CDP, VTP, DTP, PAgP/LACP, and by default PVST+ management traffic. Cisco's long-standing guidance is that VLAN 1 should never carry user data. When user endpoints share VLAN 1 with control traffic, an attacker on such a port has visibility of, and an injection surface into, the control plane and any management adjacency riding VLAN 1. VLAN 1 also cannot be deleted and typically spans every trunk by default, so a compromise or broadcast storm in VLAN 1 has unusually wide reach. Because VLAN 1 is the native VLAN default on trunks, mixing user data into it also amplifies VLAN-hopping exposure (see SW-002).

Blast radius: VLAN 1 often extends switch-wide and even fabric-wide by default, so hosts in it share a large broadcast domain and sit alongside management/control-plane traffic. An attacker gains reconnaissance of CDP/VTP topology data and a broad L2 blast radius for spoofing/flooding attacks (MITRE ATT&CK Network Sniffing T1040, Adversary-in-the-Middle T1557).

Platform nuance: IOS/IOS-XE syntax identical. Remediation is to move user ports to purpose-built data VLANs and to prune VLAN 1 from trunks where it is not required (`switchport trunk allowed vlan remove 1`). Best practice also parks unused ports in a black-hole VLAN rather than VLAN 1. Note VLAN 1 cannot be deleted; the goal is to stop USING it for data, not remove it.

Compliance: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 5.5 (do not use VLAN 1 for user data), NIST 800-53 SC-7 / SC-32 segmentation, CWE-1188 insecure default.
steps: List ports explicitly in VLAN 1: `show running-config | include switchport access vlan 1` and confirm against the scanner list.
Determine the correct purpose-built data VLAN for each affected user population (e.g. 10 for staff, 20 for guests).
Create the target VLAN(s) if absent: `configure terminal`, `vlan 10`, `name USER-DATA`, `exit`.
Confirm the target VLAN has a valid SVI/gateway and DHCP scope so moved hosts keep connectivity: `show ip interface brief | include Vlan10`.
Plan a brief window: moving a port's VLAN bounces host connectivity momentarily and forces a new DHCP lease.
Enter config: `configure terminal`.
Select the affected access port(s): `interface range GigabitEthernet1/0/5 - 24`.
Ensure static access mode: `switchport mode access`.
Reassign the port off VLAN 1 to the data VLAN: `switchport access vlan 10`.
For voice ports, set the voice VLAN explicitly rather than relying on VLAN 1: `switchport voice vlan 30`.
Exit and prune VLAN 1 from trunks where it is not needed: `interface GigabitEthernet1/0/1`, `switchport trunk allowed vlan remove 1`.
Move any unused ports currently in VLAN 1 into a black-hole VLAN and shut them (ties in with SW-004): `switchport access vlan 998` then `shutdown`.
Exit config: `end`.
Verify no user ports remain in VLAN 1: `show vlan brief` (VLAN 1 membership should be empty or infrastructure-only) and `show interfaces status`.
Confirm moved hosts obtained addressing in the new VLAN: `show mac address-table vlan 10`.
Save configuration: `copy running-config startup-config`.
Re-audit to confirm SW-005 clears and validate end-user reachability with a ping/DHCP test.
SW-005.cli:
configure terminal
vlan 10
 name USER-DATA
 exit
interface range GigabitEthernet1/0/5 - 24
 switchport mode access
 switchport access vlan 10
end
interface GigabitEthernet1/0/1
 switchport trunk allowed vlan remove 1
end
copy running-config startup-config
SW-005.verify: `show vlan brief` should show the moved ports under VLAN 10 (and VLAN 1 with no user ports). `show interfaces GigabitEthernet1/0/5 switchport` shows `Access Mode VLAN: 10 (USER-DATA)`. `show interfaces trunk` shows VLAN 1 removed from the allowed list on pruned trunks. `show running-config | include access vlan 1` returns nothing for user ports.
SW-005.rollback: `interface range GigabitEthernet1/0/5 - 24` then `switchport access vlan 1` (and `switchport trunk allowed vlan add 1` on trunks if pruned); `copy running-config startup-config`. Caveat: reverting puts user data back onto the control-plane/default VLAN 1, re-mixing endpoints with CDP/VTP/STP traffic and widening the L2 blast radius.
SW-005.impact: Moderate/disruptive per host at the moment of change: reassigning a port's access VLAN briefly drops the endpoint's connectivity and forces a new DHCP lease in the target VLAN — do it in a window and ensure the destination VLAN has gateway + DHCP ready. Pruning VLAN 1 from trunks is safe if no service rides VLAN 1. No reload required.
references: Cisco Layer 2 Best Practices — Avoid VLAN 1 for User Data

SW-006.risk:
This is a global (device-wide) check: the scanner tests `has_line(r"^spanning-tree portfast (bpduguard|edge bpduguard) default")`, so it accepts either the classic `spanning-tree portfast bpduguard default` or the newer `spanning-tree portfast edge bpduguard default`. If neither is present in running-config, SW-006 fires (MEDIUM). It applies to IOS / IOS-XE Catalyst switches. The finding means BPDU Guard is not armed as the default behaviour for all PortFast-enabled edge ports.

PortFast is applied to access/edge ports so they transition straight to forwarding without waiting through STP listening/learning. The trade-off is that a PortFast port that suddenly receives a spanning-tree BPDU indicates something is wrong — a switch, a hub with STP, or an attacker has been connected where only an end host should be. BPDU Guard's job is to err-disable such a port the instant it sees a BPDU. Without the global default, an attacker on an edge port can inject crafted superior BPDUs (via Yersinia's STP attack) to win the root-bridge election, which reshapes the spanning tree so traffic is redirected through the attacker (STP-based Adversary-in-the-Middle, MITRE ATT&CK T1557) or to trigger repeated topology changes that flap the network into a denial of service.

Blast radius: STP is a switch-wide/domain-wide control protocol. A rogue root-bridge or a BPDU flood does not stay on one port — it can destabilize the entire Layer-2 topology, black-hole or reroute traffic across multiple switches, and cause broadcast disruption affecting every VLAN sharing that spanning tree.

Platform nuance: the global command arms BPDU Guard only on ports that have PortFast enabled (edge ports); trunk/uplink ports are unaffected, which is correct. On classic IOS the command is `spanning-tree portfast bpduguard default`; newer IOS-XE trains render/accept the `edge` keyword (`spanning-tree portfast edge bpduguard default`) — the scanner regex matches both. This global default does not replace enabling PortFast itself (`spanning-tree portfast`/`spanning-tree portfast edge` on the port, or globally with `spanning-tree portfast default`). Complementary controls: `spanning-tree bpduguard enable` per-port for explicit ports, and `spanning-tree guard root` on ports facing toward non-root neighbors. Recovery from err-disable can be automated with `errdisable recovery cause bpduguard`.

Compliance: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 5.6 (BPDU Guard default), NIST 800-53 SC-5 (DoS protection) / SC-7, CWE-400 uncontrolled resource / CWE-284.
steps: Check current global STP hardening state: `show running-config | include spanning-tree portfast|bpduguard|bpdufilter`.
Confirm which edge ports have PortFast so you understand the scope BPDU Guard will cover: `show spanning-tree interface GigabitEthernet1/0/5 portfast` or `show spanning-tree summary`.
Verify NO uplink/trunk port has PortFast enabled (it must not), so BPDU Guard won't err-disable a legitimate switch link: `show spanning-tree summary`.
This is low-risk but validate you will not disable a downlink to a legitimate downstream switch that lacks PortFast.
Enter global config: `configure terminal`.
Arm BPDU Guard as the default on all PortFast edge ports (classic syntax): `spanning-tree portfast bpduguard default`.
On newer IOS-XE that uses the edge keyword, the equivalent is: `spanning-tree portfast edge bpduguard default` (functionally the same global default).
Ensure edge ports actually have PortFast (prerequisite for the guard to apply) — per port: `interface GigabitEthernet1/0/5` then `spanning-tree portfast` (or `spanning-tree portfast edge`).
Optionally set PortFast as the default for access ports globally: `spanning-tree portfast default`.
Add automatic recovery so an accidentally-tripped port self-heals: `errdisable recovery cause bpduguard` and `errdisable recovery interval 300`.
For explicit high-value ports, also set per-port guard: `interface GigabitEthernet1/0/5` then `spanning-tree bpduguard enable`.
Exit config: `end`.
Verify the global default is active: `show spanning-tree summary` (look for the Portfast BPDU Guard line).
Confirm no legitimate port was err-disabled by the change: `show interfaces status err-disabled`.
Save configuration: `copy running-config startup-config`.
Monitor logs for `%SPANTREE-2-BLOCK_BPDUGUARD` events indicating a real rogue-device catch: `show logging | include BPDUGUARD`.
Re-run the audit to confirm SW-006 is cleared.
SW-006.cli:
configure terminal
spanning-tree portfast default
spanning-tree portfast bpduguard default
errdisable recovery cause bpduguard
errdisable recovery interval 300
end
copy running-config startup-config
SW-006.verify: `show spanning-tree summary` should include `Portfast BPDU Guard Default is enabled`. `show running-config | include bpduguard default` returns the configured line. If a rogue device is later connected, `show interfaces status err-disabled` will list the port with reason `bpduguard` and the log shows `%SPANTREE-2-BLOCK_BPDUGUARD_VPORT`.
SW-006.rollback: `configure terminal` then `no spanning-tree portfast bpduguard default` (and `no spanning-tree portfast edge bpduguard default` on edge-keyword trains); `copy running-config startup-config`. Caveat: reverting removes automatic protection against rogue BPDUs on edge ports, re-exposing the switch to STP root-hijack / topology-flap denial of service.
SW-006.impact: Low and essentially non-disruptive to end hosts, because the guard only acts on PortFast edge ports that receive BPDUs (i.e., where a switch/bridge was wrongly connected). The one caution: if PortFast was mistakenly enabled on a port facing a legitimate downstream switch, arming BPDU Guard will err-disable that link — hence the pre-check. No reload required; global config takes effect immediately.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 5.6 (BPDU Guard Default)
Cisco STP Security — BPDU Guard / Root Guard

SW-007.risk:
SW-007 only evaluates when DHCP snooping is already enabled globally: the scanner first requires a line matching `^ip dhcp snooping` in running-config, and only then inspects access ports matching `(GigabitEthernet|FastEthernet)` (`switchport mode access`) for the presence of `ip dhcp snooping limit rate`. Access ports missing the per-port rate limit are reported (LOW severity), IOS / IOS-XE Catalyst only. The rationale: DHCP snooping without a rate limit leaves the snooping process itself and the DHCP server exposed to flooding.

DHCP snooping classifies ports as trusted (toward the real DHCP server/uplink) or untrusted (access ports) and validates DHCP messages, dropping rogue OFFER/ACK from untrusted ports. The per-port `limit rate` caps how many DHCP packets per second an untrusted port may source. Without it, a malicious or malfunctioning client can mount a DHCP starvation / exhaustion attack (MITRE ATT&CK Network Denial of Service, T1498) by flooding DISCOVER packets — typically with spoofed client MACs (the classic dhcpstarve/Yersinia technique) — to consume the DHCP scope, deny addresses to legitimate users, and set up a rogue-DHCP follow-on. A high packet rate can also spike CPU on the switch's DHCP-snooping process; when the configured rate is exceeded, IOS err-disables the port, so the limit both protects the server and localizes abuse.

Blast radius: DHCP-scope exhaustion denies IP addressing to every client relying on that scope — a network-wide availability hit for the affected VLAN(s). Excessive DHCP traffic can also degrade the control plane of the switch handling snooping.

Platform nuance: syntax `ip dhcp snooping limit rate <pps>` is applied on the (untrusted) access interface; Cisco commonly recommends around 15 pps for edge access ports. Trusted ports toward the DHCP server should generally NOT be rate-limited (or given a high limit). Enabling per-VLAN snooping (`ip dhcp snooping vlan <list>`) is a prerequisite for meaningful protection. Because SW-007 is gated on snooping being enabled, the fix is purely additive tuning, not a new feature rollout. Since exceeding the limit err-disables the port, pair it with `errdisable recovery cause dhcp-rate-limit`.

Compliance: Cisco Switch Hardening (DHCP Rate Limiting), CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark DHCP-snooping guidance, NIST 800-53 SC-5 (DoS protection), CWE-770 allocation without limits / CWE-400.
steps: Confirm DHCP snooping is enabled (it must be, or this check would not fire): `show ip dhcp snooping`.
Identify the snooped VLANs and trusted vs. untrusted ports: `show ip dhcp snooping`.
List access ports lacking a rate limit and reconcile with the scanner output: `show running-config | include interface|switchport mode access|snooping limit rate`.
Decide the per-port rate: ~15 pps is a common safe value for user access ports; document the chosen value.
Confirm trusted uplink/server-facing ports are marked trusted and will NOT be rate-limited: `show ip dhcp snooping` (Trusted column).
Enter global config: `configure terminal`.
Ensure snooping is armed on the relevant VLANs (prerequisite): `ip dhcp snooping` and `ip dhcp snooping vlan 10,20`.
Select untrusted access ports as a range: `interface range GigabitEthernet1/0/5 - 24`.
Apply the per-port rate limit: `ip dhcp snooping limit rate 15`.
Do NOT apply the limit to trusted server/uplink ports; instead confirm they carry `ip dhcp snooping trust`.
Add automatic recovery so a port tripped by a burst self-heals: `errdisable recovery cause dhcp-rate-limit` and `errdisable recovery interval 300`.
Optionally align Option-82 handling with your DHCP relay design: `ip dhcp snooping information option` (or `no ip dhcp snooping information option` if the relay strips it).
Exit config: `end`.
Verify the rate limit is applied per port: `show ip dhcp snooping` (Rate limit column shows 15).
Confirm no port was err-disabled by legitimate traffic after tuning: `show interfaces status err-disabled`.
Save configuration: `copy running-config startup-config`.
Monitor for `%DHCP_SNOOPING-4-DHCP_SNOOPING_ERRDISABLE_WARNING` events and adjust the rate if false trips occur.
Re-run the audit to confirm SW-007 clears on all access ports.
SW-007.cli:
configure terminal
ip dhcp snooping
ip dhcp snooping vlan 10,20
interface range GigabitEthernet1/0/5 - 24
 ip dhcp snooping limit rate 15
 exit
errdisable recovery cause dhcp-rate-limit
errdisable recovery interval 300
end
copy running-config startup-config
SW-007.verify: `show ip dhcp snooping` lists each access interface with a rate limit of 15 (untrusted) and trusted ports as unlimited. `show running-config interface GigabitEthernet1/0/5` contains `ip dhcp snooping limit rate 15`. If a flood occurs, `show interfaces status err-disabled` shows the port with reason `dhcp-rate-limit` and the log records the DHCP snooping errdisable warning.
SW-007.rollback: `interface range GigabitEthernet1/0/5 - 24` then `no ip dhcp snooping limit rate`; `copy running-config startup-config`. Caveat: removing the limit re-exposes the DHCP server/scope and the snooping process to starvation/flooding DoS from an untrusted port.
SW-007.impact: Low. Applying a per-port rate limit is non-disruptive to normal clients (legitimate DHCP is a few packets per lease). The operational caution is picking too low a value: bursts (e.g. mass power-on after an outage, or a small downstream hub) can exceed it and err-disable the port — mitigate with a sensible pps and errdisable auto-recovery. Never rate-limit trusted server/uplink ports. No reload required.
references: Cisco Switch Hardening — DHCP Snooping Rate Limiting
CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark — DHCP Snooping
CWE-770 Allocation of Resources Without Limits or Throttling

SW-008.risk:
Like SW-007, SW-008 is gated on DHCP snooping being enabled globally (a line matching `^ip dhcp snooping` must be present). When it is, the scanner inspects access ports matching `(GigabitEthernet|FastEthernet)` (`switchport mode access`) for `ip verify source`, and flags access ports that lack it — but only reports if FEWER than 50 such ports are found (a guard to avoid noise on switches where the feature is simply not deployed fleet-wide). The finding is MEDIUM, IOS / IOS-XE Catalyst only. `ip verify source` is the CLI that enables IP Source Guard (IPSG).

IPSG builds on the DHCP snooping binding table (and optionally static bindings) to install a per-port filter that only permits traffic whose source IP — and, with port-security, source MAC — matches a valid binding learned on that port. Without IPSG, a host on an access port can spoof any source IP address (MITRE ATT&CK Adversary-in-the-Middle T1557, and source-address spoofing used in T1498 reflection/DoS). IP spoofing enables ARP-poisoning-adjacent attacks, bypass of IP-based ACLs/firewall rules that trust source addresses, impersonation of another host's IP to hijack sessions, and reflected/amplified DoS with forged sources that are hard to trace.

Blast radius: on a shared segment, a spoofing host can intercept or redirect traffic destined for the impersonated IP and can defeat any downstream access control that relies on source-IP trust, undermining segmentation and audit/attribution. Spoofed-source floods also complicate incident response because the true origin is masked.

Platform nuance: prerequisites matter — DHCP snooping must be enabled and populating bindings for the VLAN, or IPSG will drop legitimate traffic once applied (hosts with static IPs need `ip source binding` entries or they lose connectivity). The base command `ip verify source` filters on source IP; `ip verify source port-security` (older) / `ip verify source tracking port-security` (newer trains) additionally validates source MAC and requires port-security to be configured — confirm the exact keyword with `ip verify source ?` on the target train. Because the check only reports when <50 ports are missing, remediation is typically a targeted per-port rollout on user-facing access ports fed by DHCP. Complementary with Dynamic ARP Inspection (`ip arp inspection vlan`), which also relies on the snooping binding table.
