Compliance: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 5.7 (IP Source Guard), Cisco Layer 2 hardening (IPSG/DAI/DHCP snooping trio), NIST 800-53 SC-7 / SI-4, CWE-290 spoofing / CWE-291 reliance on IP address.
steps: Confirm DHCP snooping is enabled and has a populated binding table: `show ip dhcp snooping` and `show ip dhcp snooping binding`.
Identify access ports missing IPSG and reconcile with the scanner list: `show running-config | include interface|switchport mode access|ip verify source`.
Enumerate hosts with STATIC IPs on those ports (printers, servers) — they need static bindings or they will be blocked: check IPAM / `show mac address-table`.
Create static bindings for known static-IP hosts BEFORE enabling IPSG: `configure terminal` then `ip source binding 0011.2233.4455 vlan 10 192.0.2.50 interface GigabitEthernet1/0/7`.
Plan a maintenance window: enabling IPSG can drop traffic from any host without a valid binding until DHCP re-learns or a static binding exists.
Enter config: `configure terminal`.
Select DHCP-served user access ports: `interface range GigabitEthernet1/0/5 - 24`.
Ensure the port is static access (prerequisite): `switchport mode access`.
Enable IP Source Guard (source-IP filtering): `ip verify source`.

For stronger MAC+IP validation, first ensure port-security is configured, then use `ip verify source port-security` (or `ip verify source tracking port-security` on newer trains — confirm with `ip verify source ?`).
Consider enabling Dynamic ARP Inspection on the same VLANs to complete the trio: `ip arp inspection vlan 10,20`.
Exit config: `end`.
Force clients to obtain fresh leases if needed, or wait for binding population, so the table reflects live hosts: `show ip dhcp snooping binding`.
Verify IPSG is active and filtering per port: `show ip verify source`.
Confirm no legitimate host lost connectivity — check for drops and reachability: `show ip verify source interface GigabitEthernet1/0/7` and ping tests from static hosts.
Save configuration: `copy running-config startup-config`.
Monitor logs for IPSG deny events and add any missing static bindings: `show logging | include IP_SOURCE_GUARD|verify source`.
Re-run the audit to confirm SW-008 clears on targeted ports.
SW-008.cli:
configure terminal
ip dhcp snooping
ip dhcp snooping vlan 10,20
ip source binding 0011.2233.4455 vlan 10 192.0.2.50 interface GigabitEthernet1/0/7
interface range GigabitEthernet1/0/5 - 24
 switchport mode access
 ip verify source
 exit
ip arp inspection vlan 10,20
end
copy running-config startup-config
SW-008.verify: `show ip verify source` should list each protected interface with Filter-type `ip`, Filter-mode `active`, and the permitted IP/MAC pulled from the snooping binding. `show running-config interface GigabitEthernet1/0/5` contains `ip verify source`. `show ip dhcp snooping binding` shows the bindings IPSG is enforcing. Any spoofed source is dropped and logged.
SW-008.rollback: `interface range GigabitEthernet1/0/5 - 24` then `no ip verify source` (and remove any unwanted static binding with `no ip source binding ...`); `copy running-config startup-config`. Caveat: reverting lets hosts on those ports spoof arbitrary source IP/MAC again, re-enabling impersonation, source-based ACL bypass, and spoofed-source DoS.
SW-008.impact: Moderate/disruptive if bindings are incomplete: once `ip verify source` is active, any host lacking a valid DHCP-snooping or static binding is filtered and loses connectivity — statically-addressed devices are the common casualty. Enable in a window, pre-create static bindings, and verify. It does not require a reload and is transparent to DHCP clients that have valid, learned bindings.
references: CIS Cisco IOS Benchmark 5.7 (IP Source Guard)
Cisco Layer 2 Security — IP Source Guard / DAI / DHCP Snooping
CWE-291 Reliance on IP Address for Authentication

WIFI-001.risk:
The scanner walked every `wlan` profile block parsed out of the running-config (the Catalyst 9800 IOS-XE `wlan <profile> <id> <ssid>` construct captured by get_section("wlan")) and flagged WIFI-001 as CRITICAL because at least one WLAN either contains the literal `open` without an accompanying `web-auth` policy, or contains a `wep` cipher/key. In C9800 terms this means a WLAN was left with `no security wpa` (Open System) or configured with `security static-wep-key`. The check evaluates OPEN first and WEP second, so it lists each offending SSID with the reason.

This is a real and severe exposure. An Open WLAN transmits every frame in cleartext: any adjacent attacker running airodump-ng/Wireshark in monitor mode passively captures credentials, session cookies, and internal traffic (ATT&CK T1040 Network Sniffing, T1557 Adversary-in-the-Middle). WEP is cryptographically broken — the RC4 keystream and 24-bit IV allow full key recovery in minutes with aircrack-ng/FMS/PTW attacks, so WEP provides no meaningful confidentiality.

Blast radius is large: an open or WEP SSID is an unauthenticated on-ramp to whatever VLAN the WLAN is mapped to. If that VLAN reaches internal servers, the attacker gains a foothold behind the perimeter without ever touching a wired port, and can pivot, scan, and relay stolen credentials.

Platform nuance: on Catalyst 9800 (IOS-XE) the fix is applied inside the `wlan` profile with the `security wpa` family; on AireOS WLC the equivalent is the flat `config wlan security ...` command set. This check runs on both `wlc` and `iosxe` device types because it keys on the presence of `wlan` sections. A guest captive-portal SSID that legitimately runs open plus `web-auth` is intentionally NOT flagged (the scanner suppresses OPEN when `web-auth` is present), so a hit here means an SSID with neither encryption nor a portal.

Compliance-wise this violates the confidentiality-in-transit and cryptographic-strength expectations of NIST SP 800-97/800-153, PCI DSS v4.0 (strong wireless encryption, no WEP), and NIST 800-53 SC-8/SC-13/AC-18.
steps: Inventory affected WLANs from the report, then confirm live state with `show wlan summary` and `show wlan id <id>` (C9800) or `show wlan summary` / `show wlan <id>` (AireOS).
Identify the client population on each open/WEP SSID with `show wireless client summary` (C9800) or `show client summary` (AireOS) so you can size the migration and warn users.
Decide the target security model per SSID: WPA2/WPA3-Enterprise (802.1X) for corporate, WPA3-SAE or WPA2-PSK (strong key) for IoT/BYOD, or open plus `web-auth` only for true guest portals.

For 802.1X targets, pre-stage AAA on C9800: `aaa new-model`, `radius server RAD1` / `address ipv4 <ip> auth-port 1812 acct-port 1813` / `key 0 <secret>`, then a server GROUP `aaa group server radius RAD-GRP` / `server name RAD1`, and `aaa authentication dot1x DOT1X-AUTH group RAD-GRP` — the auth list must point at the server group, not the bare `radius server`.
Enter config mode with `configure terminal` (C9800). AireOS has no config-terminal — commands are entered directly at the exec prompt.
Administratively down the WLAN before changing security to avoid a half-configured live SSID: `wlan <profile> <id> <ssid>` then `shutdown` (C9800) / `config wlan disable <id>` (AireOS).
Remove insecure settings: `no security static-wep-key` (C9800), and if the WLAN was open confirm it is not left relying on `web-auth`.

Enable WPA2/AES on the profile: `security wpa wpa2 ciphers aes` (C9800 — setting a cipher plus an AKM implicitly enables the WPA master and WPA2 mode; a bare `security wpa` is an incomplete command) / `config wlan security wpa enable <id>` + `config wlan security wpa wpa2 enable <id>` + `config wlan security wpa wpa2 ciphers aes enable <id>` (AireOS).
For Enterprise, set the AKM and auth list: `security wpa akm dot1x` and `security dot1x authentication-list DOT1X-AUTH` (C9800).
For PSK targets instead, set `security wpa akm psk` and `security wpa psk set-key ascii 0 <20+char-passphrase>` (C9800) — see WIFI-004 for key strength.
Add 802.11w to harden the new WLAN: `security pmf optional` (or `mandatory` where all clients support it).
Re-enable the WLAN: `no shutdown` (C9800) / `config wlan enable <id>` (AireOS).

Schedule the cutover in a maintenance window and push the matching SSID profile/PSK or 802.1X supplicant config to clients (GPO/MDM) beforehand — clients on the old open/WEP SSID WILL drop at cutover.
Save the change: `end` then `write memory` (C9800) / `save config` (AireOS).
Validate encryption is active per SSID with `show wlan id <id>` (C9800) / `show wlan <id>` (AireOS) and confirm WPA2/AES Enabled and Open/WEP Disabled.

Confirm clients successfully re-associate under the new security with `show wireless client summary` (C9800) / `show client summary` (AireOS) and monitor RADIUS/auth logs for failures during the window.
WIFI-001.cli:
! Catalyst 9800 (IOS-XE) — convert an OPEN/WEP WLAN to WPA2-Enterprise
configure terminal
aaa new-model
radius server RAD1
 address ipv4 10.10.10.10 auth-port 1812 acct-port 1813
 key 0 <radius-shared-secret>
aaa group server radius RAD-GRP
 server name RAD1
aaa authentication dot1x DOT1X-AUTH group RAD-GRP
wlan CORP-PROFILE 1 CORP-SSID
 shutdown
 no security static-wep-key
 security wpa wpa2 ciphers aes
 security wpa akm dot1x
 security dot1x authentication-list DOT1X-AUTH
 security pmf optional
 no shutdown
end
write memory
WIFI-001.verify: C9800: `show wlan id 1` should show `WPA2 (RSN IE) : Enabled`, `AES Cipher : Enabled`, `802.1x : Enabled`, and no static WEP / Open. AireOS: `show wlan <id>` should show `WPA2 (RSN IE)..... Enabled` and `AES Cipher..... Enabled`. `show wireless client summary` (C9800) / `show client summary` (AireOS) should list clients associated with the secured SSID.
WIFI-001.rollback: Reverse the profile on C9800: `wlan CORP-PROFILE 1 CORP-SSID` / `shutdown` / `no security wpa akm dot1x` / `no security wpa wpa2 ciphers aes` / `no security wpa` / `no shutdown`. Caveat: reverting to Open/WEP re-exposes all client traffic to passive interception and, for WEP, trivial key cracking.
WIFI-001.impact: Disruptive at cutover: toggling `shutdown`/`no shutdown` and changing the security model forces every client on the SSID to disconnect and re-associate with new credentials/PSK, so run it in a maintenance window with client config pre-staged. No controller reload is required and other WLANs are unaffected. Enabling `security pmf mandatory` can break pre-802.11w clients — use `optional` if unsure.
references: NIST SP 800-97 (Establishing Wireless Robust Security Networks, 802.11i)
PCI DSS v4.0 Req 2.2 / 4.2.1 (strong wireless encryption, no WEP)
NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5: SC-8, SC-13, AC-18
CWE-327 (Broken/Risky Crypto — WEP), CWE-319 (Cleartext Transmission — Open)

WIFI-002.risk:
WIFI-002 (HIGH) fires when a `wlan` profile block contains the substring `wpa` but neither `wpa2` nor `wpa3` anywhere in its joined lines. On Catalyst 9800 this corresponds to a WLAN left on legacy WPA version 1 only — e.g. `security wpa wpa1` with no `security wpa wpa2`/`wpa3`. The check emits one finding per offending section, named `WPA1 only on <section>`.

WPA1 is a security risk because it mandates the TKIP cipher (RC4-based with the Michael MIC). TKIP inherits WEP's RC4 weaknesses and is subject to the Beck-Tews and Ohigashi-Morii attacks, which let an adversary recover the MIC key and inject/forge a limited number of short packets (ARP/DNS) without knowing the PMK. Combined with the deprecated MIC, WPA1 offers substantially weaker integrity and confidentiality than WPA2-CCMP or WPA3-GCMP.

A concrete attack: an on-path attacker within RF range performs a chopchop/Beck-Tews style attack to decrypt and forge management-plane-adjacent packets, enabling ARP poisoning and DNS spoofing on the wireless segment (ATT&CK T1557 Adversary-in-the-Middle), or captures the 4-way handshake and mounts offline dictionary attacks against a PSK far faster than against a modern deployment.

Blast radius: every client on the WPA1 SSID shares the weakened cipher, so one SSID drags an entire user population and its mapped VLAN down to a broken standard. Many endpoints negotiate the weakest mutually-supported cipher, so leaving WPA1 enabled alongside WPA2 can still down-negotiate vulnerable clients.

Platform nuance: C9800 uses `security wpa wpa1|wpa2|wpa3` sub-commands; AireOS uses `config wlan security wpa wpa1|wpa2 ...`. WPA3 (`security wpa wpa3` + SAE) requires PMF. The check runs on both `wlc` and `iosxe`. Compliance: WPA1/TKIP fails PCI DSS strong-crypto expectations and NIST SP 800-97/800-153 guidance; maps to NIST 800-53 SC-8/SC-13 and CWE-327.
steps: List the WLANs flagged, then confirm the WPA version live with `show wlan id <id>` (C9800) / `show wlan <id>` (AireOS) — look for `WPA (SSN IE) : Enabled` with WPA2/WPA3 Disabled.
Check the client mix with `show wireless client summary` (C9800) / `show client summary` (AireOS) to ensure endpoints support WPA2-AES (virtually all post-2006 devices do).
Choose target: WPA2-AES (broad compatibility) or WPA2+WPA3 transition mode for PSK, or WPA2/WPA3-Enterprise for 802.1X.
Enter `configure terminal` (C9800). On AireOS enter commands at the exec prompt directly.
Disable the WLAN during the change: `wlan <profile> <id> <ssid>` then `shutdown` (C9800) / `config wlan disable <id>` (AireOS).

Enable WPA2/AES: `security wpa wpa2 ciphers aes` (C9800 — this enables WPA2 mode and the AES cipher together) / `config wlan security wpa wpa2 enable <id>` + `config wlan security wpa wpa2 ciphers aes enable <id>` (AireOS).
Explicitly disable WPA1 so clients cannot down-negotiate: `no security wpa wpa1` (C9800) / `config wlan security wpa wpa1 disable <id>` (AireOS).
Ensure TKIP is off (WPA1 pulls in TKIP): `no security wpa wpa2 ciphers tkip` and `no security wpa wpa1 ciphers tkip` (C9800).
Optionally move to WPA3 transition: `security wpa wpa3` and `security wpa akm sae` with `security pmf optional` for the transition SSID (C9800, 17.x+).
Preserve the existing AKM: keep `security wpa akm dot1x` for Enterprise or `security wpa akm psk` for PSK as appropriate.
Re-enable: `no shutdown` (C9800) / `config wlan enable <id>` (AireOS).
Because dropping WPA1 can strand truly ancient clients, verify no legacy devices remain before the window and communicate the change.
Save: `end` then `write memory` (C9800) / `save config` (AireOS).
Validate the WLAN now advertises WPA2 (and/or WPA3) only via `show wlan id <id>`.
Watch `show wireless client summary` / `show client summary` and AAA logs during cutover to catch any client that fails to associate under WPA2/WPA3.
Update RF/wireless design docs and monitoring baselines to reflect WPA2/WPA3-only as the sanctioned standard.
WIFI-002.cli:
! Catalyst 9800 (IOS-XE) — remove WPA1, enforce WPA2-AES (optional WPA3 transition)
configure terminal
wlan CORP-PROFILE 1 CORP-SSID
 shutdown
 security wpa wpa2 ciphers aes
 no security wpa wpa2 ciphers tkip
 no security wpa wpa1
 no security wpa wpa1 ciphers tkip
 ! optional WPA3 transition mode:
 security wpa wpa3
 security wpa akm sae
 security pmf optional
 no shutdown
end
write memory
WIFI-002.verify: C9800 `show wlan id 1` should show `WPA (SSN IE) : Disabled` and `WPA2 (RSN IE) : Enabled` with `AES Cipher : Enabled`, `TKIP Cipher : Disabled`. AireOS `show wlan <id>` should show `WPA (SSN IE)..... Disabled` and `WPA2 (RSN IE)..... Enabled`.
WIFI-002.rollback: `wlan CORP-PROFILE 1 CORP-SSID` / `security wpa wpa1` / `security wpa wpa1 ciphers tkip` re-enables WPA1 (C9800). Caveat: restoring WPA1/TKIP re-exposes the SSID to Beck-Tews packet forgery and weak-integrity down-negotiation.
WIFI-002.impact: Disruptive to clients still using WPA1/TKIP — they will disconnect at cutover and must be replaced or upgraded; modern clients re-associate transparently. No reload needed and only the edited WLAN is affected. If you add WPA3 with PMF mandatory, pre-802.11w clients on that AKM will fail; use optional/transition mode to avoid lockout.
references: PCI DSS v4.0 Req 4.2.1 (strong cryptography for wireless)
CWE-327 (Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm)

WIFI-003.risk:
WIFI-003 (HIGH) triggers when a `wlan` profile block contains the substring `tkip` but does not contain `aes` or `ccmp`. In Catalyst 9800 terms this is a WLAN whose cipher suite is `security wpa wpa2 ciphers tkip` (or WPA1 TKIP) with no AES-CCMP configured. The finding is emitted per offending section: `TKIP cipher on <section> (no AES)`.

TKIP (Temporal Key Integrity Protocol) is a stop-gap cipher designed to run on WEP-era hardware. It uses RC4 for confidentiality and the Michael algorithm for integrity, both of which are weak: the Beck-Tews and Ohigashi-Morii attacks exploit Michael's MIC-failure countermeasures and TKIP's per-packet key construction to decrypt and forge short packets (ARP, DNS) in minutes, enabling injection-based adversary-in-the-middle activity (ATT&CK T1557). TKIP also caps throughput to 54 Mbps and blocks 802.11n/ac/ax high-throughput rates — so it is both insecure and a performance drag.

Blast radius: any client that negotiates TKIP on the SSID gets the weak cipher, and because negotiation picks the weakest mutually supported suite, leaving TKIP enabled alongside AES lets an attacker's crafted client force TKIP. All traffic on the SSID and its mapped VLAN is affected.

Platform nuance: C9800 configures ciphers per WPA generation (`security wpa wpa2 ciphers aes|tkip`); AireOS uses `config wlan security wpa wpa2 ciphers {aes|tkip} {enable|disable} <id>`. WPA3 uses GCMP/CCMP and never TKIP. This check runs on both `wlc` and `iosxe`. Compliance: TKIP-only fails PCI DSS strong-crypto and IEEE 802.11i/NIST SP 800-97 robust-security-network guidance; maps to NIST 800-53 SC-8/SC-13 and CWE-327.
steps: Confirm each flagged WLAN's cipher live with `show wlan id <id>` (C9800) / `show wlan <id>` (AireOS) and note `TKIP Cipher : Enabled`, `AES Cipher : Disabled`.
Verify client capability (all Wi-Fi CERTIFIED devices since 2006 support AES-CCMP) via `show wireless client summary` (C9800) / `show client summary` (AireOS).
Enter `configure terminal` (C9800); AireOS commands run at the exec prompt.
Disable the WLAN during the cipher change: `wlan <profile> <id> <ssid>` then `shutdown` (C9800) / `config wlan disable <id>` (AireOS).
Enable AES-CCMP: `security wpa wpa2 ciphers aes` (C9800) / `config wlan security wpa wpa2 ciphers aes enable <id>` (AireOS).
Disable TKIP explicitly: `no security wpa wpa2 ciphers tkip` (C9800) / `config wlan security wpa wpa2 ciphers tkip disable <id>` (AireOS).
If WPA1 is also present, remove it since it forces TKIP: `no security wpa wpa1` (C9800).
For future-proofing, consider WPA3 which mandates AES/GCMP and PMF: `security wpa wpa3` + `security wpa akm sae` + `security pmf mandatory` (C9800 17.x+).
Keep the existing AKM (`security wpa akm dot1x` or `security wpa akm psk`) intact.
Re-enable the WLAN: `no shutdown` (C9800) / `config wlan enable <id>` (AireOS).
Ensure any legacy TKIP-only clients (very old handhelds/scanners) are identified and replaced before the window — they will lose connectivity.
Save the config: `end` then `write memory` (C9800) / `save config` (AireOS).
Re-run `show wlan id <id>` and confirm `AES Cipher : Enabled`, `TKIP Cipher : Disabled`.
Validate 802.11n/ac/ax high-throughput rates are now available (TKIP no longer caps to legacy rates) with `show wireless client mac-address <mac> detail` (C9800).
Monitor `show wireless client summary` / `show client summary` and AAA logs during cutover for association failures.
Record AES-CCMP (and WPA3/GCMP where deployed) as the mandated cipher standard in the wireless baseline.
WIFI-003.cli:
! Catalyst 9800 (IOS-XE) — enforce AES-CCMP, remove TKIP
configure terminal
wlan CORP-PROFILE 1 CORP-SSID
 shutdown
 security wpa wpa2 ciphers aes
 no security wpa wpa2 ciphers tkip
 no security wpa wpa1
 no shutdown
end
write memory
WIFI-003.verify: C9800 `show wlan id 1` should show `AES Cipher : Enabled` and `TKIP Cipher : Disabled`. AireOS `show wlan <id>` should show `AES Cipher..... Enabled` and `TKIP Cipher..... Disabled`.
WIFI-003.rollback: `wlan CORP-PROFILE 1 CORP-SSID` / `security wpa wpa2 ciphers tkip` re-adds TKIP (C9800). Caveat: re-enabling TKIP restores Beck-Tews packet-forgery exposure and caps the SSID to legacy 54 Mbps rates.
WIFI-003.impact: Disruptive only to clients that had negotiated TKIP; AES-capable clients re-associate seamlessly. No reload required; only the edited WLAN changes. Removing TKIP also lifts the 802.11n/ac/ax high-throughput block, so most clients see improved performance.

WIFI-004.risk:
WIFI-004 (HIGH) parses each line of every `wlan` block with the regex `(psk|pre-shared-key|wpa-psk)\s+(\S+)` and, when the captured token immediately following the keyword is shorter than 12 characters, raises `Weak PSK on <section> (length: N)`. In practice this targets a Catalyst 9800 `security wpa psk set-key ascii 0 <key>` (or AireOS `config wlan security wpa akm psk set-key ascii <key> <id>`) where the passphrase is short. The check measures the literal characters after the keyword token, so it detects short cleartext/ASCII keys embedded in the config.

A short PSK is the single most common wireless compromise vector. The WPA2 4-way handshake can be captured passively, or forced via a deauthentication burst (ATT&CK T1557, relevant to T1110 Brute Force), then cracked entirely offline with hashcat/aircrack-ng. An 8-10 character key drawn from a human-memorable space falls to GPU dictionary+rule attacks in hours; there is no lockout, no online rate limit, and no defender visibility during the offline crack. WPA3-SAE resists offline cracking but WPA2-PSK does not.

Blast radius: the PSK is a shared secret. Once cracked, the attacker joins the SSID as a trusted client, reaches the mapped VLAN, and — because the PSK is the same for everyone — the key must be rotated across the entire user/device fleet to recover, which is operationally painful. Cracked keys also allow passive decryption of any handshake the attacker captured.

Platform nuance: C9800 stores the key (rendered obfuscated/type-8 in running-config after entry) under the wlan profile; AireOS uses the flat `config wlan security wpa akm psk set-key ...` form. This check runs on both `wlc` and `iosxe`. The durable fix is 802.1X (per-user credentials, no shared secret) or WPA3-SAE. Compliance maps to NIST 800-53 IA-5 (authenticator strength), NIST SP 800-63B, and CWE-521 (Weak Password Requirements)/CWE-326.
steps: Identify each WLAN and PSK length from the report; confirm the SSID uses PSK AKM with `show wlan id <id>` (C9800) / `show wlan <id>` (AireOS).
Decide strategy per SSID: strongly prefer migrating corporate SSIDs to 802.1X (no shared secret); for IoT/BYOD use WPA3-SAE or a long random WPA2 PSK.
Generate a high-entropy passphrase of 20+ characters (up to the 63-char max) from a password manager — do not reuse or make it guessable.
Enter `configure terminal` (C9800); AireOS commands run at the exec prompt.
Disable the WLAN before rotating the key to avoid a transient mismatch: `wlan <profile> <id> <ssid>` then `shutdown` (C9800) / `config wlan disable <id>` (AireOS).
Set the new strong PSK: `security wpa psk set-key ascii 0 <20+char-passphrase>` and `security wpa akm psk` (C9800) / `config wlan security wpa akm psk set-key ascii <passphrase> <id>` (AireOS).
Ensure the cipher is AES: `security wpa wpa2 ciphers aes` (C9800).

Better: convert to WPA3-SAE which defeats offline cracking — `security wpa wpa3`, `security wpa akm sae`, `security pmf mandatory` (C9800 17.x+), and set the key with `security wpa psk set-key ascii 0 <passphrase>`.
Best: convert to 802.1X — `security wpa akm dot1x`, `security dot1x authentication-list DOT1X-AUTH`, and remove PSK with `no security wpa akm psk` (C9800), backed by RADIUS.
Re-enable: `no shutdown` (C9800) / `config wlan enable <id>` (AireOS).
Distribute the new PSK / 802.1X profile to clients via MDM/GPO before cutover — all clients on the old PSK will drop.
Rotate on a schedule and treat the PSK as a secret: store it in a vault, not the config repo (the scanner reads it in cleartext).
Save: `end` then `write memory` (C9800) / `save config` (AireOS).
Confirm the change did not leave a short key: re-scan or inspect `show wlan id <id>` (the running key is now obfuscated/type-8).
Validate clients re-associate with the new credential via `show wireless client summary` (C9800) / `show client summary` (AireOS).
For long-term hygiene, prefer per-SSID unique keys and move sensitive SSIDs off PSK entirely.
WIFI-004.cli:
! Catalyst 9800 (IOS-XE) — replace weak PSK (best: convert to 802.1X)
configure terminal
wlan IOT-PROFILE 3 IOT-SSID
 shutdown
 security wpa wpa2 ciphers aes
 security wpa akm psk
 security wpa psk set-key ascii 0 <REDACTED-24char-random-passphrase>
 ! preferred hardening — WPA3-SAE (transition example shown):
 security wpa wpa3
 security wpa akm sae
 security pmf mandatory
 no shutdown
end
write memory
WIFI-004.verify: C9800 `show wlan id 3` should show `PSK : Enabled` (or `SAE : Enabled` for WPA3) and `AES Cipher : Enabled`; the raw key is not printed. Re-running the scanner should no longer report a PSK under 12 chars. AireOS `show wlan <id>` shows `PSK..... Enabled`.
WIFI-004.rollback: Re-apply the old key with `security wpa psk set-key ascii 0 <old-key>` (C9800). Caveat: reverting to a short PSK re-exposes the SSID to offline handshake cracking, and the recovered key grants full VLAN access to any attacker.
WIFI-004.impact: Disruptive: rotating a PSK (or moving to 802.1X/SAE) disconnects every client on the SSID until they re-authenticate with the new credential, so pre-stage clients and use a maintenance window. No reload needed; only the edited WLAN changes. WPA3-SAE with PMF mandatory can exclude pre-802.11w clients.
references: Wi-Fi Alliance WPA3 / PSK best practices
CWE-521 (Weak Password Requirements), CWE-326 (Inadequate Encryption Strength)

WIFI-005.risk:
WIFI-005 (HIGH) inspects `wlan` profile blocks whose profile/SSID name contains any of `mgmt`, `admin`, `management`, or `infra`, and flags the WLAN when its joined lines contain none of `802.1x`, `eap`, or `dot1x`. The intent: an SSID that by naming convention carries management/infrastructure traffic but authenticates with something weaker than 802.1X (typically a PSK or open). The finding is `Management WLAN without 802.1X: <section>`.

Management WLANs are high-value: they often reach device management planes, jump hosts, or infrastructure VLANs. Authenticating such an SSID with a shared PSK means every person who ever learned the key — including departed staff and anyone who cracked a captured handshake (see WIFI-004) — can reach the management network. There is no per-user identity, no certificate binding, and no clean revocation. This is precisely the credential-reuse/shared-secret weakness adversaries exploit for lateral movement (ATT&CK T1078 Valid Accounts, T1557 for handshake capture).

Blast radius is disproportionate: compromise of a management SSID can expose controller/switch/router administration, bypass network segmentation intended to isolate infrastructure, and provide a launch point to push malicious configs fleet-wide. 802.1X/EAP-TLS with per-device certificates removes the shared secret, binds access to identity, and integrates with RADIUS CoA for immediate revocation.

Platform nuance: C9800 requires an authentication-list (`security dot1x authentication-list <name>`) plus `aaa new-model` and a RADIUS server group; AireOS uses `config wlan security wpa akm 802.1x enable <id>` and a configured RADIUS auth server mapped to the WLAN. This check runs on both `wlc` and `iosxe`. Consider whether the traffic should be on Wi-Fi at all — a dedicated wired OOB management path is stronger. Compliance maps to NIST 800-53 IA-2/AC-18, NIST SP 800-153, and CWE-287 (Improper Authentication).
steps: Confirm each flagged management-named SSID and its current AKM with `show wlan id <id>` (C9800) / `show wlan <id>` (AireOS).
Question whether management traffic belongs on wireless at all; if a wired OOB path exists, plan to retire the SSID rather than harden it.
Stand up RADIUS/EAP-TLS: ensure a RADIUS server (e.g. ISE) is reachable and configured with the correct device/user certificate policy.

On C9800, configure AAA: `aaa new-model`, `radius server RAD1` / `address ipv4 <ip> auth-port 1812 acct-port 1813` / `key 0 <secret>`, a server group `aaa group server radius RAD-GRP` / `server name RAD1`, then `aaa authentication dot1x DOT1X-AUTH group RAD-GRP` — the auth-list references the GROUP, not the bare server.
On AireOS, add the RADIUS auth server (`config radius auth add <index> <ip> 1812 ascii <secret>`) and enable it for network users / management as required.
Enter `configure terminal` (C9800); AireOS commands run at the exec prompt.
Disable the WLAN during conversion: `wlan <profile> <id> <ssid>` then `shutdown` (C9800) / `config wlan disable <id>` (AireOS).
Remove PSK auth: `no security wpa akm psk`; if the WLAN was open, ensure WPA is enabled by setting a cipher/AKM below (C9800).

Enable 802.1X AKM: `security wpa akm dot1x` and bind the list `security dot1x authentication-list DOT1X-AUTH` (C9800) / `config wlan security wpa akm 802.1x enable <id>` + map the RADIUS server (AireOS).
Enforce WPA2 + AES: `security wpa wpa2 ciphers aes` (or WPA3-Enterprise with `security wpa wpa3` + PMF) (C9800).
Add PMF for the management SSID: `security pmf mandatory` (management clients are modern and should support 802.11w).
Re-enable: `no shutdown` (C9800) / `config wlan enable <id>` (AireOS).
Pre-provision management endpoints with the EAP supplicant profile and certificates before cutover.
Save: `end` then `write memory` (C9800) / `save config` (AireOS).
Validate 802.1X is active with `show wlan id <id>` (`802.1x : Enabled`) and test an EAP authentication end-to-end, watching RADIUS logs.
Confirm the shared PSK is fully retired and rotate/revoke it everywhere it was distributed.
WIFI-005.cli:
! Catalyst 9800 (IOS-XE) — convert a management SSID from PSK to 802.1X
configure terminal
aaa new-model
radius server RAD1
 address ipv4 10.10.10.10 auth-port 1812 acct-port 1813
 key 0 <radius-shared-secret>
aaa group server radius RAD-GRP
 server name RAD1
aaa authentication dot1x DOT1X-AUTH group RAD-GRP
wlan MGMT-PROFILE 5 MGMT-SSID
 shutdown
 no security wpa akm psk
 security wpa wpa2 ciphers aes
 security wpa akm dot1x
 security dot1x authentication-list DOT1X-AUTH
 security pmf mandatory
 no shutdown
end
write memory
WIFI-005.verify: C9800 `show wlan id 5` should show `802.1x : Enabled`, `PSK : Disabled`, `AES Cipher : Enabled`, and PMF Required. AireOS `show wlan <id>` should show `802.1x..... Enabled` under Auth Key Management. A test client should authenticate via RADIUS (confirm in ISE/RADIUS live logs).
WIFI-005.rollback: `wlan MGMT-PROFILE 5 MGMT-SSID` / `no security wpa akm dot1x` / `security wpa akm psk` / `security wpa psk set-key ascii 0 <key>` (C9800). Caveat: reverting to PSK restores a single shared secret to the management network with no per-user identity or revocation.
WIFI-005.impact: Disruptive: management clients must be pre-provisioned with 802.1X supplicant settings and certificates or they lose access at cutover; do it in a window with console/OOB access to the controller as a fallback. No reload required. PMF mandatory may exclude any legacy management endpoint lacking 802.11w.
references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5: IA-2, AC-18, AC-17
Cisco Catalyst 9800 / WLC Security Best Practices

WIFI-006.risk:
WIFI-006 (HIGH) is a global-line check: it fires when the running-config contains a line matching the regex `management\s+(via|over)\s+wireless.*enable` — i.e. a management-via/over-wireless directive that is turned on. The code's own remediation targets the AireOS control `config network mgmt-via-wireless`, whose enabled state is reflected in `show network summary` as `Mgmt Via Wireless ... Enable`. The finding is `WLC management via wireless is enabled`.

Management-via-wireless allows administrators to reach the controller's management interface (HTTPS/SSH GUI/CLI) from clients associated to the wireless network. That collapses a critical trust boundary: the wireless client population — including guests, BYOD, and any attacker who has cracked or bypassed WLAN security — can now attempt to authenticate to, brute-force, or exploit the controller's management plane. This is a textbook management-plane exposure (ATT&CK T1078 Valid Accounts against the mgmt interface, T1190 if a controller CVE is in play).

Blast radius is the entire wireless estate: the WLC controls every joined AP and WLAN, so a compromised controller means attacker-controlled SSIDs, client de-auth, traffic redirection, and config exfiltration across the whole deployment. Management access should be constrained to a dedicated wired management VLAN reachable only by admin hosts/jump servers.

Platform nuance: this is fundamentally an AireOS concept (`config network mgmt-via-wireless`). On Catalyst 9800 (IOS-XE) there is no single equivalent toggle — you instead restrict the management/wireless-management interface with management ACLs, `ip http` access-class restrictions, and by keeping the wireless management interface off client-facing VLANs. The scanner's IOS-XE branch (`wireless management interface.*wireless`) is a no-op today, so a hit here reflects the AireOS-style directive.

Compliance maps to NIST 800-53 AC-17/AC-18/SC-7 and CWE-284 (Improper Access Control).
steps: Confirm the current state on AireOS with `show network summary` and check the `Mgmt Via Wireless Interface` field (expect it to read Enable when flagged).
Identify how admins currently reach the controller (wired mgmt VLAN vs wireless) so disabling this does not orphan the only access path.
Ensure you have out-of-band or wired management connectivity to the WLC before making the change (serial console or wired mgmt IP).
On AireOS, disable the feature: `config network mgmt-via-wireless disable`.
Save the AireOS config: `save config` (answer `y` to confirm).
Re-verify with `show network summary` that `Mgmt Via Wireless Interface..... Disable` is now shown.

Restrict management further with a CPU/management ACL permitting only admin subnets: `config acl create MGMT-ACL`, add rules with `config acl rule ...`, then apply it to the controller's management path.
Confirm SSH/HTTPS management reachability from an authorized wired admin host after the change.

On Catalyst 9800 (IOS-XE), there is no `mgmt-via-wireless` toggle — instead restrict HTTP/HTTPS management with `ip http access-class ipv4 MGMT-ACL` and harden `ip http secure-server` / disable `ip http server`.
On C9800, restrict SSH/VTY with `line vty 0 15` / `access-class MGMT-ACL in` and `transport input ssh`.
On C9800, ensure the wireless management interface is on a dedicated management VLAN not shared with client SSIDs (`wireless management interface Vlan<mgmt>`).
Define `MGMT-ACL` on C9800 to permit only admin/jump-host subnets: `ip access-list standard MGMT-ACL` / `permit <admin-subnet> <wildcard>` / `deny any log`.
Document the approved management path (wired VLAN + admin hosts) in the network standard.
Save on C9800: `end` then `write memory`.
Validate that a client on a normal WLAN can no longer reach the controller management ports (test HTTPS/SSH from a wireless client — should fail/timeout).
Add monitoring/alerting for management-plane auth attempts sourced from wireless subnets.
WIFI-006.cli:
! AireOS WLC — disable management over wireless
config network mgmt-via-wireless disable
save config

! (Catalyst 9800 / IOS-XE equivalent — restrict mgmt plane instead)
configure terminal
ip access-list standard MGMT-ACL
 permit 10.99.0.0 0.0.0.255
 deny   any log
ip http access-class ipv4 MGMT-ACL
line vty 0 15
 access-class MGMT-ACL in
 transport input ssh
end
write memory
WIFI-006.verify: AireOS: `show network summary` must show `Mgmt Via Wireless Interface..... Disable`. C9800: `show ip http server status` should reflect the access-class, and `show running-config | include access-class|transport input` should show the VTY/HTTP restrictions; a wireless client attempting SSH/HTTPS to the controller should be denied.
WIFI-006.rollback: AireOS: `config network mgmt-via-wireless enable` / `save config`. C9800: remove the access-class bindings. Caveat: re-enabling wireless management re-exposes the controller's management plane to every wireless client, including guests and attackers who bypassed WLAN security.
WIFI-006.impact: Low traffic impact but potentially self-locking: if the admin performing the change is connected via wireless management, disabling it drops that session — always have wired/OOB console access first. No reload and no client-traffic disruption. C9800 ACL restrictions could lock out admins if the wrong subnets are permitted, so validate the ACL before saving.
references: Cisco Wireless LAN Controller Configuration Best Practices (management access)
NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5: AC-17, AC-18, SC-7

WIFI-007.risk:
WIFI-007 (MEDIUM) fires only when `device_type == wlc` (AireOS) AND the config shows no line matching any of `rogue.*detection`, `wps.*rogue`, or `rogue ap`. In other words, the running-config contains no visible rogue-AP detection/containment configuration. Finding: `Rogue AP detection not configured`.

Rogue AP detection is a core wireless IPS control. Without it, an attacker (or a careless employee) can stand up an unauthorized AP — either a physical device plugged into the wired network or a software Evil Twin advertising a legitimate SSID — and the controller will neither alarm nor contain it. Evil-twin / karma attacks (ATT&CK T1557 Adversary-in-the-Middle, T1200 Hardware Additions for wired rogues) let the adversary harvest credentials, capture handshakes, and man-in-the-middle clients that roam to the stronger signal.

Blast radius: a wired-connected rogue creates a backdoor bridging the wireless RF space directly into an internal VLAN, bypassing NAC/802.1X on the wired side; an over-the-air evil twin can siphon an entire office's clients. Detection with RLDP (Rogue Location Discovery Protocol) can determine whether a rogue is on the wired network, and containment can de-auth its clients — though containment carries legal/operational caveats (you must only contain rogues on your own network).

Platform nuance: AireOS uses the `config rogue ...` / `config wps ...` family and per-AP monitor mode; Catalyst 9800 uses the `wireless wps rogue ...` family and aWIPS. Because this check is gated on `device_type == wlc`, it will NOT fire on a C9800 (iosxe) config even if rogue policy is absent there. Compliance: rogue/unauthorized-AP detection is explicitly required by PCI DSS v4.0 Req 11.2.1 (detect and identify unauthorized wireless access points) and maps to NIST 800-53 SI-4/AC-18.
steps: Confirm the current rogue posture on AireOS with `show rogue ap summary` and `show wps summary` (expect little/no policy configured when flagged).
Review AP modes with `show ap summary` to decide which APs run local vs monitor mode (dedicated monitor APs improve scanning coverage).
Enable rogue detection with an appropriate security level: `config rogue detection security-level high` (or `critical` for high-security sites).
Enable RLDP to classify whether rogues are on the wired network: `config rogue ap rldp enable`.
Set the rogue detection report/scan cadence as needed: `config rogue detection report-interval <10-300>` and `config rogue detection min-rssi <-128 to -70>`.
Optionally dedicate scanning APs: `config ap mode monitor <ap-name>` (requires AP reboot; schedule accordingly).
Create classification rules to auto-classify malicious rogues: `config rogue rule add ap priority <n> classify malicious <rule-name>` and set conditions (e.g. managed-ssid, client-count).
CAUTION before containment: containing an AP transmits de-auth frames — only contain rogues confirmed on YOUR network to avoid disrupting/attacking neighbors (legal risk).
Enable auto-containment conservatively for RLDP-confirmed wired rogues: `config rogue ap rldp auto-contain monitor-ap-only` and `config rogue auto-contain level 1`.
Enable ad-hoc rogue handling in alarm mode first: `config rogue adhoc alarm-only` (start with alarm-only, not auto-contain).

On Catalyst 9800 (IOS-XE) rogue detection is on by default; tune it with `wireless wps rogue detection report-interval ` / `wireless wps rogue detection min-rssi `, define classification rules with `wireless wps rogue rule priority

` then `classify malicious`, and enable wired detection under `wireless wps rogue ap rldp ...`.
Integrate alarms with monitoring: forward WLC traps/syslog for rogue events to the SIEM/Catalyst Center/Prime.
Save on AireOS: `save config`.
Validate detection is active: `show wps summary` and `show rogue ap summary` should now show rogue policy and any detected APs with a classification.
Verify RLDP classification on a test rogue (if safe to test) shows On-Wire status.
Document the containment policy and the legal boundary (only contain confirmed on-network rogues) in the security runbook.
WIFI-007.cli:
! AireOS WLC — enable rogue AP detection + conservative containment
config rogue detection security-level high
config rogue ap rldp enable
config rogue detection report-interval 60
config rogue rule add ap priority 1 classify malicious managed-rogue
config rogue ap rldp auto-contain monitor-ap-only
config rogue adhoc alarm-only
save config

! Catalyst 9800 (IOS-XE) — detection is on by default; define a rule
configure terminal
wireless wps rogue rule managed-rogue priority 1
 classify malicious
exit
end
write memory
WIFI-007.verify: AireOS: `show wps summary` should show Rogue detection/policy enabled and `show rogue ap summary` should list detected APs with a Class (Malicious/Unclassified) and, with RLDP, On-Wire status. C9800: `show wireless wps rogue ap summary` and `show wireless wps summary` should show rogue detection active.
WIFI-007.rollback: AireOS: `config rogue detection security-level low` / `config rogue ap rldp disable` / `config rogue ap rldp auto-contain disable` (or remove rules). Caveat: disabling rogue detection re-blinds the controller to evil-twin and wired-backdoor APs, reopening a stealthy AitM/foothold path.
WIFI-007.impact: Detection itself is non-disruptive. Switching an AP to monitor mode reboots that AP and removes it from client service (plan capacity). Containment IS disruptive and legally sensitive — it de-auths clients of the targeted AP, so start with alarm-only and only auto-contain RLDP-confirmed on-network rogues. No controller reload required.
references: PCI DSS v4.0 Req 11.2.1 (detect unauthorized wireless access points)
Cisco WLC / Catalyst 9800 Rogue Management design guide

WIFI-008.risk:
WIFI-008 (MEDIUM) fires when `device_type == wlc` (AireOS) and the config has no line matching the regex `client.exclusion.*enable` — i.e. a client-exclusion-enabled directive is not visibly present. The code's own remediation suggests the AireOS global form `config wps client-exclusion all enable`. Finding: `Client exclusion policies not enabled`.

Client exclusion is a wireless brute-force / DoS mitigation. When enabled, the controller automatically quarantines (temporarily blocks) a client MAC that trips defined failure conditions — repeated 802.11 association failures, repeated 802.1X/WebAuth authentication failures, PSK/IP-theft, or excessive 4-way-handshake failures. Without it, an attacker can hammer an SSID with endless authentication attempts to brute-force a PSK or credentials (ATT&CK T1110 Brute Force) and repeatedly exhaust association/auth resources as a denial-of-service against legitimate users, with no automatic backoff.

Blast radius: a single misbehaving or malicious client can degrade service for everyone on an AP by consuming association slots and RADIUS transactions, and unlimited auth retries materially speed up online guessing against weaker credentials. Exclusion adds a temporary lockout (default ~60s, tunable) that throttles these behaviours and buys defenders time and log signal.

Platform nuance: on AireOS, the global Client Exclusion Policies (which failure categories trigger exclusion) live under Wireless Protection Policies, and per-WLAN exclusion + timeout is configured with `config wlan exclusionlist <wlan-id> <timeout>`. On Catalyst 9800 (IOS-XE) client exclusion lives in the policy profile (`wireless profile policy <name>` -> `exclusionlist timeout <seconds>`). Because the regex is AireOS-oriented and the check is gated on `device_type == wlc`, it will not fire on a C9800 config. Compliance maps to NIST 800-53 AC-7 (unsuccessful logon attempts) and SC-5 (DoS protection).
steps: Confirm current state on AireOS with `show wps summary` (Client Exclusion Policies section) and `show wlan <id>` (Exclusionlist Timeout per WLAN).
Review whether any legitimate automation/scanner MACs might trip exclusion so you can whitelist or tune thresholds before enabling.

Enable the global exclusion policy categories (excessive 802.11 assoc failures, 802.1X auth failures, WebAuth failures, IP theft/reuse) via the Wireless Protection Policies page or the corresponding `config wps client-exclusion ...` commands.
Set a sane global exclusion timeout (default 60s; consider 180s for higher security).
Enable exclusion per WLAN with a timeout: `config wlan exclusionlist <wlan-id> 180` (this enables exclusion and sets the 180-second timeout).
Repeat the per-WLAN exclusion enable for each production SSID that should enforce it.
Whitelist any known-good MACs that must never be excluded (e.g. critical fixed devices) using the exclusion/MAC-filter allow mechanism.
Confirm no legitimate clients are being excluded after enabling: `show exclusionlist` (lists currently excluded MACs and reason).
On Catalyst 9800 (IOS-XE), configure exclusion in the policy profile: `wireless profile policy <name>` then `exclusionlist timeout 180`.
On C9800, tie the policy profile to the WLAN via the policy tag so exclusion actually applies to clients.
Integrate exclusion events with the SIEM: forward WLC syslog/traps for client-exclusion events for brute-force visibility.
Tune the timeout upward if brute-force attempts persist, balancing against legitimate roaming reconnection.
Save on AireOS: `save config`.
Save on C9800: `end` then `write memory`.
Validate protection: from a lab client, trigger repeated auth failures and confirm the MAC appears in `show exclusionlist` (AireOS) / `show wireless exclusionlist` (C9800).
Document the exclusion thresholds and timeout in the wireless security baseline.
WIFI-008.cli:
! AireOS WLC — enable client exclusion (global policies + per-WLAN timeout)
config wps client-exclusion all enable
config wlan exclusionlist 1 180
config wlan exclusionlist 2 180
save config

! Catalyst 9800 (IOS-XE) — exclusion in the policy profile
configure terminal
wireless profile policy CORP-POLICY
 exclusionlist timeout 180
end
write memory
WIFI-008.verify: AireOS: `show wps summary` should show the Client Exclusion Policies enabled and `show wlan <id>` should show a non-zero `Exclusion List Timeout`; `show exclusionlist` shows any currently excluded MACs. C9800: `show wireless profile policy detailed CORP-POLICY` should show the exclusion timeout set.
WIFI-008.rollback: AireOS: `config wlan exclusionlist <wlan-id> disable` (per WLAN) and `config wps client-exclusion all disable`. C9800: `no exclusionlist timeout` under the policy profile. Caveat: disabling exclusion removes automatic throttling of brute-force and association-flood clients, easing online credential/PSK guessing and client-level DoS.
WIFI-008.impact: Low disruption when tuned correctly: legitimate clients are unaffected. The main risk is a too-aggressive threshold excluding a flapping-but-valid client or a shared NAT/scanner MAC — whitelist such devices and start with a moderate timeout. No reload required and no impact to well-behaved traffic.
references: Cisco WLC / Catalyst 9800 Security Best Practices (Client Exclusion)

WIFI-009.risk:
WIFI-009 (HIGH) fires when `device_type == wlc` and either the config has a line matching `transfer.*telnet`, or it has a `telnet` line without any `ssh` line present. The intent is to detect a WLC whose CLI management is reachable over Telnet rather than (or instead of) SSH. Finding: `WLC management via Telnet (not SSH)`.

Telnet transmits the entire management session — including administrator username and password — in cleartext. Any attacker positioned on-path (a compromised switch, an ARP-poisoned segment, a span/tap, or the wireless side if management-via-wireless is also on) captures admin credentials verbatim (ATT&CK T1040 Network Sniffing, T1552.001 Credentials in traffic). Once the WLC admin credentials are captured, the attacker owns the controller and, transitively, every AP and WLAN it manages.

Blast radius: full compromise of the wireless control plane — rogue SSID creation, mass client de-auth, traffic redirection, config theft. Because the same admin credential is often reused across the network estate, cleartext capture here can seed lateral movement well beyond wireless.

Platform nuance: on AireOS, SSH/Telnet are toggled globally with `config network ssh enable` and `config network telnet disable`, and shown in `show network summary`. On Catalyst 9800 (IOS-XE) this is standard IOS hardening — set a hostname and domain name, generate an RSA key, set `ip ssh version 2`, and restrict VTY with `transport input ssh` (omitting telnet). This check is gated on `device_type == wlc`, so it targets the AireOS WLC form. Order matters on IOS-XE: the RSA crypto key (which requires hostname + domain name) must exist BEFORE SSH will start.

Compliance maps to CIS Cisco hardening (management-plane), NIST 800-53 AC-17/IA-2/SC-8, and CWE-319 (Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information).
steps: Confirm current state on AireOS with `show network summary` — check the `Secure Shell (ssh)` and `Telnet` fields (flagged when Telnet is Enable and/or SSH absent).
Ensure you have a working SSH client and a reachable management IP/OOB console before disabling Telnet so you are not locked out.
On AireOS, enable SSH first: `config network ssh enable`.
Verify SSH works by logging in over SSH from an admin host before turning Telnet off.
On AireOS, disable Telnet: `config network telnet disable`.
Restrict management access to admin subnets with a CPU/management ACL (`config acl create MGMT-ACL`, add rules, apply) so even SSH is limited.
Save the AireOS config: `save config`.
Re-check `show network summary` to confirm `Secure Shell (ssh)..... Enable` and `Telnet..... Disable`.
On Catalyst 9800 (IOS-XE), first ensure a `hostname` and `ip domain name <domain>` are configured — RSA key generation requires them.
On C9800, generate the RSA key FIRST (SSH will not start without it): `crypto key generate rsa modulus 2048` (or 4096).
On C9800, enable SSHv2: `ip ssh version 2` and optionally `ip ssh time-out 60` / `ip ssh authentication-retries 3`.
On C9800, restrict VTY to SSH only: `line vty 0 15` then `transport input ssh` and `login local` or AAA.
On C9800, ensure no `transport input telnet` / `transport input all` remains on the VTY lines, and add `access-class MGMT-ACL in` to limit source subnets.
Save on C9800: `end` then `write memory`.
Validate: attempt a Telnet connection to the controller (should be refused/timeout) and an SSH connection (should succeed); on C9800 confirm with `show ip ssh` (Version 2.0 enabled).
Update jump-host/admin runbooks and monitoring to expect SSH-only management and alert on any Telnet attempts.
WIFI-009.cli:
! AireOS WLC — enable SSH, disable Telnet
config network ssh enable
config network telnet disable
save config

! Catalyst 9800 (IOS-XE) equivalent — hostname + domain + RSA key BEFORE ssh
configure terminal
hostname WLC-9800
ip domain name example.com
crypto key generate rsa modulus 2048
ip ssh version 2
ip ssh time-out 60
ip ssh authentication-retries 3
line vty 0 15
 transport input ssh
 login local
end
write memory
WIFI-009.verify: AireOS: `show network summary` must show `Secure Shell (ssh)..... Enable` and `Telnet..... Disable`. C9800: `show ip ssh` should report `SSH Enabled - version 2.0`, and `show running-config | section vty` should show `transport input ssh`. A Telnet attempt to the device should fail.
WIFI-009.rollback: AireOS: `config network telnet enable` / `config network ssh disable` / `save config`. C9800: `line vty 0 15` / `transport input telnet ssh`. Caveat: re-enabling Telnet restores cleartext transmission of admin credentials and the management session, exposing the entire wireless control plane to sniffing.
WIFI-009.impact: Enabling SSH is non-disruptive. Disabling Telnet drops any active Telnet management session — reconnect over SSH; keep console/OOB access as a fallback. On C9800, generating the RSA key and enabling SSHv2 does not affect data traffic or clients and requires no reload. Applying a management ACL with wrong subnets could lock out admins, so validate first.
references: CIS Cisco Benchmarks (management plane — SSH over Telnet)
NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5: AC-17, IA-2, SC-8
Cisco WLC / IOS-XE hardening guides (SSH configuration)

WIFI-010.risk:
WIFI-010 (MEDIUM) fires when `device_type == wlc` and no config line matches the regex `(management frame protection|mfp|pmf)` — indicating 802.11w Protected Management Frames / Management Frame Protection is not configured anywhere. Finding: `Management Frame Protection (MFP/PMF) not configured`.

802.11 management frames (association, disassociation, deauthentication, action frames) are, by default, unauthenticated and unencrypted. Without 802.11w PMF, an attacker within RF range can forge deauthentication or disassociation frames spoofing the AP's or client's MAC and knock clients off the network at will (ATT&CK T1498 Network Denial of Service; classic aireplay-ng `--deauth` / mdk4 attacks). Deauth is also the enabler for handshake-capture and evil-twin attacks: force a client off, capture the WPA2 4-way handshake on reconnect, then crack offline, or lure the client to a rogue AP. PMF cryptographically protects these management frames (SA Query / MIC), defeating the forged-deauth DoS and hardening the handshake path.

Blast radius: without PMF, wireless availability is trivially deniable for any SSID in range, and the DoS doubles as the first stage of credential/PSK attacks. Enabling PMF is also a prerequisite for WPA3 (which mandates PMF) and for the strongest transition modes.

Platform nuance: on AireOS, infrastructure MFP is `config wps mfp infrastructure enable`, and 802.11w client PMF is per-WLAN: `config wlan security pmf {disable|optional|required} <wlan-id>` (plus association-comeback and SA-query-retry timers). On Catalyst 9800 (IOS-XE) PMF is set under the wlan profile with `security pmf {optional|mandatory}`. Use optional for mixed fleets (protects capable clients, admits legacy) and mandatory/required where all clients support 802.11w (required for WPA3). Because this check is gated on `device_type == wlc`, it targets AireOS. Compliance maps to IEEE 802.11w, NIST 800-53 SC-5 (DoS protection)/SC-40, and Wi-Fi Alliance PMF requirements.
steps: Confirm current MFP/PMF state on AireOS with `show wps summary` (Infrastructure MFP) and `show wlan <id>` (PMF field) — flagged when neither is configured.
Assess client capability: PMF-required needs 802.11w-capable clients; use `optional` first if legacy devices exist to avoid excluding them.
Enable infrastructure MFP globally on AireOS: `config wps mfp infrastructure enable`.
Enable client PMF per WLAN in optional mode initially: `config wlan security pmf optional <wlan-id>`.
Set the PMF timers per WLAN: `config wlan security pmf association-comeback <1-20> <wlan-id>` and `config wlan security pmf saquery-retrytimeout <100-500> <wlan-id>`.
Monitor for a period, then move well-supported SSIDs to required: `config wlan security pmf required <wlan-id>`.
For WPA3 SSIDs, PMF must be required — pair the SAE AKM config with `config wlan security pmf required <wlan-id>` (AireOS) as applicable.
On Catalyst 9800 (IOS-XE), enable PMF under the wlan profile: `wlan <profile> <id> <ssid>` then `security pmf optional` (or `security pmf mandatory`).
On C9800, tune timers if needed: `security pmf association-comeback <seconds>` and `security pmf saquery-retry-time <ms>`.
On C9800, for WPA3 the wlan must use `security pmf mandatory` alongside `security wpa wpa3` / `security wpa akm sae`.
Stage the change during a maintenance window if moving straight to mandatory, since non-802.11w clients will be unable to join.
Save on AireOS: `save config`.
Save on C9800: `end` then `write memory`.
Validate PMF is active: AireOS `show wlan <id>` shows the PMF field as Optional/Required; C9800 `show wlan id <id>` shows `PMF Support` set to Optional/Mandatory.
Test resilience: run a deauth flood in a lab against a PMF-required SSID and confirm capable clients are NOT dropped.
Document PMF policy (optional vs mandatory per SSID) and the WPA3 dependency in the wireless baseline.
WIFI-010.cli:
! AireOS WLC — enable infrastructure MFP + client PMF per WLAN
config wps mfp infrastructure enable
config wlan security pmf optional 1
config wlan security pmf association-comeback 1 1
config wlan security pmf saquery-retrytimeout 200 1
save config

! Catalyst 9800 (IOS-XE) equivalent (per wlan profile)
configure terminal
wlan CORP-PROFILE 1 CORP-SSID
 security pmf optional
end
write memory
WIFI-010.verify: AireOS: `show wlan <id>` should show the PMF field as Optional (or Required) and `show wps summary` should show Infrastructure MFP enabled. C9800: `show wlan id 1` should show `PMF Support` with Optional/Mandatory set. A lab deauth attack should no longer disconnect PMF-protected clients.
WIFI-010.rollback: AireOS: `config wlan security pmf disable <wlan-id>` and `config wps mfp infrastructure disable`. C9800: `wlan CORP-PROFILE 1 CORP-SSID` / `no security pmf`. Caveat: disabling PMF re-exposes the SSID to forged deauth/disassociation DoS and eases handshake-capture/evil-twin attacks; it also breaks WPA3, which requires PMF.
WIFI-010.impact: PMF optional is low-risk — it protects capable clients and still admits legacy ones, so it is safe to enable broadly. PMF mandatory IS disruptive: any client lacking 802.11w support cannot associate, so validate the fleet or use optional first. No controller reload required; changes apply per WLAN.
references: CWE-940 (Improper Verification of Source of a Communication Channel)

CISCO-CVE-001.risk:
The device reports a software release on an affected train (9.16, 9.18, 9.19, 9.20, 7.0, 7.2, 7.4, 7.6) for Cisco ASA, Cisco FTD, which NSS matches to CVE-2025-20333 — ASA/FTD VPN web server arbitrary code execution (ArcaneDoor). Crafted HTTPS requests to the VPN web server allow an authenticated VPN user to execute arbitrary code as root on the ASA/FTD device. Actively exploited by the ArcaneDoor threat actor since 2024.

Cisco scores this CRITICAL at CVSS 9.9. it is listed in the CISA Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog, which carries a binding federal remediation due date and is a strong signal for every organisation to patch on an accelerated timeline. Cisco and/or threat-intel reporting confirm in-the-wild exploitation.

Exploitation requires the WebVPN / AnyConnect SSL VPN web server to be present/enabled on this device. NSS's CVE-reachability pass evaluates that from the configuration and downranks the finding when the feature is disabled — but it never suppresses it, and the KEV floor still applies, because a feature that is off today can be turned on tomorrow.

NSS matches at major.minor train granularity because Cisco renders the per-release 'first fixed' table dynamically and it cannot be scraped offline. This finding therefore flags the affected TRAIN, not a patch-precise build — confirm the exact running version against the Cisco Software Checker in the advisory before deciding whether a fix is already installed. A device already on the fixed maintenance release within this train is not vulnerable and can be marked as a false positive with evidence.
steps: Confirm the exact running version and platform: `show version` (record the full build, e.g. 17.09.04a, not just the 9.16, 9.18, 9.19, 9.20, 7.0, 7.2, 7.4, 7.6 train).

Open the Cisco PSIRT advisory (https://sec.cloudapps.cisco.com/security/center/content/CiscoSecurityAdvisory/cisco-sa-asaftd-webvpn-rce-7BUbXLuM) and load the affected-platform list to confirm this device's model/software is in scope.
Use the Cisco Software Checker (linked in the advisory) with your exact running build to get the FIRST-FIXED release for this train — do not guess the fixed version.
Assess reachability on THIS device: `show running-config | include webvpn|anyconnect|ssl` to confirm whether the vulnerable feature is actually enabled (drives urgency, not whether to patch).
Apply the interim mitigation/workaround from the advisory while you schedule the upgrade: Apply Cisco's September 2025 ASA/FTD security advisory patches immediately.
Assess blast radius and change risk: identify dependent services (VPN, routing, wireless) and book a maintenance window; a software upgrade requires a reload/failover.
Download the first-fixed image from Cisco.com using an entitled account.
Verify image integrity before staging: confirm the MD5/SHA512 published on Cisco.com and, where supported, the digital signature (`verify /md5 <image>` or install-time signature validation).
Back up the current configuration and note the current image so you can roll back: `copy running-config startup-config` and record `show version`/`dir flash:`.
Stage the fixed image and set it as the boot image for the platform (see the Configuration commands block for the platform-correct sequence).
For an HA pair/stack, upgrade the standby/members first and fail over to minimise downtime.
Notify stakeholders, then apply the upgrade and reload during the window.
After reboot, verify the fixed release is running: `show version` should show the first-fixed build (or later) for this train.
Re-run the NSS scan (append fresh `show version` output to the config) and confirm this CVE no longer matches; use `--verify-against` against the pre-change report to prove closure.
If you applied an interim workaround that the fixed release makes unnecessary, remove it and re-test so you do not carry unneeded config.
Record the change in your CMDB / vulnerability tracker. For a KEV-listed CVE, close it by the CISA due date and capture the evidence (before/after version) for audit.
Monitor syslog and device health for several hours post-upgrade for stability or regression.
CISCO-CVE-001.cli:
! On ASA/FTD (ASA software):
show version | include Version
! copy the verified image to disk0, then set boot image and reload:
copy tftp://<server>/<fixed-image>.SPA disk0:
configure terminal
 boot system disk0:/<fixed-image>.SPA
 write memory
reload noconfirm
! FTD managed by FMC: upgrade via System > Updates (push the FTD upgrade package).
CISCO-CVE-001.verify:
show version
! Expected: a build at or above the advisory's first-fixed release for the 9.16, 9.18, 9.19, 9.20, 7.0, 7.2, 7.4, 7.6 train.
show running-config | include webvpn|anyconnect|ssl
! (confirms whether the vulnerable component is enabled on this device)
CISCO-CVE-001.rollback: Reboot to the previously-recorded image (re-set the boot statement to the old image and reload, or 'install rollback' on IOS-XE / 'install all' the prior NX-OS image). Caveat: rolling back re-introduces the vulnerability — only do so to recover from an upgrade defect, and re-apply any interim workaround immediately.
CISCO-CVE-001.impact: Disruptive: a Cisco software upgrade requires a reload (or HA failover), dropping traffic, VPN and management sessions on that device for the duration of the reboot. Plan a maintenance window; use HA/stack member sequencing to reduce the outage.

CISCO-CVE-009.risk:
The device reports a software release on an affected train (8.x, 9.0, 9.1, 9.2, 9.3, 9.4, 9.5, 9.6) for Cisco ASA, which NSS matches to CVE-2018-0101 — ASA WebVPN double-free remote code execution. A double-free in the SSL VPN feature allows an unauthenticated remote attacker to cause a reload or execute arbitrary code on Cisco ASA devices. CVSS 10.0.

Cisco scores this CRITICAL at CVSS 10.0. it is listed in the CISA Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog, which carries a binding federal remediation due date and is a strong signal for every organisation to patch on an accelerated timeline. Cisco and/or threat-intel reporting confirm in-the-wild exploitation.

Exploitation requires the WebVPN / AnyConnect SSL VPN web server to be present/enabled on this device. NSS's CVE-reachability pass evaluates that from the configuration and downranks the finding when the feature is disabled — but it never suppresses it, and the KEV floor still applies, because a feature that is off today can be turned on tomorrow.

NSS matches at major.minor train granularity because Cisco renders the per-release 'first fixed' table dynamically and it cannot be scraped offline. This finding therefore flags the affected TRAIN, not a patch-precise build — confirm the exact running version against the Cisco Software Checker in the advisory before deciding whether a fix is already installed. A device already on the fixed maintenance release within this train is not vulnerable and can be marked as a false positive with evidence.
steps: Confirm the exact running version and platform: `show version` (record the full build, e.g. 17.09.04a, not just the 8.x, 9.0, 9.1, 9.2, 9.3, 9.4, 9.5, 9.6 train).

Open the Cisco PSIRT advisory (https://sec.cloudapps.cisco.com/security/center/content/CiscoSecurityAdvisory/cisco-sa-20180129-asa1) and load the affected-platform list to confirm this device's model/software is in scope.
Use the Cisco Software Checker (linked in the advisory) with your exact running build to get the FIRST-FIXED release for this train — do not guess the fixed version.
Assess reachability on THIS device: `show running-config | include webvpn|anyconnect|ssl` to confirm whether the vulnerable feature is actually enabled (drives urgency, not whether to patch).
Apply the interim mitigation/workaround from the advisory while you schedule the upgrade: Upgrade to 9.6.4.3 / 9.4.4.16 / 9.1.7.20 or later.
Assess blast radius and change risk: identify dependent services (VPN, routing, wireless) and book a maintenance window; a software upgrade requires a reload/failover.
Download the first-fixed image from Cisco.com using an entitled account.
Verify image integrity before staging: confirm the MD5/SHA512 published on Cisco.com and, where supported, the digital signature (`verify /md5 <image>` or install-time signature validation).
Back up the current configuration and note the current image so you can roll back: `copy running-config startup-config` and record `show version`/`dir flash:`.
Stage the fixed image and set it as the boot image for the platform (see the Configuration commands block for the platform-correct sequence).
For an HA pair/stack, upgrade the standby/members first and fail over to minimise downtime.
Notify stakeholders, then apply the upgrade and reload during the window.
After reboot, verify the fixed release is running: `show version` should show the first-fixed build (or later) for this train.
Re-run the NSS scan (append fresh `show version` output to the config) and confirm this CVE no longer matches; use `--verify-against` against the pre-change report to prove closure.
If you applied an interim workaround that the fixed release makes unnecessary, remove it and re-test so you do not carry unneeded config.
Record the change in your CMDB / vulnerability tracker. For a KEV-listed CVE, close it by the CISA due date and capture the evidence (before/after version) for audit.
Monitor syslog and device health for several hours post-upgrade for stability or regression.

CISCO-CVE-015.risk:
The device reports a software release on an affected train (15.2, 15.6, 15.7, 15.9, 16.12, 17.3, 17.6, 17.9, 17.12) for Cisco IOS, Cisco IOS-XE, which NSS matches to CVE-2023-20109 — IOS / IOS-XE GET VPN remote code execution. Insufficient validation of GDOI/G-IKEv2 protocol attributes lets an attacker who has compromised either a key server or a group member execute arbitrary code on a Cisco IOS / IOS-XE group member.

Cisco scores this HIGH at CVSS 6.6. it is listed in the CISA Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog, which carries a binding federal remediation due date and is a strong signal for every organisation to patch on an accelerated timeline. Cisco and/or threat-intel reporting confirm in-the-wild exploitation.

Whether this specific device is exploitable can depend on which features and interfaces are enabled; treat the running train as vulnerable until the fixed release is confirmed installed.

NSS matches at major.minor train granularity because Cisco renders the per-release 'first fixed' table dynamically and it cannot be scraped offline. This finding therefore flags the affected TRAIN, not a patch-precise build — confirm the exact running version against the Cisco Software Checker in the advisory before deciding whether a fix is already installed. A device already on the fixed maintenance release within this train is not vulnerable and can be marked as a false positive with evidence.
steps: Confirm the exact running version and platform: `show version` (record the full build, e.g. 17.09.04a, not just the 15.2, 15.6, 15.7, 15.9, 16.12, 17.3, 17.6, 17.9, 17.12 train).

Open the Cisco PSIRT advisory (https://sec.cloudapps.cisco.com/security/center/content/CiscoSecurityAdvisory/cisco-sa-getvpn-bRTpZmkA) and load the affected-platform list to confirm this device's model/software is in scope.
Use the Cisco Software Checker (linked in the advisory) with your exact running build to get the FIRST-FIXED release for this train — do not guess the fixed version.
Apply the interim mitigation/workaround from the advisory while you schedule the upgrade: Upgrade per the advisory. If GET VPN is not used, the feature can be removed.
Assess blast radius and change risk: identify dependent services (VPN, routing, wireless) and book a maintenance window; a software upgrade requires a reload/failover.
Download the first-fixed image from Cisco.com using an entitled account.
Verify image integrity before staging: confirm the MD5/SHA512 published on Cisco.com and, where supported, the digital signature (`verify /md5 <image>` or install-time signature validation).
Back up the current configuration and note the current image so you can roll back: `copy running-config startup-config` and record `show version`/`dir flash:`.
Stage the fixed image and set it as the boot image for the platform (see the Configuration commands block for the platform-correct sequence).
For an HA pair/stack, upgrade the standby/members first and fail over to minimise downtime.
Notify stakeholders, then apply the upgrade and reload during the window.
After reboot, verify the fixed release is running: `show version` should show the first-fixed build (or later) for this train.
Re-run the NSS scan (append fresh `show version` output to the config) and confirm this CVE no longer matches; use `--verify-against` against the pre-change report to prove closure.
If you applied an interim workaround that the fixed release makes unnecessary, remove it and re-test so you do not carry unneeded config.
Record the change in your CMDB / vulnerability tracker. For a KEV-listed CVE, close it by the CISA due date and capture the evidence (before/after version) for audit.
Monitor syslog and device health for several hours post-upgrade for stability or regression.

Whether this specific device is exploitable can depend on which features and interfaces are enabled; treat the running train as vulnerable until the fixed release is confirmed installed.

NSS matches at major.minor train granularity because Cisco renders the per-release 'first fixed' table dynamically and it cannot be scraped offline. This finding therefore flags the affected TRAIN, not a patch-precise build — confirm the exact running version against the Cisco Software Checker in the advisory before deciding whether a fix is already installed. A device already on the fixed maintenance release within this train is not vulnerable and can be marked as a false positive with evidence.
steps: Confirm the exact running version and platform: `show version` (record the full build, e.g. 17.09.04a, not just the 9.3, 10.2, 10.3 train).

Open the Cisco PSIRT advisory (https://sec.cloudapps.cisco.com/security/center/content/CiscoSecurityAdvisory/cisco-sa-nxos-ebgp-dos-L3QCwVJ) and load the affected-platform list to confirm this device's model/software is in scope.
Use the Cisco Software Checker (linked in the advisory) with your exact running build to get the FIRST-FIXED release for this train — do not guess the fixed version.
Apply the interim mitigation/workaround from the advisory while you schedule the upgrade: Fixed via SMU/upgrade at 9.3(12), 10.2(6), 10.3(4a) — see advisory
Assess blast radius and change risk: identify dependent services (VPN, routing, wireless) and book a maintenance window; a software upgrade requires a reload/failover.
Download the first-fixed image from Cisco.com using an entitled account.
Verify image integrity before staging: confirm the MD5/SHA512 published on Cisco.com and, where supported, the digital signature (`verify /md5 <image>` or install-time signature validation).
Back up the current configuration and note the current image so you can roll back: `copy running-config startup-config` and record `show version`/`dir flash:`.
Stage the fixed image and set it as the boot image for the platform (see the Configuration commands block for the platform-correct sequence).
For an HA pair/stack, upgrade the standby/members first and fail over to minimise downtime.
Notify stakeholders, then apply the upgrade and reload during the window.
After reboot, verify the fixed release is running: `show version` should show the first-fixed build (or later) for this train.
Re-run the NSS scan (append fresh `show version` output to the config) and confirm this CVE no longer matches; use `--verify-against` against the pre-change report to prove closure.
If you applied an interim workaround that the fixed release makes unnecessary, remove it and re-test so you do not carry unneeded config.
Record the change in your CMDB / vulnerability tracker. For a KEV-listed CVE, close it by the CISA due date and capture the evidence (before/after version) for audit.
Monitor syslog and device health for several hours post-upgrade for stability or regression.
CISCO-CVE-046.cli:
show version | include "system:\|NXOS"
! verify the image, then install:
copy tftp://<server>/<fixed-image>.bin bootflash:
install all nxos bootflash:<fixed-image>.bin
! (install all validates compatibility and reloads in sequence)
CISCO-CVE-046.verify:
show version
! Expected: a build at or above the advisory's first-fixed release for the 9.3, 10.2, 10.3 train.
CISCO-CVE-046.rollback: Reboot to the previously-recorded image (re-set the boot statement to the old image and reload, or 'install rollback' on IOS-XE / 'install all' the prior NX-OS image). Caveat: rolling back re-introduces the vulnerability — only do so to recover from an upgrade defect, and re-apply any interim workaround immediately.
CISCO-CVE-046.impact: Disruptive: a Cisco software upgrade requires a reload (or HA failover), dropping traffic, VPN and management sessions on that device for the duration of the reboot. Plan a maintenance window; use HA/stack member sequencing to reduce the outage.

CISCO-CVE-047.risk:
The device reports a software release on an affected train (9.3, 9.4, 10.1, 10.2, 10.3, 10.4) for Cisco NX-OS, which NSS matches to CVE-2025-20111 — Cisco Nexus 3000/9000 Health Monitoring Diagnostics Denial of Service Vulnerability. Incorrect handling of specific Ethernet frames in the health monitoring diagnostics (L2ACLRedirect and RewriteEngineLoopback tests) lets an unauthenticated, adjacent (Layer 2) attacker send a sustained rate of crafted Ethernet frames to reload the device, resulting in DoS. Affects Nexus 3100/3200/3400/3600 and Nexus 9200/9300/9400 in standalone NX-OS mode.

CISCO-CVE-048.risk:
The device reports a software release on an affected train (17.14, 17.15, 17.16, 17.17, 17.18) for Cisco IOS-XE, Cisco WLC, which NSS matches to CVE-2026-20086 — Cisco IOS XE Wireless Controller (Catalyst CW9800) CAPWAP Denial of Service. Network-reachable (AV:N), no auth, no user interaction.

CISCO-CVE-049.risk:
The device reports a software release on an affected train (17.2, 17.3, 17.4, 17.5, 17.6, 17.7) for Cisco IOS-XE, Cisco WLC, which NSS matches to CVE-2024-20303 — Cisco IOS XE WLC Multicast DNS (mDNS) Gateway Denial of Service. Improper handling of mDNS client entries in the multicast DNS gateway feature of Cisco IOS XE Software for Wireless LAN Controllers lets an unauthenticated, adjacent attacker (connected to the wireless network) send continuous crafted mDNS packets, driving high controller CPU that can disconnect APs and cause a DoS. Vulnerable only when mDNS gateway is enabled and APs are in FlexConnect mode. AireOS not affected.

The device reports a software release on an affected train (9.3, 9.4, 10.2, 10.3, 10.4, 10.5) for Cisco NX-OS, which NSS matches to CVE-2024-20397 — Cisco NX-OS Software Image Verification Bypass Vulnerability. CISCO-CVE-053.risk:
The device reports a software release on an affected train (all listed releases) for Cisco IOS, Cisco IOS-XE, which NSS matches to CVE-2018-0171 — Cisco IOS and IOS XE Software Smart Install Remote Code Execution Vulnerability. Improper validation of packet data in the Smart Install client feature allows an unauthenticated remote attacker sending crafted messages to TCP/4786 to cause a buffer overflow, reloading the device (DoS) or executing arbitrary code. Actively exploited (including by Salt Typhoon against unpatched switches); on CISA KEV.

Cisco scores this CRITICAL at CVSS 9.8. it is listed in the CISA Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog, which carries a binding federal remediation due date and is a strong signal for every organisation to patch on an accelerated timeline. Cisco and/or threat-intel reporting confirm in-the-wild exploitation.

Exploitation requires the Smart Install client (vstack, TCP/4786) to be present/enabled on this device. NSS's CVE-reachability pass evaluates that from the configuration and downranks the finding when the feature is disabled — but it never suppresses it, and the KEV floor still applies, because a feature that is off today can be turned on tomorrow.

NSS matches at major.minor train granularity because Cisco renders the per-release 'first fixed' table dynamically and it cannot be scraped offline. This finding therefore flags the affected TRAIN, not a patch-precise build — confirm the exact running version against the Cisco Software Checker in the advisory before deciding whether a fix is already installed. A device already on the fixed maintenance release within this train is not vulnerable and can be marked as a false positive with evidence.
steps: Confirm the exact running version and platform: `show version` (record the full build, e.g. 17.09.04a, not just the all listed releases train).

Use the Cisco Software Checker (linked in the advisory) with your exact running build to get the FIRST-FIXED release for this train — do not guess the fixed version.

Check the advisory's 'Workarounds' section; if a documented workaround exists (e.g. disabling the vulnerable feature or an ACL), apply it as interim mitigation.
Assess blast radius and change risk: identify dependent services (VPN, routing, wireless) and book a maintenance window; a software upgrade requires a reload/failover.
Download the first-fixed image from Cisco.com using an entitled account.
Verify image integrity before staging: confirm the MD5/SHA512 published on Cisco.com and, where supported, the digital signature (`verify /md5 <image>` or install-time signature validation).
Back up the current configuration and note the current image so you can roll back: `copy running-config startup-config` and record `show version`/`dir flash:`.
Stage the fixed image and set it as the boot image for the platform (see the Configuration commands block for the platform-correct sequence).
For an HA pair/stack, upgrade the standby/members first and fail over to minimise downtime.
Notify stakeholders, then apply the upgrade and reload during the window.
After reboot, verify the fixed release is running: `show version` should show the first-fixed build (or later) for this train.
Re-run the NSS scan (append fresh `show version` output to the config) and confirm this CVE no longer matches; use `--verify-against` against the pre-change report to prove closure.
If you applied an interim workaround that the fixed release makes unnecessary, remove it and re-test so you do not carry unneeded config.
Record the change in your CMDB / vulnerability tracker. For a KEV-listed CVE, close it by the CISA due date and capture the evidence (before/after version) for audit.
Monitor syslog and device health for several hours post-upgrade for stability or regression.

For an HA pair/stack, upgrade the standby/members first and fail over to minimise downtime.
Notify stakeholders, then apply the upgrade and reload during the window.
After reboot, verify the fixed release is running: `show version` should show the first-fixed build (or later) for this train.
Re-run the NSS scan (append fresh `show version` output to the config) and confirm this CVE no longer matches; use `--verify-against` against the pre-change report to prove closure.
If you applied an interim workaround that the fixed release makes unnecessary, remove it and re-test so you do not carry unneeded config.
Record the change in your CMDB / vulnerability tracker. For a KEV-listed CVE, close it by the CISA due date and capture the evidence (before/after version) for audit.
Monitor syslog and device health for several hours post-upgrade for stability or regression.
CISCO-CVE-058.cli:
! On ASA/FTD (ASA software):
show version | include Version
! copy the verified image to disk0, then set boot image and reload:
copy tftp://<server>/<fixed-image>.SPA disk0:
configure terminal
 boot system disk0:/<fixed-image>.SPA
 write memory
reload noconfirm
! FTD managed by FMC: upgrade via System > Updates (push the FTD upgrade package).
CISCO-CVE-058.verify:
show version
! Expected: a build at or above the advisory's first-fixed release for the 9.7, 9.8, 9.9, 9.10, 9.12, 9.13, 9.14, 9.15, 6.4, 6.6, 6.7 train.
show running-config | include webvpn|anyconnect|ssl
! (confirms whether the vulnerable component is enabled on this device)
CISCO-CVE-058.rollback: Reboot to the previously-recorded image (re-set the boot statement to the old image and reload, or 'install rollback' on IOS-XE / 'install all' the prior NX-OS image). Caveat: rolling back re-introduces the vulnerability — only do so to recover from an upgrade defect, and re-apply any interim workaround immediately.
CISCO-CVE-058.impact: Disruptive: a Cisco software upgrade requires a reload (or HA failover), dropping traffic, VPN and management sessions on that device for the duration of the reboot. Plan a maintenance window; use HA/stack member sequencing to reduce the outage.
